The exact molecular reason for this selectivity is still debated, but the empirical skew is why it's favoured for lean gains and by women.

mechSteps.t: At the muscle, it switches protein-building genes ON
mechSteps.predict: The androgen receptor isn't a channel or a brake — it's a switch that does what, exactly?
mechSteps.d: The activated androgen receptor travels into the cell nucleus, binds DNA, and turns up genes for muscle protein synthesis. This is transcription, not a fast hit — which is why lean mass accrues over weeks of dosing and why the felt effect isn't tied to the blood-level clock.

mechSteps.t: Your brain reads the androgen and pulls back
mechSteps.predict: Your body constantly measures its own androgen level. What does it do when a drug raises that signal?
mechSteps.d: The hypothalamus and pituitary sense androgen present and throttle down LH and FSH — the hormones that tell your testes to make testosterone. In the HIV-wasting trial this was measured directly: significant suppression of LH, FSH, SHBG, and total and free testosterone. Even the "mild" oral shuts down your own production.

framework.target: The androgen receptor (AR) — a nuclear hormone receptor that acts as a gene switch. Oxandrolone is a full steroidal agonist: it turns AR on. Crucially, unlike a SARM, it is NOT tissue-selective at the receptor — it's a steroid that engages AR body-wide. Its "mildness" comes from low androgenic potency and non-aromatisation, not from sparing any tissue.
framework.affinity: Here's the honest surprise: oxandrolone's raw binding affinity for AR is actually modest — classic assays put its relative binding well below reference androgens — yet its anabolic bioassay potency is high (an anabolic:androgenic ratio often cited around 300–600 : 24 versus methyltestosterone). So affinity alone doesn't predict its effect. Unlike caffeine, where you ask whether a real dose even reaches the target, the informative number here isn't a Ki — it's that anabolic-to-androgenic ratio.
framework.adme: Orally active because the 17α-methyl group resists first-pass breakdown; peak plasma at roughly 1 h; elimination half-life ≈ 9–10 h (longer than most oral steroids, and longer in the elderly). About a quarter of a dose leaves unchanged in urine. Non-aromatisable — no estrogen conversion. But note: the anabolic effect is set by weeks of transcription, not the blood curve.
framework.genetics: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic switch. AR sensitivity varies generally with the AR gene's CAG-repeat length, but this has never been characterised for oxandrolone specifically — so treat individual response and side-effect susceptibility as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Moderate and honest (3/5). Real randomised controlled trials exist — but in clinical wasting populations (severe burns, HIV-associated weight loss, Klinefelter syndrome), where it reliably adds lean mass and speeds recovery. There is essentially no controlled evidence — and no long-term safety data — for healthy people using it to build physique.

pk.onset: days to weeks — the anabolic effect is gene transcription, not an acute hit
pk.peak: plasma peak ~1 h after an oral dose
pk.duration: twice-daily dosing was used in trials to maintain levels
pk.note: Don't read this half-life like a stimulant's stopwatch. It sets how often you'd dose to keep blood levels steady, but lean mass accrues over weeks of continuous AR activation — and HPTA suppression and lipid/liver effects build over that same slow timescale rather than clearing overnight.

journey.d: You swallow it. It has a legitimate prescription history (burns, HIV wasting), but as a gray-market physique product, independent testing repeatedly finds oral "anavar" mislabeled, underdosed, or replaced with a cheaper, harsher steroid. Its 17α-methyl armour lets it survive first-pass and reach the blood.
It circulates to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues. With a ~9–10 h half-life, twice-daily dosing keeps levels steady rather than spiking and vanishing.

It binds the androgen receptor, drives it into the nucleus, and turns up muscle-protein-synthesis genes. Over weeks this is where lean mass rises — with little estrogenic water weight, because it can't aromatise. This is the 'dry' look people chase.

The hypothalamus and pituitary detect the androgen signal and throttle back LH and FSH, so your own testosterone winds down. Trials measured this directly: suppressed LH, FSH, SHBG and testosterone. This shutdown outlasts the last dose, and recovery is variable.

The very liver-resistance that makes it an oral is what burdens the organ. In the HIV trial transaminases rose significantly; a 2025 meta-analysis of burn trials found hepatotoxicity in ~19% on oxandrolone vs ~5% on placebo. HDL falls and LDL climbs — a genuine cardiovascular cost.

About a quarter leaves unchanged in urine, the rest as metabolites — and this is why athletes get caught. Oxandrolone is a Schedule III controlled substance and WADA-banned in and out of competition, with metabolites detectable long after use.

myths.myth: Anavar is the "mild" steroid, so it's basically safe.
myths.truth: "Mild" refers to low androgenicity and no estrogen conversion — not to safety. It still suppresses your own testosterone (measured in trials), lowers HDL and raises LDL, and stresses the liver. Gentler on some fronts, genuinely harmful on others.

myths.myth: It doesn't aromatise, so it won't mess with your hormones.
myths.truth: No estrogen conversion is true — but hormonal shutdown doesn't run through estrogen. Your brain suppresses LH/FSH in response to the androgen itself, so your natural testosterone falls regardless. Non-aromatising and non-suppressive are two completely different things.

myths.myth: It's a "women's steroid," so women can take it safely.
myths.truth: It's favoured in women because the virilisation risk is lower than with other steroids — not zero. Masculinising effects (voice deepening, clitoral enlargement, hair changes) still occur in a dose- and time-dependent way, and some are irreversible.

myths.myth: Being "liver-resistant" means it's easy on the liver.
myths.truth: Backwards. The 17α-alkyl group makes it resist breakdown so it survives as a pill — and that same resistance is precisely what burdens the liver. Trials show significant transaminase elevation and a cholestatic pattern; burn meta-analysis puts hepatotoxicity near 19% vs 5% on placebo.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is "lean, dry strength gains with less water, less gyno, and less virilisation than harsher steroids — in a pill." The lean-mass part has genuine trial support in wasting patients; the 'mild = safe' part is the trap.

**The hormonal cost:** it suppresses your own testosterone. Trials directly measured lowered LH, FSH, SHBG, and total and free testosterone. Recovery after stopping is variable and not well characterised.
**The cardiovascular cost:** it markedly lowers HDL ("good") cholesterol and raises LDL — a well-documented, expected androgen effect that matters over time.

**The liver cost:** as a 17α-alkylated oral it raises hepatic transaminases and can cause cholestasis; a 2025 burn-trial meta-analysis found hepatotoxicity in ~19% of treated patients vs ~5% on placebo.
**The legal and testing cost:** it's a Schedule III controlled substance and WADA-banned in and out of competition, with metabolites detectable long after use. For a tested athlete this ends seasons.
**The product-quality cost:** gray-market oral "anavar" is frequently mislabeled, underdosed, or swapped for a cheaper, harsher steroid — so you often don't know what you're actually taking.

**The honest evidence picture:** the real human data is in burns, HIV wasting and Klinefelter syndrome — clinical populations losing muscle. There is essentially no controlled safety data for healthy people using it to build physique. This page explains the pharmacology; it is not a cycle guide.

start.q: In one line: what does "mild" actually mean for oxandrolone?
start.a: Low androgenic (masculinising) potency plus no estrogen conversion — gentler on those fronts, but it still suppresses your own testosterone, lowers HDL, and stresses the liver. Mild is not safe.

how.q: Why can oxandrolone be swallowed as a pill when most steroids must be injected — and what's the catch?
how.a: A 17α-methyl group makes it resist the liver's first-pass breakdown, so enough survives to work orally. The catch: that same liver-resistance is exactly what burdens the liver (rising transaminases, cholestasis in trials).

use.q: Why does its ~9–10 h half-life tell you little about how fast you'll see results?
use.a: Because the anabolic effect isn't a blood-level phenomenon — it's the androgen receptor turning genes on over weeks. The half-life sets dosing frequency; muscle gain (and HPTA suppression) run on the slow transcription clock.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, and in whom?
evidence.a: Moderate (3/5), and concentrated in clinical wasting: severe burns, HIV-associated weight loss, Klinefelter syndrome. It reliably adds lean mass and speeds recovery there — but with hepatotoxicity and lipid worsening, and there's no safety data for healthy physique use.

canExplain: Why "mild" describes low androgenicity and no estrogen conversion — not safety
Why a 17α-methyl group makes it an oral, and why that same feature stresses the liver
Why it still shuts down your own testosterone even though it can't convert to estrogen
Why its effect builds over weeks (gene transcription) rather than tracking the blood level
Where the real human evidence comes from — burns, HIV wasting, Klinefelter — and why that doesn't validate physique use

selfTest.q: A friend says Anavar is safe because "it's mild and doesn't aromatise." Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) "Mild" and "doesn't aromatise" describe low androgenicity and no estrogen conversion — not safety. (2) HPTA shutdown runs through the androgen signal, not estrogen, so your own testosterone still falls; and it lowers HDL, raises LDL, and stresses the liver via its 17α-alkyl group.

selfTest.q: Injectable steroids exist because the liver destroys oral androgens. What single modification makes oxandrolone an effective pill, and what price does that feature carry?
selfTest.a: The 17α-methyl group blocks rapid hepatic breakdown so it survives first-pass. The price is hepatic strain — transaminase elevation and cholestasis, seen in trials and meta-analysis — because liver-resistance and liver-burden are two sides of the same molecule.

selfTest.q: Oxandrolone binds the androgen receptor only modestly, yet is a potent anabolic. What number captures its real character, and why?
selfTest.a: Its anabolic-to-androgenic ratio (bioassay), not a binding Ki. Raw AR affinity is modest, but the DHT base plus 2-oxa A-ring give strong muscle-building relative to masculinising effect and no estrogen conversion — so the ratio, not the affinity, is what defines it.

evi.howStrong: For an anabolic steroid, oxandrolone has an unusually real clinical evidence base — multiple randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials — but the key caveat is WHERE that evidence comes from. The trials are in clinical wasting: severe burns, HIV-associated weight loss, and paediatric/Klinefelter growth. In those settings it consistently adds lean mass and speeds recovery. What does NOT exist is controlled evidence, or any long-term safety data, for healthy adults using it to build physique — that use is entirely off-label and gray-market.
evi.whatItShows: Across trials the anabolic signal is consistent: oxandrolone increases lean body mass and body cell mass versus placebo, and in burns it accelerates recovery. But the clinically important endpoints are mixed — in burns it shortens hospital stay without improving survival, and it raises hepatotoxicity. So 'it builds muscle' is well supported; 'it's benign' is not.
evi.effect: Concrete numbers from the trials: in the multicentre burn RCT, hospital length of stay was shorter on oxandrolone (~31.6 days) than placebo (~43.3 days), and the trial was stopped early at interim for benefit. In the HIV-wasting trial, body cell mass gains exceeded placebo at the 40 and 80 mg doses — but alongside significant transaminase rises, higher LDL, lower HDL, and suppression of LH, FSH, SHBG and testosterone. A 2025 burn meta-analysis put hepatotoxicity at ~19% vs ~5% on placebo.
evi.whoBenefits: The studied beneficiaries are catabolic patients losing muscle: severe-burn patients, HIV-associated weight loss, and children/adolescents with growth or Klinefelter indications. Its use in healthy people for bodybuilding is off-label, unstudied for safety, and legally controlled.
evi.caveats: Read the numbers as best-case, under dosed and monitored clinical conditions — not what a mislabeled gray-market capsule delivers. The benefits ride alongside near-universal HPTA suppression, an adverse HDL/LDL shift, documented hepatotoxicity, dose-dependent virilisation in women, a Schedule III legal status, and a WADA ban. 'Mild' steroid, real costs.

trials.finding: Multicentre RCT in 81 adults with 20–60% TBSA burns: oxandrolone 10 mg every 12 h shortened hospital length of stay (~31.6 vs ~43.3 days) versus placebo; the trial was stopped early at interim analysis for benefit, with a recommendation to monitor hepatic transaminases.
trials.ref: Wolf et al., 2006, J Burn Care Res 27(2):131–139

trials.finding: RCT in 262 men with HIV-associated weight loss (20/40/80 mg/day): gains in body weight and body cell mass exceeded placebo at 40 mg (and body cell mass at 40–80 mg), but with significant rises in transaminases and LDL, a fall in HDL, and suppression of SHBG, LH, FSH and total/free testosterone.
trials.ref: HIV-associated weight loss RCT, 2006, J Acquir Immune Defic Syndr (PubMed 16540931)

trials.finding: Systematic review and updated meta-analysis of burn RCTs (2005–2025): oxandrolone significantly improved lean mass and shortened hospitalisation, with no mortality benefit and increased hepatotoxicity (~19% vs ~5%, p=0.002).
trials.ref: 2025 systematic review & meta-analysis, World J Emerg Surg 20:648-series (doi:10.1186/s13017-025-00648-w)

tech.chem: Oxandrolone (CAS 53-39-4; PubChem CID 5878) is 17β-hydroxy-17α-methyl-2-oxa-5α-androstan-3-one — a synthetic derivative of dihydrotestosterone (DHT). Two defining modifications: carbon-2 of the steroid A-ring is replaced by an oxygen atom (the '2-oxa' feature that gives the drug its name and its anabolic skew), and a methyl group is added at 17α (17α-alkylation) to resist first-pass hepatic metabolism and enable oral activity. Being a 5α-reduced (DHT-based) steroid, it lacks the structure needed for aromatisation, so it cannot convert to estrogen.

targets.action: full steroidal agonist (not tissue-selective at the receptor)
targets.affinity: raw relative binding affinity for AR is modest — well below reference androgens like metribolone in classic assays — yet anabolic bioassay potency is high; the defining figure is an anabolic:androgenic ratio often cited around 300–600 : 24 vs methyltestosterone, not a binding Ki
targets.role: the sole primary target; a nuclear hormone receptor / ligand-activated transcription factor. Anabolic activation in muscle and bone with comparatively low androgenic (prostate/skin/scalp) and no estrogenic activity.

tech.signaling: The androgen receptor is a nuclear hormone receptor. Unliganded, it sits in the cytoplasm complexed with chaperones (e.g. HSP90). Ligand binding triggers chaperone release, receptor dimerisation, nuclear translocation, and binding to androgen-response elements in DNA, driving transcription of anabolic genes — a slow (days–weeks) program, not an acute signal. Systemically, the same androgen signal engages hypothalamic–pituitary negative feedback, suppressing GnRH/LH/FSH and hence endogenous testosterone; this HPTA suppression was measured directly in the HIV-wasting trial.

adme.absorb: Orally active; the 17α-methyl group resists first-pass hepatic breakdown, and plasma peaks at roughly 1 h.
adme.distribute: Distributes to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues including skeletal muscle and bone; being 5α-reduced it is not an aromatase substrate, so no estrogenic distribution effects.
adme.metabolise: Hepatic, but less extensively than non-alkylated androgens (the point of the 17α-methyl group); partial metabolism includes sulfation to 17-epioxandrolone. This liver-resistance is mechanistically tied to its hepatotoxic potential (transaminase elevation, cholestasis).
adme.excrete: About 28% is excreted unchanged in urine, the remainder as metabolites; elimination half-life ≈ 9–10 h (longer, ~13 h, in older adults). Parent and metabolites underpin WADA anti-doping detection.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. AR sensitivity varies generally with AR-gene CAG-repeat length, but this has not been characterised for oxandrolone, so individual response and side-effect susceptibility should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: Trial/clinical doses were monitored: severe burns used 10 mg twice daily (paediatric burns ~0.1 mg/kg twice daily); the HIV-wasting program used 20–80 mg/day. Gray-market physique use is commonly cited around 20–80 mg/day, at doses never validated for safety in healthy people — this is a description, not a recommendation. It is a Schedule III controlled substance with legitimate approved indications (e.g. weight regain after catabolic illness).
tech.tolerance: There is no receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense. The dominant time-dependent consequences are cumulative HPTA suppression (falling endogenous testosterone), an adverse HDL/LDL shift, and hepatic strain that build with continued use and recover variably after stopping — this, not tachyphylaxis, is what governs 'on/off' cycling in practice. Counterfeit and mislabeled gray-market product adds a further layer of unpredictability.

contrasts.title: Oxandrolone vs testosterone — a filed-and-armoured key vs the master key
contrasts.point: Same lock (the androgen receptor), different reach. Testosterone is the steroid master key that turns every androgen lock — muscle, prostate, scalp, skin — and aromatises to estrogen (water, breast tissue). Oxandrolone is that key filed down (low androgenicity, so weaker prostate/scalp effects) and armour-plated (17α-methyl, so it survives as a pill), and it can't aromatise (no estrogen). Milder masculinising bite and oral convenience — but the master key's biggest alarm still trips on both: your brain sees an androgen in the lock and shuts down your own production, and the armour is what costs the liver.

contrasts.title: Oxandrolone vs a SARM (ostarine) — a re-tuned steroid vs a non-steroid
contrasts.point: Both chase the same idea — muscle without the full masculinising toll — by different routes. Oxandrolone is a true steroid that engages AR body-wide and gets its 'mildness' from low androgenic potency plus non-aromatisation. A SARM like ostarine is a non-steroidal molecule that tries to build selectivity into the receptor's shape itself. Different chemistry, same unavoidable catch: both still suppress your own testosterone and lower HDL, because both feed the same androgen signal your brain is monitoring.

questions: Why do bodybuilders run "Winny" for the last weeks before a show, but almost never for off-season bulking?
Why does a steroid that grips the androgen receptor only weakly still build muscle — and still shut down your own testosterone?
Why does that dry, hard, photo-ready look come bundled with aching joints and a wrecked cholesterol panel?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Stanozolol can't convert to estrogen, so it adds no water — you get a flat, dry, hard look with no bloat, which is exactly what a stage or photoshoot rewards and off-season growth doesn't need. (2) Its own grip on the androgen receptor is weak (~22% of DHT's), but it also crashes SHBG — the protein that keeps circulating androgen locked up — so it frees up more of your existing hormone; and any androgen signal, from any source, is read by your brain as "enough," which shuts down LH/FSH and your own testosterone. (3) It's 17α-alkylated to survive as a pill, and that same modification punishes the liver and collapses HDL cholesterol; the joint pain is a well-known anecdotal cost of the "dry" state, though the exact mechanism has never been pinned down.

stanozolol-winstrol.bigIdea: Stanozolol's signature isn't raw size — it's a dry, hard look, achieved by never converting to estrogen and by crashing the protein (SHBG) that keeps your androgens locked up, all bought with real liver, cholesterol, and joint costs.
stanozolol-winstrol.analogy: Picture SHBG as a fleet of taxis that keep your sex hormones locked in the back seat, inactive while they ride around. Stanozolol slashes the number of taxis, so more hormone spills out onto the street — free and active. And because its shape can't be converted to estrogen, none of that androgen turns into the hormone that would puff you up with water. More free signal, zero bloat: the dry, hard look.

mechSteps.t: Built from DHT — and locked out of estrogen
mechSteps.predict: Testosterone can be converted into estrogen, which holds water. Stanozolol is built on a different backbone with a fused pyrazole ring. What does that do to the "dry vs bloated" question?
mechSteps.d: Stanozolol is derived from dihydrotestosterone (DHT) with a pyrazole ring fused to its A-ring. That structure can't be touched by aromatase, the enzyme that turns testosterone into estrogen. No estrogen means no water retention — the flat, dry, hard look it's chased for.

mechSteps.t: A 17α-methyl group lets it survive as a pill
mechSteps.predict: Swallowed testosterone is destroyed by the liver before it works. Stanozolol carries an added methyl group at carbon 17. What does that buy — and what does it cost?
mechSteps.d: The 17α-alkyl group blocks the liver from breaking it down on the first pass, so it works orally (~9 h half-life). But that same hard-to-metabolise modification is exactly what makes 17α-alkylated orals harsh on the liver.

mechSteps.t: It crashes SHBG, freeing your androgens
mechSteps.predict: Most of your circulating androgen is bound up and inactive, carried by a protein called SHBG. What happens to the "free," active fraction if you sharply lower that protein?
mechSteps.d: Stanozolol lowers SHBG sharply. SHBG is the carrier that keeps testosterone and other androgens bound and inactive — drop it, and the free, active fraction of whatever androgen is present rises. This is a major part of how a weak receptor-binder still produces a strong androgenic effect.

mechSteps.t: It grips the androgen receptor — weakly — and turns genes on
mechSteps.predict: Its affinity for the androgen receptor is only about a fifth of DHT's. So is the receptor even the whole story here?
mechSteps.d: Stanozolol binds the androgen receptor as an agonist, but only weakly (~22% of DHT's affinity). The bound receptor moves into the nucleus and turns up muscle-protein genes — transcription, which is why the effect builds over weeks. But the weak grip is a clue: much of its punch comes from the SHBG crash and free-hormone rise, not receptor occupancy alone.

mechSteps.t: Your brain reads the signal and shuts you down
mechSteps.predict: Your body constantly measures how much androgen is around. What does it do when a drug keeps that signal high?
mechSteps.d: The hypothalamus and pituitary sense androgen and dial down LH and FSH — the hormones that tell your testes to make testosterone. So even a "cutting" steroid suppresses your own production. That's HPTA suppression, and it's the unavoidable hormonal cost.

framework.target: The androgen receptor (AR) — a nuclear hormone receptor that acts as a gene switch. Stanozolol is an agonist: it turns AR on rather than blocking it. But note a second, non-receptor lever unusual for this class: it sharply lowers SHBG, the binding protein, raising the free (active) fraction of circulating androgen.
framework.affinity: Relative binding to AR is only ~22% of DHT's — a genuinely weak binder, the opposite of a high-affinity SARM like enobosarm. That weakness is the tell: stanozolol's real-world androgenic effect leans heavily on crashing SHBG (freeing existing hormone) and on high oral exposure, not on gripping the receptor hard.
framework.adme: 17α-alkylated, so it's orally active with an oral elimination half-life of ~9 h (the aqueous injectable runs longer, ~24 h). Non-aromatising, hepatically metabolised. The felt effect isn't set by the blood curve — it's weeks of gene transcription plus a sustained SHBG drop.
framework.genetics: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic switch. AR sensitivity varies generally with the AR gene's CAG-repeat length, but this has not been characterised for stanozolol — treat individual response as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Mixed and narrow (3/5). There are real controlled human trials — but for hereditary angioedema, where it raises C1-inhibitor and C4, not for physique. Its muscle-building use in healthy people is off-label, gray-market, and essentially unstudied for either benefit size or long-term safety.

pk.onset: days to weeks — the anabolic effect is gene transcription, not an acute hit
pk.duration: oral effect requires daily (often split) dosing to hold blood levels
pk.note: Don't read the ~9 h half-life like a stimulant's stopwatch. It only sets how often you'd have to redose to keep blood levels up — lean tissue, the SHBG crash, and HPTA suppression all run on the slow transcription/protein timescale of weeks, not hours.

journey.d: You take it — an oral tablet or the aqueous "Depot" injection. Real caution: gray-market steroid products are frequently mislabeled, underdosed, or contaminated, so what's in the bottle often isn't what the label claims. The 17α-methyl group lets the oral form survive the first pass through the liver.

journey.stage: 🩸 The blood — and the SHBG crash
journey.d: It circulates and sharply lowers SHBG, the carrier protein that keeps androgens bound and inactive. Free, active androgen rises. Because it can't be aromatised, none of it converts to estrogen — so no water is held, and the look stays flat and dry.

It binds the androgen receptor (weakly) and drives it into the nucleus, turning up muscle-protein genes. Over weeks this is where the hard, lean look develops — modest true mass, but no bloat masking it.
The hypothalamus and pituitary detect the androgen signal and throttle back LH and FSH, so your own testosterone production winds down. This suppression outlasts the last dose.

The 17α-alkyl group that made it oral now makes it hepatotoxic. Documented consequences of 17α-alkylated steroids include cholestatic drug-induced liver injury, sharply lowered HDL ("good") cholesterol, and — with prolonged use — peliosis hepatis (blood-filled cysts) and hepatic tumors.

Metabolites leave in urine — and this is where athletes get caught. Stanozolol is one of the most historically famous doping positives (Ben Johnson, Seoul 1988); it's WADA-banned and its metabolites are detectable long after use.

myths.myth: Stanozolol is a mild, cosmetic "cutting" drug, so it's relatively safe.
myths.truth: "Mild" describes its lack of bloat, not its safety. As a 17α-alkylated oral it's one of the harsher steroids on the liver and on cholesterol — it strongly suppresses HDL and has caused documented cholestatic liver injury. Dry-looking is not the same as low-risk.

myths.myth: It builds huge slabs of muscle.
myths.truth: Its appeal is the dry, hard, defined look, not raw size. Because it doesn't aromatise, it adds no water-driven bulk, and its receptor binding is actually weak — so honest expectations are a modest, cosmetic lean-tissue effect, not dramatic mass.

myths.myth: It "lubricates" or heals joints.
myths.truth: The opposite reputation is more common — users blame it for dry, aching joints. But be honest about the evidence: that joint pain is anecdotal and its mechanism has never been established. Ironically, in laboratory (in-vitro) studies stanozolol actually stimulates collagen synthesis via TGF-β1. So neither the "heals joints" nor a proven "dries joints" mechanism is settled.

myths.myth: It's not really a steroid, just a fitness supplement.
myths.truth: It's a full anabolic-androgenic steroid derived from DHT, a controlled substance, WADA-banned, and prescription-only where approved (for hereditary angioedema). It is not a supplement.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a "dry, hard, vascular" look for a contest or photoshoot — no water retention, no estrogen bloat, taken as a pill. The no-bloat part is real (it can't aromatise); the "mild and safe" part is not.

**The liver cost:** as a 17α-alkylated oral it's genuinely hepatotoxic — documented cholestatic liver injury, and with prolonged use the risk of peliosis hepatis (blood-filled liver cysts) and hepatic tumors.
**The cholesterol cost:** it strongly lowers HDL ("good") cholesterol, a real cardiovascular hit that's characteristic of oral 17α-alkylated steroids.
**The hormonal cost:** like any androgen, it suppresses your own testosterone (HPTA shutdown); recovery after stopping is variable.

**The joint/tendon cost:** aching, dry-feeling joints are a widely reported (if mechanistically unexplained) complaint, and anabolic steroids are broadly linked to tendon injury — take that seriously even though the human data are thin.
**The legal and testing cost:** it's a controlled substance and one of the most famous WADA doping positives in history; a positive test ends seasons and careers.

**The honest evidence picture:** the real controlled human trials are for hereditary angioedema, not muscle-building. Physique use is off-label and gray-market, with essentially no long-term safety data in healthy people. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a cycle.

start.q: In one line: what makes stanozolol produce a "dry" look rather than a bloated one?
start.a: It can't be aromatised into estrogen, so it holds no water — you get a flat, dry, hard look instead of a puffy one.

how.q: Its grip on the androgen receptor is weak (~22% of DHT). So how does it still produce a strong androgenic effect?
how.a: It sharply lowers SHBG, the protein that keeps circulating androgens bound and inactive — raising the free, active fraction. Much of its punch comes from that free-hormone rise plus high oral exposure, not from receptor affinity alone.

use.q: Why is the 17α-methyl group a double-edged sword?
use.a: It lets the drug survive the liver so it works as a pill — but that same resistance to metabolism is what makes 17α-alkylated orals hepatotoxic and hard on HDL cholesterol.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really — and for what?
evidence.a: The controlled human trials are for hereditary angioedema (it raises C1-inhibitor and C4), not physique. Its muscle-building use in healthy people is off-label and essentially unstudied for benefit or long-term safety.

canExplain: Why stanozolol gives a dry, hard look instead of a bloated one (it can't aromatise to estrogen)
How a weak receptor-binder still hits hard — by crashing SHBG and freeing existing androgen
Why the 17α-methyl group makes it work as a pill but also makes it hepatotoxic and bad for HDL
Why even a "cutting" steroid still shuts down your own testosterone
What the human evidence actually covers (hereditary angioedema) versus what it doesn't (physique use)

selfTest.q: A friend says stanozolol is "safe because it's mild and doesn't bloat you." Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) "Doesn't bloat" just means it can't convert to estrogen, so it holds no water — that's a cosmetic feature, not a safety feature. (2) As a 17α-alkylated oral it's actually one of the harsher steroids on the liver (cholestatic injury) and cholesterol (sharply lowered HDL), and it still suppresses your own testosterone.

selfTest.q: Stanozolol binds the androgen receptor only ~22% as strongly as DHT. Why doesn't that make it weak in practice?
selfTest.a: Because receptor affinity isn't its only lever. It sharply lowers SHBG, the carrier protein that keeps androgens bound and inactive, so the free active fraction of circulating hormone rises — and high oral bioavailability keeps exposure up. The clinical androgenic effect leans on those routes, not on receptor grip alone.

selfTest.q: Why does the ~9 h oral half-life tell you almost nothing about how fast you'll see the physique effect?
selfTest.a: Because the effect isn't a blood-level phenomenon like a stimulant — it's gene transcription (plus a sustained SHBG drop) that builds over weeks. The half-life only sets how often you'd have to redose to keep blood levels up; muscle and hormonal changes run on the slow transcription timescale.

evi.howStrong: The honest picture is narrow. Stanozolol has real, controlled human trials — but almost entirely for hereditary angioedema (HAE), a complement disorder, where it works by raising C1-esterase-inhibitor and C4. For the thing people actually take it for — building a dry, muscular physique in healthy people — there is essentially no controlled trial evidence and no long-term safety data. The site's 3/5 rating reflects that split: solid clinical data for a use unrelated to bodybuilding, and near-anecdote for the bodybuilding use itself.
evi.whatItShows: In HAE, long-term stanozolol reduces attack frequency and raises the deficient complement proteins — a genuine, reproducible clinical effect at low doses (0.5–2 mg/day). What the literature does NOT establish is a quantified muscle or performance benefit in healthy trained people, or the safety of the much higher doses used off-label.
evi.effect: The best-documented numbers are from the HAE setting: maintenance doses of 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/day corresponded to attack frequencies of roughly one every 8, 7, and 15 weeks respectively. For physique use, honest framing is that the effect is a modest, cosmetic dry/lean look rather than a large mass gain — and there's no clean trial number to quote for it.
evi.whoBenefits: The studied beneficiaries are HAE patients (and it has historical use in some anemias and hereditary conditions). Use for muscularity in healthy people is entirely off-label and unstudied for safety.
evi.caveats: Watch the gap between the evidence and the use: controlled data exist at 0.5–2 mg/day for a disease, while gray-market physique use runs far higher, unmonitored, with products that are frequently mislabeled or contaminated. Even the reassuring HAE safety data show virilization and menstrual changes in women and raised liver/muscle enzymes; extrapolating that to high-dose bodybuilding use understates the risk. Treat any physique claim as anecdote, not trial-backed fact.

trials.finding: 27 hereditary-angioedema patients treated with 0.5–2 mg/day stanozolol over 2 years: minimal effective doses controlled attack frequency and produced statistically significant rises in C1-inhibitor function and C4, with virilization/menstrual changes in some women and raised creatine phosphokinase in some men.
trials.ref: Sheffer, Fearon & Austen, 1981, J Allergy Clin Immunol

trials.finding: Long-term safety review of hereditary-angioedema patients treated with stanozolol for 20–40 years: treatment-related symptoms developed in about half, but stanozolol was judged safe and effective for long-term HAE management at low doses.
trials.ref: Sheffer, Sloane, Lee et al., 2007, J Allergy Clin Immunol

trials.finding: In-vitro/mechanistic work: stanozolol upregulates dermal collagen synthesis (α1(I)/α1(III) procollagen mRNA) largely through TGF-β1 — a finding relevant to its use in fibrotic/vascular skin conditions, and a caution against the folk claim that it simply "dries out" connective tissue.
trials.ref: Falanga et al., J Invest Dermatol / J Am Acad Dermatol, 1998

tech.chem: Stanozolol (Winstrol; PubChem CID 25249) is a synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid derived from 5α-dihydrotestosterone (DHT), with a pyrazole ring fused to the A-ring (a [3,2-c]pyrazole) and a 17α-methyl group. The pyrazole ring removes the 3-keto group that aromatase needs, making it non-aromatisable (no estrogen conversion). The 17α-methyl group confers oral activity by resisting hepatic first-pass metabolism — the structural basis of both its oral use and its hepatotoxicity.

targets.action: agonist (weak binder)
targets.affinity: relative binding affinity ~22% of DHT — a comparatively weak AR ligand
targets.role: the classical anabolic-androgenic target: a nuclear hormone receptor / ligand-activated transcription factor driving muscle-protein and other androgen-responsive genes. Note that stanozolol's clinical androgenic effect is amplified by a non-receptor route — see SHBG.

targets.action: sharply lowers circulating SHBG, raising the free/active fraction of androgens
targets.affinity: stanozolol itself binds SHBG only weakly (~5% of testosterone's affinity), so the effect is on SHBG levels, not competitive displacement alone
targets.role: a major reason a weak AR binder produces a strong androgenic effect; also the basis of the clinical "stanozolol SHBG response" androgen-sensitivity test

tech.signaling: The androgen receptor is a nuclear hormone receptor: in the unliganded state it sits complexed with chaperones (e.g. HSP90); ligand binding triggers chaperone release, dimerisation, nuclear translocation, and binding to androgen-response elements to drive transcription. Systemically, the same androgen signal engages hypothalamic-pituitary negative feedback, suppressing GnRH/LH/FSH and hence endogenous testosterone. A separate, well-documented action is suppression of hepatic SHBG production, raising free androgen; and in fibroblasts stanozolol upregulates collagen synthesis via TGF-β1.

adme.absorb: Orally active due to 17α-alkylation, which blocks hepatic first-pass breakdown; also available as an aqueous IM suspension.
adme.distribute: Distributes to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues; being an androgen it is lipophilic and enters muscle, and it acts on the liver to lower SHBG output.
adme.metabolise: Hepatic metabolism (hydroxylation and conjugation) to characteristic metabolites, including 3'-hydroxystanozolol, which form the basis of anti-doping detection assays.
adme.excrete: Metabolites eliminated in urine; parent and metabolites are detectable long enough to underpin WADA testing. Oral elimination half-life ~9 h; aqueous IM ~24 h with a longer duration of action.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. AR sensitivity varies generally with AR-gene CAG-repeat length, but this has not been characterised for stanozolol, so individual variation should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: Approved clinical dosing is low and monitored: 0.5–2 mg/day for hereditary angioedema. Gray-market/anecdotal physique use runs far higher (commonly cited in the tens of milligrams per day, oral or injectable) at doses never validated for safety — this is a description, not a recommendation. No approved physique-enhancement dose exists.
tech.tolerance: There is no receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense. The dominant time-dependent consequences are cumulative and toxic rather than adaptive: HPTA suppression (falling endogenous testosterone), progressively lowered HDL cholesterol, and rising hepatic risk (cholestatic injury, and with prolonged use peliosis hepatis and hepatic tumors) — these, not tachyphylaxis, are what govern how long it can be used.

questions: If it's basically injectable Dianabol, why doesn't it wreck your liver the way Dianabol does?
Why do people say EQ gives a slow, 'clean' bulk with big appetite and endurance — and why is that same endurance boost secretly dangerous?
Why does a single dose keep working for weeks, and still flag a drug test five months later?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Dianabol is boldenone with one extra piece bolted on — a 17-alpha-methyl group that lets it survive as an oral pill but forces the liver to process it, which is what strains the liver. Boldenone lacks that group, so it's injected instead and skips that particular liver toxicity. (2) The slow, steady feel comes from the long undecylenate ester (a ~14-day half-life) plus the fact that muscle grows over weeks of gene transcription, not hours; the appetite and endurance come partly from a marked rise in red blood cells — and that same red-cell surge is exactly what can thicken your blood toward dangerous polycythemia. (3) The long ester keeps releasing the drug for weeks, and its metabolites linger in urine for months, which is why WADA can catch it long after the last shot.

boldenone-equipoise.bigIdea: Boldenone is just testosterone with one extra double bond — the same androgen-receptor gene switch as any steroid, tuned for a slow, steady release and a big red-blood-cell surge whose flip side is dangerously thick blood.
boldenone-equipoise.analogy: Picture testosterone with a governor fitted to the engine. The single added double bond doesn't build a new machine — it smooths and slows the delivery, softens some of the harsher androgenic conversion, and keeps the core engine (the androgen-receptor gene switch) running exactly as before. What it adds is a pump that floods your blood with extra red cells: more oxygen carried, but thicker, stickier blood.

mechSteps.t: It's testosterone plus one double bond
mechSteps.predict: Boldenone's structure is testosterone with a single small change. What would adding one double bond to a hormone do to how the body handles it?
mechSteps.d: Boldenone is Δ1-testosterone — testosterone with an extra double bond between carbons 1 and 2. That one change is the whole story: it doesn't invent a new mechanism, it re-tunes an existing one. It is also, atom for atom, Dianabol minus the 17-alpha-methyl group.

mechSteps.t: It flips the same androgen-receptor gene switch
mechSteps.predict: The androgen receptor isn't a channel or a brake — it's a switch that does what, exactly?
mechSteps.d: Like every anabolic steroid, boldenone binds the androgen receptor, which travels into the nucleus and turns up the genes for muscle-protein synthesis and nitrogen retention. This is transcription, not a fast hit — which is why lean mass accrues over weeks of dosing rather than in an afternoon.

mechSteps.t: The double bond re-routes its metabolism
mechSteps.predict: Steroids get chewed up into other hormones — some nastier than the original. How might one double bond change what boldenone turns into?
mechSteps.d: That double bond makes boldenone a poor substrate for 5-alpha-reductase, so it isn't efficiently converted into a potent DHT-like androgen — softening the scalp/prostate-type androgenic hit relative to its anabolic strength. It still aromatizes to an estrogen, but less readily than testosterone does, so estrogenic effects tend to be milder.

mechSteps.t: It floods the bone marrow with red blood cells
mechSteps.predict: Boldenone's signature effect isn't in the muscle at all. Where does its famous endurance-and-appetite reputation actually come from — and why is it a double-edged sword?
mechSteps.d: Androgens strongly stimulate red-blood-cell production, and boldenone is notably erythropoietic — it drives up hematocrit. More red cells mean more oxygen delivery (the endurance 'feel') and it raises appetite, but the same rise thickens the blood toward polycythemia, raising clot, stroke and heart-attack risk.

mechSteps.t: Your brain reads the androgen and shuts down your own testosterone
mechSteps.predict: Your body constantly measures its own androgen level. What does it do when a drug keeps that signal high for weeks?
mechSteps.d: The hypothalamus and pituitary sense an androgen present and dial down GnRH, LH and FSH — the hormones that tell your testes to make testosterone. So your own production shuts down, and the long ester means that suppression is sustained and slow to recover.

framework.target: The androgen receptor (AR) — a nuclear hormone receptor that acts as a gene switch. Boldenone is a full agonist: it turns AR on across the body. Unlike a SARM, there's no clever tissue selectivity here — it's a genuine steroid hitting the same receptor testosterone does, everywhere testosterone would.
framework.affinity: There's no reliable published binding constant (Ki) to quote for boldenone specifically, so an honest page won't invent one. What the classic relative-binding assays do show is that it binds AR with roughly testosterone-level affinity and clearly engages the receptor at the doses used — the anabolic effect is real, not a threshold curiosity. Its reported profile is roughly testosterone-level anabolic activity with about half the androgenic activity.
framework.adme: Injected as the undecylenate ester in oil — a slow-release depot, not an oral pill. The ester is cleaved gradually, giving an elimination half-life of about 14 days, so blood levels stay steady for weeks. Because it lacks a 17-alpha-methyl group, it isn't orally active in this form and doesn't carry the oral 17-alkylated steroids' liver toxicity. Hepatic metabolism; metabolites are detectable in urine for months.
framework.genetics: No validated boldenone-specific pharmacogenetic switch exists. AR sensitivity varies generally with the AR gene's CAG-repeat length, but this hasn't been characterised for boldenone, so treat individual response — including how far hematocrit climbs — as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Weak in the way that matters: boldenone is essentially a veterinary drug (Equipoise, mainly for horses), formerly used in human medicine and long since discontinued. The pharmacology is well understood because it's a classic androgen, but there are no modern human randomised trials of it for physique or performance — the human picture is anecdotal, not trial-based.

pk.onset: days to weeks — the anabolic effect is gene transcription, not an acute hit
pk.duration: weeks per injection; urinary metabolites detectable for months
pk.note: Don't read this half-life like a stimulant's stopwatch. The ~14-day ester sets how slowly the drug clears and why blood levels are smooth, but lean mass — and HPTA suppression, and the rise in hematocrit — build over weeks of continuous receptor activation. The long tail is also why it flags anti-doping tests for up to several months after the last dose.

journey.d: It's injected into muscle as an oily depot of boldenone undecylenate. A serious caution: much of the supply is underground or veterinary-grade, and gray-market vials are frequently mislabeled, underdosed, or contaminated — you often don't truly know the dose or contents. The ester is slowly cleaved to release active boldenone.
Freed boldenone circulates and seeks out androgen receptors. The ~14-day half-life means each dose accumulates into a steady level rather than spiking and vanishing.

It binds the androgen receptor, which enters the nucleus and turns up muscle-protein-synthesis and nitrogen-retention genes. Over weeks, this is where the slow, lean gains people chase actually accrue.
Its signature effect: it strongly stimulates red-blood-cell production, raising hematocrit. This drives the endurance 'feel' and appetite — and, unchecked, pushes blood toward dangerous polycythemia.

The hypothalamus and pituitary detect the androgen and throttle back LH and FSH, so your own testosterone production winds down — a suppression that outlasts the last injection because the ester lingers.
The liver metabolises it, and metabolites leave in urine — where WADA's assays catch it for months. It's banned in and out of competition, so a single course leaves a long detectable trail.

myths.myth: EQ is mild and safe because the gains are slow and lean.
myths.truth: Slow is not the same as safe. It fully suppresses your own testosterone like any steroid, and its standout effect — a marked rise in red blood cells — can push hematocrit into polycythemia, thickening the blood toward clot, stroke and heart-attack risk. 'Gentle-feeling' and 'low-risk' are not the same thing.

myths.myth: Boldenone doesn't aromatize, so estrogen isn't a concern.
myths.truth: It does aromatize to an estrogen — just less readily than testosterone. Estrogenic effects are usually milder than with testosterone, not absent, and individual response varies. 'Low estrogen' is a tendency, not a guarantee.

myths.myth: It's basically injectable Dianabol, so it's the same drug.
myths.truth: They share the same core molecule, but Dianabol adds a 17-alpha-methyl group that makes it an oral pill and burdens the liver. Boldenone lacks that group — it's injected via a long ester, avoids that specific liver toxicity, and acts far more slowly. One methyl group changes the route, the liver risk, and the entire time course.

myths.myth: The big appetite and endurance boost prove it's working great.
myths.truth: Both are real, but the endurance lift comes largely from more red blood cells — and that red-cell rise is precisely the thing that thickens your blood dangerously. The effect you're enjoying and the risk you're running are the same mechanism.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a slow, steady, 'lean' bulk with a big appetite and endurance boost, milder estrogenic effects than testosterone, and no oral-steroid liver strain. The endurance and appetite parts are real — but they ride largely on the red-blood-cell rise that is also its main danger.

**The blood cost (the headline one):** boldenone is strongly erythropoietic. It raises hematocrit and can drive polycythemia — thick, sticky blood that raises the risk of clots, stroke and heart attack. This is the watch-out that defines the compound.
**The hormonal cost:** like every anabolic steroid it suppresses your own testosterone (HPTA shutdown), and the long ester means recovery after stopping is slow and variable.
**The mood cost:** anxiety and irritability are commonly reported by users — worth naming honestly even though it's not from controlled data.

**The legal and testing cost:** it's a controlled substance, not FDA-approved for humans (a veterinary drug), and WADA-banned in and out of competition — with metabolites detectable for months. For a tested athlete this ends seasons.

**The evidence picture:** there are no modern human trials of boldenone for physique or performance. The pharmacology is well understood, but the human 'how it performs and how safe it is' picture is anecdotal. This page exists to explain the mechanism, not to help you run a cycle.

start.q: In one line: what is boldenone, structurally, and why does that matter?
start.a: It's testosterone with one extra double bond (Δ1-testosterone) — the same androgen-receptor gene switch as any steroid, just re-tuned for slow release, milder androgenic conversion, and a big red-blood-cell surge.

how.q: Boldenone shares its core molecule with Dianabol — so why doesn't it carry Dianabol's liver toxicity?
how.a: Dianabol adds a 17-alpha-methyl group so it can survive as an oral pill, and that's what burdens the liver. Boldenone lacks that group, so it's injected via a long ester and skips that specific oral-steroid liver toxicity.

use.q: Why is the endurance/appetite boost also its biggest danger?
use.a: Both come largely from a marked rise in red blood cells (higher hematocrit). That same rise thickens the blood toward polycythemia — raising clot, stroke and heart-attack risk. The benefit and the hazard are one mechanism.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really?
evidence.a: Thin. Boldenone is essentially a veterinary drug with no modern human trials for physique or performance. Its pharmacology is well understood as a classic androgen, but the human effectiveness-and-safety picture is anecdotal, not trial-based.

canExplain: Why boldenone is 'just testosterone plus one double bond' — and what that single change re-tunes
Why it hits the same androgen receptor as any steroid, with no SARM-style tissue selectivity
Why it shares Dianabol's molecule yet avoids Dianabol's liver toxicity
Why its signature red-blood-cell surge is both the appeal and the main danger
Why the human evidence is essentially anecdotal despite the pharmacology being well understood

selfTest.q: A friend says EQ is 'the safe steroid' because the gains come on slowly and lean. Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) Slow onset is about the long ester and gene-transcription timescale, not safety — it still fully suppresses your own testosterone like any steroid. (2) Its standout effect is a strong rise in red blood cells, which can drive polycythemia (dangerously thick blood, raising clot/stroke/heart-attack risk). Gentle-feeling is not low-risk.

selfTest.q: Boldenone and Dianabol are nearly the same molecule. What single group is different, and what three things does it change?
selfTest.a: Dianabol has a 17-alpha-methyl group boldenone lacks. That group (1) makes Dianabol orally active, (2) gives it oral-steroid liver toxicity, and (3) speeds its time course — whereas boldenone is injected as a slow ester, avoids that liver toxicity, and acts over weeks.

selfTest.q: Why does boldenone's ~14-day half-life tell you little about how fast you'll see muscle results — but a lot about drug testing?
selfTest.a: Muscle growth is driven by the androgen receptor turning genes on over weeks, not by the blood curve — so the half-life sets dosing frequency and clearance, not the speed of gains. But that long ester plus lingering metabolites is exactly why it stays detectable in urine for months, catching athletes long after the last dose.

evi.howStrong: Honestly weak for humans. Boldenone is essentially a veterinary anabolic (Equipoise, mostly for horses) that was used in human medicine decades ago and discontinued; it has never been through modern human randomised trials for muscle-building or performance. So unlike caffeine or creatine, there is no evidence pyramid here — no meta-analyses, no position stands, no controlled physique data. What we have is (a) well-established androgen pharmacology it shares with all anabolic steroids, and (b) user anecdote. The site's 3/5 rating reflects mechanistic certainty about how it works far more than clinical proof of what it does in people.
evi.whatItShows: The mechanism is not in doubt — it's an androgen-receptor agonist, so it will drive muscle-protein synthesis and raise red-blood-cell count like other steroids. What is genuinely missing is any controlled human measurement of how much muscle, how much performance, and at what risk, in physique users.
evi.effect: No credible human effect size can be quoted, because the controlled trials to produce one don't exist. Quoting a specific kilogram or percentage figure here would be inventing precision. What's reliable is directional: real anabolic effect via AR, and a notably large erythropoietic (hematocrit-raising) effect that stands out even among steroids.
evi.whoBenefits: In practice it's used off-label by physique and strength athletes chasing slow lean gains, appetite and endurance — none of it studied for safety in that population. Its legitimate, approved use is veterinary.
evi.caveats: Treat everything beyond the core pharmacology as unproven in humans. The two hard, well-supported cautions are near-universal HPTA suppression and a strong tendency to raise hematocrit toward polycythemia (with clot/cardiovascular risk). Add commonly reported anxiety, its controlled-substance status, WADA ban with months-long detectability, and the frequent mislabeling/contamination of gray-market and veterinary-grade product. The absence of human trials cuts both ways — it does not mean 'safe', it means 'unstudied'.

tech.chem: Boldenone is 1-dehydrotestosterone (Δ1-testosterone; 17β-hydroxyandrosta-1,4-dien-3-one) — testosterone with an added C1–C2 double bond. It is the non-17α-alkylated analogue of metandienone (Dianabol): structurally identical to Dianabol except that Dianabol carries a 17α-methyl group. The commercial form, boldenone undecylenate (Equipoise), is the C17β undecylenate ester in oil, which delays release and extends the half-life. The Δ1 double bond both lowers 5α-reductase conversion and reduces aromatization relative to testosterone.

targets.action: full agonist (non-selective steroidal androgen)
targets.affinity: binds AR with roughly testosterone-level affinity in relative-binding assays; no reliable compound-specific Ki is published, so none is quoted here
targets.role: the primary target — a nuclear hormone receptor / ligand-activated transcription factor driving muscle-protein synthesis, nitrogen retention and, via androgen-driven erythropoiesis, increased red-cell production

tech.signaling: The androgen receptor sits in the cytoplasm bound to chaperones until ligand binding triggers dimerisation, nuclear translocation, and binding to androgen-response elements in DNA, upregulating anabolic gene transcription. Two downstream themes define boldenone specifically: (1) strong stimulation of erythropoiesis — androgens both raise erythropoietin and directly stimulate erythroid progenitors, producing the hallmark hematocrit rise; and (2) the same systemic androgen signal engaging hypothalamic-pituitary negative feedback, suppressing GnRH/LH/FSH and hence endogenous testosterone.

adme.absorb: Administered by intramuscular injection as the undecylenate ester in oil (a depot); the ester is cleaved slowly to release active boldenone. Not orally active in this esterified form.
adme.distribute: Distributes to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues including skeletal muscle and bone marrow.
adme.metabolise: Hepatic metabolism. The Δ1 double bond makes it a poor 5α-reductase substrate (limited conversion to a DHT-like androgen) and reduces aromatization relative to testosterone, though estrogenic metabolites still form.
adme.excrete: Metabolites eliminated in urine; parent and metabolites remain detectable for months, underpinning WADA testing. Elimination half-life ≈ 14 days for the undecylenate ester.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. AR sensitivity varies generally with AR-gene CAG-repeat length, but this has not been characterised for boldenone, so individual variation — including the magnitude of the hematocrit rise — should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: No approved human therapeutic dose exists; boldenone undecylenate is approved only for veterinary use, dosed by body weight in horses. Human physique use is entirely off-label and gray-market, at doses never validated for safety in people — described here, not recommended.
tech.tolerance: There is no receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense. The dominant time-dependent consequences are cumulative HPTA suppression (falling endogenous testosterone) and a progressive rise in hematocrit toward polycythemia — these, not tachyphylaxis, are what govern 'on/off' cycling and the real risk ceiling.

contrasts.title: Boldenone vs Dianabol — one methyl group apart
contrasts.point: They are the same core molecule: Dianabol is boldenone plus a 17-alpha-methyl group. That one addition changes almost everything practical. The methyl lets Dianabol survive as an oral pill but forces the liver to process it, giving it real hepatotoxicity and a fast, sharp time course. Boldenone lacks the methyl, so it's injected as a slow ester, sidesteps that oral-steroid liver toxicity, and acts smoothly over weeks. Same receptor, same gene switch — but route, liver risk and tempo all hinge on a single group.

contrasts.title: Boldenone vs a SARM like ostarine — real steroid vs filed-down key
contrasts.point: Both end at the androgen receptor, but boldenone is a genuine steroid master key that turns every androgen lock in the body with no tissue selectivity, whereas a SARM is engineered to favour muscle and bone. That's why boldenone's androgenic and red-cell effects are body-wide and pronounced. The one alarm both trip identically: your brain sees the androgen and shuts down your own testosterone.

questions: Why do people balloon by 4–5 kg in the first two weeks on Dbol — before they could possibly have built that much muscle?
Why is Dianabol a pill when almost every other serious steroid is injected — and why does that exact trick punish your liver?
If it binds the androgen receptor more weakly than your own testosterone, how is it still one of the most notorious mass-builders ever made?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Most of that early weight is water and glycogen pulled into the muscle plus estrogen-driven fluid retention — not new contractile tissue, which is why it deflates fast when you stop. In Hervey's controlled trial the potassium and nitrogen retained were disproportionately large for the modest true lean gain. (2) Chemists welded a 17α-methyl group onto testosterone so it survives the liver's first pass and works orally — but that same modification jams the liver's bile-export machinery, which is the root of its cholestatic hepatotoxicity. (3) Its raw receptor affinity is lower than testosterone's, but it barely binds SHBG (about 10% of testosterone's affinity), so a large fraction stays free and active — and taken orally at high milligram doses it saturates the androgen receptor anyway.

methandrostenolone-dianabol.bigIdea: Dianabol is testosterone re-engineered to survive being swallowed — and the very modification that makes it an oral pill is what poisons your liver, while much of the dramatic early weight is water, not muscle.
methandrostenolone-dianabol.stakes: It is the archetypal oral anabolic steroid — synthesised by Ciba in the 1950s, marketed as Dianabol from 1958, and the drug that effectively launched the steroid era in strength sport. Today it is a controlled substance with no legitimate performance use.
methandrostenolone-dianabol.analogy: Think of testosterone as a key that works — but only when injected, because your liver shreds it the moment you swallow it. Chemists bolted a methyl 'armour plate' onto that key so it survives the trip through the gut and liver. Now you can take it as a pill. But the armour plate wedges itself into the liver's bile plumbing on the way through — so you buy oral convenience and fast size at the price of a backed-up, inflamed liver.

mechSteps.t: Chemists rebuilt testosterone so you could swallow it
mechSteps.predict: Plain testosterone is destroyed by the liver if swallowed. What two chemical tweaks let Dbol survive the trip — and what's the hidden cost of one of them?
mechSteps.d: Metandienone is testosterone with a 17α-methyl group added and an extra double bond between C1 and C2. The 17α-methyl blocks the liver from instantly deactivating it, giving high oral bioavailability. That same methyl group is exactly what makes it hepatotoxic.

mechSteps.t: It activates the androgen receptor — weakly on paper, strongly in practice
mechSteps.predict: Its receptor binding is weaker than testosterone's. So why isn't it a weak drug?
mechSteps.d: Like every anabolic steroid it works through the androgen receptor, a gene switch. Its raw affinity is lower than testosterone's — but it barely binds SHBG (~10% of testosterone's affinity), so more stays free, and it's dosed high by mouth. The net effect on muscle is potent.

mechSteps.t: Inside the muscle it turns anabolic genes on — and drags water in
mechSteps.predict: The receptor doesn't act in seconds like a stimulant. What does it switch on, and why is so much of the early weight water?
mechSteps.d: The activated receptor enters the nucleus and turns up genes for protein synthesis, while driving nitrogen and glycogen retention. Water follows the glycogen and the sodium into the cell — so the scale jumps fast, but a large share is fluid, not contractile tissue.

mechSteps.t: It aromatises into a stubborn estrogen
mechSteps.predict: Dbol converts to estrogen in the body. Why does even a 'slow' conversion still cause big estrogenic problems?
mechSteps.d: Aromatase converts it to methylestradiol. The 17α-methyl slows this conversion — but the estrogen produced resists breakdown, so it lingers. That drives fluid retention, rising blood pressure, and the risk of gynecomastia (breast tissue growth).

mechSteps.t: Two bills come due: the liver and your own hormones
mechSteps.predict: Name the two systems that pay the price for that oral, high-dose androgen signal.
mechSteps.d: The 17α-methyl group impairs the liver's bile-salt export pump (BSEP/ABCB11), backing up bile — cholestatic liver stress. Meanwhile your brain reads the androgen flood and shuts down LH and FSH, throttling your own testosterone production.

framework.target: The androgen receptor (AR) — a nuclear hormone receptor that acts as a gene switch. Metandienone is an agonist: it turns AR on. A secondary target is aromatase, which converts it into a metabolism-resistant estrogen — so it drives an estrogenic signal too, not just an androgenic one.
framework.affinity: Its relative AR binding affinity is lower than testosterone's — a precise Ki for metandienone is not well established, so no number should be quoted. What makes it potent anyway is not tight receptor binding but a very low affinity for SHBG (~10% of testosterone's), leaving more drug free, combined with high oral doses. Unlike caffeine — where you must ask whether a realistic dose even reaches the target — here a swallowed dose clearly saturates AR.
framework.adme: Orally active because the 17α-methyl group resists first-pass hepatic deactivation; high oral bioavailability. Extensively metabolised in the liver (6β-hydroxylation, reduction, 17-epimerisation) and conjugated, then excreted in urine. Elimination half-life is short — roughly 3–6 hours — but the anabolic effect is transcriptional and builds over weeks, not set by the blood curve.
framework.genetics: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic switch. General androgen sensitivity varies with AR-gene CAG-repeat length, and aromatase (CYP19A1) activity influences how much estrogen you make — but neither is characterised specifically for metandienone, so individual response and side-effect load are unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Moderate but old and thin: small double-blind crossover trials from the 1970s–80s (Freed 1975; Hervey 1981) show real strength and weight gains, but showed much of the weight was water and that the retained potassium/nitrogen was disproportionate to true lean tissue. There are essentially no modern controlled trials and no long-term safety data in healthy people using it for physique.

pk.onset: days to weeks for the anabolic effect — it's gene transcription, not an acute hit
pk.note: The short blood half-life is why users historically split the daily dose — plasma levels rise and fall within hours. But don't read that clock as the effect timer: muscle protein synthesis and HPTA suppression run on the slow transcription timescale of weeks, while the fast early scale-weight jump is water and glycogen, not the drug's blood peak.

journey.d: You swallow it. Serious caution: gray-market oral steroids are frequently mislabeled, underdosed, or contaminated. The 17α-methyl group lets it survive the gut and first-pass liver — the trick that makes an oral steroid possible.
It circulates, and because it barely binds SHBG (~10% of testosterone's affinity), an unusually large fraction stays free and biologically active, seeking androgen receptors.

It binds AR, drives it into the nucleus, and turns up protein-synthesis genes while forcing nitrogen and glycogen retention. Water follows — so size climbs fast, but much of the very early gain is fluid.
Aromatase turns some of it into a stubborn, breakdown-resistant estrogen. That fuels fluid retention, rising blood pressure, and the risk of gynecomastia.
The 17α-methyl group that made it oral now jams the bile-salt export pump (BSEP/ABCB11), backing up bile. This cholestatic stress is the mechanistic root of its hepatotoxicity.

The hypothalamus and pituitary sense the androgen flood and shut down LH/FSH, suppressing your own testosterone. Metabolites (like 6β-hydroxymetandienone) leave in urine — the basis of WADA doping detection.

myths.myth: I gained 5 kg in two weeks, so Dbol built me 5 kg of muscle.
myths.truth: Most of that early weight is water and glycogen inside the muscle plus estrogen-driven fluid retention — which is why it deflates quickly when you stop. In Hervey's controlled trial the retained potassium and nitrogen were disproportionately large for the true lean-tissue gain; the scale moved far more than real muscle did.

myths.myth: Its receptor binding is weak, so it must be a mild, low-risk steroid.
myths.truth: Weak AR affinity doesn't mean mild. Low SHBG binding and high oral dosing make it a potent anabolic — and it still aromatises to estrogen, raises blood pressure, is hepatotoxic, and shuts down your own testosterone.

myths.myth: It's 17α-methylated but 'doesn't really aromatise,' so estrogen isn't a concern.
myths.truth: It aromatises more slowly than testosterone, but the estrogen it produces resists breakdown, so it accumulates. Estrogenic effects — water retention, high blood pressure, gynecomastia — are among its most characteristic side effects.

myths.myth: Liver-support supplements make oral Dbol safe.
myths.truth: The cholestatic hepatotoxicity comes from the 17α-alkyl structure itself impairing bile export — it's intrinsic to the molecule. No supplement is proven to prevent it, and it does nothing for the hypertension, estrogen load, or testosterone shutdown.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a fast, dramatic 'kickstart' to a bulk — big early scale gains and strength, in an oral pill instead of an injection. The strength effect has genuine (if old and small) trial support; the size is real but heavily inflated by water.
**The water caveat:** a large share of the rapid gain is glycogen and fluid, not muscle, and much of it disappears within weeks of stopping. The headline numbers are not what they look like.
**The liver cost:** the 17α-alkylation that makes it oral is directly hepatotoxic, impairing bile flow (cholestasis). Prolonged or high-dose use risks real liver injury.
**The cardiovascular and estrogen cost:** it aromatises to a metabolism-resistant estrogen, driving fluid retention, hypertension, and gynecomastia — a classic, well-documented side-effect triad.
**The hormonal cost:** it suppresses your own testosterone (LH/FSH shutdown), with variable recovery after stopping.

**The legal and testing cost:** it's a controlled substance with no approved performance use, banned by WADA, and its urinary metabolites are detectable long after use — this ends careers for tested athletes.

**The honest evidence picture:** the human data is a handful of small crossover trials from the 1970s–80s plus decades of anecdote — no modern controlled trials and essentially no long-term safety data in healthy users. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a cycle.

start.q: In one line: why is Dianabol a pill when most steroids are injected — and what's the catch?
start.a: A 17α-methyl group was added to testosterone so it survives the liver's first pass and works orally. The catch: that same group is what makes it hepatotoxic, impairing bile export.

how.q: Dbol binds the androgen receptor more weakly than testosterone. So why is it still a potent mass-builder?
how.a: It barely binds SHBG (~10% of testosterone's affinity), so more stays free and active, and it's taken orally at high doses — so it saturates AR and drives protein synthesis anyway, while also pulling in nitrogen, glycogen and water.

use.q: Why is the fast early weight gain misleading?
use.a: Much of it is water and glycogen inside the muscle plus estrogen-driven fluid retention, not new contractile tissue — which is why it drops off quickly after stopping. Hervey's trial showed retained potassium/nitrogen out of proportion to true lean gain.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really?
evidence.a: Moderate but old and thin: small double-blind crossover trials from the 1970s–80s show real strength and weight effects, but much of the weight was water, and there are no modern controlled trials and no long-term safety data in healthy users.

canExplain: Why a swallowed steroid is even possible — and why the same trick damages the liver
Why weak receptor affinity doesn't make it a weak or safe drug
Why the dramatic first-week weight gain is mostly water, not muscle
Why it still floods you with estrogen despite 'slow' aromatisation — and why that means high blood pressure and gyno
How thin the real human evidence is, and why it's a controlled substance with no approved performance use

selfTest.q: A friend says Dbol is 'safer than injectables because it's just a pill you swallow.' Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) It's a pill precisely because of 17α-alkylation, which resists first-pass metabolism — and that same feature is directly hepatotoxic (cholestatic), so 'oral' means more liver risk, not less. (2) It still aromatises to estrogen (water, hypertension, gyno) and shuts down your own testosterone, none of which the oral route avoids.

selfTest.q: Metandienone binds the androgen receptor less tightly than testosterone. Why isn't it a weaker drug in the body?
selfTest.a: Because potency in vivo isn't just receptor affinity. It barely binds SHBG (~10% of testosterone's affinity), so a large free fraction remains active, and it's dosed high orally — so it fully engages AR and drives strong anabolic transcription regardless of the lower raw affinity.

selfTest.q: Someone gains 6 kg in three weeks and credits it all to muscle. What does the controlled-trial evidence suggest is really happening?
selfTest.a: Much of that is water and glycogen plus estrogen-driven fluid retention, not contractile tissue. Hervey's double-blind trial found the retained potassium and nitrogen were disproportionately large for the true lean gain — the weight is inflated and largely reversible on stopping.

evi.howStrong: Weak by modern standards. The human evidence is a small number of double-blind crossover trials from the 1970s and early 1980s in a few dozen trained men total, plus decades of gym anecdote. There are no modern randomised controlled trials, no long-term safety studies in healthy people using it for physique, and — as a controlled substance — no realistic prospect of them. Treat the old trials as directionally real but tiny and dated.
evi.whatItShows: Consistently, the trials show metandienone produces measurable gains in body weight and strength versus placebo during training. What they also show, crucially, is that much of the weight is not normal muscle: it's water, glycogen, and disproportionate potassium/nitrogen retention.
evi.effect: In Hervey's 100 mg/day, 6-week trial the mean weight gain was roughly 2–3 kg, confined to the lean compartment but with potassium and nitrogen retained out of proportion to true muscle. In Freed's trial at 10–25 mg/day, strength improvements were significantly greater than placebo, but body weight rose partly from water and systolic blood pressure rose significantly, with several men withdrawing for side effects.
evi.whoBenefits: The trials studied trained young men on high-protein diets. Any use in healthy people to build physique is off-label, illegal, gray-market, and unstudied for long-term safety — the trial numbers are a best case under supervised, dosed conditions, not what a black-market tablet delivers.
evi.caveats: The evidence base is old, small, and short, and the 'gains' are inflated by fluid. Against modest, partly-water benefits stand documented hepatotoxicity, significant blood-pressure rise, estrogenic effects including gynecomastia, and suppression of endogenous testosterone. The risk-to-evidence ratio is unfavourable, and product quality on the gray market is unreliable.

trials.finding: Double-blind crossover trial in 13 experienced weightlifters (methandienone 10–25 mg/day): strength improvements significantly greater than placebo, but body-weight rise was associated with water retention, systolic blood pressure rose significantly, and three men withdrew due to side effects.
trials.ref: Freed et al., 1975, BMJ

trials.finding: Double-blind crossover trial in 11 athletic men (methandienone 100 mg/day, 6 weeks): weight gain (~2–3 kg) confined to lean mass, but retained potassium and nitrogen were disproportionately large for the weight gained — i.e. the gain was not normal muscle.
trials.ref: Hervey et al., 1981, Clinical Science (earlier reported in Lancet, 1976)

tech.chem: Metandienone (methandrostenolone; brand Dianabol; CAS 72-63-9) is 17α-methyl-δ1-testosterone: testosterone bearing a 17α-methyl group and an additional double bond between C1 and C2. The 17α-methyl group confers oral activity by resisting first-pass hepatic 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase deactivation, and is the structural feature responsible for its cholestatic hepatotoxicity. The C1–C2 double bond reduces 5α-reductase conversion (only trace methyl-1-testosterone forms) and slows aromatisation relative to testosterone.

targets.action: agonist
targets.affinity: relative binding affinity lower than testosterone; a precise Ki for metandienone is not well established (not quoted here). Potency in vivo is driven by very low SHBG binding (~10% of testosterone's) and high oral dosing rather than tight receptor binding.
targets.role: the primary target — a nuclear hormone receptor / ligand-activated transcription factor driving anabolic gene expression in muscle and bone; also the route of androgenic side effects and, via central negative feedback, HPTA suppression.

targets.action: substrate → converted to 17α-methylestradiol
targets.affinity: aromatisation is slower than for testosterone, but the resulting estrogen is metabolism-resistant and accumulates
targets.role: source of the estrogenic effects — fluid retention, hypertension, and gynecomastia risk. It has no progestogenic activity.

tech.signaling: The androgen receptor sits in the cytoplasm complexed with chaperones (e.g. HSP90); ligand binding triggers chaperone release, dimerisation, nuclear translocation, and binding to androgen-response elements, driving transcription of anabolic genes and promoting nitrogen and glycogen retention (with osmotic water following). Systemically the same androgen signal engages hypothalamic-pituitary negative feedback, suppressing GnRH/LH/FSH and endogenous testosterone. Separately, the 17α-alkyl structure impairs the canalicular bile-salt export pump (BSEP/ABCB11), producing cholestasis — the mechanistic basis of 17α-alkylated steroid hepatotoxicity.

adme.absorb: High oral bioavailability; the 17α-methyl group resists first-pass hepatic deactivation.
adme.distribute: Very low affinity for SHBG (~10% of testosterone's, ~2% of DHT's), leaving a high free, active fraction; distributes to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues including muscle and bone.
adme.metabolise: Extensive hepatic phase I metabolism — 6β-hydroxylation, 3α/3β-oxidation, 5β-reduction, 17-epimerisation — followed by phase II glucuronide and sulfate conjugation. The C1–C2 double bond largely prevents 5α-reduced metabolite formation.
