Adult compounded 'anti-aging' regimens are typically dosed at night subcutaneously but are not standardised or validated for safety — this is description, not recommendation.
tech.tolerance: No classic receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense; trials in deficient children sustained a growth response over months. The practical limiter is pituitary reserve and physiological feedback (somatostatin/IGF-1), not tachyphylaxis. Note the theoretical concern that continuous non-pulsatile stimulation could blunt a system that normally runs on pulses — an argument for the nightly-pulse dosing pattern.

contrasts.title: Sermorelin vs recombinant HGH — asking vs overriding
contrasts.point: Same destination, opposite route. Recombinant HGH (somatropin) is the hormone itself, poured in from outside — it overrides your control system and can drive levels far past physiological, which is why it carries the bigger side-effect and misuse profile. Sermorelin asks your own pituitary to release GH through the native GHRH receptor, so somatostatin and IGF-1 feedback keep output pulsatile and self-limiting. That's the whole appeal — and also why sermorelin is milder and capped by your own capacity.

contrasts.title: Sermorelin vs ghrelin mimetics (GHRPs like ipamorelin) — two different doors to the same room
contrasts.point: Both make your pituitary release its own GH, but through different receptors. Sermorelin works the GHRH receptor (the 'GHRH arm'). Ghrelin mimetics such as ipamorelin work the growth-hormone-secretagogue receptor, GHSR (the 'ghrelin arm'), and also blunt somatostatin. Because they pull different levers, the two are often combined for a larger, still-endogenous pulse — a genuine mechanistic sibling, not a rival.

questions: Why does a single shot of the DAC version keep your growth hormone elevated for almost a week, when your body's own GHRH lasts only minutes?
If it just tells your pituitary to release GH, why does raising GH around the clock behave so differently from your natural rhythm of pulses?
It reliably raises GH and IGF-1 in human trials — so why was it never approved, and why is there still no proof it builds muscle?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) The DAC is a molecular grappling hook: it covalently clips onto albumin, the blood's most abundant week-long courier, at cysteine-34 — turning a peptide that would wash out in minutes into one with a 5.8–8.1-day half-life (Teichman 2006). (2) Your natural GH comes in sharp pulses that fall back to near-zero between bursts; CJC-1295 raises the floor. In the Ionescu & Frohman trial the pulses actually persisted, but trough GH rose ~7.5-fold and mean 24-hour GH ~46% — a sustained 'bleed' of elevated baseline that your body never normally sees. (3) Development was discontinued and no regulator ever approved it. Crucially, the only real human trials measured hormones (GH and IGF-1) — not muscle, strength, fat loss, or long-term safety. 'It raises GH' is proven; 'it safely builds muscle' is not.

cjc-1295-dac.bigIdea: CJC-1295 doesn't add growth hormone — it leans on the pituitary's own 'release GH' button, and the DAC clips the drug to albumin so that lean lasts days instead of minutes, raising your baseline GH into a sustained state your body never normally experiences.
cjc-1295-dac.analogy: Your pituitary has a 'release GH now' button that your own GHRH taps in short rhythmic bursts. CJC-1295 is a copy of the finger that presses it. On its own it presses a little longer than natural. Add the DAC — a grappling hook that latches onto albumin, a courier protein that circulates in your blood for a week — and the finger stays gently resting on the button for days. Not a harder press. A press that won't lift off.

mechSteps.t: It's a copy of GHRH's business end
mechSteps.predict: Your own growth-hormone-releasing hormone is 44 amino acids long, but only part of it does the work. Which part did CJC-1295 copy?
mechSteps.d: CJC-1295 is built on GRF(1-29) — the first 29 amino acids of GHRH, the fragment that carries essentially all of the receptor-activating power. Same message your hypothalamus sends, in a shorter, synthetic form.

mechSteps.t: Small tweaks stop enzymes chewing it up
mechSteps.predict: Native GHRH is destroyed within minutes of release. If you wanted it to last, what would you have to defend it against?
mechSteps.d: An enzyme called DPP-IV clips native GHRH almost immediately. Four amino-acid substitutions in the peptide block that cut, extending its working life from a couple of minutes to roughly 30 — this is the 'no-DAC' (Modified GRF 1-29) version.

mechSteps.t: The DAC clips it onto albumin for a multi-day ride
mechSteps.predict: Thirty minutes is still short. How do you turn a peptide that clears in minutes into one that lasts days — without changing what it does at the receptor?
mechSteps.d: The DAC (Drug Affinity Complex) is a reactive maleimide group on a lysine linker. After injection it forms a covalent bond to cysteine-34 of circulating albumin — a long-lived blood protein. Now the drug rides around attached to albumin, giving a measured half-life of 5.8–8.1 days.

mechSteps.t: It presses the GH-release button on the pituitary
mechSteps.predict: The receptor it hits is a G-protein-coupled receptor, not a channel. What second messenger climbs when you switch it on — and what does the cell do?
mechSteps.d: CJC-1295 binds the GHRH receptor (GHRHR) on pituitary somatotroph cells, a Gs-coupled receptor. That raises cAMP, which drives synthesis and release of growth hormone. Because the DAC keeps the drug present continuously, the stimulation is tonic rather than a single tap.

mechSteps.t: The floor rises, and IGF-1 climbs behind it
mechSteps.predict: Growth hormone is mostly a middleman. Which downstream hormone actually carries out much of GH's growth signal — and where is it made?
mechSteps.d: Sustained GH tells the liver to pump out IGF-1, the hormone that does much of GH's downstream growth work. In trials, IGF-1 rose 1.5–3-fold and stayed elevated for 9–11 days after one dose. The key change is a raised baseline — a continuous 'bleed' of signal, not sharper pulses.

framework.target: The GHRH receptor (GHRHR) — a Gs-coupled G-protein-coupled receptor on the pituitary's somatotroph cells. CJC-1295 is an agonist: it switches the receptor on (raising cAMP → GH release), the opposite of a blocker like caffeine. Note it works one step upstream — it doesn't supply GH, it commands your own pituitary to release it, so a functioning pituitary is required.
framework.affinity: Here honesty matters: there is no published human binding constant (Ki) for CJC-1295 specifically. What's established is that the GRF(1-29) fragment retains essentially full GHRH agonist activity, and the modifications were designed to change stability and duration, not receptor potency. So unlike a drug where you can quote a hard Ki, treat the affinity as 'GHRH-like agonist' and don't trust any specific nanomolar figure you see quoted on a seller's page.
framework.adme: Given by subcutaneous injection. The DAC covalently binds albumin → half-life 5.8–8.1 days (the no-DAC/Mod GRF form is ~30 minutes). But read the timing carefully: the felt effect is not set by a blood-level stopwatch. GH rises for ~6 days and IGF-1 for 9–11 days after a single dose — the downstream hormonal cascade, not the peptide's own clearance, is what you'd experience.
framework.genetics: No validated pharmacogenetic switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. The real determinant of response is your pituitary somatotroph reserve — how much GH your gland can still make. In someone whose pituitary is failing, pressing the button harder does little; the drug can only amplify a working axis.
framework.evidence: Split, and worth stating plainly: moderate for the pharmacology — two genuine human trials show it robustly and durably raises GH and IGF-1 in healthy adults. Essentially zero for outcomes — no trial has tested muscle gain, fat loss, strength, or long-term safety in healthy people. Clinical development was discontinued and it was never approved for anything.

journey.d: You inject it under the skin. Real caution here: CJC-1295 is a gray-market research chemical, and independent testing of peptide products routinely finds wrong doses, wrong compounds, or contamination — you often don't truly know what's in the vial.

In the bloodstream the DAC's reactive maleimide latches covalently onto cysteine-34 of albumin. Now bound to a protein that circulates for days, the drug accumulates to a steady level instead of vanishing — the whole point of the DAC.

It reaches GHRH receptors on the pituitary's somatotroph cells and switches them on, raising cAMP and driving growth hormone out. Because the drug is continuously present, it lifts the baseline GH level — trough GH rose ~7.5-fold in the Ionescu & Frohman trial.

Sustained GH signals the liver to release IGF-1, the downstream growth hormone. This is also where the costs live: GH excess drives water retention, joint aches and carpal-tunnel tingling, and pushes blood sugar up (insulin resistance).

The peptide and its downstream signature are cleared over days, but this is also where athletes get caught: GHRH analogs and secretagogues are banned by WADA in and out of competition. Using it ends tested careers.

myths.myth: CJC-1295 builds muscle directly.
myths.truth: It builds nothing directly — it acts one step upstream, telling your pituitary to release more GH. And critically, the human trials measured hormones (GH and IGF-1), not body composition. No published trial shows it increases muscle, strength, or reduces fat in healthy people. The physique claims are extrapolation, not data.

myths.myth: The DAC just makes it 'stronger'.
myths.truth: The DAC changes duration, not potency — it clips the drug to albumin so it lasts days instead of minutes. It doesn't press the receptor harder. Arguably it makes the effect less natural: your body's GH normally comes in sharp pulses with near-empty troughs, and constant elevation is a state your physiology never evolved to sit in.

myths.myth: It's safe because it just uses your own growth hormone.
myths.truth: 'Your own hormone' is not the same as 'physiological'. Round-the-clock GH and IGF-1 elevation carries the known costs of GH excess — insulin resistance and higher blood sugar, fluid retention, joint pain and carpal tunnel — and IGF-1 is a growth signal, which raises a real theoretical concern about promoting the growth of existing tumours. It's unapproved and unregulated on top of that.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is an 'upstream, more natural' way to raise GH — you press your own pituitary rather than injecting synthetic HGH. The DAC form's multi-day half-life means weekly rather than daily needles, and it's usually stacked with a ghrelin-mimetic like ipamorelin so one raises 'how much' GH and the other triggers 'release now'.

**The evidence reality:** the only solid human data is pharmacodynamic — it durably raises GH and IGF-1. There is no trial evidence that this translates into muscle, strength, fat loss, recovery, or any health outcome, and no long-term safety data in healthy users at all.

**The metabolic cost:** sustained GH/IGF-1 elevation pushes blood sugar up (insulin resistance), causes water retention, and commonly brings joint aches and carpal-tunnel-type tingling. Because IGF-1 is a growth signal, chronic elevation raises a genuine theoretical cancer-promotion concern.
**The regulatory cost:** never approved for any use, its clinical development was discontinued, and it's banned by WADA in and out of competition. For a tested athlete this ends seasons and careers.

**The product-quality cost:** it's sold only as a 'research chemical'. Independent analyses of gray-market peptides repeatedly find mislabeling, wrong doses, or contamination — so the vial's contents are frequently not what the label claims. This page explains the pharmacology; it is not a protocol.

start.q: In one line: does CJC-1295 supply growth hormone, or make your body release its own?
start.a: It makes your body release its own. It's a GHRH-receptor agonist that presses the pituitary's GH-release button — so it needs a working pituitary and adds no hormone itself.

how.q: What does the DAC physically do, and why does it matter?
how.a: It forms a covalent bond to cysteine-34 of albumin, a blood protein that circulates for days. That anchors the drug so its half-life stretches to 5.8–8.1 days instead of the ~30 minutes of the no-DAC version — sustained stimulation instead of a brief tap.

use.q: Why is the constant, non-pulsatile signal a concern rather than a bonus?
use.a: Your natural GH comes in sharp pulses that drop to near-zero between them. CJC-1295 raises the baseline floor — a state your body never normally sees — and chronic GH/IGF-1 elevation carries the costs of GH excess: insulin resistance, fluid retention, joint pain, and a theoretical cancer-promotion concern from IGF-1.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really?
evidence.a: Moderate for the pharmacology, near-zero for outcomes. Two real trials (Teichman 2006; Ionescu & Frohman 2006) show it durably raises GH and IGF-1 in healthy adults. No trial has tested muscle, strength, fat loss, or long-term safety, development was discontinued, and it was never approved.

canExplain: Why a copy of just the first 29 amino acids of GHRH still fully activates the receptor
Exactly what the DAC does — covalently binding albumin at Cys34 to stretch the half-life to days
Why raising GH continuously is different from — and potentially riskier than — your natural pulses
Why the drug raises GH and IGF-1 in humans yet has never been shown to build muscle
Why it was never approved, and why using it fails a WADA drug test

selfTest.q: A friend says CJC-1295 is 'basically injecting growth hormone but safer'. Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) It isn't growth hormone — it's a GHRH-receptor agonist that tells your own pituitary to release GH, so it needs a functioning pituitary and works one step upstream. (2) 'Safer' is unproven: it raises GH and IGF-1 around the clock (an unnatural, non-pulsatile state), carrying the costs of GH excess — insulin resistance, fluid retention, joint pain, and a theoretical IGF-1 cancer concern — with no long-term safety data.

selfTest.q: The DAC version has a half-life of nearly a week, but that number tells you little about the effect you'd feel. Why?
selfTest.a: Because the effect isn't a blood-level phenomenon like a stimulant. The peptide's job is to trigger a downstream hormonal cascade — GH stays elevated ~6 days and IGF-1 for 9–11 days after one dose. The half-life sets how often you'd dose; the hormonal cascade, not the peptide's clearance, is what the body actually experiences.

selfTest.q: It robustly raised GH and IGF-1 in human trials, yet you should not conclude it works for physique. What's the gap?
selfTest.a: The trials measured only the hormonal signal (GH, IGF-1 levels), not the endpoints that matter for physique — muscle mass, strength, fat loss — or long-term safety. Raising a hormone that is upstream of growth does not prove downstream benefit, and no trial ever tested it. That missing link, plus discontinued development, is why it was never approved.

evi.howStrong: For a gray-market peptide the pharmacology is unusually well-characterised: there are two genuine, published human trials in healthy adults, both in a mainstream endocrinology journal. But 'well-characterised pharmacology' is a narrow claim. Both trials measured pharmacokinetics and hormone levels — how long the drug lasts and how much GH and IGF-1 it releases. Neither tested body composition, strength, recovery, fat loss, or long-term safety. Clinical development was discontinued and no regulator has approved it for any indication.
evi.whatItShows: Consistently, it does what a long-acting GHRH agonist should: it durably raises circulating GH and IGF-1. What it has never been shown to do is produce any downstream benefit you'd actually want — more muscle, more strength, less fat — because those endpoints were never studied in humans.
evi.effect: In numbers from the trials: a single subcutaneous dose raised plasma GH roughly 2–10-fold for about 6 days and IGF-1 about 1.5–3-fold for 9–11 days, with a measured half-life of 5.8–8.1 days (Teichman 2006). In a separate study, continuous stimulation raised trough GH ~7.5-fold and mean 24-hour GH ~46% while GH pulse frequency and amplitude were unchanged (Ionescu & Frohman 2006) — evidence that it lifts the baseline rather than sharpening pulses.
evi.whoBenefits: The studied population was healthy adults, used to characterise the drug's PK/PD — not a patient group with a demonstrated outcome benefit. Its use in healthy people to alter physique is entirely off-label, gray-market, and unstudied for both efficacy and safety.
evi.caveats: The central caveat is the outcome gap: raising GH and IGF-1 is proven, but that they translate into benefit — or are safe long-term — is not. Add the costs of chronic GH excess (insulin resistance, edema, joint/carpal-tunnel symptoms, a theoretical IGF-1 cancer concern), the fact that development was halted and it was never approved, WADA prohibition, and gray-market products that are frequently mislabeled or contaminated. Treat the trial numbers as clean-drug, short-term pharmacology — not a guide to what an unregulated vial does over months.

trials.finding: Single-dose escalation in healthy adults: CJC-1295 raised plasma GH ~2–10-fold for ~6 days and IGF-1 ~1.5–3-fold for 9–11 days, with an estimated half-life of 5.8–8.1 days; repeated weekly dosing produced sustained IGF-1 elevation.
trials.ref: Teichman et al., 2006, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 91(3):799–805

trials.finding: Continuous stimulation by CJC-1295 in healthy men preserved pulsatile GH secretion (pulse frequency and amplitude unchanged) while raising trough GH ~7.5-fold, mean 24-hour GH ~46% and IGF-I ~45%.
trials.ref: Ionescu & Frohman, 2006, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 91(12):4792–4797

tech.chem: CJC-1295 is a synthetic analog of GHRH built on the GRF(1-29) fragment (the first 29 residues, which retain full biological activity). Four amino-acid substitutions render it resistant to DPP-IV cleavage, extending stability from ~2 minutes to ~30 minutes — this is the 'no-DAC' form, also called Modified GRF(1-29) or tetrasubstituted GRF(1-29). The 'with DAC' form adds a Drug Affinity Complex: a maleimidoproprionic-acid group on a lysine linker that reacts covalently with the free thiol of cysteine-34 on endogenous serum albumin, converting the peptide into a long-circulating albumin conjugate (half-life 5.8–8.1 days). Sources disagree on the exact substitution positions, so specific position claims should be treated with caution.

targets.action: agonist
targets.affinity: no published compound-specific human Ki for CJC-1295; GRF(1-29) retains essentially full GHRH agonist activity, and the modifications were engineered for stability/half-life rather than to change receptor potency
targets.role: the sole primary target — a Gs-coupled GPCR on anterior-pituitary somatotrophs; activation drives GH synthesis and secretion. It works upstream of GH, requiring an intact pituitary.

tech.signaling: GHRHR is a class-B (secretin-family) Gs-coupled GPCR. Agonist binding activates adenylyl cyclase → rising cAMP → PKA activation → CREB-mediated transcription of the GH gene and Ca²⁺-dependent exocytosis of stored GH. Sustained GH acts on hepatic GH receptors (JAK2/STAT5) to raise IGF-1. Because the DAC keeps the receptor tonically engaged, the normal pulsatile drive is overlaid with a raised basal signal; physiological negative feedback (somatostatin tone and IGF-1) still operates, which is why pulsatility can persist even as the baseline rises.

adme.absorb: Administered by subcutaneous injection (intravenous and subcutaneous routes used in trials); systemically bioavailable as a peptide.
adme.distribute: The DAC form binds covalently to circulating serum albumin (Cys34), so the drug distributes as an albumin conjugate and accumulates in plasma with repeated dosing.
adme.metabolise: As a peptide it is ultimately degraded by proteolysis; the no-DAC form is limited chiefly by DPP-IV, which the substitutions resist.
adme.excrete: Cleared as peptide fragments; the DAC's albumin binding is what extends the elimination half-life to 5.8–8.1 days versus ~30 minutes for the no-DAC form.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. The dominant determinant of response is pituitary somatotroph reserve (an intact, responsive GH axis); in pituitary insufficiency, upstream stimulation has little to amplify.
tech.dose: Human trials used single or repeated subcutaneous/intravenous doses in healthy adults to characterise PK/PD; no therapeutic dose is established because it was never approved. Gray-market physique use is described (not recommended) as weekly dosing for the DAC form — exploiting its multi-day half-life — commonly stacked with a ghrelin-receptor agonist such as ipamorelin. These uses are unvalidated for efficacy or safety.
tech.tolerance: Not a receptor-downregulation story like caffeine's. The relevant time-dependent limits are physiological negative feedback (somatostatin and IGF-1 restraining GH output) and the cumulative metabolic consequences of sustained GH/IGF-1 elevation — insulin resistance, fluid retention, joint and carpal-tunnel symptoms — which build with continued use rather than a fading effect.

contrasts.title: CJC-1295 vs ipamorelin — 'how much' vs 'release now'
contrasts.point: This is the classic stack, and the two act on different receptors. CJC-1295 hits the GHRH receptor — it sets how much GH is available to release (amplitude). Ipamorelin mimics ghrelin at the GHSR (ghrelin receptor) — it triggers the release pulse itself and blunts somatostatin, the brake. One raises the ceiling, the other opens the gate — which is the pharmacological logic behind pairing them. Neither, it should be said, has trial evidence for building muscle in healthy people.

contrasts.title: CJC-1295 vs sermorelin — same backbone, different lifespan
contrasts.point: Both are GRF(1-29) GHRH agonists hitting the same receptor — but sermorelin is the bare, unmodified fragment with a half-life of only minutes, and it was actually FDA-approved (for diagnostic use and paediatric GH deficiency) before being withdrawn from the market for commercial reasons. CJC-1295 is what you get when you armor that same backbone against enzymes and bolt on an albumin hook: the identical receptor action, stretched from minutes to days. The comparison shows exactly what the modifications buy — duration, not a new mechanism.

pk.onset: hours — GH rises after dosing, but the meaningful effect is the downstream hormonal cascade, not an acute hit
pk.duration: GH elevated ~6 days and IGF-1 elevated 9–11 days after a single DAC dose
pk.note: Don't read this like a stimulant's stopwatch. The multi-day half-life sets how often you'd dose, but what the body experiences is a sustained rise in GH and then IGF-1 over days — a hormonal cascade, not a blood-level peak you feel. With repeated weekly DAC dosing, IGF-1 accumulates rather than returning to baseline between doses.

questions: If it releases growth hormone, why isn't ipamorelin just the same as injecting HGH — and why can't you keep raising the dose for more?
Why is it called the "clean" or "selective" peptide when older versions did the same job?
Why is it almost always paired with a second peptide, CJC-1295?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) It doesn't add outside GH — it presses your own pituitary's release button, so your body's brake (somatostatin) still caps each pulse. That's why it gives pulses, not the flat supraphysiologic levels of injected HGH, and why past a point more dose just means more receptor engagement, not more GH. (2) The older GHRPs (GHRP-6, GHRP-2) released GH but also spiked hunger, ACTH and cortisol; ipamorelin was engineered to trigger the same GH release while leaving those alone — that selectivity IS its whole reason for existing. (3) Ipamorelin and CJC-1295 hit two DIFFERENT receptors (the ghrelin receptor vs the GHRH receptor), so together they push the same GH pulse harder than either alone.

ipamorelin.bigIdea: Ipamorelin doesn't give you growth hormone — it cleanly presses your own pituitary's \"release a pulse of GH now\" button, without the hunger and stress-hormone spikes of the older peptides that did the same thing.
ipamorelin.analogy: Think of your pituitary as a water tower with a release valve. Injecting HGH is trucking in outside water and flooding the system. Ipamorelin instead just yanks the valve open so the tower dumps its own stored water in a pulse — and a float-valve (somatostatin) still limits how much comes out. The older peptides yanked that valve too, but also knocked over the pantry (hunger) and the alarm panel (cortisol) on the way. Ipamorelin was filed down to pull only the valve.

mechSteps.t: It impersonates ghrelin
mechSteps.predict: Your stomach makes a hunger hormone that also happens to trigger GH release. Which receptor does ipamorelin borrow — and why would mimicking a gut hormone move growth hormone?
mechSteps.d: Ipamorelin is a five-amino-acid peptide that docks into the growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHSR-1a) — the same receptor your natural hunger hormone ghrelin uses. It binds with genuinely high, low-nanomolar affinity, so microgram-scale doses fully engage the target.

mechSteps.t: It tells the pituitary to fire a GH pulse
mechSteps.predict: That receptor sits on the GH-making cells of your pituitary. What do you think happens when it's switched on — a steady trickle, or a burst?
mechSteps.d: GHSR-1a activation drives the somatotroph cells of your anterior pituitary to release a single pulse of stored growth hormone. This works alongside your natural GHRH signal and by blunting somatostatin (the brake) — amplifying the pulses your body already produces rather than replacing them.

mechSteps.t: The trick is what it DOESN'T switch on
mechSteps.predict: GHRP-6 and GHRP-2 hit the same receptor but made people ravenous and spiked cortisol. Why doesn't ipamorelin?
mechSteps.d: Ipamorelin was engineered to trigger the GH-release pathway while barely touching the ACTH/cortisol and prolactin pathways — and with minimal hunger. In the original preclinical work it matched GHRP-6 for GH release but, strikingly, did not raise cortisol or ACTH above what plain GHRH does. That selectivity is the entire point of the molecule.

mechSteps.t: The pulse becomes IGF-1 — but stays self-limiting
mechSteps.predict: GH itself is short-lived. What does the liver turn that pulse into, and why can't you just crank the dose for endless GH?
mechSteps.d: The GH pulse travels to the liver, which converts it into IGF-1, the mediator of most of GH's downstream repair and growth effects. Crucially, because you're using your own axis, negative feedback (somatostatin and IGF-1 itself) caps the response — so it produces pulses, not the sustained, supraphysiologic flood of injected HGH.

framework.target: The growth hormone secretagogue receptor, GHSR-1a (the ghrelin receptor) — a G-protein-coupled receptor on pituitary somatotrophs. Ipamorelin is a selective agonist: it switches the receptor on, driving GH release.
framework.affinity: Genuinely high — low-nanomolar (reported on the order of ~1 nM). Unlike caffeine, where you must ask whether a real dose even reaches the target, here it clearly does: the affinity is why microgram-scale peptide doses fully engage the receptor. This is a real receptor with a real binding constant, not a substrate or a deficiency-correction.
framework.adme: A peptide, so it's injected subcutaneously — swallow it and your gut digests it. Terminal half-life is short, ~2 hours in human studies, cleared by peptidases and the kidneys. The felt event is the GH pulse itself, which peaks within ~40 minutes and is over well before the drug fully clears.
framework.genetics: No validated ipamorelin-specific pharmacogenetic switch. GHSR gene variants exist and baseline GH-axis responsiveness varies with age, body fat and sleep, but none of this has been characterised for ipamorelin — treat individual response as uncharacterised.
framework.evidence: Thin and old. Human data is essentially early pharmacokinetic/safety work (it demonstrably raises GH in people) plus a Phase II program for postoperative ileus that was discontinued for lack of efficacy. There are no controlled trials showing it improves muscle, fat loss, recovery or aging in healthy people, and no long-term safety data for that use.

pk.onset: 15–30 min (subcutaneous)
pk.peak: GH peaks ~40 min after dosing
pk.duration: a single GH pulse, largely resolved within ~2–3 h
pk.note: The ~2 h half-life and single sharp GH pulse are why the effect is a brief burst, not a plateau. This short duration is exactly why grey-market protocols split it into multiple daily injections to mimic the body's natural pulsatility — but note that no controlled trial validates that those repeated pulses produce any physique or recovery benefit.

journey.d: It's injected under the skin — it can't be taken orally because your gut would digest the peptide. Real caution: grey-market peptides are frequently mislabeled, underdosed or contaminated, so what's in the vial often isn't verified.
It circulates briefly, seeking the ghrelin receptor. With a ~2 h half-life it doesn't linger — it's a short window, not a steady state.
It binds GHSR-1a on the GH-making cells and triggers a single pulse of your own growth hormone, peaking around 40 minutes — while sparing the cortisol and prolactin pathways the older peptides hit.
The GH pulse reaches the liver, which converts it into IGF-1, the messenger behind most of GH's repair and growth effects. Your body's feedback loops cap how big this gets.
The peptide is broken down by peptidases and cleared via the kidneys. Metabolites are detectable in urine — which is why it appears on anti-doping prohibited lists and produces positive tests.

myths.myth: Ipamorelin is basically the same as taking HGH.
myths.truth: It isn't. Injected HGH floods your body with outside hormone at flat, often supraphysiologic levels. Ipamorelin triggers your own pituitary to fire a pulse, which your feedback system (somatostatin, IGF-1) still limits. Milder, self-capping, and dependent on your own axis working.

myths.myth: "Selective" and "clean" mean side-effect-free.
myths.truth: Clean is relative to the older GHRPs — it avoids their cortisol, ACTH and hunger spikes. But it still raises IGF-1, commonly causes water retention, and has no long-term safety data in healthy people. "Selective" describes which pathways it spares, not that it's harmless.

myths.myth: It's clinically proven to build muscle, burn fat and reverse aging.
myths.truth: No. Human data shows it raises GH acutely and includes a Phase II trial for postoperative ileus that was discontinued for lack of efficacy. There are zero controlled trials demonstrating body-composition, recovery or anti-aging benefit in healthy people. Those claims are extrapolation from "GH went up."

myths.myth: It's a legal supplement.
myths.truth: No regulator has approved it for any use. It's sold as a "research chemical," it's on anti-doping prohibited lists (a banned peptide hormone/secretagogue), and its own drug development was abandoned.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a "clean" GH boost using your own pituitary — pulses of your own growth hormone for sleep, recovery and body composition, without the hunger and cortisol of older peptides, and usually stacked with CJC-1295. The GH-release part is real; the benefit part is where it thins out.

**The evidence cost:** human data shows ipamorelin raises GH acutely — and stops there. There are no controlled trials showing it improves muscle, fat, recovery or aging in healthy people, and its one serious clinical program (postoperative ileus) was discontinued for lack of efficacy.

**The physiological cost:** it raises IGF-1 (a growth signal with unresolved long-term questions), commonly causes water retention and joint discomfort, and has essentially no long-term safety data for physique use.

**The regulatory cost:** not approved anywhere; development was abandoned. It appears on anti-doping prohibited lists and metabolites are detectable in urine — a positive test ends seasons for tested athletes.

**The product-quality cost:** it's a grey-market injectable peptide. Independent testing repeatedly finds such products mislabeled, underdosed, overdosed or contaminated — so you often don't know what you're injecting.

**The honest bottom line:** this page exists to explain the pharmacology of a genuinely elegant, selective molecule — not to help you run a protocol. "It cleanly releases your own GH" is true; "therefore it will recomposition your body" is unproven.

start.q: In one line: does ipamorelin add growth hormone to your body?
start.a: No — it presses your own pituitary's button to release a pulse of your own GH, which your feedback system still caps. It's a secretagogue, not exogenous hormone.

how.q: Ipamorelin and GHRP-6 hit the same receptor — so what makes ipamorelin "selective"?
how.a: It triggers the GH-release pathway while sparing the ACTH/cortisol, prolactin and hunger pathways that GHRP-6 and GHRP-2 also switched on. Same receptor, cleaner downstream profile.

use.q: Why is it paired with CJC-1295 rather than just using more ipamorelin?
use.a: CJC-1295 hits a different receptor (the GHRH receptor); ipamorelin hits the ghrelin receptor. Two different levers on the same GH pulse push it harder together than raising either alone — which mostly just saturates one receptor.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence for the benefits people want?
evidence.a: Weak for those benefits. It's proven to raise GH acutely in humans, but there are no controlled trials for muscle, fat or aging, and its main clinical program was discontinued for lack of efficacy.

canExplain: Why triggering your own pituitary is different from — and self-limiting compared to — injecting HGH
The exact receptor it hits (GHSR-1a, the ghrelin receptor) and why microgram doses fully engage it
What "selective" really means here — which pathways it fires and which it spares
Why it's paired with CJC-1295: two different receptors, one bigger GH pulse
Why "it raises GH" is proven but "it builds muscle / reverses aging" is not

selfTest.q: A friend says ipamorelin is safer than HGH because "it's natural — it's your own growth hormone." Give the accurate two-part response.
selfTest.a: (1) It's true the GH is your own and the response is self-capping via feedback, so it's milder than a flat HGH flood. (2) But "your own" doesn't mean studied — it still raises IGF-1, causes water retention, is unapproved and banned, and has no long-term safety data in healthy users.

selfTest.q: Ipamorelin demonstrably raises GH in humans, yet it earns only cautious evidence marks. Why?
selfTest.a: Because raising GH is a mechanism, not an outcome. The one serious clinical program (postoperative ileus) failed for lack of efficacy, and no trial shows the popular physique/recovery/aging benefits — so the proven fact (GH rises) doesn't establish the claimed results.

selfTest.q: Why does ipamorelin's ~2 h half-life tell you little about whether repeated daily dosing "works"?
selfTest.a: The half-life sets how brief each GH pulse is and why people split doses — but it says nothing about efficacy. No controlled trial has shown that mimicking natural pulsatility with repeated injections produces any body-composition or recovery benefit in healthy people.

evi.howStrong: Honest and thin. Ipamorelin has real human pharmacokinetic/safety data showing it releases GH acutely, and solid preclinical work establishing its defining selectivity. But it never earned an approval: its most serious clinical program — Phase II for postoperative ileus under Helsinn — was discontinued for lack of efficacy. There are no randomised controlled trials for the reasons people actually take it (muscle, fat loss, recovery, anti-aging), and no long-term safety data in healthy users. Treat the popular benefits as extrapolation from a mechanism, not proven outcomes.
evi.whatItShows: What's genuinely shown: in humans, ipamorelin produces a clean, dose-related pulse of growth hormone without the cortisol, ACTH or prolactin rises of older GHRPs. What's NOT shown: that this pulse translates into meaningful changes in body composition, athletic recovery or healthy aging.
evi.effect: In human PK/PD work the GH response was a single pulse peaking around 40 minutes and declining to negligible levels within a couple of hours — a real, measurable acute effect on GH. No comparable, verifiable effect size exists for downstream physique or performance endpoints in healthy people.
evi.whoBenefits: Studied contexts were early-phase human volunteers (safety/PK) and postoperative-ileus patients (where it failed). Its use in healthy people for physique or longevity is entirely off-label, grey-market and unstudied for both efficacy and long-term safety.
evi.caveats: The crux is the gap between mechanism and outcome: "GH went up" is well documented; "you will gain muscle or age better" is not. Add the discontinued development, unapproved status, anti-doping ban, IGF-1 elevation, and the fact that grey-market products are frequently mislabeled or contaminated. The curated human-evidence rating reflects that real human data exists — but that data is about GH release and safety, not the benefits it's marketed for.

trials.finding: Ipamorelin was the first GH secretagogue shown to release GH with potency/efficacy similar to GHRP-6 while — strikingly — NOT raising ACTH or cortisol above GHRH levels, establishing its defining selectivity (preclinical: rat pituitary cells and swine).
trials.ref: Raun et al., 1998, Eur J Endocrinol 139:552–561

trials.finding: In healthy human volunteers, intravenous ipamorelin showed dose-proportional pharmacokinetics with a terminal half-life of ~2 h and produced a single GH pulse peaking at ~0.67 h — direct human evidence that it releases GH acutely.
trials.ref: Gobburu et al., 1999, Pharm Res 16:1412–1416

trials.finding: Ipamorelin (Novo Nordisk NNC 26-0161) advanced to Phase II for postoperative ileus under Helsinn Therapeutics but was discontinued for lack of efficacy; it has no approved status and is marketed only as a research peptide.
trials.ref: Development history (per manufacturer/regulatory record; summarised in public compound monographs)

tech.chem: Ipamorelin (developmental code NNC 26-0161; PubChem CID 9831659) is a synthetic pentapeptide, Aib-His-D-2-Nal-D-Phe-Lys-NH2, derived from GHRP-1. The alpha-aminoisobutyric acid (Aib) cap, the unnatural D-amino acids and the C-terminal amide confer metabolic stability and drive its high receptor affinity. It is not a steroid and not structurally related to growth hormone itself — it's a small peptide agonist that acts upstream, on the receptor that controls GH release.

targets.action: selective agonist
targets.affinity: low-nanomolar (reported on the order of ~1 nM) — genuinely high, so microgram-scale doses fully engage it
targets.role: the sole primary target; a Gq/11-coupled GPCR on pituitary somatotrophs. Activation drives a pulse of GH release while, uniquely for this class, sparing the ACTH/cortisol and prolactin pathways.

tech.signaling: GHSR-1a is a class-A GPCR coupling chiefly to Gq/11. Agonist binding activates phospholipase C, raising IP3/DAG and intracellular calcium in somatotrophs, which triggers exocytosis of stored growth hormone. The receptor also has high constitutive (ligand-independent) activity. Functionally the GH pulse is shaped by the interplay of this ghrelin-receptor drive with hypothalamic GHRH (stimulatory) and somatostatin (inhibitory) tone — which is why ipamorelin synergises with GHRH-receptor agonists like CJC-1295 (two distinct receptors converging on the same pulse) and why the response remains self-limiting via feedback. Ipamorelin's selectivity is attributed to a receptor-activation profile that favours the GH-release output over the ACTH/prolactin outputs seen with GHRP-6 and GHRP-2.

adme.absorb: Not orally bioavailable (a peptide, digested in the gut); administered by subcutaneous injection in practice, intravenously in the human PK study.
adme.distribute: Small steady-state volume of distribution (~0.22 L/kg in human study), consistent with limited tissue distribution for a peptide.
adme.metabolise: Degraded by peptidases rather than hepatic CYP enzymes; its D-amino-acid and Aib modifications slow this relative to natural peptides.
adme.excrete: Cleared renally with a clearance of ~0.078 L/h/kg and a terminal half-life of ~2 h; peptide and metabolites are detectable in urine, the basis for anti-doping detection.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. GHSR gene variants and general GH-axis responsiveness (modulated by age, adiposity, sleep and sex-steroid status) will influence response, but none has been characterised for ipamorelin — individual variation should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: No approved therapeutic dose exists. Human research used intravenous infusions across a dose range for PK characterisation; grey-market physique use commonly cites microgram-scale subcutaneous doses (often ~100–300 mcg) given once or multiple times daily to mimic natural pulsatility — this is a description of what is reported, not a recommendation, and none of these regimens is validated for efficacy or safety.
tech.tolerance: There is no classic receptor-downregulation tolerance documented at typical use, but continuous (non-pulsatile) GHSR stimulation can desensitise the GH response, which is the rationale offered for pulsatile dosing. The more relevant time-dependent concern is sustained IGF-1 elevation and its unresolved long-term safety, not tachyphylaxis.

questions: If it's a pill, how can it raise growth hormone when real GH has to be injected?
Why does it deepen your sleep and blow up your appetite at the same time — is that one switch or two?
If a year-long trial proved it adds muscle and restores 'youthful' IGF-1, why did no regulator ever approve it — for anything?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) It isn't growth hormone — it's a non-peptide molecule that survives your gut and switches ON the ghrelin receptor (GHSR-1a), so your OWN pituitary keeps firing natural GH pulses. (2) One switch: deep sleep, appetite and the GH surge all ride the same ghrelin signal, which is exactly why they arrive together. (3) Because it moved the biomarkers but not the outcomes — a year of dosing raised IGF-1 to young-adult levels and added ~1.1 kg of lean mass yet produced no gain in strength or physical function (Nass 2008), and the hip-fracture trial missed its functional endpoint while flagging heart failure (Adunsky 2011). Biomarker up, benefit flat — and it raises blood sugar and retains water on the way.

mk-677-ibutamoren.bigIdea: MK-677 adds no growth hormone of its own — it holds down your body's own ghrelin switch so your pituitary keeps releasing GH in natural pulses; the deeper sleep, the appetite, the higher IGF-1 and the higher blood sugar all follow from that one sustained signal.
mk-677-ibutamoren.stakes: It's the most popular oral 'GH optimiser' on the gray market precisely because it's the only ghrelin-mimetic that works as a once-daily pill instead of a needle — which is also why it's sold far more widely than its record (zero approvals in 25+ years of trials) could ever justify.
mk-677-ibutamoren.analogy: It's not GH poured into the tank — it's a hand held down on your body's own GH throttle. Real ghrelin taps that throttle for a moment when your stomach is empty; MK-677 leans on it for about a day, so your pituitary keeps firing its own natural pulses instead of receiving a foreign flood.

mechSteps.t: It copies your hunger hormone
mechSteps.predict: An empty stomach releases a hormone that makes you hungry AND triggers a growth-hormone pulse. MK-677 mimics it — which receptor does it grab?
mechSteps.d: Ibutamoren is a non-peptide that imitates ghrelin, the 'empty stomach' hormone, and docks into ghrelin's own receptor, GHSR-1a, on your pituitary and hypothalamus. Same receptor ghrelin uses, a completely different (non-peptide) scaffold.

mechSteps.t: A pill that survives — and won't let go
mechSteps.predict: Ghrelin-mimicking peptides have to be injected. What lets this one work swallowed, once a day?
mechSteps.d: Because it's non-peptide, it isn't digested like a peptide — it's absorbed orally and cleared slowly, with an elimination half-life around 24 hours. One daily dose keeps the receptor engaged around the clock instead of for a brief spike.

mechSteps.t: Your own pituitary keeps pulsing GH
mechSteps.predict: It turns the receptor ON rather than flooding you with hormone. What does your pituitary do in response — and what does that preserve?
mechSteps.d: Agonising GHSR-1a makes your pituitary release its OWN growth hormone in natural pulses (largest during deep sleep), amplifying GHRH and easing off the 'stop' hormone somatostatin. It enhances your natural rhythm rather than clamping a fixed foreign level.

mechSteps.t: Sustained GH becomes elevated IGF-1
mechSteps.predict: Growth hormone doesn't act on muscle directly for most of its anabolic effect. What does the liver make from it — the real growth signal?
mechSteps.d: Day-round GH drives your liver to raise IGF-1, the systemic anabolic mediator, toward youthful levels. This builds over days to weeks of steady dosing — which is why lean mass creeps up slowly rather than overnight.

mechSteps.t: The costs ride the very same signal
mechSteps.predict: If one ghrelin switch drives everything, what unwanted effects arrive bundled with the muscle and sleep?
mechSteps.d: The same ghrelin activation that deepens sleep also spikes appetite (hypothalamic GHSR), and sustained GH/IGF-1 retains water and pushes fasting glucose up while lowering insulin sensitivity. These aren't side quirks — they're the on-target price of the mechanism.

framework.target: GHSR-1a, the ghrelin receptor — a Gq/11-coupled GPCR on pituitary somatotrophs and the hypothalamus. MK-677 is an agonist: it turns the receptor ON (the opposite of caffeine, which blocks its target). Crucially it's a secretagogue, not a hormone — it makes your gland secrete GH, it isn't GH itself.
framework.affinity: High — sub-nanomolar affinity for GHSR-1a. Unlike caffeine, where you have to ask whether a realistic dose even reaches the target, here it clearly does: single-digit-to-25 mg oral doses fully engage the receptor. That's why the GH/IGF-1 elevation is reliable, and why the appetite and metabolic effects come bundled with it — they're all one over-driven receptor.
framework.adme: Orally active non-peptide, ~60% bioavailability, peak blood level at ~2–3 h, cleared by the liver (chiefly CYP3A4), elimination half-life ≈24 h → once-daily dosing reaches steady state in about a week. But note: the felt/measured effects (IGF-1, muscle, sleep) run on a days-to-weeks timescale of sustained signalling, not the blood-clearance clock.
framework.genetics: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. The GH/IGF-1 axis becomes less responsive with age (the reason trials targeted older adults), and how much someone's fasting glucose and fluid balance shift varies person to person — but no single-gene predictor has been characterised, so treat individual metabolic response as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Moderate and split. It reliably raises GH/IGF-1, reverses short-term diet-induced protein wasting, and deepens slow-wave/REM sleep. But the endpoints that matter failed: a one-year trial added lean mass with no gain in strength or function (Nass 2008), and the hip-fracture Phase IIb missed its functional endpoint and flagged excess heart failure (Adunsky 2011). It has never been approved for any use, and there is essentially no long-term safety data in healthy people using it for physique.

pk.onset: GH pulse and appetite within hours; deeper sleep within nights
pk.peak: blood level peaks ~2–3 h after the dose
pk.duration: all-day coverage from one dose; steady state in ~1 week
pk.note: The ~24 h half-life is why one dose a day is enough, and why night dosing lines up with your natural nocturnal GH and deep-sleep pulse. But don't read it like a stimulant's stopwatch: IGF-1 elevation and any body-composition change accrue over weeks of steady dosing, and the insulin-resistance cost builds over the same timescale rather than clearing overnight.

journey.d: You swallow it. Real caution here: gray-market products are frequently mislabeled, underdosed, overdosed, or contain something else entirely. Being non-peptide, it survives digestion (a peptide would be destroyed) and is absorbed across the gut — roughly 60% gets in.
It circulates with a ~24 h half-life, so a daily dose accumulates to a steady level over about a week rather than spiking and vanishing.
It binds the ghrelin receptor on pituitary somatotrophs, triggering your own GH pulses — largest during deep sleep. The same receptor in the hypothalamus drives the surge in appetite.
GHSR activity in sleep circuits deepens slow-wave and REM sleep, and the nocturnal GH surge rides that deep-sleep pulse — the effect users notice first.

Sustained GH acts on the liver to raise IGF-1; muscle and bone see an anabolic signal over weeks. But fluid retention, joint aches, and rising fasting glucose with falling insulin sensitivity ride along — on-target, not bad luck.

The liver metabolises it (mainly via CYP3A4) and metabolites leave in urine. WADA bans GH secretagogues in and out of competition, and MK-677 is detectable — a career-ending positive for tested athletes.

myths.myth: It's growth hormone in a pill — the same as injecting HGH.
myths.truth: It contains no GH. It's a secretagogue that makes your OWN pituitary release GH in natural pulses — so it does nothing if your gland can't respond, and it preserves your pulsatile rhythm instead of flooding you with a fixed foreign dose.

myths.myth: It builds muscle and makes you stronger.
myths.truth: In the one-year Nass trial it added ~1.1 kg of lean mass but produced NO improvement in strength or physical function — and a good chunk of the visible 'fullness' is water retention, not contractile muscle. More mass on a scan is not more strength.

myths.myth: It's basically a harmless supplement.
myths.truth: It reliably raises fasting glucose and lowers insulin sensitivity, causes water retention and joint aches, and in the elderly hip-fracture trial an excess of heart-failure cases stopped the study early. It's explicitly not for diabetics or anyone with cardiac risk, and not approved anywhere.

myths.myth: It's legal because it's not a steroid.
myths.truth: 'Not a steroid' describes its chemistry, not its status. It's an unapproved investigational drug sold as a 'research chemical,' WADA-banned as a GH secretagogue, and gray-market bottles routinely don't contain what the label claims.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** an oral, needle-free way to keep GH and IGF-1 elevated — famous for deep sleep, appetite and fuller-looking muscle. The GH/IGF-1 rise and the sleep effect have genuine trial support; that part isn't hype.

**The muscle reality:** the best long-term human trial (Nass 2008, one full year at 25 mg/day) added ~1.1 kg lean mass but NO measurable strength or function — and much of the visible fullness is water, not contractile tissue.

**The metabolic cost:** it consistently raises fasting glucose and lowers insulin sensitivity. This is why it's flatly contraindicated in diabetes and pre-diabetes — an on-target effect of sustained GH/IGF-1, not a rare reaction.

**The cardiac cost:** in the elderly hip-fracture trial an excess of congestive-heart-failure cases appeared in the MK-677 arm and the study was stopped early. Fluid retention and edema are built into the mechanism.
**The legal & testing cost:** not approved for any use anywhere, WADA-banned as a GH secretagogue, and detectable — a career-ending positive for any tested athlete.

**The product-quality cost:** sold gray-market as a 'research chemical'; independent testing repeatedly finds these products underdosed, overdosed, or containing a different compound. This page exists to explain the pharmacology — not to hand you a protocol.

start.q: In one line: does MK-677 put growth hormone into your body?
start.a: No. It's a secretagogue — it flips your own ghrelin receptor so your pituitary secretes its OWN GH in natural pulses. No pituitary response, no effect.

how.q: MK-677 copies ghrelin — what does binding GHSR-1a actually set off?
how.a: It agonises the ghrelin receptor on the pituitary and hypothalamus, triggering pulsatile GH release; the liver turns that sustained GH into elevated IGF-1. The same signal also drives appetite, water retention and insulin resistance.

use.q: Why is night-time dosing the usual choice — and why is timing otherwise loose?
use.a: The ~24 h half-life gives all-day coverage from one dose, and night dosing lines up with your natural nocturnal GH and deep-sleep pulse. But IGF-1 and any body-composition change accrue over weeks of steady dosing, so it's a daily habit, not a pre-workout stopwatch.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really?
evidence.a: Moderate for the biomarker: it reliably raises GH/IGF-1, reverses diet-induced nitrogen wasting, and deepens sleep. But the endpoints that matter failed — a year of dosing added mass without strength (Nass 2008), and the hip-fracture trial missed its functional endpoint and flagged heart failure (Adunsky 2011). Never approved; no long-term safety data in healthy users.

canExplain: Why MK-677 raises growth hormone without containing any GH — and why it needs a working pituitary
The exact receptor it turns on (GHSR-1a), and why a pill can do what other ghrelin-mimetics need an injection for
Why it deepens sleep and boosts appetite through one and the same ghrelin signal
Why it reliably raises IGF-1 and lean mass yet failed to make people stronger or recover better
Why higher blood sugar, water retention and a heart-failure signal are on-target costs, not bad luck

selfTest.q: A friend says MK-677 is 'growth hormone you can swallow.' Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) It contains no GH — it's a secretagogue that makes your own pituitary release GH, so it does nothing if the gland can't respond, and it keeps your natural pulsatile rhythm rather than delivering a fixed foreign dose. (2) That same ghrelin receptor it activates also raises appetite, retains water and pushes blood sugar up — the costs are inseparable from the effect.

selfTest.q: MK-677 reliably raises IGF-1 and added ~1 kg of lean mass over a year — so why has no regulator ever approved it?
selfTest.a: Because the mass didn't translate into function. The year-long Nass trial showed no strength or physical-function gain, and the hip-fracture Phase IIb missed its functional co-endpoint while showing an excess heart-failure signal. A biomarker moving the right way is not a clinical benefit.

selfTest.q: The half-life is ~24 h. Why doesn't that tell you when you'll 'feel' results?
selfTest.a: Because the meaningful effects aren't a blood-level hit. A GH pulse and deeper sleep can come within a night, but IGF-1 elevation, any muscle change, and the insulin-resistance cost build over days-to-weeks of steady receptor activation. The half-life sets once-daily dosing, not the results clock.

evi.howStrong: Unusually good for a gray-market compound — MK-677 has real Merck-run randomised human trials, including a one-year placebo-controlled study, which is far more than most 'research chemicals' can claim. But 'good for the class' hides the verdict: essentially every biomarker endpoint succeeded and every endpoint that mattered clinically failed. That's why, after more than 25 years, it is approved for nothing and has no long-term safety data in healthy people using it for physique.
evi.whatItShows: Consistently, it does what a secretagogue should: raises pulsatile GH and IGF-1 toward youthful levels, reverses short-term diet-induced protein loss, and deepens slow-wave and REM sleep. What it has NOT shown is that any of this makes people stronger, more functional, or clinically better off.
evi.effect: In the pivotal one-year trial (25 mg/day, healthy adults aged 60–81) fat-free mass rose ~1.1 kg versus a ~0.5 kg loss on placebo, with IGF-1 restored to the young-adult range — but strength and physical function did not improve, while fasting glucose rose and insulin sensitivity fell. In hip-fracture recovery, IGF-1 climbed sharply yet the functional endpoint was not met.
evi.whoBenefits: Studied populations were people with a blunted GH axis — healthy older adults and catabolic or hip-fracture patients — where the goal was restoring lean mass and IGF-1. Use by young healthy people for physique or 'anti-aging' is entirely off-label, gray-market, and unstudied for long-term safety.
evi.caveats: The recurring theme is biomarker-without-benefit, plus real costs: insulin resistance and higher fasting glucose, fluid retention and joint aches, and a congestive-heart-failure signal that stopped the hip-fracture trial early. It's contraindicated in diabetes and active cancer (IGF-1 is a growth signal). Treat the clean trial numbers as best-case under dosed, monitored conditions — a mislabeled gray-market capsule is not that.

trials.finding: Once-daily oral MK-677 for up to 4 weeks in healthy adults aged 64–81 enhanced pulsatile GH release and, at 25 mg/day, restored serum IGF-1 to young-adult levels.
trials.ref: Chapman et al., 1996, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 81(12):4249–4257

trials.finding: In healthy volunteers made catabolic by caloric restriction, MK-677 reversed diet-induced nitrogen wasting (mean nitrogen balance +0.31 vs −1.48 g/day on placebo) alongside raised GH/IGF-1.
trials.ref: Murphy et al., 1998, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 83(2):320–325

trials.finding: Prolonged oral MK-677 improved sleep quality, increasing stage IV (slow-wave) sleep ~50% in young subjects and increasing REM sleep in both young and older adults.
trials.ref: Copinschi et al., 1997, Neuroendocrinology 66(4):278–286

trials.finding: One-year randomised trial in 65 healthy older adults (25 mg/day): MK-677 raised IGF-1 to young-adult levels and increased fat-free mass ~1.1 kg vs a ~0.5 kg loss on placebo, but did NOT improve strength or physical function, and raised fasting glucose while reducing insulin sensitivity.
trials.ref: Nass et al., 2008, Ann Intern Med 149(9):601–611

trials.finding: Phase IIb hip-fracture recovery trial (123 patients, 25 mg/day): IGF-1 rose ~84% vs 17% on placebo, but the stair-climb-power functional endpoint was not statistically significant, and an excess of congestive-heart-failure cases led to early termination.
trials.ref: Adunsky et al., 2011, Arch Gerontol Geriatr 53(2):183–189

tech.chem: Ibutamoren (MK-677; developmental codes MK-0677 / L-163,191; commonly supplied as the mesylate salt) is a non-peptide spiropiperidine growth-hormone secretagogue — a small, orally active molecule, neither a peptide nor a hormone. Being non-peptide is the whole point: peptide ghrelin-mimetics such as GHRP-6 and ipamorelin are digested and must be injected, whereas MK-677 survives the gut and works as a once-daily pill. It mimics the endogenous ligand ghrelin at its receptor despite sharing none of ghrelin's peptide structure.

targets.action: agonist
targets.affinity: high, sub-nanomolar — fully engaged at oral doses of a few to 25 mg
targets.role: the sole primary target; a Gq/11-coupled GPCR on pituitary somatotrophs and hypothalamus. Agonism drives pulsatile GH release, appetite, and slow-wave sleep.

tech.signaling: GHSR-1a is a Gq/11-coupled GPCR notable for high constitutive activity. Agonism activates phospholipase C → IP3/DAG → a rise in intracellular Ca²⁺ in pituitary somatotrophs, triggering GH secretion; it also potentiates GHRH signalling and blunts somatostatin ('stop') tone, which is why it amplifies natural GH pulsatility rather than clamping a fixed level. Sustained GH acts on hepatic GH receptors to raise IGF-1, the systemic anabolic mediator. Central GHSR on hypothalamic arcuate NPY/AgRP neurons drives the orexigenic (appetite) effect, and GHSR activity in sleep-regulating circuits underlies the slow-wave/REM enhancement.

adme.absorb: orally active non-peptide, ~60% bioavailability; peak plasma concentration at ~2–3 h.
adme.distribute: reaches the pituitary, hypothalamus and peripheral GH/IGF-1 target tissues; acts through its GPCR rather than accumulating in a depot.
adme.metabolise: hepatic, chiefly CYP3A4-mediated oxidation.
adme.excrete: metabolites eliminated in urine and feces; parent and metabolites are detectable, underpinning WADA anti-doping assays. Terminal half-life ≈24 h supports once-daily dosing, with steady state in ~1 week.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker analogous to caffeine's CYP1A2. GH/IGF-1 axis responsiveness declines with age (the rationale for testing it in older adults), and the magnitude of the glucose/insulin-resistance and fluid-retention response varies between individuals — but no single-gene switch has been characterised, so individual metabolic response should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: Trial doses were 2, 10 and 25 mg/day, with 25 mg/day the effective dose in the Chapman, Nass and Adunsky studies; steady state in ~1 week. Gray-market physique use is commonly cited around 10–25 mg taken at night — a description, not a recommendation. No approved therapeutic dose exists for any indication.
tech.tolerance: Not a caffeine-style receptor-downregulation tolerance over the short term — GH/IGF-1 elevation is sustained across dosing. The dominant time-dependent consequences are instead metabolic: cumulative rise in fasting glucose and fall in insulin sensitivity, plus fluid retention, that build with continued use. The GH/IGF-1 axis renormalises after stopping.

contrasts.title: MK-677 vs injected HGH — throttle vs flood
contrasts.point: Same goal (more GH signalling), opposite machinery. Injected human GH pours a fixed, foreign dose straight into your blood, overriding and suppressing your own axis. MK-677 leans on your own throttle — it agonises the ghrelin receptor so your pituitary releases its OWN GH in natural pulses, which preserves the pulsatile rhythm and has a built-in ceiling (it can't work if your gland can't respond). Milder and needle-free — but it drags along ghrelin's appetite plus the same fluid retention and insulin resistance, and it's still unapproved.

contrasts.title: MK-677 vs SARMs (ostarine) — the same failed exam
contrasts.point: Two different molecules, one identical verdict. Each reliably moves a headline biomarker — ostarine adds lean mass, MK-677 raises IGF-1 and lean mass — and each then failed the endpoint that actually matters: physical function and strength. Both were rejected by regulators despite 'working' on paper. It's the cleanest lesson on this site — a number moving the right way is not the same as a person being better off.

questions: If growth hormone is what builds you, why does injecting it barely raise your strength even as the scale and the scan say you gained 'muscle'?
Why does a hormone with a half-life of only a few hours keep working for days after the injection has cleared?
Why does the same molecule that a deficient child genuinely needs turn into a diabetes-and-swelling machine when a healthy adult uses it for physique?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Most of the early 'lean mass' is water and connective tissue, and the anabolic signal runs mostly through IGF-1 and collagen turnover — real body-composition change, but in healthy adults the controlled trials show little or no gain in actual strength or aerobic capacity (Liu 2007; Liu 2008; Blackman 2002). (2) The hormone itself is gone in hours, but it orders your liver to pump out IGF-1, and that slower-rising, slower-clearing messenger — plus the gene transcription it drives — is what carries the effect long after the shot. (3) GH is a body-wide metabolic signal: pushed above the physiological range it antagonises insulin (raising blood sugar), holds fluid (edema, carpal tunnel), and drives tissue overgrowth (acromegalic features, and a real concern about feeding existing tumours).

human-growth-hormone-somatropin.bigIdea: Somatropin isn't a muscle-builder you feel — it's a body-wide growth and metabolism command that mostly speaks through a second messenger, IGF-1, and the same signal that heals a truly deficient body overgrows and dysregulates a healthy one.
human-growth-hormone-somatropin.analogy: Think of your pituitary as a thermostat that releases growth hormone in careful pulses. Injecting somatropin is like bypassing the thermostat and holding the furnace open by hand: you get heat, but you've overridden the feedback that kept it safe — and the furnace doesn't warm the room directly, it tells the boiler (your liver) to make the IGF-1 that does the actual work. More heat than the house was built for is exactly how you crack the pipes: insulin resistance, swelling, and slow overgrowth.

mechSteps.t: You inject the finished hormone, not a nudge to make more
mechSteps.predict: Your pituitary already makes GH in pulses. What changes when you inject the hormone itself instead of stimulating the gland?
mechSteps.d: Somatropin is recombinant human growth hormone — the identical 191-amino-acid protein your pituitary secretes. Injecting it floods the system continuously rather than in the tightly controlled pulses your body normally uses, overriding the natural rhythm and its feedback brakes.

mechSteps.t: It clamps a receptor pair into the 'on' shape
mechSteps.predict: The GH receptor already sits on the cell surface as a pre-formed pair. If it's already paired, what does GH binding actually switch on?
mechSteps.d: One GH molecule grips two growth hormone receptors (GHR) through two different binding faces, twisting the pre-existing receptor dimer into an active conformation. That twist activates the receptor-bound kinase JAK2, which lights up the STAT5 signalling cascade.

mechSteps.t: STAT5 carries the order into the nucleus
mechSteps.predict: JAK2 has fired. The receptor is a cell-surface antenna, not a gene — so how does the message reach the DNA?
mechSteps.d: Activated JAK2 phosphorylates STAT5, which travels to the nucleus and switches on growth genes — most importantly the gene for IGF-1. Because this is transcription, the muscle-and-bone effects build over days and weeks, not minutes.

mechSteps.t: The liver ships out IGF-1 — the real growth messenger
mechSteps.predict: GH clears from your blood in a few hours, yet the effect lasts far longer. What outlasts it?
mechSteps.d: GH's biggest anabolic effects are indirect: it drives the liver to release IGF-1, a longer-lived hormone that circulates and tells muscle and bone to build protein and collagen. Meanwhile GH acts directly on fat cells to mobilise fat (lipolysis) and directly antagonises insulin.

mechSteps.t: The same signal, body-wide, is also the cost
mechSteps.predict: This is a growth-and-metabolism command sent to every tissue at once. Name one predictable downside of that.
mechSteps.d: Because GH raises blood sugar (anti-insulin), holds sodium and water, and pushes growth everywhere, supraphysiologic dosing produces insulin resistance, edema, carpal tunnel, and — over time — acromegalic changes, with a real concern that GH/IGF-1's mitogenic signal could accelerate existing tumours.

framework.target: The growth hormone receptor (GHR) — a single-pass cytokine-family receptor, not a nuclear receptor. Somatropin is a full agonist: it activates GHR, which signals through JAK2/STAT5. Note the two-layer design — GH acts directly on some tissues (fat, and insulin antagonism) but delivers most of its growth signal indirectly, via liver-made IGF-1.
framework.affinity: High. One GH engages two receptors through two non-equivalent faces: the first (site 1) binds with sub-nanomolar affinity and the second (site 2) with a Kd around 4 nM. So therapeutic doses fully occupy the target — this is genuinely high-affinity, which is exactly why the effect isn't a fluke of overdosing and why feedback suppression of your own axis is unavoidable while dosing.
framework.adme: Given by subcutaneous injection (~80% absorbed). Its metabolic half-life is short — roughly 20–30 minutes — but slow release from the injection depot stretches the observed blood half-life to about 2–4 hours. Crucially, the felt effect is set by IGF-1 and gene transcription over days, not by that blood curve.
framework.genetics: There's no caffeine-style master switch, but the GHR exon-3-deleted variant (d3-GHR) has been linked in some pediatric studies to a stronger growth response to GH therapy. The finding is inconsistent across populations, so treat individual response as only loosely predictable.
framework.evidence: Split by context. Strong and legitimate for diagnosed GH deficiency and specific approved conditions (replacement restores body composition, bone density and quality of life). For healthy adults chasing physique or anti-aging, randomised trials show small body-composition shifts but no reliable gain in strength or aerobic capacity, alongside frequent adverse events — and no long-term safety data for that use.

pk.onset: lipolysis within hours; anabolic (IGF-1-driven) effects over days to weeks
pk.peak: blood GH peaks ~2–6 h after a subcutaneous dose
pk.halfLife: ~2–4 h (subcutaneous, absorption-limited; true metabolic half-life ~20–30 min)
pk.duration: biological effect outlasts the hormone by days
pk.note: This is the compound's signature trap: the hormone is cleared within hours, but it works by ordering the liver to make IGF-1 and by switching genes on — so reading its short half-life like a stimulant's stopwatch tells you almost nothing about how long it acts, or how slowly the axis recovers.

journey.d: You inject it under the skin. About 80% is absorbed, and it seeps out of the depot slowly — which is why blood levels linger for hours even though the hormone itself is broken down fast. Black-market vials are a real hazard: counterfeit, underdosed, and non-sterile product is common.
It circulates and reaches receptor-bearing tissues everywhere — this is a whole-body hormone, not a targeted one. Peak blood levels land roughly 2–6 hours after the shot.
GH binds the GHR pair on these cells, firing JAK2/STAT5. Directly, it mobilises fat for fuel (lipolysis). It also blunts insulin's action here — the root of the blood-sugar problems.
The liver reads the GH signal and manufactures IGF-1, the longer-lived messenger that carries most of the growth order to muscle and bone. This relay is why the effect outlasts the hormone.

The hypothalamus and pituitary sense the high GH and IGF-1 and clamp down on your own GH release (via somatostatin and reduced GHRH drive) — so exogenous GH suppresses the natural pulses while you're on it.

GH is degraded in the liver and kidney and largely broken down rather than excreted intact. But the downstream IGF-1 signal — and the tissue changes it drove — fade far more slowly than the hormone that started them.

myths.myth: HGH is a proven fountain of youth.
myths.truth: The famous 1990 Rudman study showed older men gained lean mass and lost fat — and the media ran with it. But the fuller picture is sobering: a 2007 systematic review of GH in healthy elderly (Liu et al., Annals of Internal Medicine) found only small body-composition changes, more adverse events, and concluded it cannot be recommended as anti-aging therapy.

myths.myth: More lean mass on the scan means you got stronger.
myths.truth: Much of the early gain is fluid and connective tissue. In controlled trials of healthy adults, GH increased lean body mass but did not reliably improve muscle strength or aerobic capacity (Blackman 2002; Liu 2008) — the number on the scan moved, the performance didn't.

myths.myth: It's safe because it's just a hormone your body already makes.
myths.truth: Your body makes it in small, controlled pulses. Held above that range it antagonises insulin (glucose intolerance, even diabetes), retains fluid (edema, carpal tunnel), and drives tissue overgrowth (acromegalic jaw, hands, and organs) — plus a genuine concern that GH/IGF-1 could accelerate an existing tumour.

myths.myth: It's a legal grey-area supplement.
myths.truth: Somatropin is a prescription drug. In the US it's uniquely restricted — federal law makes it illegal to distribute or possess GH for any use not specifically approved by the FDA, which explicitly excludes anti-aging and athletic enhancement. It's also banned by WADA in and out of competition.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **The legitimate case:** diagnosed growth hormone deficiency (in children and adults) and a short list of specific approved conditions. Here replacement is genuinely restorative — better body composition, bone density and quality of life — and this is the only context with strong evidence behind it.

**The physique/anti-aging pitch:** fat loss, faster 'recovery,' better skin and sleep. The fat-mobilising and IGF-1 effects are real, but the performance and rejuvenation claims fail in controlled trials — GH did not reliably raise strength or aerobic capacity in healthy adults (Liu 2008; Blackman 2002).
**The metabolic cost:** it antagonises insulin, so it raises blood sugar and can push toward glucose intolerance or diabetes; it retains fluid, causing edema, joint aches and carpal tunnel syndrome.

**The growth cost:** sustained supraphysiologic GH drives acromegalic changes — enlarging jaw, hands, feet and internal organs — and carries a real concern about accelerating existing or occult tumours, since GH/IGF-1 is a growth signal.
**The legal and testing cost:** prescription-only and, in the US, illegal to distribute for non-approved uses; WADA-banned for athletes. This is not a grey area.
**The product-quality cost:** grey-market GH is frequently counterfeit, underdosed or non-sterile — you often don't know what's in the vial. This page explains the pharmacology; it is not a protocol.

start.q: In one line: does injected GH build muscle directly, or through a messenger?
start.a: Mostly through a messenger. GH's biggest anabolic effects run through IGF-1 released by the liver; GH also acts directly to mobilise fat and blunt insulin.

how.q: GH binds a receptor that's already a pair. So what does binding actually switch on?
how.a: It twists the pre-formed GHR dimer into an active shape, firing JAK2, which activates STAT5 — STAT5 then enters the nucleus and turns on growth genes, chiefly IGF-1.

use.q: Why does GH's short (~2–4 h) half-life tell you almost nothing about how long it acts?
use.a: Because the effect is carried by IGF-1 and by gene transcription, which rise and fade over days. The hormone clears in hours; its downstream order lasts far longer.

evidence.q: How strong is the evidence — and for whom?
evidence.a: Strong for diagnosed GH deficiency (restorative). For healthy adults seeking physique or anti-aging: small body-composition changes but no reliable strength or endurance gain, plus frequent adverse events (Liu 2007; Liu 2008; Blackman 2002).

canExplain: Why injected GH overrides your body's carefully pulsed, feedback-controlled release
How one GH molecule activates a receptor pair, and why it works through JAK2/STAT5 rather than as a nuclear receptor
Why most of GH's growth effect is indirect — carried by liver-made IGF-1 — and why that makes its short half-life misleading
Why 'lean mass' on a scan went up in trials while strength and endurance largely didn't
Why the same signal that heals a deficient body causes insulin resistance, edema and overgrowth in a healthy one

selfTest.q: A friend injecting GH says the scale is up 3 kg in two weeks, proof it's 'building muscle fast.' Give the honest correction.
selfTest.a: Two weeks is far too fast for real muscle protein accrual, which runs on IGF-1-driven transcription over weeks. Much of that early gain is fluid retention (GH holds sodium and water) plus connective tissue — and controlled trials show lean-mass gains in healthy adults don't reliably translate into strength.

selfTest.q: Somatropin's blood half-life is only a few hours, yet it's dosed for a lasting effect. Explain the disconnect.
selfTest.a: GH itself clears fast, but it acts largely by ordering the liver to secrete IGF-1 — a longer-lived messenger — and by switching on growth genes. Both the IGF-1 signal and the transcription it drives persist for days, so the biological effect far outlasts the hormone.

selfTest.q: Why is glucose intolerance an almost built-in consequence of supraphysiologic GH, not a rare side effect?
selfTest.a: GH directly antagonises insulin's action in fat and muscle (that's part of its normal metabolic role). Held above the physiological range continuously, that anti-insulin effect dominates — raising blood sugar and pushing toward glucose intolerance, seen even in monitored trials like Blackman 2002.

evi.howStrong: The evidence splits sharply by who's using it. For diagnosed growth hormone deficiency and a defined set of approved conditions, replacement GH is well-established therapy with clear benefits — this is real medicine. For healthy adults using it for physique, performance or anti-aging, the evidence is genuinely against the hype: multiple randomised trials and two systematic reviews (Liu 2007 on the healthy elderly; Liu 2008 on athletic performance) show small body-composition changes but no reliable functional gain, alongside frequent adverse events. There is essentially no long-term safety data for that off-label use.
evi.whatItShows: Consistently, GH shifts body composition in healthy adults — lean body mass up, fat mass down. What it does NOT convincingly show is that this translates into being stronger, fitter or younger: strength and aerobic-capacity endpoints repeatedly failed to improve.
evi.effect: In the landmark Rudman 1990 trial, older men gained ~4.7 kg lean mass and lost ~3.5 kg fat over six months — but with rising blood pressure and glucose. Later, better-controlled trials (Blackman 2002) reproduced the body-composition shift while finding no improvement in muscle strength or VO2max, and frequent glucose intolerance. Liu's 2008 review of athletes found GH did not improve strength or endurance and increased side effects like edema and fatigue.
evi.whoBenefits: People with a genuine deficiency — GH-deficient children and adults, and specific approved medical conditions — benefit clearly and legitimately. Healthy adults using it for muscle, sport or youth are off-label, unstudied for long-term safety, and the trial data says the trade is mostly downside.
evi.caveats: The functional-endpoint failures are the crux: more mass on a scan is not more capability.
