Add near-universal fluid retention, glucose intolerance, joint and nerve symptoms, acromegalic changes with chronic use, a real tumour-growth concern, prescription/legal restriction, and grey-market products that are frequently counterfeit or contaminated. Trial numbers reflect clean, dosed, monitored conditions — not a black-market vial.

trials.finding: 21 healthy men aged 61–81 given GH (~0.03 mg/kg 3×/week) for 6 months gained ~4.7 kg lean body mass and lost ~3.5 kg fat mass, with increased lumbar-spine density — but also rising systolic blood pressure and fasting glucose. The trial that launched the anti-aging hype.
trials.ref: Rudman et al., 1990, N Engl J Med 323:1–6

trials.finding: Randomised controlled trial in healthy aged men and women: GH increased lean mass and decreased fat mass but did NOT improve muscle strength or VO2max, and adverse events — especially glucose intolerance — were frequent.
trials.ref: Blackman et al., 2002, JAMA 288:2282–2292

trials.finding: Systematic review of GH in the healthy elderly: only small body-composition changes and increased rates of adverse events; concluded GH cannot be recommended as an anti-aging therapy.
trials.ref: Liu et al., 2007, Ann Intern Med 146:104–115

trials.finding: Systematic review of GH and athletic performance: GH did not improve strength or aerobic capacity in healthy young adults and increased adverse events (notably edema and fatigue).
trials.ref: Liu et al., 2008, Ann Intern Med 148:747–758

tech.chem: Somatropin is recombinant human growth hormone — a 191-amino-acid, ~22 kDa single-chain polypeptide with two intramolecular disulfide bonds, sequence-identical to pituitary-derived hGH and produced in E. coli or mammalian cell lines. It is a protein hormone of the somatotropin/prolactin cytokine family, not a steroid; it cannot cross membranes and acts entirely through a cell-surface receptor.

targets.action: full agonist — one GH crosslinks a pre-formed receptor dimer via two non-equivalent binding sites, driving the conformational change that activates receptor-associated JAK2
targets.affinity: high: site 1 sub-nanomolar, site 2 Kd ≈ 4 nM — fully engaged at therapeutic exposure
targets.role: the sole primary target; a single-pass class-I cytokine receptor. Signals through JAK2/STAT5 to drive lipolysis, protein synthesis, collagen turnover and hepatic IGF-1 output

tech.signaling: GHR is a class-I cytokine receptor with no intrinsic kinase activity; it pre-associates as a dimer with JAK2 bound to each intracellular tail. GH binding rotates/repositions the transmembrane helices so the two JAK2 molecules transactivate one another. Active JAK2 phosphorylates receptor tyrosines that recruit STAT5 (and STAT1/3); phosphorylated STAT5 dimerises, translocates to the nucleus, and transcribes target genes including IGF1. Parallel arms include the MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT pathways. Systemically, GH and IGF-1 feed back on the hypothalamus and pituitary (↑somatostatin, ↓GHRH drive), suppressing endogenous GH pulses during exogenous dosing.

adme.absorb: Subcutaneous injection with roughly 80% bioavailability; slow release from the injection depot governs the blood-level time course.
adme.distribute: Distributes to GHR-bearing tissues (liver, muscle, bone, adipose); as a large hydrophilic protein it stays extracellular and acts at the cell surface.
adme.metabolise: Cleared by receptor-mediated uptake and proteolytic degradation, chiefly in the liver and kidney; broken down to amino acids rather than to discrete drug metabolites. Metabolic half-life ~20–30 min; observed subcutaneous half-life ~2–4 h.
adme.excrete: Minimal intact renal excretion — elimination is via tissue catabolism, not urinary excretion of unchanged hormone.

tech.pgx: No validated single-gene switch governing response. The GHR exon-3 deletion polymorphism (d3-GHR) has been associated in some pediatric cohorts with a greater growth response to GH therapy, but results are inconsistent across studies and populations, so it is not a reliable predictor. IGF-1 pathway variation may modulate response but is not clinically actionable here.
tech.dose: Therapeutic dosing is weight- or condition-based and physician-titrated to IGF-1 levels — adult deficiency typically uses small daily microgram-per-kg subcutaneous doses. Historical study doses like Rudman's ~0.03 mg/kg three times weekly are now considered high. Grey-market physique use runs well above replacement, at exposures never validated for safety — a description, not a recommendation. No approved dose exists for anti-aging or athletic use.
tech.tolerance: No receptor-desensitisation tolerance in the caffeine sense. The dominant time-dependent phenomena are (1) suppression of endogenous GH pulses during dosing via negative feedback, and (2) cumulative metabolic and structural consequences of sustained supraphysiologic signalling — insulin resistance, fluid retention, and acromegalic tissue changes — that build with continued use rather than fading.

questions: Growth hormone is the famous muscle hormone — so why would anyone inject IGF-1 instead of GH?
What does the cryptic "LR3" in the name actually change — and why does that tiny edit make it far more potent than the IGF-1 your own body makes?
If it builds muscle by telling cells to grow, what stops it from telling a tumour the exact same thing?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) GH barely touches muscle directly — it mostly works by ordering your liver and muscle to produce IGF-1, and IGF-1 is the molecule that actually drives the growth; injecting IGF-1 LR3 skips the middleman entirely. (2) "LR3" = Long R3: a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension plus an arginine swapped in at position 3. That edit drops the molecule's grip on the IGF-binding proteins roughly 1,000-fold — and since those proteins normally keep about 99% of your natural IGF-1 leashed and inactive, a version that dodges them stays free and active far longer. (3) Nothing stops it — that is the core danger. IGF1R sits on almost every cell in your body, so the same "grow and survive" command that thickens muscle also feeds any tumour carrying the receptor, which is why circulating IGF-1 levels track with cancer risk in population studies.

igf-1-lr3.bigIdea: IGF-1 LR3 is the growth signal GH actually works through, re-engineered to slip past the binding proteins that normally keep it leashed — so it floods the IGF-1 receptor continuously, telling muscle to grow but telling every other tissue, tumours included, the exact same thing.
igf-1-lr3.analogy: Think of GH as a foreman shouting \"grow\" and IGF-1 as the actual work order it hands to the cell. Your natural IGF-1 comes chaperoned — binding proteins hold ~99% of it in reserve, releasing only a trickle. IGF-1 LR3 is that same work order with the chaperone cut loose and the paper laminated so it can't be shredded: it stays active far longer, and it reaches everywhere, not just the muscle you meant.

mechSteps.t: It's GH's real messenger, made injectable
mechSteps.predict: GH is the famous muscle hormone — yet GH barely acts on muscle directly. So what molecule actually does the growing?
mechSteps.d: Growth hormone works largely by instructing your liver and muscle to make insulin-like growth factor-1. IGF-1 is the downstream signal that truly drives tissue growth. IGF-1 LR3 is a lab-made IGF-1 analog injected directly, cutting GH out of the loop.

mechSteps.t: The "LR3" edits cut the leash
mechSteps.predict: Your natural IGF-1 is almost entirely bound to carrier proteins that keep it inactive. What happens if you engineer a version those proteins can't grab?
mechSteps.d: Native IGF-1 is 70 amino acids; LR3 is 83 — a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension plus an arginine substituted at position 3. Those changes lower its affinity for the IGF-binding proteins roughly 1,000-fold, so it escapes the reservoir that normally sequesters and clears IGF-1 — and stays free and active far longer.

mechSteps.t: At the receptor, it switches on the growth machinery
mechSteps.predict: IGF1R is a receptor tyrosine kinase — a gene-level switch. Name the two things it turns up inside a muscle cell.
mechSteps.d: Bound IGF-1 activates IGF1R, firing the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway (ramping up muscle protein synthesis) and the Ras/MAPK pathway (proliferation). It also activates satellite cells — the muscle's stem cells — the route by which IGF-1 can, in models, add new muscle nuclei and fibres (hyperplasia).

mechSteps.t: It also looks enough like insulin to crash your blood sugar
mechSteps.predict: IGF-1 and insulin are molecular cousins built on the same scaffold. What dangerous side effect does that shared shape create?
mechSteps.d: Because IGF-1 resembles insulin, it binds the insulin receptor with low affinity and drives glucose out of the blood into cells while suppressing the liver's glucose output. Push the dose and blood sugar can fall dangerously — hypoglycemia is the most immediate, real risk.

mechSteps.t: The "grow" signal doesn't discriminate
mechSteps.predict: If the message is "divide and survive" to any cell carrying the receptor, what is the long-term worry?
mechSteps.d: IGF1R sits on nearly every tissue. The same pro-growth, anti-apoptosis signal that thickens muscle also stimulates organs (heart, spleen, kidney can enlarge) and feeds any tumour expressing the receptor. Higher circulating IGF-1 is associated with elevated risk of several cancers in epidemiological studies.

framework.target: The IGF-1 receptor (IGF1R) — a receptor tyrosine kinase that acts as a growth switch. IGF-1 LR3 is an agonist: it turns IGF1R on, hard and continuously, rather than blocking anything. It also spills onto the closely related insulin receptor at low affinity.
framework.affinity: The LR3 edits were designed to leave IGF1R binding essentially intact — LR3 retains high (nanomolar-range) affinity for its receptor, comparable to native IGF-1. What the modifications drastically changed is affinity for the IGF-binding proteins (~1,000-fold lower). So unlike caffeine — where you must ask whether a realistic dose even reaches the target — here the target is fully and continuously engaged; that is precisely why the growth signal is so hard to contain.
framework.adme: Injected (a peptide, destroyed if swallowed), typically subcutaneously. Its defining feature is extended action: by evading the binding proteins that normally sequester and clear IGF-1, it circulates free for much longer. A half-life in the ~20–30 hour range is widely cited, but that figure comes from gray-market and preclinical sources, not rigorous human pharmacokinetic studies — treat it as approximate, not established.
framework.genetics: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic switch. Individual response is shaped by baseline IGF-1/IGFBP levels, IGF1R expression and insulin sensitivity — all variable — so the effect (and the hypoglycemia risk) is genuinely unpredictable person to person.
framework.evidence: Essentially absent for this use. IGF-1 LR3 (as LONG R3 IGF-I) is manufactured and sold as a cell-culture reagent, not a human drug. There are no human trials of injected IGF-1 LR3 for muscle building, and no long-term human safety data. The only approved IGF-1 product is native rhIGF-1 (mecasermin), and only for severe primary IGF-1 deficiency — a completely different context.

journey.d: It's injected, usually under the skin — swallowing would digest it. Real caution: gray-market peptides are frequently mislabeled, underdosed or contaminated, so what's in the vial often isn't what the label claims.

In circulation it does what native IGF-1 can't: it slips past the IGF-binding proteins that normally grab ~99% of IGF-1 and hold it in reserve. Staying free and undegraded is exactly why it lingers and acts for so long.

It docks onto IGF1R and fires the Akt/mTOR growth machinery, ramping up protein synthesis and waking satellite cells. Over days-to-weeks of signalling, this is the muscle-growth effect people are chasing.

Its insulin-like shape lets it hit the insulin receptor too, pulling glucose out of the blood and shutting down the liver's glucose output. This is where the acute danger lives — blood sugar can drop hard enough to be an emergency.

IGF1R is nearly everywhere, so the "grow and survive" signal isn't confined to muscle. Organs can enlarge, and any tumour bearing the receptor gets the same growth message — the mechanistic basis of the cancer concern.

It is not approved for human use, carries no established human safety profile for physique use, and growth factors of this class are prohibited in sport. The exit from the body is far better understood than the long-term consequences of putting it in.

myths.myth: It's just a stronger, cleaner version of the IGF-1 your body already makes.
myths.truth: The very edit that makes it "stronger" — evading the binding proteins — is what makes it dangerous. Those binding proteins are a built-in safety brake, buffering how much active IGF-1 reaches your tissues. Cutting the leash removes the regulation, it doesn't purify the molecule.

myths.myth: It builds brand-new muscle fibres (hyperplasia) in humans, unlike steroids.
myths.truth: Satellite-cell activation and hyperplasia are shown mainly in cell and animal models. Solid human evidence that injecting IGF-1 LR3 adds new muscle fibres is essentially absent — the mechanism is real, but the human translation is unproven.

myths.myth: You can inject it locally to grow just one muscle ("site enhancement").
myths.truth: It enters the systemic circulation and signals body-wide through a receptor found on nearly every tissue. Reliable muscle-selective, local-only growth in humans is not established — the same signal is reaching your organs and any cancer-prone tissue.

myths.myth: It's legal because it's sold as a research chemical.
myths.truth: "Research chemical" describes how it's marketed to dodge regulation, not its safety or legality for you. No regulator has approved it for human use, it isn't a supplement, and it's banned in tested sport.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a GH-level, or stronger, muscle-growth signal delivered directly — with the hope of true hyperplasia (new muscle cells) that steroids don't offer. The mechanism is genuinely potent; the human payoff for physique use is unproven.
**The blood-sugar cost:** its insulin-like action can cause hypoglycemia — sometimes severe. This is the most immediate, concrete danger, and it's why the watch-out list leads with it.

**The cancer and organ cost:** IGF1R is a pro-growth, anti-apoptosis switch on nearly every cell. Higher circulating IGF-1 is associated with increased risk of several cancers, and organ enlargement (including the heart) is a documented concern with sustained IGF-1 elevation.

**The evidence cost:** there are no human trials of injected IGF-1 LR3 for muscle building and no long-term human safety data. It's manufactured as a lab reagent, not a medicine. You would be the experiment.
**The product-quality cost:** gray-market peptides are routinely mislabeled, underdosed, overdosed or contaminated — so you often don't actually know the identity or amount of what you're injecting.
**The legal and testing cost:** not approved for human use, and prohibited in tested sport. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a protocol.

start.q: In one line: why inject IGF-1 LR3 instead of growth hormone?
start.a: Because GH barely acts on muscle directly — it works by making IGF-1, which is the molecule that actually drives growth. LR3 delivers that signal directly and, by evading the binding proteins, keeps it active far longer.

how.q: What did the "LR3" modifications change — and what did they deliberately leave alone?
how.a: They added a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension and an Arg-for-Glu swap at position 3, dropping affinity for the IGF-binding proteins ~1,000-fold. They left IGF1R binding essentially intact — so the receptor signal is unchanged but far less regulated.

use.q: Why is hypoglycemia the risk that shows up first?
use.a: IGF-1 is an insulin cousin and binds the insulin receptor at low affinity, driving glucose into cells and suppressing the liver's glucose output. Push the dose and blood sugar can crash — fast and dangerously.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence for building muscle with it?
evidence.a: There essentially isn't any. IGF-1 LR3 is sold as a cell-culture reagent; there are no human muscle-building trials and no long-term safety data. The only approved IGF-1 (native mecasermin) is for severe deficiency, not physique.

canExplain: Why GH's muscle effect actually runs through IGF-1 — and why LR3 skips GH entirely
What the "LR3" edits change (binding-protein evasion) and, crucially, what they leave intact (receptor binding)
Why cutting the binding-protein leash removes a safety brake rather than making the molecule cleaner
Why it crashes blood sugar — the insulin-receptor connection
Why the same signal that grows muscle also raises organ-growth and cancer concerns, and why the human evidence is essentially absent

selfTest.q: A friend says IGF-1 LR3 is safer than steroids because "it's just natural IGF-1 that lasts longer." Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) The reason it lasts longer is that it evades the IGF-binding proteins — and those proteins are a natural safety buffer, so "longer-lasting" means "less regulated," not "safer." (2) It still fires IGF1R on nearly every tissue, so it carries hypoglycemia, organ-growth and cancer-signal risks that have no human safety data behind them for this use.

selfTest.q: Why does IGF-1 LR3 cause hypoglycemia when it's marketed as a muscle drug, not a diabetes drug?
selfTest.a: IGF-1 shares a scaffold with insulin and binds the insulin receptor at low affinity. That's enough to pull glucose into cells and suppress hepatic glucose output, so blood sugar can fall dangerously — an unavoidable consequence of the molecule's insulin-like shape, not a dosing accident.

selfTest.q: The mechanism (satellite-cell activation, hyperplasia) sounds impressive. Why is that not enough to conclude it safely builds muscle in humans?
selfTest.a: Because most of that evidence is from cell and animal models. There are no human trials of injected IGF-1 LR3 for physique and no long-term safety data — a robust mechanism doesn't prove a real, safe human effect, and here the translation simply hasn't been tested.

evi.howStrong: For this use, the human evidence is essentially non-existent. IGF-1 LR3 is manufactured and sold as a laboratory cell-culture reagent (LONG R3 IGF-I), not as a medicine — its "purpose" in the catalog is to grow cells in a dish. There are no randomized human trials of injected IGF-1 LR3 for muscle building, and no long-term human safety data for that use. What IS well established is the underlying biology: IGF-1 signalling through IGF1R and the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway is a genuine, thoroughly characterized driver of muscle growth in cell and animal models. The gap is entirely in the translation — from a real mechanism to a proven, safe human physique effect.
evi.whatItShows: The mechanistic literature shows IGF-1 reliably activates muscle protein synthesis and satellite cells. It does NOT show that injecting IGF-1 LR3 safely and predictably builds muscle in healthy humans — that step has not been tested.
evi.effect: An honest number for human physique use cannot be given, because the studies don't exist. The only human IGF-1 dosing data come from native rhIGF-1 (mecasermin) used to treat severe IGF-1 deficiency, where the goal and population are entirely different — and where hypoglycemia and tissue overgrowth are recognized adverse effects even under medical supervision.
evi.whoBenefits: In legitimate medicine, only patients with severe primary IGF-1 deficiency benefit from IGF-1 (as mecasermin), for growth failure. Use by healthy people to build muscle is entirely off-label, gray-market and unstudied for safety.
evi.caveats: Treat every physique claim as extrapolation from mechanism, not from human outcome data. Layer on the concrete hazards — hypoglycemia, organ enlargement, an IGF-1/cancer-risk association in epidemiology — plus the fact that gray-market vials are frequently mislabeled or contaminated. A strong mechanism with no human safety trials is a reason for caution, not confidence.

tech.chem: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1) is an 83-amino-acid recombinant analog of human insulin-like growth factor-1 (native IGF-1 is 70 residues). It carries two engineered changes: a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension and an arginine substituted for glutamate at position 3. Together these lower affinity for the IGF-binding proteins (IGFBP-1 through -6) by roughly 1,000-fold while preserving the IGF1R-binding domain, yielding a molecule that circulates largely unbound and stays biologically active far longer than native IGF-1. It is a peptide (parenterally administered) and is commercially produced as a cell-culture supplement (LONG R3 IGF-I).

targets.action: agonist (full activation of the receptor tyrosine kinase)
targets.affinity: high (nanomolar-range), comparable to native IGF-1 — the LR3 edits were designed to preserve receptor binding
targets.role: the primary target; a receptor tyrosine kinase driving PI3K/Akt/mTOR (protein synthesis, survival) and Ras/MAPK (proliferation)

targets.action: low-affinity agonist
targets.affinity: low relative to IGF1R, but sufficient to produce insulin-like glucose lowering
targets.role: the basis of the hypoglycemia risk; IGF-1's structural kinship with insulin lets it cross-activate INSR (and hybrid IGF1R/INSR receptors)

tech.signaling: Ligand binding autophosphorylates the IGF1R tyrosine kinase, recruiting IRS-1/2 and Shc. Two arms follow: PI3K → Akt → mTOR/p70S6K drives muscle protein synthesis and suppresses atrophy genes (FoxO-controlled), while Akt also blocks apoptosis; and Ras → Raf → MEK → ERK/MAPK drives proliferation and differentiation. In skeletal muscle, IGF1R signalling additionally activates satellite (stem) cells, the route to added myonuclei and potential hyperplasia. The anti-apoptotic, pro-proliferative output of this same cascade in non-muscle tissue is what underlies the organ-growth and cancer-promotion concerns.

adme.absorb: Not orally bioavailable (a peptide); administered by injection, typically subcutaneous.
adme.distribute: Circulates largely free rather than IGFBP-bound, distributing to IGF1R-bearing tissues throughout the body — muscle, but also organs and the CNS periphery.
adme.metabolise: Cleared by proteolytic degradation and renal handling as for other peptide growth factors; the LR3 modifications slow degradation and reduce the IGFBP-mediated clearance that shortens native IGF-1's active life.
adme.excrete: Peptide fragments cleared renally. A frequently cited ~20–30 h half-life circulates in gray-market and preclinical sources but lacks confirmation from rigorous human pharmacokinetic studies.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. Response and risk are modulated by baseline IGF-1/IGFBP status, IGF1R and insulin-receptor expression, and insulin sensitivity — all variable — so individual response, and especially hypoglycemia susceptibility, should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: No approved human dose exists for physique use. Gray-market and anecdotal use commonly cites microgram-range daily subcutaneous doses, but these are unvalidated for safety or efficacy and are described here only for accuracy, not as guidance. The one legitimate human IGF-1 context is native rhIGF-1 (mecasermin) prescribed and monitored for severe primary IGF-1 deficiency — a different molecule, indication and setting.
tech.tolerance: Not a caffeine-style receptor tolerance. The dominant time-dependent consequences are cumulative: sustained IGF1R activation can drive organ enlargement and theoretically promote existing neoplasia, while the insulin-like activity poses a per-dose hypoglycemia hazard. Chronic ligand exposure can also downregulate receptor availability, but the governing concern is accumulating biological risk, not fading effect.

contrasts.title: IGF-1 LR3 vs native IGF-1 — the cut leash
contrasts.point: Same receptor, same growth signal — the difference is regulation. Your natural IGF-1 is held on a leash: binding proteins sequester ~99% of it and release only a controlled trickle, buffering how much reaches your tissues. IGF-1 LR3 is that molecule with the leash cut — the ~1,000-fold drop in binding-protein affinity is the whole point. So the "upgrade" over natural IGF-1 is precisely the removal of a built-in safety brake, which is why it acts longer, stronger and far less controllably.

contrasts.title: IGF-1 vs insulin — cousins that share a receptor
contrasts.point: IGF-1 and insulin are built on the same molecular scaffold and their receptors are close relatives that even form hybrids. That kinship is why IGF-1 LR3 spills onto the insulin receptor and can crash your blood sugar — the hypoglycemia isn't a contaminant or an accident, it's the predictable cost of a growth factor shaped like insulin.

questions: Why does a spoonful of the sweetest, simplest amino acid help you fall asleep faster — without knocking you out like a sedative?
How can the same molecule be a calming brain signal AND the main structural brick in your skin and tendons?
Why do longevity researchers get excited about something you can buy for a few cents a gram?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Glycine lowers your core body temperature — the physiological 'go to sleep' cue — by acting on NMDA receptors in your brain's clock (the SCN) that open up blood flow to your skin; it cues sleep onset rather than sedating you, so REM is preserved. (2) Glycine wears two hats: it's an inhibitory neurotransmitter at glycine receptors AND every third amino acid in the collagen triple-helix — one job is signalling, the other is scaffolding. (3) Glycine is the principal methyl-group acceptor that clears excess methionine, so loading it partly mimics methionine restriction — the reason an 8% glycine diet extended mouse lifespan a few percent.

glycine.bigIdea: Glycine isn't a sedative that shuts your brain off — it's a cooling and quieting signal: it lowers your core temperature to trigger sleep, calms nerve firing, and doubles as the structural brick that holds collagen together. One tiny molecule, several jobs.
glycine.analogy: Think of glycine as the building's thermostat AND its mortar. As a thermostat it nudges your core temperature down a fraction of a degree — the signal your body reads as 'time to sleep' — by opening the skin's radiators. As mortar it's the small brick slotted into every third gap of the collagen wall, the only amino acid tiny enough to fit where the triple helix twists tightest.

mechSteps.t: The smallest amino acid, with two day jobs
mechSteps.predict: Glycine is the tiniest amino acid — its side chain is just a single hydrogen. Why would being that small make it uniquely useful in a tightly-wound structure like collagen?
mechSteps.d: Glycine's side chain is a lone hydrogen atom, so it fits into spaces no other amino acid can. That same molecule also acts as a signalling chemical in your nervous system — it is both a message and a building material.

mechSteps.t: It quiets nerve firing (the inhibitory job)
mechSteps.predict: In your spinal cord and brainstem, glycine opens a channel that lets chloride ions flow into neurons. Does that make a neuron more likely or less likely to fire?
mechSteps.d: At glycine receptors (built from the GLRA1 subunit), glycine opens a chloride channel that hyperpolarises the neuron — pushing it further from firing. It's one of your main inhibitory 'calm down' neurotransmitters, alongside GABA.

mechSteps.t: It cools you down to trigger sleep (the NMDA job)
mechSteps.predict: Falling asleep is preceded by a small drop in core body temperature. How could a molecule lower your core temperature by acting on your brain's master clock?
mechSteps.d: Here glycine acts as a co-agonist at NMDA receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus — your circadian clock. Activating them widens blood vessels in your skin, dumping heat and dropping core temperature ~0.3 °C. That drop is a natural sleep-onset cue, which is why it shortens sleep latency without sedating you (shown mechanistically in animals).

mechSteps.t: It's the mortar in collagen
mechSteps.predict: Collagen is a triple helix of three chains wound tightly together. Roughly what fraction of every collagen chain is glycine — and why must it be exactly there?
mechSteps.d: Collagen's sequence is a relentless Gly-X-Y repeat, so glycine is every third residue — about one-third of the whole protein. Only glycine is small enough to sit at the crowded centre where the three strands twist together. Pair collagen or gelatin with vitamin C (a required cofactor) and you supply this brick plus the enzyme's helper.

mechSteps.t: It helps clear methionine (the longevity lever)
mechSteps.predict: Diets low in the amino acid methionine extend lifespan in rodents. How could adding glycine partly imitate eating less methionine?
mechSteps.d: Glycine is the principal acceptor for excess methyl groups, via the enzyme GNMT — the main route for disposing of surplus methionine. Loading glycine speeds that clearance, partly mimicking methionine restriction. This is why an 8% glycine diet modestly extended mouse lifespan — but note this lever is animal-evidence only.

framework.target: Two genuine receptors plus a structural role. As a neurotransmitter glycine is the endogenous agonist at inhibitory glycine receptors (GLRA1, a chloride channel) and a co-agonist at excitatory NMDA receptors (it must bind the GluN1 site for glutamate to open the channel). Separately it's a substrate — a raw material woven into collagen and used in glutathione and creatine synthesis.
framework.affinity: There's no drug-style Ki worth quoting, because glycine is the body's own ligand, not a foreign key — it works at the physiological concentrations your neurons already use, and a 3 g oral bolus nudges those levels. The honest framing: the sleep effect is receptor-mediated (NMDA in the SCN) but the detailed mechanism is demonstrated mainly in animals; in humans we have the downstream result (faster sleep onset, lower core temp), not a measured occupancy curve.
framework.adme: Well absorbed orally; freely water-soluble; distributes through body water and crosses into the brain. It's not cleared by a single bottleneck enzyme like caffeine — it's woven into proteins, feeds one-carbon metabolism, and its plasma half-life is dose-dependent (measured ~26 min at low doses up to ~245 min as the dose climbs), so there's no clean single number.
framework.genetics: No caffeine-style fast/slow switch. Response is shaped by your baseline: your body makes and consumes large amounts daily and dietary intake is often below what's used, so 'demand' varies. Rare mutations in GLRA1 cause hyperekplexia (an exaggerated startle disorder), underlining how central the inhibitory role is.
framework.evidence: Mixed by claim, which is why it sits at 3/5. Sleep: small but real human RCTs. Collagen/tendon: solid mechanism plus gelatin+vitamin-C trials (not always pure glycine). Longevity: animal-only. Treat each benefit on its own evidence.

pk.onset: ~30–60 min (taken before bed, it shortens time to fall asleep)
pk.note: Glycine breaks the usual stopwatch rules. Its plasma half-life is dose-dependent (measured anywhere from ~26 to ~245 min as the dose climbs), and the sleep benefit isn't driven by a single blood peak — it's a physiological trigger (a core-temperature drop) plus a slow structural role. So think of it as 'take it ~30–60 min before bed,' not as a level you time to the minute.

journey.d: You stir 3–5 g into water — it's naturally sweet, which is where its name comes from (Greek glykys, sweet). It dissolves easily and is absorbed across your gut.
It enters your bloodstream and distributes through body water. Small and simple, it crosses into the brain rather than being trapped at a single gateway.

In the spinal cord and brainstem it quiets neurons at glycine receptors; in the suprachiasmatic nucleus it acts on NMDA receptors, opening skin blood vessels so heat escapes and core temperature falls — the sleep cue.
Elsewhere it's pulled into building projects: every third brick of collagen for skin, tendon and bone, plus glutathione (your master antioxidant) and creatine.

Surplus glycine feeds one-carbon metabolism — notably accepting excess methyl groups via GNMT to help clear methionine — before nitrogen leaves as urea. It's recycled into your metabolism, not simply flushed intact.

myths.myth: Glycine is a sedative — it knocks you out like a sleeping pill.
myths.truth: It doesn't. It shortens how long you take to fall asleep by dropping core temperature (a natural cue) and it preserves normal sleep architecture, including REM — the opposite of benzodiazepines, which fragment sleep. You wake less groggy, not more.

myths.myth: Collagen supplements work because your body reassembles the collagen you eat.
myths.truth: Not directly — any collagen or gelatin you eat is digested into amino acids first. The plausible benefit is supplying the raw materials (glycine, proline) plus vitamin C at a time your body is building tissue. Glycine is the brick, not a pre-built wall.

myths.myth: Glycine will extend your lifespan.
myths.truth: That's animal-only so far. An 8% glycine diet extended mouse median lifespan by a modest ~4–6% (more in males than females), plausibly by mimicking methionine restriction. There is no human lifespan trial. Interesting mechanism, unproven in people.

whenToUse.intro: Put it together — here's how to actually use it, by goal. It's cheap, very safe, and mild, so experimenting has little downside:
items: **For sleep: 3 g in water, ~30–60 min before bed.** This matches the dose used in the human trials. Expect a gentle nudge to falling asleep faster, not a knockout — and give it several nights.

**For tendons/connective tissue: 5–10 g, ideally with collagen or gelatin plus ~50 mg vitamin C**, taken ~1 h before loading the tissue (a walk, jumps, rehab). Vitamin C is a required cofactor for building collagen.
**As an everyday amino-acid top-up:** typical diets often run below the amount your body uses for collagen and glutathione, so a few grams daily is a reasonable, low-risk supplement.
**Expect subtlety, not a jolt.** Unlike caffeine you won't 'feel' it working; the sleep effect is small and the structural effects are slow and cumulative.

**Cautions:** it's very well tolerated, but higher doses (10 g+) can cause mild GI upset or loose stools in some people, so start low. If you have serious kidney or liver disease, check with a doctor, since these organs handle amino-acid and nitrogen load.

start.q: In one line: does glycine work by sedating your brain?
start.a: No — it lowers your core body temperature (a natural sleep cue) and gently quiets nerve firing, so you fall asleep faster while keeping normal, un-fragmented sleep.

how.q: Name glycine's two receptor roles and what each does.
how.a: At glycine receptors (GLRA1) it's inhibitory — opens a chloride channel to calm neurons. At NMDA receptors in the SCN it's a co-agonist — triggering skin vasodilation that drops core temperature to cue sleep.

use.q: Why pair glycine or gelatin with vitamin C for tendons?
use.a: Glycine supplies the raw brick for collagen, and vitamin C is a required cofactor for the enzymes that build the collagen helix. You're providing the material and the tool together.

evidence.q: Rank glycine's three main claims by how strong the human evidence is.
evidence.a: Sleep: real but small human RCTs. Collagen/tendon: good mechanism plus gelatin+vitamin-C trials. Longevity: animal-only, no human trials. That spread is why it's 3/5.

canExplain: Why glycine helps you fall asleep faster without acting like a sedative — the core-temperature drop
Its two distinct receptor roles: inhibitory at glycine receptors, co-agonist at NMDA receptors
Why glycine is exactly one-third of collagen and why only it fits there
How glycine partly mimics methionine restriction, and why that longevity claim is animal-only
How to dose it differently for sleep versus connective tissue, and why to add vitamin C

selfTest.q: Your friend says glycine 'didn't knock me out like melatonin, so it must not work.' What's the flaw in that reasoning?
selfTest.a: Glycine isn't meant to sedate. It works by lowering core temperature to shorten sleep latency and preserves normal sleep architecture — so a lack of grogginess is expected, not evidence it failed. It's a subtle nudge, not a knockout.

selfTest.q: Collagen is a Gly-X-Y repeat. Explain both why glycine must be every third residue and why simply eating collagen isn't the same as injecting a finished protein.
selfTest.a: Only glycine's single-hydrogen side chain is small enough to fit the crowded core where the three helical strands twist together, so it has to sit at every third position. Eaten collagen is fully digested to amino acids first — you're supplying bricks (glycine, proline) plus the vitamin-C cofactor at build time, not delivering an intact wall.

selfTest.q: Explain the mechanistic link between glycine and the lifespan-extension seen with methionine restriction.
selfTest.a: Glycine is the principal acceptor of excess methyl groups via GNMT, the main pathway for clearing surplus methionine. Loading glycine speeds methionine disposal, partly imitating a low-methionine diet — which is why an 8% glycine diet extended mouse lifespan. It's an animal finding, not proven in humans.

evi.howStrong: Glycine's evidence is best read claim-by-claim, not as one number — which is exactly why it sits at 3/5. The sleep data are genuine randomised human trials but small (a few dozen participants total) and mostly from one research group. The connective-tissue case rests on a strong biochemical rationale plus a handful of gelatin-and-vitamin-C exercise trials (often testing gelatin, not isolated glycine). The longevity case is mechanistically elegant but entirely animal-based. So: promising and cheap, with real human sleep signal, but nothing here is settled the way creatine's strength benefit is.
evi.whatItShows: For sleep, the consistent finding is faster sleep onset and better subjective sleep quality with 3 g before bed, without disturbing sleep architecture — plus less next-day sleepiness. For tissue, the signal is a short-term rise in blood markers of collagen synthesis when gelatin+vitamin C is taken before loading. For ageing, it's a modest lifespan extension in mice.
evi.effect: Small and specific. Sleep-onset latency shortens and subjective quality improves; the core-temperature drop underpinning it is on the order of ~0.3 °C. Collagen-synthesis markers roughly doubled acutely in the gelatin trial. Mouse median lifespan rose ~4–6% on an 8% glycine diet (more in males than females), with a smaller rise in maximum (90th-percentile) lifespan.
evi.whoBenefits: People with mildly unsatisfactory sleep are the clearest beneficiaries of the sleep effect. Those doing tendon/ligament rehab or heavy connective-tissue loading are the rationale for the collagen use. Diets low in glycine-rich tissue (little skin, bone broth, gelatin) may have the most room to gain.
evi.caveats: The human trials are small and short; the collagen studies often use gelatin rather than pure glycine, so don't over-transfer; and the lifespan data are animal-only with no human evidence. It's very safe and cheap, so the risk of trying is low — but keep expectations proportionate to a small, subtle effect.

trials.finding: 3 g glycine before bed shortened polysomnographic latency to sleep onset and to slow-wave sleep, improved subjective sleep quality, and reduced daytime sleepiness, without altering sleep architecture.
trials.ref: Yamadera et al., 2007, Sleep and Biological Rhythms

trials.finding: Glycine's sleep-promoting and hypothermic effects were mediated by NMDA receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus, via peripheral vasodilation (animal study).
trials.ref: Kawai et al., 2015, Neuropsychopharmacology

trials.finding: An 8% glycine diet produced a small (~4–6%) but statistically significant increase in median lifespan (larger in males), with a more modest rise in maximum lifespan, in genetically heterogeneous mice across three sites (Interventions Testing Program).
trials.ref: Miller et al., 2019, Aging Cell

trials.finding: Vitamin C-enriched gelatin (15 g) taken ~1 h before intermittent exercise roughly doubled a blood marker of collagen synthesis (gelatin, not isolated glycine).
trials.ref: Shaw et al., 2017, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

tech.chem: Aminoacetic acid (H2N-CH2-COOH) — the smallest and simplest of the 20 proteinogenic amino acids, and the only one that is achiral (its side chain is a single hydrogen). Its minimal side chain is what lets it occupy the sterically crowded interior of the collagen triple helix at every third position. It is naturally sweet-tasting. The body synthesises glycine (chiefly from serine via serine hydroxymethyltransferase, and from threonine and choline), but demand for collagen, glutathione and creatine synthesis is large enough that dietary intake is often considered conditionally limiting.

targets.action: endogenous agonist → opens a Cl⁻-selective channel → hyperpolarisation
targets.affinity: the native ligand of an ionotropic receptor, not a drug binding a foreign target; strychnine is the classic competitive antagonist here
targets.role: fast inhibitory neurotransmission in spinal cord and brainstem; loss-of-function mutations cause hyperekplexia

targets.action: obligatory co-agonist → glutamate cannot open the channel unless the GluN1 glycine site is occupied
targets.affinity: a required co-agonist site, distinct from the glutamate site; also served by D-serine
targets.role: in the suprachiasmatic nucleus this pathway drives the vasodilation and core-temperature drop behind glycine's sleep effect (shown in animals)

targets.action: structural substrate — incorporated as every third residue
targets.role: ~1/3 of collagen by residue count; also a substrate for glutathione and creatine synthesis

tech.signaling: Two opposite ionotropic roles. At GLRA1 (a pentameric Cys-loop chloride channel), glycine binding opens Cl⁻ flux, hyperpolarising neurons and dampening firing — classic inhibition in spinal cord and brainstem. At the NMDA receptor, glycine is not inhibitory: it's the obligatory co-agonist at the GluN1 subunit, so glutamate-driven excitation requires it. The sleep/thermoregulatory action is the NMDA arm: glycine acting on NMDA receptors in the SCN shell elevates cutaneous blood flow, dissipating heat and lowering core temperature to promote sleep onset (Kawai et al., 2015). Beyond signalling, glycine feeds one-carbon metabolism and is a building block for collagen, glutathione and creatine.

adme.absorb: Well absorbed orally from the small intestine (via amino-acid transporters); highly water-soluble.
adme.distribute: Distributes through total body water and crosses the blood–brain barrier; taken up into the CNS and peripheral tissues for both signalling and biosynthesis.
adme.metabolise: Not cleared by a single enzyme. Interconverts with serine (SHMT), is cleaved by the glycine cleavage system, and accepts excess methyl groups via GNMT (glycine N-methyltransferase) — the principal route for disposing of surplus methionine, the basis of the methionine-restriction-mimic idea.
adme.excrete: Nitrogen is ultimately excreted as urea; only a minor fraction of intact glycine appears in urine under normal loads. The apparent plasma half-life is dose-dependent (reported ~26–245 min as the infused dose rises; Hahn, 1993).

tech.pgx: No dominant single-gene pharmacogenetic switch analogous to caffeine's CYP1A2. Rare loss-of-function GLRA1 (and GLRB) mutations cause hyperekplexia (exaggerated startle), and defects in the glycine cleavage system cause glycine encephalopathy — illustrating how tightly glycine levels are regulated, but these are disease alleles, not common response modifiers for supplementation.
tech.dose: Sleep: 3 g in water ~30–60 min before bed (the trial dose). Connective tissue: 5–10 g, ideally with collagen/gelatin plus ~50 mg vitamin C, ~1 h before loading. General supplementation of a few grams daily is common. Doses up to ~15–20 g have been used in studies; the practical ceiling is GI tolerance.
tech.tolerance: No receptor down-regulation or dependence in the caffeine sense — it's an endogenous amino acid, so there's nothing to cycle. The main limit is gastrointestinal (loose stools, mild nausea) at higher single doses; start low and split large amounts. Caution with significant kidney or liver disease given the amino-acid/nitrogen handling load.

contrasts.title: Glycine vs GABA — two brakes, different rooms
contrasts.point: Both are your brain's inhibitory 'calm down' neurotransmitters, and both work by opening a chloride channel to quiet neurons. The difference is location and division of labour: GABA is the dominant inhibitory brake across the higher brain (cortex, forebrain), while glycine handles fast inhibition mainly in the spinal cord and brainstem. Same trick — chloride in, neuron hushed — different circuits. And glycine has a second life GABA doesn't: as a co-agonist at excitatory NMDA receptors and as a structural building block.

glycine.stakes: Glycine is quietly one of the most-used molecules in your body: it's the single most abundant amino acid in collagen — the most abundant protein in you — and your daily demand for it (collagen, glutathione, creatine, methyl clearance) is large enough that many diets fall short. Yet it costs cents per gram.

questions: Why does semaglutide's older cousin need a shot every single day, when the newer one lasts a whole week?
If it's the same GLP-1 mechanism, why does it deliver only about half the weight loss of weekly semaglutide?
Why is a drug that clearly works — and even cuts heart attacks — now called 'superseded'?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Liraglutide grips blood albumin with just a 16-carbon fatty acid, giving it a ~13-hour half-life — enough for once-daily dosing, but nowhere near semaglutide's engineered week. (2) In a head-to-head trial (STEP 8), daily liraglutide 3.0 mg produced about -6.4% bodyweight versus semaglutide's -15.8% — same receptor, weaker real-world result. (3) It's 'superseded' not because it fails — it lowers weight and cut major cardiovascular events in LEADER — but because a newer molecule on the same target does more with one shot a week instead of seven.

liraglutide-saxenda-victoza.bigIdea: Liraglutide is the first-generation, once-a-day GLP-1 drug: a copy of a gut fullness hormone that tells your brain 'I'm full' so you eat less — the same mechanism as semaglutide, but engineered to last a day instead of a week, and delivering proportionally less.
liraglutide-saxenda-victoza.analogy: Liraglutide is a forgery of your gut's 'I'm satisfied' hormone, tethered to a blood-protein life-raft by a short fatty-acid rope. The rope is real but short — it holds the signal on for about a day, not the week semaglutide's longer, cleverer rope manages. Same trick, earlier draft.

mechSteps.t: It impersonates GLP-1, a gut hormone
mechSteps.predict: Your gut releases a hormone after eating that says 'food's here, wind down.' Liraglutide copies it — but natural GLP-1 is destroyed in a couple of minutes. Why copy something so fragile?
mechSteps.d: Liraglutide is a near-identical analogue of human GLP-1 (about 97% sequence homology), the incretin hormone your gut releases after a meal. Copying it lets liraglutide switch on the very same receptor — the fragility is a problem the molecule's engineering then solves.

mechSteps.t: It switches the GLP-1 receptor ON
mechSteps.predict: Caffeine works by blocking a receptor. Liraglutide does the opposite. If it activates the GLP-1 receptor, what happens in the pancreas when blood sugar is high?
mechSteps.d: Liraglutide is an agonist — it docks into the GLP-1 receptor and turns it on. In the pancreas it boosts insulin and suppresses glucagon, but only when glucose is elevated, which is why it rarely causes low blood sugar by itself.

mechSteps.t: It quiets the brain's hunger and 'food noise'
mechSteps.predict: GLP-1 receptors sit in the hypothalamus and reward circuits, not just the gut. What would switching those on do to appetite?
mechSteps.d: Activating GLP-1 receptors in the brain's appetite centres and reward pathways dampens hunger and intrusive food-thoughts, and slows gastric emptying so meals feel filling for longer. You eat less without white-knuckle willpower — and the slowed stomach is also why nausea is the classic early side effect.

mechSteps.t: A short fatty acid makes it last a day, not a week
mechSteps.predict: Native GLP-1 is gone in minutes; liraglutide lasts about 13 hours. A fatty-acid chain was bolted on for a reason. What does clinging to a blood protein buy you here?
mechSteps.d: A 16-carbon fatty acid lets liraglutide grip blood albumin (>98% bound), hiding it from clearance and slowing its release from the injection site to a ~13-hour half-life. That's enough for once-daily dosing — but it's a shorter tether than semaglutide's longer fatty-diacid, which is exactly why liraglutide is daily and semaglutide is weekly.

framework.target: The GLP-1 receptor (GLP1R) — a Gs-coupled GPCR in the pancreas, hypothalamic appetite centres, and vagal afferents. Like semaglutide and unlike caffeine, liraglutide activates its target (an agonist), it doesn't block it.
framework.affinity: Genuinely high-affinity, comparable to native GLP-1 — it engages the receptor at the low concentrations achieved in the body, so this is a real, engaged target, not a paper one. (Published sub-nanomolar Kd values are cleaner for semaglutide; for liraglutide the honest statement is 'high-affinity, GLP-1-like.') The clever second target is albumin: the fatty-acid tail binds it, and that — not raw potency — is what stretches a minutes-long hormone into a once-daily drug.
framework.adme: Injected subcutaneously (no oral form). >98% albumin-bound, small volume of distribution. Cleared not by liver CYP enzymes but by proteolytic breakdown of the peptide, the same way your body handles any large protein. Half-life ~13 hours — hence once daily.
framework.genetics: No caffeine-style single-gene switch (no CYP1A2 equivalent) governs the response, and there is no clinically actionable pharmacogenetic test to predict who responds. Response varies widely person to person, but honestly: it's not a one-gene story.
framework.evidence: Strong drug-grade evidence: large phase-3 RCTs (the SCALE weight programme, the LEAD diabetes programme) plus a dedicated cardiovascular-outcomes trial (LEADER) in over 9,000 people. Curated 4/5 — a notch below semaglutide mainly because the newer drug simply does more.

pk.onset: appetite effects within days; full effect builds over weeks
pk.peak: blood level peaks ~8–12 h after a dose (tmax)
pk.duration: ~24 h — the basis for once-daily dosing
pk.note: That ~13-hour half-life is the whole story of why liraglutide is a daily injection while semaglutide is weekly: the shorter albumin tether means the fullness signal fades within a day, so it must be topped up every day. Miss a dose and the effect drops off far faster than with the long-acting weekly agents.

journey.d: A once-daily shot goes into the fat under your skin, forming a small depot that trickles into the blood over hours — refreshed every day.
It grips blood albumin — over 98% rides along bound to it. That shield hides it from clearance and stretches its stay to about a day (but not the week a longer tether would buy).
It reaches the hypothalamic appetite centres and reward circuits and switches on GLP-1 receptors there. Hunger and intrusive food-thoughts fade — the core of the effect.
It slows the stomach's emptying so meals feel filling for longer, and prompts glucose-dependent insulin while damping glucagon in the pancreas.
There's no single liver enzyme to blame. The peptide is slowly snipped apart by proteases the way any protein is digested, with fragments cleared over the following day.
With a ~13-hour half-life, most of a dose is gone within a day — which is exactly why the next injection can't be skipped, and why appetite returns quickly once dosing stops.

myths.myth: Liraglutide burns fat directly.
myths.truth: It doesn't touch a fat-burning switch. It lowers how much you eat by acting on appetite and fullness signals — the fat loss is the downstream result of a sustained calorie deficit, not a metabolic bonfire.

myths.myth: It's basically the same as semaglutide, just cheaper.
myths.truth: Same receptor and mechanism, but not the same result. In a head-to-head trial (STEP 8), daily liraglutide 3.0 mg produced roughly -6.4% bodyweight versus semaglutide's -15.8% — less than half. The engineering difference (a shorter albumin tether, daily vs weekly) translates into a real difference in outcome.

myths.myth: You take it, lose the weight, then stop.
myths.truth: Like the whole GLP-1 class, it manages the appetite signal rather than resetting it. Stop the daily injections and hunger — and usually weight — return. For most people it's a chronic medication, like a blood-pressure drug, not a course you finish.

myths.myth: Victoza and Saxenda are different drugs.
myths.truth: Same molecule, different brand and dose: Victoza (up to 1.8 mg/day) is approved for type 2 diabetes, Saxenda (up to 3.0 mg/day) for chronic weight management. The higher-dose weight indication is separately trialled, not off-label improvisation.

whenToUse.intro: How it's used clinically — this is prescription-only, and needs a doctor to prescribe, titrate and monitor it. This is not a self-administration protocol:
items: **Type 2 diabetes (Victoza, up to 1.8 mg/day):** to improve blood-sugar control, with weight loss as a bonus and, in high-risk patients, a proven reduction in cardiovascular events (LEADER).

**Chronic weight management (Saxenda, up to 3.0 mg/day):** prescribed for obesity (BMI ≥30), or BMI ≥27 with a weight-related condition, alongside diet and activity — not for cosmetic or mild weight loss.

**Where the weekly agents aren't suitable:** its role has narrowed since weekly semaglutide and tirzepatide arrived, but it remains an option (including a paediatric obesity indication) where a daily GLP-1 is preferred or available.

**Contraindications a doctor screens for:** a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2 (a class black-box warning), prior pancreatitis, and pregnancy — it should be stopped before a planned pregnancy.

**Monitoring while on it:** the dose is titrated up slowly (0.6 mg weekly steps) to limit nausea, and muscle-preserving resistance training plus adequate protein are considered essential, since a share of the weight lost on any GLP-1 is lean mass.

start.q: In one line: does liraglutide burn fat, or make you eat less?
start.a: It makes you eat less. It copies a fullness hormone so hunger and food-thoughts fade; the fat loss follows from eating less, not from a fat-burning effect.

how.q: Liraglutide activates the same receptor GLP-1 does — name two things switching it on accomplishes.
how.a: It quiets hunger in the brain's appetite centres and slows the stomach's emptying (and, in the pancreas, prompts glucose-dependent insulin while suppressing glucagon).

use.q: Why is liraglutide injected every day when semaglutide is only weekly?
use.a: Its shorter 16-carbon fatty-acid tether binds albumin less durably, giving a ~13-hour half-life — enough for a day, not the week semaglutide's longer tail achieves.

evidence.q: How strong is the evidence, and how does it compare to semaglutide?
evidence.a: Strong — large RCTs (SCALE for weight, LEAD for diabetes) plus the LEADER cardiovascular-outcomes trial. But head-to-head (STEP 8), it produced less than half semaglutide's weight loss, which is why it's largely been superseded.

canExplain: Why liraglutide makes you eat less without burning fat directly
The exact receptor it activates (GLP1R) — and why it's an agonist, the opposite of a blocker like caffeine
Why it's a daily shot while semaglutide is weekly, down to the length of its fatty-acid tether
Why 'same mechanism' doesn't mean 'same result' — the STEP 8 head-to-head gap
Who it's actually prescribed for, that it also cut cardiovascular events, and why it's now second-line

selfTest.q: Liraglutide and semaglutide hit the identical receptor. Why does one need daily dosing and the other weekly?
selfTest.a: Both use a fatty acid to bind albumin and slow clearance, but liraglutide's is a shorter 16-carbon chain giving a ~13-hour half-life (daily), while semaglutide's longer fatty-diacid plus a DPP-4-resistant backbone stretches it to ~1 week.

selfTest.q: A patient asks why they can't just use liraglutide instead of the pricier weekly semaglutide 'since it's the same drug class.' What does the head-to-head evidence say?
selfTest.a: In STEP 8, daily liraglutide 3.0 mg produced about -6.4% bodyweight versus semaglutide's -15.8% at 68 weeks — less than half the effect. Same class, but a clinically meaningful difference in outcome, which is why liraglutide is now largely second-line.

selfTest.q: Liraglutide boosts insulin, yet rarely causes dangerous low blood sugar on its own. Why?
selfTest.a: Its insulin-stimulating effect is glucose-dependent — it only drives insulin when blood glucose is elevated, so it eases off as glucose normalises.

evi.howStrong: Liraglutide sits high on the drug evidence pyramid: multiple large, randomised, placebo-controlled phase-3 trials (the SCALE programme for weight, the LEAD programme for diabetes) plus a dedicated cardiovascular-outcomes trial, LEADER, in over 9,000 people. This is regulatory-grade evidence in tens of thousands of participants across the programmes — not a handful of small studies. The honest context: it has been directly outperformed by weekly semaglutide in a head-to-head trial, which is the main reason it's now considered a second-line GLP-1.
evi.whatItShows: Consistently, liraglutide produces clinically meaningful weight loss and better glucose control, and — in high-risk diabetes — fewer cardiovascular events. The effect is real and well-proven, but distinctly smaller than the newer weekly agents. It's an adjunct to diet and activity, not a replacement for them.
evi.effect: In plain numbers: SCALE showed ~8.0% average bodyweight loss over 56 weeks versus ~2.6% on placebo. LEADER showed a reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events (hazard ratio 0.87) in high-risk type 2 diabetes. But head-to-head in STEP 8, liraglutide 3.0 mg managed only ~6.4% versus semaglutide's ~15.8%. Common early trade-off: gastrointestinal side effects (nausea, occasionally vomiting), which is why the dose is titrated up slowly.
evi.whoBenefits: Adults with type 2 diabetes, and adults with obesity (or overweight plus a weight-related condition) — with clear added value in high-risk diabetics who also gain cardiovascular protection. It's not for cosmetic weight loss in healthy-weight people, and is contraindicated in those with a history of medullary thyroid cancer or MEN2.
evi.caveats: Effects depend on staying on the daily injection — weight largely returns after stopping. A share of the weight lost is lean mass without resistance training and protein, and rare risks (pancreatitis, gallbladder disease, and the thyroid C-cell black-box warning based on rodent data) mean it belongs under medical supervision. Its biggest practical limitation is simply that a better same-class drug now exists.

trials.finding: SCALE Obesity and Prediabetes: liraglutide 3.0 mg produced -8.0% bodyweight at 56 weeks vs -2.6% with placebo (n=3,731).
trials.ref: Pi-Sunyer et al., 2015, N Engl J Med

trials.finding: LEADER: in type 2 diabetes at high cardiovascular risk, liraglutide cut major adverse cardiovascular events vs placebo (HR 0.87; 13.0% vs 14.9%; n=9,340).
trials.ref: Marso et al., 2016, N Engl J Med

trials.finding: STEP 8 head-to-head: weekly semaglutide 2.4 mg produced -15.8% bodyweight vs -6.4% for daily liraglutide 3.0 mg at 68 weeks.
trials.ref: Rubino et al., 2022, JAMA

tech.chem: An acylated analogue of human GLP-1(7-37), about 97% homologous to the native hormone. It carries a single Lys34→Arg substitution and a 16-carbon palmitoyl fatty-acid chain attached via a γ-glutamic-acid linker to Lys26. That fatty acid is the albumin anchor: it binds serum albumin non-covalently, shields the peptide from DPP-4 and renal clearance, and slows absorption from the injection depot — turning a minutes-long hormone into a once-daily drug. Note the contrast with semaglutide, which uses a longer C18 fatty-diacid plus an Aib8 backbone swap for a full week of action.

targets.action: agonist — activates the receptor (a class-B Gs-coupled GPCR)
targets.affinity: high-affinity, comparable to native GLP-1; selective for GLP1R with negligible GIP or glucagon receptor activity
targets.role: the entire mechanism: pancreatic insulin/glucagon regulation, hypothalamic appetite suppression, slowed gastric emptying

targets.action: reversible non-covalent binding via the C16 palmitoyl side chain
targets.affinity: >98% bound in plasma
targets.role: not a therapeutic target but the pharmacokinetic engine — the depot and DPP-4 shield that give the ~13-hour half-life

tech.signaling: GLP1R is Gs-coupled: activation raises cAMP and activates PKA. In pancreatic beta cells this amplifies glucose-dependent insulin secretion and suppresses alpha-cell glucagon. In the hypothalamus, GLP1R signalling activates anorexigenic POMC/CART neurons and inhibits orexigenic AgRP/NPY neurons, reducing appetite; vagal afferent and hindbrain signalling reinforces satiety and slows gastric emptying.

adme.absorb: Subcutaneous injection only (no oral formulation). Absorption from the depot is slow, giving a tmax of roughly 8–12 hours.
adme.distribute: Small volume of distribution; >98% bound to plasma albumin via the fatty-acid side chain, which underlies its extended circulation.
adme.metabolise: Not a CYP substrate. Metabolised like a large peptide — endogenous proteolytic cleavage (including by DPP-4 and neutral endopeptidase, though albumin binding slows this) — with no single organ or enzyme dominating clearance.
adme.excrete: Metabolite fragments are eliminated in urine and faeces; no intact liraglutide is recovered unchanged. Elimination half-life ~13 hours.

tech.pgx: No established, clinically actionable pharmacogenetic determinant of response — there is no CYP1A2-style single-gene switch. GLP1R polymorphisms have been studied in relation to incretin response but are not used to guide prescribing. Because clearance is non-CYP and non-renal, major dose adjustment is not required for renal or hepatic impairment.
tech.dose: Prescription-only, titrated under medical supervision to limit nausea: subcutaneous 0.6 mg once daily, increased in 0.6 mg weekly steps to 1.8 mg (Victoza, diabetes) or 3.0 mg (Saxenda, weight management). Not to be self-dosed outside clinical care.
tech.tolerance: No classic receptor down-regulation that erodes efficacy. Gastrointestinal side effects (nausea, vomiting) typically wane as the dose is titrated and the body adapts. Weight loss plateaus as intake and expenditure re-balance; stopping leads to appetite and weight rebound rather than a withdrawal syndrome.

questions: Why does one of these peptides (GHRP-6) make you ravenously hungry at the same time it spikes your growth hormone?
If these were the first and most powerful GH-releasing peptides ever made, why did the field quietly abandon them for ipamorelin?
Why does hexarelin — the most potent one of the three — stop working the fastest?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) These peptides are synthetic ghrelin. Ghrelin is your stomach's 'I'm empty' hormone, and its receptor drives both appetite and a GH pulse — so forging that signal bundles hunger in with the growth hormone, most strongly with GHRP-6. (2) They came with baggage the newer peptides don't: GHRP-2 and hexarelin also push up cortisol and prolactin (stress and milk hormones), which is exactly the 'dirtiness' ipamorelin was designed to strip out. (3) Hexarelin is the strongest at the receptor but desensitises it fastest — the ghrelin receptor stops answering the door, and chronic-dosing studies show the GH response roughly halving over weeks.

hexarelin-ghrp-2-ghrp-6.bigIdea: These three peptides are counterfeit ghrelin — they forge your hunger hormone's signal at the pituitary to force a growth-hormone pulse, which is why the GH you want arrives bundled with the appetite, cortisol and prolactin you didn't ask for, and why the receptor eventually stops listening.
hexarelin-ghrp-2-ghrp-6.analogy: Ghrelin is your stomach's 'I'm empty — wake up, eat, and grow' signal, and its receptor sits on the cells in your pituitary that release growth hormone. These peptides are a forged copy of that signal. Slip the forgery in and you do get the GH pulse — but a forged signal trips every alarm the real one does: hunger, stress hormones, prolactin. Ring the same doorbell too often and, especially with hexarelin, the receptor stops answering at all.

mechSteps.t: They forge ghrelin's signal
mechSteps.predict: These are synthetic copies of one hormone your gut makes. Which hormone — and what receptor are they aiming at?
mechSteps.d: GHRP-6, GHRP-2 and hexarelin are synthetic hexapeptides that bind and activate the growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHSR-1a) — the very same receptor your stomach's hunger hormone ghrelin uses. That's why they're called ghrelin mimetics: same lock, man-made key.

mechSteps.t: The pituitary answers with a GH pulse
mechSteps.predict: If the receptor sits on the GH-releasing cells of your pituitary, what happens the moment you switch it on?
mechSteps.d: Activating GHSR on pituitary somatotrophs triggers a sharp burst of growth hormone. These peptides also blunt somatostatin — your body's natural 'stop making GH' brake — and amplify the effect of GHRH, so the pulse is genuinely large. This is a pulse, not a steady level: it rises within minutes and fades within hours.

mechSteps.t: But GHSR isn't only in the pituitary
mechSteps.predict: The same receptor lives in your hunger and stress circuits. So what rides along with the growth hormone?
mechSteps.d: Ghrelin's job is to make you hungry, so activating its receptor in the hypothalamus drives appetite — strongest with GHRP-6. And these peptides spill over onto the stress axis: GHRP-2 and hexarelin acutely raise ACTH, cortisol and prolactin. That side baggage is the whole reason the cleaner peptide ipamorelin was later designed.

mechSteps.t: The receptor stops answering
mechSteps.predict: You ring the same doorbell every day. What does the receptor eventually do — and which peptide hits this wall first?
mechSteps.d: Repeated stimulation desensitises and down-regulates GHSR, so the GH response shrinks over time. Hexarelin, the most potent, desensitises fastest; a 16-week human study showed the GH response falling by roughly half, then recovering after an off-period. This is why continuous use fails and why 'cycling' exists — you're waiting for the receptor to reset.

mechSteps.t: Hexarelin has a second, separate lock
mechSteps.predict: Hexarelin does something in the heart the others barely do, and it doesn't need growth hormone to do it. How?
mechSteps.d: Uniquely, hexarelin also binds CD36, a scavenger receptor on heart muscle, producing GH-independent cardiovascular effects shown in animal work (absent in CD36-null mice). This is a genuine second mechanism — interesting biology, but studied in the lab and animals, not established as a human therapy.

framework.target: The growth hormone secretagogue receptor GHSR-1a (the ghrelin receptor) — a G-protein-coupled receptor. Unlike caffeine (which blocks) these peptides are full agonists: they switch it ON, on pituitary somatotrophs and in the hypothalamus. Hexarelin additionally binds CD36 in the heart.
framework.affinity: All three are potent, nanomolar-range GHSR agonists — hexarelin the most potent — which is why microgram-per-kilogram doses fully engage the receptor. Honest limit: a single clean, universally cited human Ki for each isn't something to quote with confidence, so treat potency as an ordering (hexarelin most potent of the three), not a hard number.
framework.adme: These are peptides, so oral bioavailability is negligible (<1%) — they're injected (subcutaneous/IV) or given intranasally. Plasma half-lives are short (roughly 15–60 minutes; hexarelin ~55 min, the others in a similar short range), but the felt effect is a GH pulse, not a steady blood level.
framework.genetics: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic switch. GHSR gene variants exist but haven't been characterised for these peptides, so treat individual response as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Modest and old (2/5). There are real human endocrine studies of the acute GH/cortisol/prolactin response, and GHRP-2 (pralmorelin) is actually approved in Japan — but only as a diagnostic test for GH deficiency, not as a physique or anti-ageing drug. Controlled long-term data for the muscle/anti-ageing uses people chase is essentially absent, and desensitisation limits chronic use.

journey.d: As peptides they're destroyed by stomach acid, so oral dosing barely works (<1% absorbed) — they're injected under the skin or given as a nasal spray to reach the blood.
They circulate only briefly. Plasma half-lives are short — on the order of 15–60 minutes — so this is a quick spike, not a sustained level.
They dock onto GHSR on the growth-hormone-releasing cells, release the somatostatin brake, and trigger a burst of GH that peaks within about 15–30 minutes.
The same receptor in the hypothalamus fires appetite (strongest with GHRP-6), while GHRP-2 and hexarelin also nudge up ACTH, cortisol and prolactin — the ride-along effects.
Hexarelin alone also binds CD36 on heart muscle, producing growth-hormone-independent cardiovascular effects — real in animal models, not established in humans.
Peptidases clear the drug and metabolites leave via the kidney within hours. Ring the receptor daily and it desensitises — the GH response shrinks, and only an off-period restores it.

myths.myth: They're safer than injecting HGH because 'it's your own growth hormone.'
myths.truth: Your pituitary does the releasing, but the signal is anything but clean: GHRP-6 drives hunger, GHRP-2 and hexarelin raise cortisol and prolactin, and the response desensitises. Add unregulated gray-market product quality, and 'your own GH' is not the same as 'safe.'

myths.myth: The most potent one (hexarelin) is the best one.
myths.truth: Hexarelin releases the most GH per dose, but it also desensitises the fastest and carries a strong cortisol/prolactin bump. Being strongest is exactly why it fades quickest — potency and durability are trade-offs here.

myths.myth: These are approved, well-tested drugs.
myths.truth: Only GHRP-2 (pralmorelin) is approved anywhere, and only in Japan as a one-shot diagnostic test for GH deficiency — not for muscle, fat loss or anti-ageing. None of the three is approved for physique use, and long-term safety data for that use barely exists.

myths.myth: They release growth hormone 'cleanly.'
myths.truth: That's the pitch, but it's what the first generation got wrong. The appetite, cortisol and prolactin are the point of the story — ipamorelin was engineered specifically to keep the GH pulse and drop that baggage, which is why it largely replaced these.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a strong, cheap GH pulse from a small injection — hexarelin for maximum release, GHRP-2 for a middle path, GHRP-6 when the appetite bump is wanted. The GH-releasing effect is real and documented acutely.

**The hormonal cost:** GHRP-2 and hexarelin acutely raise cortisol and prolactin (stress and milk hormones) — the opposite of what most users assume they're getting. GHRP-6 drives real hunger via the ghrelin pathway.

**The fade:** the receptor desensitises with repeated use, hexarelin fastest, with a 16-week human study showing the GH response roughly halving. Continuous dosing defeats itself; this is why these were superseded, not a problem you can dose around.

**The regulatory reality:** none is approved for physique or anti-ageing anywhere. GHRP-2's only approval is as a diagnostic test in Japan. In most places these are sold as gray-market 'research chemicals,' banned in sport by WADA.

**The product-quality cost:** gray-market peptides are frequently mislabelled, underdosed or contaminated — independent testing of the peptide market repeatedly finds bottles that aren't what the label says.

**The honest evidence picture:** the human data is old, acute, and small — endocrine-response studies and a diagnostic approval, not long-term physique or safety trials. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a protocol.

start.q: In one line: what are these three peptides actually copying?
start.a: Ghrelin — your stomach's hunger hormone. They're synthetic ghrelin mimetics that activate the same receptor (GHSR-1a) to force a growth-hormone pulse.

how.q: They release GH — so why do you also get hunger, cortisol and prolactin?
how.a: Because GHSR isn't only in the pituitary. The same receptor drives appetite in the hypothalamus, and these peptides spill onto the ACTH/cortisol and prolactin pathways — so the side effects are baked into the mechanism, not a contaminant.

use.q: Why does continuous daily use stop working?
use.a: Repeated stimulation desensitises and down-regulates GHSR — the receptor stops responding. Hexarelin fades fastest; a 16-week study roughly halved the GH response, which recovered only after an off-period. Only a break resets it.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, honestly?
evidence.a: Weak-to-modest (2/5). Real acute endocrine studies exist and GHRP-2 is approved as a diagnostic in Japan, but there's essentially no controlled long-term data for the muscle/anti-ageing uses people actually want.

canExplain: Why a peptide that spikes growth hormone also makes you hungry — same receptor, ghrelin's
Why GHRP-2 and hexarelin raise cortisol and prolactin, and why that's the mechanism, not a side quest
Why hexarelin releases the most GH yet fades the fastest — potency versus desensitisation
Why only GHRP-2 is approved anywhere, and only as a diagnostic test, not a physique drug
Why ipamorelin replaced this whole generation — same GH pulse, without the appetite and stress-hormone baggage

selfTest.q: A friend says GHRP-6 and hexarelin are basically the same because both spike GH, so he'll take whichever's cheaper. Give him the two real differences.
selfTest.a: (1) GHRP-6 drives noticeably more hunger via the ghrelin/appetite pathway, while hexarelin is the most potent GH releaser but comes with the biggest cortisol/prolactin bump. (2) Hexarelin desensitises the receptor fastest, so its effect fades soonest under continuous use — they trade off potency against durability and side profile.

selfTest.q: Someone insists these are 'proven' because GHRP-2 is an actual approved drug. What's the catch?
selfTest.a: GHRP-2 (pralmorelin) is approved in Japan only as a single-dose diagnostic agent for growth hormone deficiency — you take it once and measure the GH response to test the pituitary. That approval says nothing about chronic use for muscle, fat loss or anti-ageing, which remains unapproved and largely untested long-term.

selfTest.q: Why is hexarelin's short (~55-minute) plasma half-life almost useless for predicting whether a daily protocol keeps working?
selfTest.a: Because the limiting factor isn't clearance from the blood — it's receptor desensitisation. Even with fresh drug in circulation each day, the GHSR down-regulates over weeks and the GH pulse shrinks. The half-life tells you the peptide is gone by the next dose; it tells you nothing about the receptor giving up.

evi.howStrong: This is old, thin, mostly acute human evidence — hence 2/5. In the 1980s–90s these peptides were studied seriously as endocrine probes: single-dose experiments measuring the GH, cortisol and prolactin response, dose-response curves, and use as a pituitary-function test. That work is real and reproducible. What barely exists is controlled, long-term data on the uses people actually chase — added muscle, fat loss, anti-ageing — in healthy people. The one durable clinical outcome is regulatory: GHRP-2/pralmorelin was approved in Japan (2004) as a diagnostic agent for GH deficiency, which validates the acute GH-releasing effect, not chronic benefit.
evi.whatItShows: Acutely, all three reliably release growth hormone in humans, and GHRP-2 and hexarelin also transiently raise cortisol and prolactin. Chronically, the story is desensitisation: the GH response shrinks with continued dosing. What the evidence does NOT show is a durable, controlled physique or longevity benefit in healthy adults.
evi.effect: The acute GH pulse is large and easy to measure — that's exactly why GHRP-2 became a diagnostic test. But the effect is a transient spike, cortisol and prolactin ride along, and the response falls (roughly halving in a 16-week hexarelin study, then recovering after a break) as the receptor desensitises.
evi.whoBenefits: The clearest documented beneficiary is a clinician testing for GH deficiency (the pralmorelin diagnostic). For healthy people using these to build muscle or slow ageing, benefit is unproven and the trade-offs — desensitisation, cortisol/prolactin, gray-market quality — are real.
evi.caveats: Nearly all the human data is acute and decades old, in small samples, using pharmaceutical-grade peptide under monitoring — not the gray-market product a physique user injects. Cortisol and prolactin elevation, receptor desensitisation, and unregulated product quality all cut against casual chronic use. Treat the acute GH numbers as the best case, not what a bottle off the internet delivers.

trials.finding: GHRP-6, the founding member of the class, released growth hormone dose-dependently in vitro and in vivo, acting directly on the pituitary to specifically release GH — establishing a GH secretagogue distinct from GHRH.
trials.ref: Bowers, Momany, Reynolds & Hong, 1984, Endocrinology 114:1537

trials.finding: In healthy adult males, intravenous hexarelin produced a dose-dependent rise not only in GH but also in cortisol and prolactin — documenting the 'side baggage' directly.
trials.ref: Massoud, Hindmarsh & Brook, 1996, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 81(12):4338

trials.finding: Twice-daily hexarelin over 16 weeks in healthy elderly subjects progressively attenuated the GH response (roughly halving the GH area-under-curve); the blunting was partial and reversible after an off-period.
trials.ref: Rahim, O'Neill & Shalet, 1998, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 83(5):1644

trials.finding: Hexarelin's cardiovascular actions are mediated by CD36, a receptor distinct from GHSR-1a, and are absent in CD36-deficient hearts — evidence for a growth-hormone-independent cardiac mechanism (animal/in vitro).
trials.ref: Bodart et al., 2002, Circulation Research 90:844
