tech.chem: Three synthetic hexapeptides (six amino acids each) built with non-natural D-amino acids for enzymatic stability. GHRP-6 = His-D-Trp-Ala-Trp-D-Phe-Lys-NH2 (MW ~873). Hexarelin (examorelin) = His-D-2-methyl-Trp-Ala-Trp-D-Phe-Lys-NH2 — GHRP-6 with a methylated Trp, raising potency. GHRP-2 (pralmorelin, CAS 158861-67-7) = D-Ala-D-2-Nal-Ala-Trp-D-Phe-Lys-NH2 (MW ~818). All descend from Cyril Bowers' and Frank Momany's enkephalin-derived work at Tulane; the endogenous ligand of their target receptor, ghrelin, was only identified later (Kojima et al., 1999).

targets.action: full agonist
targets.affinity: potent nanomolar-range agonists; hexarelin the most potent of the three
targets.role: Gq/11-coupled GPCR on pituitary somatotrophs and hypothalamic neurons — the primary target driving both the GH pulse and appetite. Notable for high constitutive (ligand-independent) activity.

targets.action: hexarelin binds (scavenger receptor)
targets.affinity: not reliably quantified as a single Ki here
targets.role: a second, GHSR-independent receptor for hexarelin (not GHRP-2/6) in cardiac and vascular tissue, mediating the growth-hormone-independent cardiovascular effects (Bodart et al., 2002).

tech.signaling: GHSR-1a couples chiefly to Gq/11 → phospholipase C → IP3/DAG → intracellular Ca²⁺ rise in somatotrophs, triggering GH exocytosis. The peptides also suppress somatostatin tone and act synergistically with GHRH, amplifying the pulse beyond what either does alone. In the hypothalamus the same receptor activates NPY/AgRP appetite circuits. GHRP-2 and hexarelin additionally engage the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, raising ACTH/cortisol and prolactin. Hexarelin's cardiac action runs through CD36, entirely separate from GHSR.

adme.absorb: Negligible oral bioavailability (<1%) — degraded by gastric and intestinal peptidases; administered subcutaneously, intravenously, or intranasally.
adme.distribute: Distributes to receptor-bearing tissue (pituitary, hypothalamus; hexarelin also heart/vasculature via CD36). Short plasma residence.
adme.metabolise: Proteolytic cleavage by peptidases, as with other small peptides.
adme.excrete: Renal elimination of peptide fragments/metabolites; plasma half-lives short (~15–60 min; hexarelin ~55 min, the others in a similar short range).

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. GHSR gene polymorphisms exist and could in principle modulate response, but this has not been characterised for these peptides — treat inter-individual variation as unpredictable.
tech.dose: The only approved dose is diagnostic: GHRP-2/pralmorelin ~1 µg/kg IV as a single GH-provocation test in Japan. Historical human research used single µg/kg boluses. Gray-market physique use commonly cites ~100 µg subcutaneous once or several times daily — doses never validated for chronic safety. This is description, not recommendation; no approved therapeutic (non-diagnostic) dose exists.
tech.tolerance: Marked, and central to the class: repeated GHSR stimulation causes receptor desensitisation and down-regulation, blunting the GH response — fastest with hexarelin, with a 16-week human study showing roughly a halving of the response (partial and reversible after a break). This tachyphylaxis (not clearance) is what forces intermittent 'cycling' and is a major reason the class was superseded by ipamorelin.

questions: Why are some cattle, whippets — and one documented human child — born with double the normal muscle, having never trained a day?
If switching off myostatin so obviously builds muscle, why was the one drug that reached human trials abruptly stopped mid-study?
Why is the 'myostatin inhibitor' sold all over the internet (YK-11) something that has never actually been tested in a single animal or human?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) They carry a broken myostatin gene. Myostatin (MSTN/GDF-8) is the body's built-in brake on muscle size; remove it from birth and muscle grows huge on its own — proven in Belgian Blue cattle (Kambadur 1997) and a German boy (Schuelke 2004, NEJM). (2) ACE-031, a decoy-receptor drug, was pulled from its Duchenne trial (Campbell 2017) after boys developed nosebleeds, gum bleeding and spidery skin vessels — because the same decoy that soaks up myostatin also soaks up BMP-9/BMP-10, signals your blood vessels need to stay intact. (3) YK-11 has exactly one piece of evidence behind it — a 2013 cell-culture study in mouse myoblasts (Kanno). No animal has taken it. No human trial exists. It is also a steroidal androgen, not the clean 'natural' agent it's marketed as.

follistatin-myostatin-inhibitors-ace-031.bigIdea: Your body caps its own muscle with a protein called myostatin — a brake, not an accelerator. These compounds don't add a growth signal; they release the brake. That single fact explains both why the mechanism is so compelling and why cutting a brake this deep in your biology turns out to be dangerous.
follistatin-myostatin-inhibitors-ace-031.analogy: Picture myostatin as a governor bolted onto your muscle's engine — a device whose only job is to keep the revs (muscle size) from ever climbing too high. Nature already ships a factory 'governor-remover' called follistatin. ACE-031 is a fleet of fake mailboxes that intercept the governor's 'slow down' letters before they arrive. YK-11 quietly cranks up your own follistatin. Both work — but the governor's letters weren't only going to muscle. Some were keeping your blood vessels sealed, and intercepting those is where the trouble starts.

mechSteps.t: Myostatin is the brake, not the gas
mechSteps.predict: Some animals are born with double the muscle and never train. One protein is broken in all of them — what is its normal job?
mechSteps.d: Myostatin (MSTN, also called GDF-8) is a member of the TGF-β family made by your own muscle. Its job is to LIMIT growth — a 'chalone', a self-imposed ceiling. Break the gene and the ceiling lifts: Belgian Blue and Piedmontese cattle, 'bully' whippets, and a documented human child all carry loss-of-function myostatin and are grossly over-muscled from birth.

mechSteps.t: The brake signals through activin receptors and SMAD2/3
mechSteps.predict: Myostatin has to deliver its 'stop growing' order somehow. Which receptor does it dock into, and what internal messengers carry the message inward?
mechSteps.d: Myostatin binds activin type-II receptors (chiefly ActRIIB / ACVR2B), which recruit a type-I co-receptor and switch on the SMAD2/3 messengers inside the cell. Those travel to the nucleus and turn DOWN muscle-building genes while damping the Akt/mTOR growth pathway. That is the physical brake line.

mechSteps.t: Cut the brake line and muscle grows
mechSteps.predict: If this brake is jammed permanently OFF from birth, what do you predict the animal looks like — and does bigger automatically mean stronger?
mechSteps.d: With myostatin gone, muscle mass climbs dramatically — this is real, reproducible, and the whole reason the target is exciting. But note the catch already visible in the animals: the extra muscle is not proportionally strong (force-per-gram often drops), so 'more mass' is not the same as 'more function'.

mechSteps.t: Two utterly different ways to release it
mechSteps.predict: You want to release this brake in an adult. Name two fundamentally different places you could intervene — the messenger itself, or the thing that catches the messenger.
mechSteps.d: ACE-031 (ramatercept) is a decoy: the outer part of the ActRIIB receptor fused to an antibody tail, injected so it floats in your blood and TRAPS myostatin before it ever reaches the real receptor. YK-11 works from the other end — it's a steroidal molecule that activates the androgen receptor and cranks up follistatin, your body's own myostatin-neutraliser, while adding a direct androgenic (SARM-like) push.

mechSteps.t: The brake was never muscle's private line
mechSteps.predict: This receptor isn't reserved for muscle. What else in the body listens on the same channel — and what breaks when you jam it?
mechSteps.d: ActRIIB also receives BMP-9 and BMP-10, the signals that keep your blood vessels sealed and healthy. A decoy that soaks up myostatin soaks these up too — which is exactly why ACE-031 caused nosebleeds, gum bleeding and telangiectasias (dilated skin vessels) and got halted. And YK-11's androgen-receptor arm carries the usual steroidal costs. Neither hits muscle cleanly.

framework.target: The myostatin brake — myostatin (MSTN/GDF-8) signalling through the ActRIIB → SMAD2/3 axis. The twist that makes this entry unusual: the two agents attack the SAME brake from opposite ends. ACE-031 is a ligand trap that removes myostatin from circulation; YK-11 is an indirect agent that raises follistatin (the natural neutraliser) and is itself an androgen-receptor agonist. There is no single 'drug binds receptor' story here.
framework.affinity: There is no one clean Ki to quote for this class, and honesty matters more than a number. ACE-031 is a high-affinity decoy built from the ActRIIB receptor — but it is deliberately promiscuous, grabbing not just myostatin but activins and BMP-9/BMP-10 as well, and that promiscuity IS the safety problem, not a side note. YK-11's binding constants in humans are essentially uncharacterised because it has never been through proper pharmacology. Do not trust any specific affinity you see quoted for YK-11.
framework.adme: ACE-031 is an injected Fc-fusion biologic, dosed subcutaneously roughly every 2–4 weeks — the long persistence typical of antibody-tail proteins. YK-11 is an orally taken steroidal small molecule, but its human absorption, half-life and clearance are unknown, because no trial has ever measured them. That single gap should colour everything else you read about it.
framework.genetics: No validated pharmacogenetic switch for either drug. The relevant genetics is the target's OWN biology: naturally occurring MSTN loss-of-function variants (cattle, dogs, the Schuelke child) are the living proof-of-concept that removing this brake enlarges muscle.
framework.evidence: Split and honest, 2/5 overall. ACE-031 has real Phase 1/2 human data — but that data is why it was STOPPED (vascular bleeding), and it was never approved for anything. YK-11 has one 2013 mouse-cell-culture paper and nothing else — no animal study, no human trial, no safety data at all. Follistatin gene therapy remains experimental. Compelling target, thin and troubling human proof.

journey.d: Two very different entries. ACE-031 is injected under the skin roughly every few weeks — a protein too big to swallow. YK-11 is taken as an oral capsule bought gray-market, where independent testing of SARM products routinely finds the wrong compound, wrong dose, or an actual steroid inside.

ACE-031 floats in your bloodstream acting as a sponge, mopping up circulating myostatin (and, unavoidably, activins and BMP-9/BMP-10). YK-11 distributes to tissues and heads for androgen receptors, where it begins nudging follistatin production upward.

With myostatin trapped or neutralised, the SMAD2/3 brake on your muscle eases and growth genes lift. Over weeks this is where mass slowly accrues — the effect people are chasing. Remember from the mechanism: mass is not automatically strength.

Here is where ACE-031 came undone. By also trapping BMP-9 and BMP-10 — signals your vessels rely on — it produced nosebleeds, gum bleeding and telangiectasias in trial subjects. These resolved when dosing stopped, but they ended the study.

YK-11's androgen-receptor arm means your brain reads an androgen signal, so expect the classic steroidal cost — suppression of your own testosterone. None of this is measured in humans, so the magnitude is genuinely unknown.
Metabolites leave in urine — and this is where athletes get caught. Myostatin-pathway agents and YK-11 are WADA-banned in and out of competition, and detectable long enough to end a season.

myths.myth: Myostatin inhibitors are a safe shortcut to a double-muscled physique.
myths.truth: The only one that reached human trials, ACE-031, was stopped for bleeding side effects — because the same decoy that traps myostatin traps the BMP signals your blood vessels need. And the popular oral one, YK-11, has never been tested in any animal or human at all. 'Compelling mechanism' is not 'proven safe'.

myths.myth: YK-11 is a natural myostatin inhibitor, not a steroid.
myths.truth: YK-11 is a synthetic STEROIDAL molecule that activates the androgen receptor. It raises follistatin as a downstream effect, but it carries androgenic/SARM-type costs (including likely testosterone suppression), it is WADA-banned, and it has zero human safety data. 'Not a steroid' is marketing, not chemistry.

myths.myth: More muscle from blocking myostatin means proportionally more strength.
myths.truth: Even in the myostatin-null animals, the extra muscle tends to produce LESS force per gram — the tissue is bigger but not proportionally stronger. Bigger on a scan is not the same as more capable, a gap this whole class has never convincingly closed in humans.

myths.myth: Follistatin is the body's 'safe' version, so raising it is harmless.
myths.truth: Follistatin isn't myostatin-specific — it also neutralises activins and related ligands that regulate far more than muscle. Flooding the system with it, whether by gene therapy or by a drug that induces it, has broad and still poorly-mapped effects. 'Natural molecule' does not mean 'safe at unnatural levels'.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is intoxicating — switch off the body's own brake on muscle and grow like a double-muscled bull without training harder. The MECHANISM is genuinely real; the cattle, the dogs and the Schuelke child prove the target works.

**The vascular cost (ACE-031):** the one agent tested in humans was halted because trapping myostatin also traps BMP-9/BMP-10, producing nosebleeds, gum bleeding and dilated skin vessels. Off-target by design — you cannot separate the muscle effect from the vessel effect with a broad ligand trap.

**The 'no data at all' cost (YK-11):** there is one 2013 mouse-cell study and nothing else. No animal has taken it, no human trial exists, its half-life and safe dose are unknown. You would be the experiment.

**The hormonal cost (YK-11):** it is a steroidal androgen-receptor agonist, so expect the standard steroidal price — suppression of your own testosterone — of unknown magnitude because no one has measured it.
**The legal and testing cost:** neither is an approved drug for physique use; both are WADA-banned and detectable. For a tested athlete this ends seasons.

**The product-quality cost:** gray-market SARM/peptide products are repeatedly found mislabelled, underdosed, or spiked with actual steroids — so you frequently don't even know what you're taking. This page explains the pharmacology; it is not a protocol.

start.q: In one line: what does myostatin actually do, and what do these compounds do to it?
start.a: Myostatin is your body's built-in BRAKE on muscle size. These compounds release the brake — they don't add a growth signal, they remove a limiter.

how.q: ACE-031 and YK-11 hit the same brake from opposite ends. Describe each.
how.a: ACE-031 is a decoy receptor that floats in the blood and traps myostatin before it reaches the real receptor. YK-11 works downstream — it's a steroidal androgen-receptor agonist that raises follistatin, your own natural myostatin-neutraliser.

use.q: Why did ACE-031 cause nosebleeds and skin-vessel changes?
use.a: Because the decoy isn't myostatin-specific — it also traps BMP-9 and BMP-10, signals your blood vessels need to stay sealed. Soak those up and vessels leak. The off-target effect is built into the mechanism, not a fluke.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really?
evidence.a: Weak and split. ACE-031 has real human data — but that data is why it was stopped (vascular safety), and it was never approved. YK-11 has a single 2013 mouse-cell-culture paper and no animal or human data whatsoever.

canExplain: Why myostatin is a brake on muscle, not an accelerator — and how loss-of-function proves it in cattle, dogs and one human child
The exact signalling line myostatin uses (ActRIIB → SMAD2/3) and why blocking it lifts the growth ceiling
How ACE-031 and YK-11 attack the same brake from completely opposite ends — a ligand trap versus a follistatin-raising androgen
Why ACE-031's nosebleeds weren't a random side effect but a direct consequence of trapping BMP-9/BMP-10
Why 'more muscle mass' from this pathway doesn't reliably mean more strength — and why the human evidence is thin enough to be honest about

selfTest.q: A friend says myostatin inhibitors are proven because 'super-muscled cattle exist'. What's the two-part correction?
selfTest.a: (1) The cattle prove the TARGET is real — loss-of-function myostatin genuinely enlarges muscle. (2) But that's proof the brake exists, not proof the DRUGS are safe or effective in humans: ACE-031 was halted for vessel bleeding, and YK-11 has never been tested in any animal or human. A real mechanism is not a proven therapy.

selfTest.q: Why did trapping myostatin with a decoy receptor cause bleeding, when myostatin itself has nothing to do with blood vessels?
selfTest.a: Because the decoy is built from the ActRIIB receptor, which naturally receives BMP-9 and BMP-10 as well as myostatin. Those BMPs maintain blood vessels. The trap can't tell them apart, so mopping up myostatin also mops up the vessel-sealing signals — producing nosebleeds and telangiectasias.

selfTest.q: YK-11 is sold as a clean 'myostatin inhibitor'. Give two reasons that framing is misleading.
selfTest.a: (1) It's a steroidal ANDROGEN-receptor agonist — it raises follistatin as a downstream effect but carries androgenic/steroidal costs and is WADA-banned. (2) Its entire evidence base is one 2013 mouse-cell-culture study; no animal or human has ever been dosed, so its half-life, safe dose and real effects are unknown.

evi.howStrong: Split down the middle, and weak on both halves. The TARGET has beautiful proof-of-concept: naturally myostatin-deficient cattle, dogs and one human child are all dramatically over-muscled, so 'release the brake, grow muscle' is not in doubt. But the DRUGS are another matter. ACE-031 reached real Phase 1/2 human trials — and the headline result is that its Duchenne study was HALTED for vascular safety (nosebleeds, gum bleeding, telangiectasias), and it was never approved for anything. YK-11, despite being everywhere online, rests on a single 2013 mouse-myoblast cell-culture study: no animal has taken it, no human trial exists, and there is no safety data at all. This is why the entry is 2/5, not higher.
evi.whatItShows: Loss-of-function genetics and animal work show that removing myostatin substantially increases muscle mass. What the human drug data has NOT shown is that you can do this safely, or that the added mass translates into proportional strength and function — the very gap that has stalled the whole field.
evi.effect: Honest and qualitative, because reliable human numbers don't exist. In myostatin-null animals, muscle mass rises markedly but force-per-gram often falls. ACE-031 produced measurable pharmacodynamic muscle changes in humans before it was stopped. YK-11's only 'effect' is increased differentiation of mouse muscle cells in a dish. Treat any specific human dose, gain, or half-life you see quoted for YK-11 as unverified.
evi.whoBenefits: The legitimate target populations are people LOSING muscle to disease — Duchenne muscular dystrophy, sarcopenia, cachexia — where preserving mass is the goal and even a modest, monitored benefit could matter. Use by healthy people for physique is entirely off-label, gray-market, and unstudied for safety.
evi.caveats: The vascular off-target effect of broad ligand traps is the crux for ACE-031; the total absence of human data is the crux for YK-11; and YK-11's androgenic nature (likely testosterone suppression, WADA ban) sits on top of that. Gray-market products are frequently mislabelled or contaminated. Read the trial record as a cautionary tale, not an endorsement.

trials.finding: A German boy with a loss-of-function mutation in both copies of the myostatin gene showed gross muscle hypertrophy and increased strength — the human proof that myostatin caps muscle growth.
trials.ref: Schuelke et al., 2004, N Engl J Med 350:2682

trials.finding: Double-muscled Belgian Blue and Piedmontese cattle carry inactivating mutations in myostatin (GDF-8) — the animal proof-of-concept for the target.
trials.ref: Kambadur et al., 1997, Genome Research 7:910

trials.finding: Phase 2 trial of the myostatin ligand-trap ACE-031 in ambulatory Duchenne boys was stopped after the second dosing regimen due to epistaxis and telangiectasias (vascular safety signals).
trials.ref: Campbell et al., 2017, Muscle & Nerve 55:458

trials.finding: YK-11 induced myogenic differentiation of mouse C2C12 myoblasts by upregulating follistatin (an effect not produced by DHT); reversed by anti-follistatin antibody. In-vitro cell culture only — no animal or human data.
trials.ref: Kanno et al., 2013, Biol Pharm Bull 36:1460

tech.chem: This entry covers a mechanism, not one molecule, so the chemistry is two unrelated things. ACE-031 (ramatercept) is a recombinant FUSION PROTEIN: the extracellular ligand-binding domain of the human activin receptor type IIB (ActRIIB / ACVR2B) fused to a human IgG1 Fc tail — a soluble decoy receptor / ligand trap, injected. YK-11 (CAS 1370003-76-1) is a small synthetic STEROIDAL molecule built on a steroid nucleus; it is classed as a selective androgen receptor modulator and, unlike a peptide, is orally taken. They share a target pathway, not a structure.

targets.action: the ligand being removed — sequestered/trapped by ACE-031, and neutralised by the follistatin that YK-11 induces
targets.affinity: no single clean Ki defines the class; ACE-031 binds it as a high-affinity decoy but non-selectively
targets.role: the master brake on skeletal-muscle mass; a TGF-β-family negative regulator ('chalone')

targets.action: the receptor myostatin signals through; ACE-031 is literally built from its extracellular domain
targets.affinity: promiscuous — also binds activins and BMP-9/BMP-10, the basis of the off-target vascular effects
targets.role: the docking point that launches SMAD2/3 signalling to suppress muscle growth

targets.action: endogenous myostatin/activin neutraliser; upregulated by YK-11
targets.affinity: a high-affinity extracellular binding protein, not a receptor
targets.role: the body's own brake-remover; the node YK-11 exploits

targets.action: YK-11 is a partial agonist here
targets.affinity: not reliably characterised in humans for YK-11
targets.role: the second, steroidal arm of YK-11's action — and the source of its androgenic costs and WADA status

tech.signaling: Myostatin binds ActRIIB, which recruits a type-I co-receptor (ALK4/ALK5) and phosphorylates SMAD2/3; the SMAD2/3–SMAD4 complex enters the nucleus and represses myogenic genes while restraining the Akt/mTOR anabolic pathway — the molecular brake. Follistatin sequesters myostatin (and activins) extracellularly before they can dock. ACE-031 acts at the same extracellular step as a decoy trap. YK-11 acts through the androgen receptor to transcriptionally upregulate follistatin, adding an indirect anti-myostatin effect on top of direct AR agonism.

adme.absorb: ACE-031: injected subcutaneously (a protein, not orally bioavailable). YK-11: orally taken, but human absorption is uncharacterised.
adme.distribute: ACE-031 stays largely in the circulation acting as a soluble sponge; YK-11 distributes to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues.
adme.metabolise: ACE-031 is cleared like other Fc-fusion proteins (proteolytic catabolism), giving a long serum persistence and every-2–4-week dosing. YK-11's human metabolism is unknown.
adme.excrete: ACE-031: protein catabolism, not renal excretion of parent. YK-11: metabolites in urine, detectable for anti-doping — but no formal human pharmacokinetic profile exists.

tech.pgx: No validated pharmacogenetic marker for either agent. The meaningful 'genetics' is the target's own: MSTN loss-of-function variants (cattle, dogs, the Schuelke child) demonstrate the phenotype that these drugs try to imitate pharmacologically.
tech.dose: ACE-031 was given in ascending subcutaneous doses every few weeks in trials before being halted; no dose is approved for any use. YK-11 has NO established or validated dose — gray-market anecdotal use is commonly cited around single-digit milligrams orally per day, but this is described, not recommended, and rests on zero human safety data.
tech.tolerance: Not a receptor-downregulation story. The limiting factor for ACE-031 is cumulative off-target vascular toxicity (BMP-9/BMP-10 trapping); for YK-11 it is cumulative androgenic exposure (expected testosterone suppression) plus the fact that its safety was never characterised at all. Neither is limited by classic tolerance — both by harm.

contrasts.title: ACE-031 vs YK-11 — the trap vs the booster
contrasts.point: Same brake, opposite ends. ACE-031 works OUTSIDE the cell — a decoy receptor that catches myostatin in the bloodstream before it can signal, which is powerful but promiscuous (it catches vessel-protecting BMPs too). YK-11 works from the OTHER side — a steroidal androgen that makes your own cells produce more follistatin, the natural myostatin-neutraliser, while also directly stimulating the androgen receptor. One is an injected protein with real (troubling) human data; the other is an oral steroid with none. Understanding this split is the whole entry.

contrasts.title: Myostatin inhibitors vs anabolic steroids — releasing a brake vs flooring the accelerator
contrasts.point: Both grow muscle, opposite logic. A steroid FLOORS THE ACCELERATOR — it drives the androgen receptor to push growth genes harder. A myostatin inhibitor RELEASES A BRAKE — it removes the ceiling the body normally imposes. (YK-11 confusingly does a bit of both, which is why it's mis-sold as 'natural'.) The brake approach is elegant on paper, but cutting a regulator this deep in TGF-β biology reaches vessels and other tissues — which is exactly why the clean-looking mechanism has been so hard to turn into a safe drug.

questions: It's cleared from your blood in about 15 minutes — so how can it possibly heal a tendon over weeks?
If it's just a fragment of a protein your own stomach makes, why is the FDA fighting over whether it's even legal?
Why does the exact mechanism that makes it heal wounds also make cancer doctors nervous?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) The blood half-life is irrelevant — BPC-157 works by changing gene expression (turning up VEGFR2 and growth-hormone-receptor genes), and once those genes are dialed up the tissue keeps building new blood vessels for days after the peptide itself is gone. That gap between a minutes-long half-life and a weeks-long effect is called the PK-PD disconnect. (2) It's a synthetic 15-amino-acid fragment, not a natural whole protein, and it has never completed a human trial. The FDA has treated it as a not-for-compounding (Category 2) substance and removed it from that list in April 2026 — and the Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee is reviewing its status on 23–24 July 2026, which is exactly why its legality is unsettled. (3) Healing runs on new blood vessels (angiogenesis) — and so does tumour growth. The same VEGFR2 switch that feeds a mending tendon could in theory feed a tumour, and no human study has ruled that out.

bpc-157.bigIdea: BPC-157 doesn't patch the wound itself — it tells injured tissue to grow a fresh blood supply, and because healing runs on blood flow, everything downstream (tendon, ligament, gut lining, muscle) mends faster. Hold that one sentence; the whole page follows from it.
bpc-157.analogy: Think of a healing wound as a construction site that has run out of supply roads. BPC-157 doesn't lay bricks — it signals the crew to build new roads (blood vessels) into the site, so oxygen, nutrients and repair cells can finally get in. The catch: the same road-building crew, pointed at the wrong site, could just as easily pave the way for a tumour — and almost every photo we have of this crew working was taken in a rat.

mechSteps.t: It's a stable fragment of a gut protein
mechSteps.predict: BPC-157 is a piece of a protein found in stomach juice — a brutal, acidic place. What property would a molecule need just to survive there?
mechSteps.d: BPC-157 is a synthetic 15-amino-acid stretch of 'body protection compound,' a protein isolated from human gastric juice. Its claim to fame is stability: unlike most peptides it resists rapid breakdown, which is why it survives long enough to act at all.

mechSteps.t: It flips on the blood-vessel growth switch
mechSteps.predict: Healing tissue is starved of a supply line. Which single receptor is the master switch for growing new blood vessels — and would you expect BPC-157 to raise it or lower it?
mechSteps.d: BPC-157 raises the amount of VEGFR2 (KDR) — the receptor that endothelial cells use to build new vessels. Note the unusual move: it doesn't just dock the receptor like a drug, it increases how much of the receptor the cell makes and helps pull it inside to fire its signal.

mechSteps.t: The signal runs down to nitric oxide
mechSteps.predict: Once VEGFR2 is switched on, the signal has to reach the enzyme that actually relaxes vessels and drives new growth. What gas does that enzyme make?
mechSteps.d: Activated VEGFR2 runs down the PI3K/Akt pathway to eNOS (NOS3), the enzyme that makes nitric oxide. A second, parallel route — Src–Caveolin-1–eNOS — converges on the same place. Nitric oxide relaxes vessels and supports the new network, so blood flow to the injury climbs.

mechSteps.t: In tendon, it primes the repair cells
mechSteps.predict: Blood supply is half the story. In a torn tendon, what would make the local repair cells (fibroblasts) both multiply and crawl into the gap?
mechSteps.d: In tendon fibroblasts, BPC-157 turns up growth-hormone-receptor genes (making the cells more responsive to your own growth hormone) and, in separate work, activates the FAK–paxillin pathway that drives cell migration. More responsive, more mobile repair cells lay down collagen faster.

mechSteps.t: The peptide leaves — the effect stays
mechSteps.predict: BPC-157 is gone from the blood in under half an hour, yet tissue keeps healing for weeks. What kind of change would outlast the molecule that caused it?
mechSteps.d: This is the PK-PD disconnect. Because BPC-157 acts by shifting gene expression (VEGFR2, GHR) rather than by sitting on a receptor, the tissue keeps running the new program long after the peptide has cleared. The blood half-life tells you almost nothing about how long the effect lasts.

framework.target: There is no single clean receptor here. BPC-157 is a pleiotropic peptide: its best-characterised action is upregulating VEGFR2 (KDR) expression and modulating the nitric-oxide system (eNOS), plus raising growth-hormone-receptor expression in tendon. It works more like a signal that re-tunes tissue than a key that fits one lock.
framework.affinity: Here's the honest gap most pages skip: no validated binding constant (Ki) exists, because no defined high-affinity receptor has been pinned down. Unlike caffeine (blocks a receptor) or ostarine (Ki ≈ 3–4 nM on the androgen receptor), BPC-157 changes how much of a receptor the cell makes rather than docking it. So 'what dose reaches the target?' isn't even the right question yet — the target itself is still fuzzy.
framework.adme: Very short serum half-life: about 15 minutes after IV in rats, under 30 minutes in both rats and dogs, undetectable in plasma within hours. It's given by injection (subcutaneous/intramuscular) in almost all use. Crucially, there is no published human pharmacokinetic data, and the effect is driven by gene expression, not the blood curve.
framework.genetics: No pharmacogenetic marker is known — there's no CYP1A2-style switch that predicts who responds. With no completed human trials, individual variation is simply uncharacterised.
framework.evidence: Weak by human standards, striking by animal standards. The preclinical rat literature is broad and consistent (tendon, gut, muscle, vessel), but completed, published, randomised placebo-controlled human trials are essentially absent — which is exactly why its regulatory status is unsettled.

journey.d: Almost all use is by subcutaneous or intramuscular injection — and a real caution starts here: gray-market peptide vials are frequently mislabeled, underdosed, or contaminated, so you often don't actually know what's in the syringe.
It circulates only briefly — cleared from plasma in minutes and gone within hours. A short visit, which is why its lasting effect is so counterintuitive.
At the wound it turns up VEGFR2 and drives the Akt–eNOS and Src–Caveolin-1–eNOS pathways, growing new blood vessels. In tendon it also primes fibroblasts to migrate and lay down collagen.

The real action is here: it raises expression of receptor genes (VEGFR2, growth-hormone-receptor). Because it reprograms the tissue rather than just occupying a receptor, the effect outlives the molecule.
It's broken down into smaller peptide fragments and amino acids that rejoin normal metabolism. No exotic metabolites, no human clearance data.

What we can't map is the safety trail. No human safety dataset exists, the pro-angiogenic mechanism carries a theoretical tumour-feeding concern, and long-term effects in healthy people are simply unstudied.

myths.myth: It's basically natural — it's a protein your own body makes, so it's safe.
myths.truth: What's sold is a synthetic 15-amino-acid fragment, not the whole natural protein, and it's manufactured by unregulated suppliers. 'Derived from a body protein' describes its origin story, not its safety — and there is no human safety dataset behind it.

myths.myth: There are hundreds of studies, so it's well proven in people.
myths.truth: The volume is real but almost entirely in rats and in cell dishes. As of 2026 there is no completed, published, randomised placebo-controlled human efficacy trial for any indication — a handful of tiny uncontrolled reports is the extent of the human record.

myths.myth: Its short half-life means it's out of your system fast, so it's low-risk.
myths.truth: The half-life is short, but the effect isn't — it changes gene expression, so tissue keeps responding for days. 'Cleared from blood' is not the same as 'done acting,' and that lingering, unstudied biological effect is where the real uncertainty lives.

myths.myth: Growing blood vessels is always good.
myths.truth: Angiogenesis is exactly what a healing wound needs — and exactly what a growing tumour needs too. The same VEGFR2 mechanism cuts both ways, and no human study has characterised that risk.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is faster recovery from tendon, ligament, muscle and gut injuries — the animal data on tendon and gut healing is genuinely striking, and that's what drives the hype. This page exists to explain that pharmacology, not to help you dose it.

**The evidence cost:** almost everything impressive is in rats or cell dishes. There is no completed human efficacy trial, so you'd be extrapolating from rodents to yourself with essentially no human bridge.

**The safety cost:** there is no human safety dataset at all — no established dose, no known long-term effects, no characterised side-effect profile. 'No reported problems' mostly means 'no one has properly looked.'

**The tumour concern:** its core mechanism is pro-angiogenic (VEGFR2). The same new-blood-vessel signal that heals tissue could in theory support tumour growth; this is a real theoretical risk that human data has not addressed.

**The product-quality cost:** it's sold as a 'research chemical' by gray-market vendors. Independent testing of this category repeatedly finds mislabeling, wrong doses, and contamination — so purity and identity are not guaranteed.

**The legal and testing cost:** BPC-157 is not approved for any use. It was placed on the FDA's Category 2 (do-not-compound) list, removed from it in April 2026, and is now under Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee review (23–24 July 2026) for possible inclusion on the 503A compounding list — so its legal status is genuinely unsettled. It is also prohibited in sport under WADA's S0 non-approved-substances category, banned at all times. For a tested athlete, that's a hard stop.

start.q: In one line: does BPC-157 patch the wound directly, or do something upstream?
start.a: Upstream — it signals injured tissue to grow a new blood supply (via VEGFR2). Healing runs on blood flow, so the repair speeds up as a downstream consequence.

how.q: What receptor does BPC-157 turn up, and where does that signal end?
how.a: It upregulates VEGFR2 (KDR); the signal runs through Akt to eNOS (and via a parallel Src–Caveolin-1–eNOS route) to make nitric oxide and drive angiogenesis.

use.q: Its blood half-life is under 30 minutes. Why does that tell you almost nothing about how long it works?
use.a: Because it acts by changing gene expression, not by holding a blood level. The tissue keeps running the new program for days after the peptide clears — the PK-PD disconnect.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, honestly?
evidence.a: Very weak. The animal literature is broad and consistent, but there is no completed, published, randomised placebo-controlled human trial — which is why its FDA status is unsettled.

canExplain: Why BPC-157 heals indirectly — by growing new blood vessels rather than patching tissue itself
The exact pathway: VEGFR2 (KDR) → Akt → eNOS → nitric oxide → angiogenesis, plus the Src–Caveolin-1–eNOS route
Why a molecule cleared from blood in minutes can still act for weeks (the PK-PD disconnect)
Why this compound has no clean receptor Ki to quote — it shifts receptor expression instead of docking a lock
Why the human evidence is almost non-existent, and why the same healing mechanism raises a tumour concern

selfTest.q: A friend says BPC-157 'can't do much because it's gone from your blood in 15 minutes.' Correct him.
selfTest.a: Blood half-life is the wrong yardstick here. BPC-157 works by turning up genes (VEGFR2, growth-hormone-receptor), so the tissue keeps building blood vessels and repairing for days after the peptide clears. Effect duration is set by the gene program, not the clearance clock.

selfTest.q: Explain why the very thing that makes BPC-157 promising for healing is also what worries oncologists.
selfTest.a: Its central mechanism is pro-angiogenic — it upregulates VEGFR2 to grow new blood vessels. Healing tissue needs that new blood supply, but so does a growing tumour. The same VEGFR2 switch could in theory feed a tumour, and no human study has ruled that out.

selfTest.q: Someone points to 'dozens of studies' as proof BPC-157 works in people. What's the gap?
selfTest.a: Nearly all of those studies are in rats or cell cultures. As of 2026 there is no completed, published, randomised placebo-controlled human efficacy trial — so the volume of preclinical work doesn't translate into proven human efficacy or safety.

evi.howStrong: This is the defining feature of BPC-157: a wide, consistent preclinical literature sitting on top of an almost empty human record. In animals — mostly rats — it accelerates healing across strikingly different tissues (tendon, ligament, gut, muscle, blood vessel), and the mechanism (VEGFR2 upregulation, eNOS/nitric-oxide signalling) has been worked out in cell and vessel models. But the human bridge barely exists: as of 2026 there is no completed, published, randomised placebo-controlled trial for any indication, only a handful of tiny uncontrolled reports. So the honest summary is 'promising in animals, essentially unproven in people.'
evi.whatItShows: The preclinical work shows BPC-157 speeds tissue repair and that it does so, at least in part, by driving angiogenesis through VEGFR2 and the nitric-oxide system. What it has not shown — because the trials don't exist — is a proven, quantified benefit in humans, or a human safety profile.
evi.effect: No reliable human effect size can be given, because there are no controlled human efficacy trials to pool. Animal effects on healing are described as robust in the literature, but translating a rat effect size to a human number would be fabrication — so the honest answer is that the human effect size is unknown.
evi.whoBenefits: In the animal models, injured tissue with a healing demand (tendon, gut, muscle) shows the clearest response. In humans this is untested; use in healthy people for recovery or physique is entirely off-label, gray-market, and unstudied for safety.
evi.caveats: Three big ones. First, the evidence is overwhelmingly rodent — species translation is not guaranteed. Second, there is no human safety data, so long-term risk (including the theoretical tumour-angiogenesis concern) is genuinely unknown, not merely 'low.' Third, gray-market product purity is unreliable, so even the anecdotes may not reflect what's on the label. Treat everything here as mechanism and animal biology, not a human guarantee.

trials.finding: BPC-157 promoted angiogenesis by increasing VEGFR2 expression and activating the VEGFR2–Akt–eNOS pathway (rat hind-limb ischemia model plus endothelial cell culture — mechanistic, not a human efficacy trial).
BPC-157 produced nitric-oxide-dependent, endothelium-dependent vasomotor effects via the Src–Caveolin-1–eNOS pathway in isolated rat aorta (mechanistic, ex vivo).
trials.ref: Hsieh et al., 2020, Scientific Reports (Nature), 10:17078, doi:10.1038/s41598-020-74022-y

trials.finding: BPC-157 dose- and time-dependently increased growth-hormone-receptor expression (mRNA and protein) in tendon fibroblasts and, with added growth hormone, activated JAK2 and boosted cell proliferation (in vitro).

BPC-157 increased tendon-fibroblast migration and outgrowth via activation of the FAK–paxillin signalling pathway (in vitro plus rat Achilles-tendon model) — this FAK–paxillin finding comes from this paper, not the GHR study.
trials.ref: Chang et al., 2011, J Appl Physiol 110(3):774–780, doi:10.1152/japplphysiol.00945.2010

trials.finding: Pharmacokinetic study reporting an elimination half-life under 30 minutes (≈15.2 min after IV in rats) in rats and dogs, with metabolism to smaller fragments and urinary/biliary excretion — no human PK.
trials.ref: He et al., 2022, Frontiers in Pharmacology 13:1026182 (PMC9794587)

tech.chem: A synthetic pentadecapeptide (15 amino acids) corresponding to a partial sequence of 'body protection compound' (BPC), a protein isolated from human gastric juice. Its notable physicochemical property is unusual stability — it resists rapid proteolytic degradation that would destroy most peptides of similar size, which is the basis of its 'gastric-juice-derived' origin story. It is not a steroid, not a hormone in the classical sense, and has no steroid or purine scaffold.

targets.action: expression upregulated (mRNA and protein) and receptor internalisation promoted → downstream Akt/eNOS and ERK1/2 signalling
targets.affinity: no validated binding constant — BPC-157 is not established as a direct high-affinity ligand; it raises receptor expression rather than docking a defined site
targets.role: the central node of the pro-angiogenic effect — the master receptor for new blood-vessel growth

targets.action: activity increased downstream of VEGFR2–Akt and via a parallel Src–Caveolin-1–eNOS route → nitric-oxide production
targets.affinity: not a direct ligand target; modulated downstream
targets.role: the nitric-oxide-producing enzyme that mediates vasomotor and angiogenic effects

targets.action: expression upregulated in tendon fibroblasts, increasing responsiveness to endogenous growth hormone
targets.affinity: not a direct binding target; expression is raised
targets.role: amplifies the anabolic/collagen-synthesis arm of tendon repair

targets.action: phosphorylation increased → drives fibroblast migration
targets.affinity: downstream signalling node, not a direct ligand target
targets.role: the motility pathway that moves repair cells into the wound

tech.signaling: BPC-157 is pleiotropic and works through expression and pathway modulation rather than a single receptor cascade. The best-characterised arm: it increases VEGFR2 (KDR) expression and internalisation, driving PI3K/Akt → eNOS (NOS3) → nitric oxide, plus ERK1/2, to promote endothelial proliferation, migration and angiogenesis. A parallel Src–Caveolin-1–eNOS pathway produces nitric-oxide-dependent vasomotor effects independent of new VEGF-A. In tendon fibroblasts it upregulates growth-hormone-receptor expression and activates FAK–paxillin to drive migration and collagen deposition. It also modulates the nitric-oxide system broadly (raising eNOS, lowering iNOS in inflamed tissue). No defined high-affinity membrane receptor for BPC-157 itself has been established.

adme.absorb: Administered by injection (subcutaneous/intramuscular) in nearly all use; reported to retain some activity via oral routes in animal work owing to its stability, but human absorption data are absent. Intramuscular bioavailability in animals is modest (~14–19% in rats, ~45–51% in dogs).
adme.distribute: Distributes to injured/target tissues; detailed human distribution is uncharacterised.
adme.metabolise: Broken down into smaller peptide fragments and constituent amino acids that enter normal metabolic pathways; no exotic metabolites reported.
adme.excrete: Cleared rapidly from plasma (undetectable within hours in animals); main routes are urine and bile in animal studies, no human excretion data. Elimination half-life ≈15 min (rat IV), under 30 min in rats and dogs.

tech.pgx: No known pharmacogenetic marker predicts response. With no completed human trials, individual variation is uncharacterised.
tech.dose: There is no approved therapeutic dose. Animal studies use a wide range (roughly µg/kg to mg/kg). Gray-market human use is anecdotally described in the low-hundreds of micrograms per day by subcutaneous injection — this is a description of unregulated practice, not a recommendation, and no dose has been validated for human safety or efficacy.
tech.tolerance: No receptor-downregulation tolerance has been described (consistent with it having no defined receptor). Because there are no human longitudinal data, whether the effect wanes, and any long-term consequence of sustained pro-angiogenic signalling, is genuinely unknown rather than reassuringly absent.

questions: If it heals your whole body, why is TB-500 just a seven-amino-acid scrap of a much bigger protein?
How can a molecule whose day job is grabbing actin *inside* cells do anything useful when you inject it *outside* them?
There are real human trials on thymosin β4 for dry eye and heart attacks — so why does the honest verdict still say 'no human trials'?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) TB-500 is Ac-LKKTETQ, the N-acetylated fragment (residues 17–23) of the 43-amino-acid protein thymosin β4 — it carries the actin-binding motif and little else. (2) Its best-documented extracellular action isn't actin-grabbing at all: that same short motif drives blood-vessel growth (angiogenesis) and endothelial cell migration at low concentrations, which is a genuinely different job from sequestering actin inside a cell. (3) Every one of those human trials used the *full* recombinant protein delivered as eye drops or an infusion — not the injected TB-500 fragment people actually buy. The fragment itself has never been tested in a human trial.

tb-500-thymosin-beta-4-fragment.bigIdea: TB-500 is a synthetic scrap of the body's own actin-managing protein — its job is to help repair cells physically crawl to a wound and to sprout new blood vessels — but the human evidence is for the whole protein, not this fragment, so it sits on animal data and analogy.
tb-500-thymosin-beta-4-fragment.analogy: Think of thymosin β4 as a full multi-tool the body carries for repair. TB-500 is one blade snapped off that tool — the actin-handling edge, Ac-LKKTETQ. That edge still cuts: it can help cells release their internal scaffolding and start migrating, and it can call in new blood vessels. But a blade is not the tool it came from, and 'the tool works in people' is not the same claim as 'the blade works in people.'

mechSteps.t: It's the working edge of a bigger protein
mechSteps.predict: TB-500 is only seven amino acids (Ac-LKKTETQ). Which part of the full 43-amino-acid protein did they keep — and why that part?
mechSteps.d: They kept the LKKTET actin-binding motif, the stretch of thymosin β4 most directly tied to cell migration, wound healing and new-vessel growth. So TB-500 is not the whole protein — it's the fragment carrying its repair-relevant business end.

mechSteps.t: The parent protein grabs loose actin
mechSteps.predict: Thymosin β4's main native job is holding onto free actin building blocks. Why would grabbing actin help a cell move?
mechSteps.d: Actin exists as free monomers (G-actin) that assemble into filaments (F-actin). Full thymosin β4 sequesters G-actin 1:1 (Kd ≈ 0.5–0.7 µM), acting as a reservoir that lets a cell rapidly build and dismantle its internal scaffold — which is exactly what a cell must do to crawl.

mechSteps.t: Repair cells migrate to the damage
mechSteps.predict: If cells can now remodel their scaffold on demand, what do they do at a wound?
mechSteps.d: They migrate. The curated mechanism is cell migration to the site of injury — and in endothelial cells the isolated actin-binding motif drives migration and blood-vessel sprouting (angiogenesis) at low nanomolar levels, bringing blood supply to healing tissue.

mechSteps.t: It dials down inflammation
mechSteps.predict: Fast repair also needs the fire put out. What does thymosin β4 do to the inflammatory signal?
mechSteps.d: Thymosin β4 reduces inflammation, part of why it's framed as a 'whole-body' healer rather than a purely local one. This — plus migration and angiogenesis — is the repair package, and it plays out over days to weeks of tissue remodeling, not minutes.

framework.target: Intracellular G-actin (monomeric actin) — thymosin β4's native job is to sequester it. Note the key difference from a drug like caffeine or ostarine: there is no established cell-surface receptor that TB-500 switches on or off. It's an actin-binding peptide, closer to a substrate/scaffold than a receptor ligand.
framework.affinity: There's no receptor Ki to quote, because there's no receptor. The relevant number is the actin binding of the full protein: Kd ≈ 0.5–0.7 µM for thymosin β4 : G-actin, 1:1. Two honest caveats: this is the parent protein's constant, not the fragment's, and actin sequestration happens inside cells — so how an injected extracellular peptide reaches that target is genuinely unclear. Its better-documented extracellular action is angiogenic (the actin-binding motif drives endothelial migration in the low-nanomolar range in vitro).
framework.adme: Peptide — not orally active, so it's injected (subcutaneous/intramuscular in practice). A reliable human elimination half-life for the TB-500 fragment isn't well characterised; plasma clearance of thymosin-β4 peptides is thought to be short, but its repair effects run on a cell-migration/tissue-remodeling timescale, not a blood-level curve.
framework.genetics: No validated pharmacogenetic marker. Individual response is uncharacterised — treat it as unknown, not personalised.
framework.evidence: Weak-to-moderate and indirect (curated 2/5). Solid animal work in cardiac, corneal and dermal repair; small human trials exist for the full/recombinant protein (dry eye, STEMI) with mixed results — but the injected TB-500 fragment itself has no human trials.

journey.d: You inject it (it's a peptide, so swallowing it wouldn't survive digestion). Real caution: gray-market products sold as 'TB-500' are frequently mislabeled — some contain the full thymosin β4 protein, the wrong dose, or fail purity testing entirely.

It enters the bloodstream and distributes through the body. Being a small water-soluble peptide, it isn't stored in fat the way a steroid is; the framing as a 'whole-body' healer comes from its systemic spread to sites of injury.

At damaged tissue it's associated with the repair package: helping cells remodel actin and migrate in, sprouting new blood vessels (angiogenesis), and reducing inflammation. This is the effect people are chasing — and where the animal evidence is strongest.

In animal studies the marquee results are in the heart (after induced heart attack), the cornea (surface wounds) and skin — models of tissue that needs cells to migrate and re-vascularise. This is preclinical, not proof in a healthy human.

It's metabolised and cleared, with metabolites appearing in urine. This is how athletes get caught: TB-500 is banned by WADA in and out of competition, and doping labs have validated assays to detect it and its metabolites.

myths.myth: TB-500 is thymosin β4.
myths.truth: It's a seven-amino-acid synthetic fragment of it — Ac-LKKTETQ, the actin-binding motif — not the full 43-amino-acid protein. They have different chemistry, different pharmacokinetics and different regulatory histories. Crucially, the human trials people cite were run on the full/recombinant protein, not this fragment.

myths.myth: There are human trials proving TB-500 works.
myths.truth: There are human trials on full thymosin β4 (RGN-259 eye drops for dry eye; recombinant protein after heart attack), and their results are small and mixed. The injected TB-500 fragment that people actually buy has never been tested in a human trial. 'The protein was studied' is not 'the fragment works.'

myths.myth: It's a natural, side-effect-free recovery peptide.
myths.truth: 'Natural' describes the parent molecule's origin, not this product's safety. TB-500 isn't approved for human use anywhere, it's WADA-banned, gray-market vials are routinely mislabeled or impure, and its core actions — driving cell migration and new blood-vessel growth — are exactly the processes you would NOT want unregulated if any cancerous cells were present. That theoretical risk is simply unstudied in humans.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a 'whole-body' healer that speeds recovery from soft-tissue and tendon injuries, often stacked with BPC-157. The biological rationale (actin-driven cell migration, angiogenesis, less inflammation) is real; the human proof for the injected fragment is not.

**The evidence cost:** the honest picture is animal studies plus small human trials of a *different* molecule (the full protein). For TB-500 itself in humans, the data is anecdotal. Preclinical promise routinely fails to replicate in people.

**The legal and testing cost:** not approved for human use anywhere, sold as a 'research chemical,' and banned by WADA in and out of competition with validated urine detection. For any tested athlete this ends seasons.

**The product-quality cost:** independent testing repeatedly finds peptides like this mislabeled, underdosed, contaminated, or containing something other than what the label claims — so you often don't know what's in the vial.

**The theoretical safety cost:** its defining actions are pro-migration and pro-angiogenesis. Those are desirable in a wound and undesirable around a tumor. There is no long-term human safety data to tell you where the balance lands. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a protocol.

start.q: In one line: what is TB-500, relative to thymosin β4?
start.a: It's a short synthetic fragment (Ac-LKKTETQ) of the full 43-amino-acid protein thymosin β4 — the actin-binding motif, not the whole molecule.

how.q: What is thymosin β4's core native job, and how does that help repair?
how.a: It sequesters free G-actin (monomeric actin), giving cells a reservoir to rapidly rebuild their internal scaffold — which is what lets repair cells migrate into a wound. The peptide is also angiogenic and anti-inflammatory.

use.q: Why does 'there are human thymosin β4 trials' not mean 'TB-500 is proven in humans'?
use.a: Those trials used the full or recombinant protein, delivered as eye drops or infusion, and got small/mixed results. The injected TB-500 fragment is a different molecule and has no human trials of its own.

evidence.q: How strong is the evidence, really?
evidence.a: Weak-to-moderate and indirect: good animal data in cardiac/corneal/dermal repair, small mixed human trials of the full protein, and no human trials of the fragment. Plausible from animal work — not proven in people.

canExplain: Why TB-500 is a fragment (Ac-LKKTETQ) of thymosin β4, not the whole protein
How sequestering G-actin lets repair cells migrate to an injury
Why it has no classic receptor — and why an injected actin-binding peptide's target is mechanistically puzzling
Why human trials on thymosin β4 don't prove the injected TB-500 fragment works
Why it's WADA-banned, unapproved, and why gray-market vials are a quality gamble

selfTest.q: A friend says 'TB-500 is clinically proven — there are phase 2 trials in dry eye and heart attack.' Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) Those trials used the full/recombinant thymosin β4 protein delivered as eye drops or infusion, not the injected TB-500 fragment. (2) Even those results are small and mixed (e.g., a STEMI trial missing its overall infarct-size endpoint). The fragment people buy has no human trials at all.

selfTest.q: TB-500's parent protein sequesters actin *inside* cells. Why is that a problem for explaining how an injected peptide works?
selfTest.a: Actin sequestration is an intracellular job, but the peptide is injected into the extracellular space, and there's no established surface receptor to carry its signal in. Its best-documented *extracellular* action is actually angiogenesis via the actin-binding motif — a different mechanism from the intracellular actin-grabbing.

selfTest.q: Why do TB-500's own advertised strengths double as its biggest theoretical safety concern?
selfTest.a: Its selling points are driving cell migration and new blood-vessel growth. Those same processes are how tumors invade and get their blood supply, so unregulated pro-migration/pro-angiogenic activity is exactly what you'd fear if any malignancy were present — and there's no long-term human safety data to weigh it.

evi.howStrong: The evidence is weak-to-moderate and, importantly, indirect. Thymosin β4 (the full protein) has genuine preclinical support — animal models of heart attack, corneal wounds and skin injury show accelerated repair, and the mechanism (actin regulation, angiogenesis, reduced inflammation) is well characterised. It even reached small human trials: a Phase 2 dry-eye program with thymosin β4 eye drops, and injectable recombinant protein after heart attack. But two honest gaps dominate. First, those human trials are small and their results mixed — this is not a settled therapy. Second, and decisively, they used the full or recombinant protein by eye drop or infusion, NOT the injected Ac-LKKTETQ fragment sold as TB-500. The fragment itself has no completed human trials. So the curated verdict — 'plausible from animal work; no human trials' — is the accurate one.
evi.whatItShows: In animals, thymosin β4 consistently promotes tissue repair: faster wound closure, new blood-vessel growth, reduced scarring and some cardioprotection after induced injury. In the human protein trials, the clearest positive signal is corneal/ocular-surface healing; the cardiac signal is weaker and inconsistent.
evi.effect: No trustworthy effect-size number exists for the injected TB-500 fragment in humans, because it hasn't been tested. Quoting a percentage here would be false precision.
evi.whoBenefits: Studied populations were patients with dry eye / ocular-surface disease and post-heart-attack patients — using the full protein. Its use by healthy people injecting the fragment for injury recovery is entirely off-label, gray-market, and unstudied for both efficacy and safety.
evi.caveats: The evidence-to-hype gap is the crux: strong-sounding mechanism, real animal data, but no human trials of the actual product. Add that gray-market vials are frequently mislabeled or impure, that it's WADA-banned and detectable, and that its pro-migration/pro-angiogenic actions carry a theoretical, unstudied cancer concern. Treat mechanistic plausibility as a hypothesis, not a result.

trials.finding: Full protein, not the TB-500 fragment: in a small Phase 2 RCT (n=9 severe dry-eye patients), thymosin β4 (RGN-259) 0.1% eye drops reduced ocular discomfort by ~35% (P=0.014) and total corneal fluorescein staining by ~59% (P=0.011) versus vehicle.
trials.ref: Sosne et al., Thymosin β4 (RGN-259) ophthalmic solution, Phase 2 severe dry-eye RCT, Clin Ophthalmol 2015

trials.finding: Mechanistic (in vitro): the seven-amino-acid actin-binding motif of thymosin β4 is essential for its angiogenic activity, driving endothelial cell migration at low concentrations — evidence the same motif TB-500 carries is angiogenic.
trials.ref: Philp et al., The actin-binding site on thymosin β4 promotes angiogenesis, FASEB J 2003

trials.finding: Full recombinant protein, not the fragment: in a 96-patient RCT after acute STEMI, recombinant human thymosin β4 did NOT significantly reduce infarct area overall; a reduction was seen only in the pre-specified subgroup treated within 8 h of PCI (n=43).
trials.ref: Recombinant human thymosin β4 in acute STEMI after reperfusion, RCT, Cardiovasc Res 2025

tech.chem: TB-500 is a synthetic N-acetylated heptapeptide, Ac-Leu-Lys-Lys-Thr-Glu-Thr-Gln (Ac-LKKTETQ), corresponding to amino acids 17–23 of thymosin β4 — the LKKTET actin-binding motif. Thymosin β4 itself (gene TMSB4X) is a highly conserved, abundant 43-amino-acid intracellular protein and the principal G-actin-sequestering peptide in mammalian cells. TB-500 reproduces the repair-relevant motif, not the whole protein; note that products marketed as 'TB-500' sometimes actually contain the full protein or mislabeled contents.

targets.action: actin sequestration — binds and holds free G-actin monomers (1:1), buffering the G-/F-actin equilibrium
targets.affinity: Kd ≈ 0.5–0.7 µM for full thymosin β4 : G-actin (the parent protein's constant, not the fragment's); this is a binding interaction, not a receptor Ki
targets.role: the native intracellular target; regulating actin dynamics underlies cell migration. Note the mechanistic tension: this action is intracellular, yet TB-500 is delivered extracellularly.

targets.action: source of the peptide — TB-500 is a fragment of this gene's product
targets.affinity: n/a
targets.role: the endogenous protein whose actin-binding motif TB-500 reproduces

tech.signaling: No classic receptor cascade is established. The validated core is bioenergetically-passive scaffolding: sequestering G-actin buffers the monomer pool so cells can rapidly polymerise and depolymerise F-actin during migration. Beyond actin, thymosin β4 has documented extracellular activities — pro-angiogenic (the isolated actin-binding motif drives endothelial migration and vessel sprouting), anti-inflammatory, and reported survival signaling (e.g., ILK/Akt pathway) — though the receptor(s) mediating these extracellular effects are not definitively identified. Treat downstream signaling as partly characterised, not fully mapped.

adme.absorb: Not orally bioavailable (a peptide, degraded by gut proteases); administered by injection (subcutaneous/intramuscular in practice).
adme.distribute: Distributes systemically as a small water-soluble peptide; the 'whole-body healer' framing reflects systemic distribution to injury sites rather than a specific reservoir.
adme.metabolise: Proteolytic breakdown to smaller peptide fragments/amino acids; characteristic metabolites underpin anti-doping detection assays.
adme.excrete: Metabolites eliminated in urine; parent and metabolites are detectable, which is the basis of WADA testing. A reliable human elimination half-life for the fragment is not well established.

tech.pgx: No validated pharmacogenetic marker; individual response is uncharacterised and should be treated as unknown.
tech.dose: No approved therapeutic dose exists for the TB-500 fragment in humans; human trials were of the full/recombinant protein (dry-eye eye drops, injectable cardiac dosing) rather than this peptide. Any physique/recovery dosing is gray-market and unvalidated for safety — described here only to be explicit that it is not evidence-based, not as a recommendation.
tech.tolerance: No receptor-downregulation tolerance is described (there's no established receptor). The governing concerns with continued use are unknown long-term safety, the pro-migration/pro-angiogenic activity as a theoretical risk, and product-quality variability — not tachyphylaxis.

contrasts.title: TB-500 vs BPC-157 — the whole-body healer vs the local one
contrasts.point: The two most-stacked 'repair' peptides, with different origins and reach. TB-500 is a fragment of thymosin β4 and works through actin regulation, cell migration and angiogenesis — framed as a systemic, 'whole-body' healer. BPC-157 is a fragment linked to a gastric protective protein and is described as acting more locally on tissue and the gut. They're commonly paired for exactly that complementary framing — but both share the same fundamental problem: strong mechanistic stories, animal data, and essentially no human trials of the actual injected peptides.

questions: Why does a peptide your own body makes drop by more than half between age 20 and 60 — and does adding it back actually do anything?
How can a molecule just three amino acids long change the activity of thousands of genes at once, without being a hormone or hitting a receptor?
Why is it always written 'GHK-Cu' — what is the copper doing there, and is it just decoration?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Your plasma GHK falls from ~200 ng/ml at 20 to ~80 ng/ml by 60 — the age-decline is real, but the proof that topping it back up helps is strong for topical skin and thin-to-absent for whole-body anti-aging. (2) It has no receptor — it works as a copper chaperone and a broad transcriptional modulator; Connectivity Map analyses report it shifts on the order of 4,000 human genes by ≥50%, toward collagen, antioxidant and DNA-repair programs. (3) The copper is the payload, not decoration: GHK binds Cu²⁺ with very high affinity (stability constant log β ≈ 16.4), just above albumin's — enough to acquire copper from it and ferry it safely into cells, where copper is a cofactor the repair enzymes need, and free copper alone would be toxic.

ghk-cu-copper-peptide.bigIdea: GHK-Cu isn't a hormone that flips one switch — it's a copper-carrying tripeptide your body makes less of with age, and adding it back nudges thousands of genes toward a younger, repair-mode setting. Best proven in skin; everything beyond skin is still mostly mechanism, not human outcomes.
ghk-cu-copper-peptide.analogy: Picture your skin's fibroblasts as an ageing construction crew whose blueprints have faded. GHK-Cu doesn't hand them energy or a hormone — it delivers a copy of the younger blueprint (it shifts gene expression toward a repair program) along with the copper their tools actually need to work. So the crew starts rebuilding collagen the way it did decades ago. The copper is the toolbox; the gene shift is the blueprint.
ghk-cu-copper-peptide.stakes: It's the best-evidenced of the cosmetic 'copper peptides' and sits in a huge number of serums — but 'best-evidenced cosmetic peptide' is a modest bar, and the gap between what's proven for skin and what's claimed for whole-body aging is where most of the hype lives.

mechSteps.t: It carries copper — safely
mechSteps.predict: The 'Cu' in the name isn't decoration. What does the peptide do with copper, and why can't the cell just use free copper on its own?
mechSteps.d: GHK binds copper(II) with very high affinity (stability constant log β ≈ 16.4) — just above albumin's, enough to pull copper off it — and ferries it into cells. Free copper is toxic and pro-oxidant, so a chaperone that delivers it precisely, as a cofactor for repair enzymes, is the whole point.

mechSteps.t: It reprograms gene expression
mechSteps.predict: It binds no receptor — so how does a three-amino-acid peptide change what thousands of genes are doing?
mechSteps.d: Inside the cell the GHK-Cu complex acts as a broad transcriptional modulator rather than a receptor ligand. In Broad Institute Connectivity Map analyses it shifts roughly 4,000 human genes by ≥50%, nudging the overall profile toward a younger, repair-mode setting.

mechSteps.t: It turns up repair and antioxidant programs
mechSteps.predict: Which gene programs get louder — and what would that do to an ageing cell?
mechSteps.d: The upregulated clusters include collagen, decorin, antioxidant-defence and DNA-repair genes, while metalloproteinases (MMPs) and TGF-β signalling are rebalanced. The net message to the cell is 'rebuild and protect the matrix.'

mechSteps.t: Fibroblasts rebuild the matrix
mechSteps.predict: In skin specifically, what visible change follows weeks of that 'rebuild' signal?
mechSteps.d: Dermal fibroblasts lay down more collagen, elastin, glycosaminoglycans and decorin. Over weeks this shows up as firmer, denser skin and faster wound healing — the one effect that actually has human evidence.

framework.target: There is no classical receptor to switch on or block. GHK-Cu is a copper chaperone and transcriptional modulator — it delivers copper into cells and broadly shifts gene expression, plus acts directly on the extracellular matrix (collagen, decorin, glycosaminoglycans, MMP balance, TGF-β). It's a signalling molecule, not an agonist or antagonist.
framework.affinity: There's no receptor Ki to quote, because there's no receptor — just like creatine, there's no lock to measure. The number that does matter is its copper-binding constant: log β ≈ 16.4, marginally above albumin's (~16.2), high enough to acquire copper from albumin and deliver it into cells. That copper affinity, not a receptor affinity, is the meaningful binding figure here.
framework.adme: Topically it penetrates skin and acts locally on dermal fibroblasts. The dramatic '~4,000 gene' story comes from cell-based Connectivity Map data, not measured human systemic pharmacokinetics. Endogenous plasma GHK runs ~200 ng/ml at age 20, falling to ~80 by 60. A human elimination half-life for exogenous dosing is not well established — a genuine data gap, so no half-life is claimed here.
framework.genetics: No validated pharmacogenetic switch exists for GHK-Cu. Baseline copper status and copper-handling genes (e.g. ATP7A/ATP7B) plausibly shape the copper-dependent effects, but response to GHK-Cu specifically has never been characterised — treat individual response as unknown.
framework.evidence: Strongest of the cosmetic peptides for topical skin (small controlled trials plus a large mechanistic gene-and-animal literature). But the systemic, anti-ageing and injectable claims rest on cell-culture and animal work, not human outcomes. Not approved as a drug for any indication.

journey.d: You apply a topical serum (or, endogenously, your body cleaves GHK from collagen and other matrix proteins at a wound). The small tripeptide-copper complex crosses the skin barrier.
GHK grabs Cu²⁺ with very high affinity — just above albumin's, enough to take it from albumin. The copper-loaded complex is the active form; the copper is the payload it's built to carry.
It reaches dermal fibroblasts and enters, delivering copper to cuproenzymes (like lysyl oxidase, which cross-links collagen) and antioxidant defences.
Inside, the complex shifts a broad panel of genes toward a repair profile — collagen, decorin, antioxidant and DNA-repair programs up, MMPs and TGF-β rebalanced.
Fibroblasts lay down collagen, elastin and glycosaminoglycans. Over weeks the skin becomes firmer and denser — the change that shows up in human trials, and the honest edge of the evidence.

myths.myth: Copper peptides work like an overnight face-lift.
myths.truth: The effect is gene-driven collagen remodelling over weeks, not an instant tightening. Human trials show a modest, gradual improvement in skin density and fine lines — real, but small and slow.

myths.myth: Injecting GHK-Cu resets your whole body's genes and reverses ageing.
myths.truth: The '~4,000 genes' finding is from cell-based Connectivity Map analysis and animal models — there's essentially no human trial showing systemic anti-ageing. Injected copper is also toxic and pro-oxidant in excess, and injectable products are grey-market and unvalidated for purity.

myths.myth: More copper peptide means more collagen.
myths.truth: It's a signalling nudge with a ceiling, and copper is redox-active — beyond a point extra copper is pro-oxidant and toxic, not helpful. 'More' buys risk, not more benefit.

myths.myth: It's in so many serums, so it must be FDA-approved and clinically proven.
myths.truth: It's sold as a cosmetic ingredient, which is a very different bar from an approved drug. Cosmetic availability is not the same as proven medicine, and no regulator has approved it for a systemic indication.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** among cosmetic peptides it has the strongest independent evidence for topical skin — collagen, firmness and wound healing. That part is genuine, if modest.
**The evidenced route is topical:** OTC serums applied to skin, where small controlled trials support gradual improvement over roughly 8–12 weeks. This is where the human data actually lives.

**The systemic/injectable leap is unproven:** the 'resets thousands of genes / reverses ageing' story is cell-culture and animal work, not human outcome trials. Injectable GHK-Cu is the reclassified, grey-market form.
**The copper cost:** copper is redox-active — in excess it's pro-oxidant and toxic, and injecting copper compounds carries real risk. Unregulated products also vary in purity and dose.
**The regulatory reality:** not approved as a drug for any systemic use anywhere. Cosmetic status is not clinical proof.

**The honest evidence picture:** skin benefit is real but rests on small, short, often industry-funded trials — the most-cited (Leyden, 2002) was a conference presentation, never peer-reviewed. Everything beyond skin is a promising mechanism with thin human data. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to sell you a protocol.

start.q: In one line: what is GHK-Cu, and does it work through a receptor?
start.a: A copper-carrying tripeptide your body makes less of with age. It has no classical receptor — it delivers copper and broadly modulates gene expression.

how.q: How can such a small molecule change thousands of genes without hitting a receptor?
how.a: It acts as a transcriptional modulator and a copper chaperone. Connectivity Map analyses show it shifts ~4,000 genes by ≥50%, turning up collagen, antioxidant and DNA-repair programs rather than flipping one receptor.

use.q: Why is topical the evidenced route and injectable the grey one?
use.a: Small controlled skin trials support topical anti-ageing; the systemic gene-reset claims are cell- and animal-only, and injectable copper carries real toxicity risk and is unapproved.

evidence.q: How strong is the evidence, really?
evidence.a: Best-evidenced cosmetic peptide for skin — small controlled trials plus strong mechanism — but systemic anti-ageing rests on cell-culture and animal data, not human outcomes.

canExplain: Why GHK-Cu has no receptor yet still changes thousands of genes
Why the copper attached to it is the active payload, not just a label
Why its skin evidence is genuinely strong while its whole-body anti-ageing evidence isn't
Why topical is sold OTC while injectable is grey-market and riskier
Why 'more' isn't better — the copper-toxicity ceiling

selfTest.q: A friend says GHK-Cu 'resets 4,000 genes to reverse ageing, so just inject it.' Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) The ~4,000-gene figure comes from cell-based Connectivity Map analysis and animal models, not a human anti-ageing outcome trial — the human evidence is essentially topical-skin only. (2) Injected copper is toxic and pro-oxidant in excess, and the injectable product is unapproved and grey-market, so you're taking real risk for an unproven systemic claim.

selfTest.q: Why can't we quote a receptor Ki for GHK-Cu the way we do for a drug?
selfTest.a: It doesn't bind a receptor — it's a gene-expression modulator and copper chaperone. The meaningful binding number is its copper-affinity constant (log β ≈ 16.4), not a receptor Ki.

selfTest.q: Your body already makes GHK — so why would adding more do anything?
selfTest.a: Plasma GHK falls from ~200 to ~80 ng/ml between ages 20 and 60, and injury releases it locally from matrix proteins. Topping it up topically restores a repair signal that has declined — but that's demonstrated for skin, not proven for systemic ageing.

evi.howStrong: For a cosmetic peptide, GHK-Cu has the strongest independent evidence — but that's a modest bar. It rests on small, short (roughly 8–12 week), often industry-funded controlled topical studies (the most-cited was a conference presentation, not a peer-reviewed trial), plus a large body of mechanistic cell-culture and animal work. There are no large, long-term human trials, and essentially none for systemic or injectable anti-ageing.
evi.whatItShows: Applied topically, it improves skin density, firmness and the appearance of fine lines, and supports wound healing — consistent with its collagen-and-matrix mechanism. The headline 'resets thousands of genes' and whole-body regeneration claims come from cells and animals, not human outcomes.
evi.effect: Modest and gradual: visible skin improvement over weeks, not a dramatic transformation. The honest framing is a real but small cosmetic effect on ageing skin.
evi.whoBenefits: Ageing or photoaged skin (topical) shows the clearest signal. People using it for systemic longevity are running on mechanism and hope, not human outcome data.
evi.caveats: Small samples, short durations, frequent industry funding, some evidence only from conference presentations, and a wide translation gap between petri-dish gene data and benefit in a living human. Copper toxicity is the safety ceiling — excess copper is pro-oxidant. Injectable and systemic use is unapproved and largely unstudied for safety.

trials.finding: Genomic (Broad Institute Connectivity Map) analysis: GHK shifts on the order of ~4,000 human genes by ≥50% (~31% of the genes analysed), upregulating collagen, antioxidant and DNA-repair programs and modulating TGF-β and metalloproteinases — the mechanistic basis of the 'gene-reset' claim (cell-based, not a human outcome trial).
trials.ref: Pickart & Margolina, 2018, Int J Mol Sci 19(7):1987

trials.finding: Controlled topical facial-cream study in ~71 photoaged women (twice daily, 12 weeks): improved skin density and thickness and reduced fine lines and laxity versus control — supportive human skin evidence, but small, industry-sponsored, and presented at a conference rather than peer-reviewed.
