trials.ref: Leyden et al., 2002 (presented at Am. Acad. Dermatology; not peer-reviewed)

tech.chem: Glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine (GHK), a naturally occurring tripeptide first isolated from human plasma albumin by Loren Pickart in 1973 (sequence confirmed as glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine in 1977). The physiologically active species is the copper(II) complex, GHK-Cu (INCI name: copper tripeptide-1); the free tripeptide is PubChem CID 73587880. It binds Cu²⁺ with very high affinity (stability constant log β ≈ 16.4), marginally above human serum albumin (~16.2) — enough to acquire copper from it. Small and water-soluble; present in plasma, saliva and urine, and released locally from extracellular-matrix proteins such as collagen during tissue injury. Plasma levels fall with age (~200 ng/ml at 20 → ~80 ng/ml at 60).

targets.action: high-affinity chelation and cellular delivery (copper chaperone)
targets.affinity: stability constant log β ≈ 16.4 — marginally above albumin's ~16.2, enough to acquire copper from it
targets.role: the active payload; copper is a cofactor for lysyl oxidase (collagen cross-linking), superoxide dismutase and other repair/antioxidant enzymes. Not a receptor — a carrier function.

targets.action: broad transcriptional modulation (not receptor-mediated)
targets.affinity: no binding constant — acts as a signalling peptide/copper complex, not a ligand at a defined receptor
targets.role: Broad Institute Connectivity Map analyses report ~4,000 human genes shifted ≥50% (~31% of those analysed), upregulating collagen, decorin, antioxidant and DNA-repair genes and modulating TGF-β and metalloproteinases.

targets.action: direct stimulation of matrix synthesis and remodelling
targets.role: drives fibroblasts toward collagen, elastin, glycosaminoglycan and decorin production, and rebalances matrix metalloproteinases with their inhibitors.

tech.signaling: There is no receptor cascade. Two intertwined routes drive the effect. (1) Copper delivery: GHK acts as a chaperone, handing Cu²⁺ to cuproenzymes such as lysyl oxidase and superoxide dismutase, supporting collagen cross-linking and antioxidant defence — while keeping free, toxic copper from running loose. (2) Transcriptional modulation: the GHK-Cu complex shifts a large gene panel toward tissue-repair, antioxidant and DNA-repair programs (per Connectivity Map analysis). Downstream, it stimulates collagen, elastin, glycosaminoglycans and decorin and rebalances metalloproteinases and their TIMP inhibitors, with TGF-β modulation.

adme.absorb: Topically it penetrates skin and reaches dermal fibroblasts — the studied route. Oral peptide is degraded and not the evidenced route.
adme.distribute: Endogenously present in plasma (~200 ng/ml at 20, ~80 at 60), saliva and urine; released locally from matrix proteins at injury sites. Systemic distribution of exogenous doses is not well characterised in humans.
adme.metabolise: As a peptide it is broken down by peptidases to its constituent amino acids, releasing copper into the cellular pool.
adme.excrete: Handled through normal amino-acid and copper metabolism. A human elimination half-life for exogenous GHK-Cu is not established — a genuine data gap, so none is quoted.

tech.pgx: No validated pharmacogenetic marker specific to GHK-Cu. Baseline copper status and copper-transport genetics (e.g. ATP7A/ATP7B; disorders such as Wilson's and Menkes disease affect copper handling generally) plausibly influence the copper-dependent effects, but response to GHK-Cu itself has not been characterised — individual variation should be treated as unknown.
tech.dose: No approved therapeutic dose exists. Cosmetic topical serums commonly contain roughly 0.1–2% GHK-Cu — a description of the market, not a recommendation. Injectable/systemic protocols are grey-market and unvalidated for safety, and excess copper is toxic.
tech.tolerance: No receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense — there is no receptor to desensitise. The practical ceiling is copper load: because copper is redox-active, more is not better, and excess becomes pro-oxidant and toxic. This is a safety ceiling, not a 'cycling' story.

contrasts.title: GHK-Cu vs a retinoid — no receptor vs a defined one
contrasts.point: Both remodel ageing skin by changing which genes fibroblasts run — but through completely different machinery. A retinoid works through a defined nuclear receptor (RAR/RXR): the molecule binds it, and that receptor drives transcription. GHK-Cu has no receptor at all — it delivers copper and broadly modulates gene expression as a signalling complex. Same visible goal (more collagen, firmer skin), but one acts through a single named lock and the other has no lock to name — which is exactly why you can quote a retinoid's target but only a copper-binding constant for GHK-Cu.

questions: How can a molecule just three amino acids long do anything at all — when most tiny peptides get shredded by your enzymes in seconds?
α-MSH is the hormone that darkens your skin — so why doesn't KPV, a piece of it, change your pigment?
If it's such a clean anti-inflammatory, why has it never been through a single human trial for the gut disease it was built for?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) It doesn't dock a receptor from outside — it hitches a ride through the PepT1 peptide transporter straight into the cell and works from within; yes it's fragile, which is exactly why oral and systemic delivery is a formulation headache. (2) The pigment effect lives in the receptor-binding CORE of α-MSH that KPV lacks — KPV is only the C-terminal tail, so it keeps the anti-inflammatory activity and drops the tanning. (3) Because the evidence is almost entirely mouse colitis and cell culture (Dalmasso 2008; Kannengiesser 2008; Getting 2003) — mechanistically clean, but no randomised human trial exists, and that gap is where most candidate drugs die.

kpv.bigIdea: KPV is the anti-inflammatory tail snapped off the hormone α-MSH — small enough to be smuggled inside a cell, where it jams the master inflammation switch NF-κB, without the pigment-changing baggage of the full hormone.
kpv.analogy: α-MSH is a Swiss Army knife with several blades — one darkens skin, one nudges appetite, one calms inflammation. KPV is just the anti-inflammatory blade snapped off: small enough to be carried directly into a cell, where it jams the inflammation alarm at its source.

mechSteps.t: Snap the tail off a hormone
mechSteps.predict: The full α-MSH hormone both darkens skin AND calms inflammation. If you keep only its last three amino acids, which job survives?
mechSteps.d: α-MSH is a 13-amino-acid hormone your pituitary cuts from a larger precursor, POMC. KPV is just its final three residues — lysine, proline, valine. The anti-inflammatory activity lives in this tail; the pigment-changing activity does not.

mechSteps.t: Smuggled inside by a transporter
mechSteps.predict: It's too small to be a classic receptor drug and too fragile to last long. So how does it get where it needs to act?
mechSteps.d: Instead of docking a receptor from outside, KPV is carried bodily into gut-lining and immune cells by PepT1 (SLC15A1) — a proton-powered peptide pump, the same one your gut uses to absorb dietary di- and tripeptides. That dependence is why the gut is its home turf.

mechSteps.t: It jams the master inflammation switch
mechSteps.predict: Inside the cell sits one transcription factor that turns on most inflammatory genes at once. Block it — what happens to TNF-α, IL-6 and IL-1β?
mechSteps.d: KPV interferes with the IKK/IκB step, keeping NF-κB trapped in the cytoplasm and out of the nucleus — so the genes for TNF-α, IL-1β and IL-6 never get switched on. It also dampens MAP-kinase signalling.

mechSteps.t: It skips the melanocortin receptors
mechSteps.predict: The parent hormone works by binding the MC1R receptor. Does KPV need that receptor too?
mechSteps.d: For its anti-inflammatory effect, KPV works largely independently of melanocortin receptors (Getting 2003) — it acts intracellularly after PepT1 uptake, not by switching on MC1R/MC3R the way full α-MSH does. That's why it calms inflammation without darkening skin.

framework.target: Intracellular NF-κB (and MAP-kinase) inflammatory signalling — not a receptor. KPV's action is reached only after the PepT1 transporter carries it inside the cell. Unlike its parent α-MSH, it does not primarily work by switching a melanocortin receptor on or off.
framework.affinity: There's no clean receptor Ki to quote, because the anti-inflammatory action is receptor-independent — it's an intracellular effect, not a lock-and-key binding you can score with a dissociation constant. What's reported instead is potency in cell models: nanomolar concentrations (around 10 nM) suppress NF-κB activation. Honest framing — KPV is a substrate that acts inside the cell, not a ligand you can rank by affinity.
framework.adme: Enters cells via PepT1 (SLC15A1), a proton-coupled di/tripeptide transporter enriched in gut epithelium and immune cells. As a bare tripeptide it's vulnerable to peptidases, so oral and systemic delivery of intact KPV is a real formulation challenge — its natural strength is local action at the gut lining. No verified human elimination half-life exists; figures on vendor sites are not from primary literature, so treat any specific number with suspicion.
framework.genetics: No validated pharmacogenetic switch. Mechanistically, how much PepT1 (SLC15A1) a tissue expresses sets how much KPV it can take up, and PepT1 is upregulated in inflamed intestine — which may make inflamed tissue more receptive. But this has not been characterised as a personal-response predictor, so treat individual response as unknown.
framework.evidence: Weak-to-moderate and almost entirely preclinical. Real, repeatable anti-inflammatory effects in mouse colitis models and cell culture (Dalmasso 2008; Kannengiesser 2008; Getting 2003), but no randomised controlled human trial for IBD, skin, or anything else — and no regulatory approval.

journey.d: KPV is the C-terminal tail (Lys-Pro-Val) of α-MSH, a hormone your own pituitary cuts from POMC. As a supplement it's sold as an unapproved 'research peptide' — and gray-market products are frequently mislabeled or impure.

A bare tripeptide is fragile — gut and blood peptidases chew it up quickly, so plain oral or injected KPV doesn't survive well. This is why formulation (enteric coating, PepT1-targeting) matters more here than dose.

Where it does reach cells, PepT1 (SLC15A1) — a proton-powered peptide pump on gut-lining and immune cells — carries KPV bodily inside. The inflamed gut, which upregulates PepT1, is its natural target tissue.
Inside the cell, KPV blocks the IKK/NF-κB step and dampens MAP-kinase, so the genes for TNF-α, IL-1β and IL-6 stay switched off. Inflammatory signalling cools at its source.
As a small peptide it's hydrolysed back into its amino acids and cleared. There's no verified human half-life to quote — its effect is local and short-lived, not a long-residence drug.

myths.myth: KPV is basically α-MSH, so it must tan your skin or affect appetite.
myths.truth: It's only the C-terminal tail. The pigment and appetite effects live in the receptor-binding core that KPV lacks — KPV keeps the anti-inflammatory activity and drops the rest. That separation is the entire reason it's interesting.

myths.myth: It's a proven gut-healing treatment.
myths.truth: The human evidence is essentially absent. Every headline finding — colitis improvement, cytokine suppression — comes from mice and cell culture. There has never been a randomised controlled trial of KPV in people, so 'proven' is the wrong word; 'promising in animals' is right.

myths.myth: It's a supplement, so it's approved and quality-controlled.
myths.truth: No regulator has approved KPV for anything. It's sold as a 'research chemical,' and independent testing of gray-market peptides routinely finds wrong doses, impurities, or entirely different contents.

myths.myth: Being tiny and 'natural' makes it inherently safe.
myths.truth: Small and body-derived is not the same as studied-safe. There's no long-term human safety data, no established dose, and no monitoring standard — the safety picture is unknown, not established.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a 'clean,' receptor-independent anti-inflammatory for gut (IBD) and skin — the anti-inflammatory upside of α-MSH with none of the pigment or systemic hormone effects. The mechanism is genuinely elegant and the animal data is real.

**The evidence cost:** that upside is almost entirely mouse-and-dish. No randomised human trial exists for any indication, so you're extrapolating from colitis mice to yourself — a leap that fails far more often than it succeeds in drug development.
**The delivery cost:** a bare tripeptide is fragile and poorly bioavailable; whatever a gray-market vial contains, how much actually reaches the target intact is unknown and unvalidated.
**The product-quality cost:** sold as an unapproved 'research chemical,' KPV products are frequently mislabeled, underdosed, or contaminated — you often don't know what's really in the vial.

**The unknown-safety cost:** there's no established human dose, no long-term safety data, and no monitoring standard. 'No reported problems' mostly reflects that almost no one has studied it in people — absence of evidence, not evidence of safety.
**The honest bottom line:** this page exists to explain the pharmacology — a clever tripeptide that jams NF-κB from inside the cell — not to help you run a protocol.

start.q: In one line: what is KPV and where does it come from?
start.a: It's the C-terminal tripeptide (Lys-Pro-Val) of the hormone α-MSH — the anti-inflammatory tail snapped off, without the pigment-changing core.

how.q: KPV isn't a receptor drug — so how does it actually calm inflammation?
how.a: The PepT1 (SLC15A1) transporter carries it inside gut and immune cells, where it blocks the IKK/NF-κB step (and dampens MAP-kinase), so inflammatory-cytokine genes stay switched off.

use.q: Why is the gut its natural home turf rather than the whole body?
use.a: Because it depends on PepT1, a peptide transporter concentrated in the gut lining and upregulated in inflamed intestine — and because a bare tripeptide is too fragile to distribute well systemically.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, honestly?
evidence.a: Essentially nonexistent — the anti-inflammatory findings are from mouse colitis and cell culture (Dalmasso 2008; Kannengiesser 2008; Getting 2003). No randomised human trial, no regulatory approval.

canExplain: Why a three-amino-acid peptide can be biologically active at all — it rides the PepT1 transporter into the cell instead of docking a receptor from outside
Why KPV calms inflammation but doesn't change skin pigment, even though its parent α-MSH does both
What NF-κB is, and why blocking it shuts down many inflammatory genes at once
Why the gut is KPV's natural target tissue — its dependence on the PepT1 transporter
Why 'promising' is honest and 'proven' is not — the evidence is animal and in-vitro only

selfTest.q: A friend says KPV is 'just α-MSH, so it'll tan you AND kill inflammation.' Correct him.
selfTest.a: KPV is only the C-terminal tail of α-MSH. The tanning (and appetite) effects come from the receptor-binding core that KPV doesn't have; KPV keeps the anti-inflammatory activity and drops the pigment effect — that's the whole design.

selfTest.q: One anti-inflammatory blocks a single cytokine like TNF-α; KPV blocks NF-κB. Why might blocking NF-κB be broader?
selfTest.a: NF-κB is an upstream master transcription factor that switches on many inflammatory genes at once (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6). Blocking it dampens the whole program rather than a single downstream messenger — broader, though also less selective.

selfTest.q: Why does KPV's dependence on the PepT1 transporter make it more of a gut drug than a systemic one?
selfTest.a: PepT1 (SLC15A1) is concentrated in the intestinal lining and upregulated in inflamed gut, so that's where KPV is most efficiently taken up — and, being a fragile tripeptide, it doesn't survive well enough to act reliably body-wide.

evi.howStrong: KPV sits near the BOTTOM of the evidence pyramid for human use. What exists is genuinely good preclinical science — repeatable anti-inflammatory effects in two independent mouse colitis models and in cultured cells, with a well-worked-out mechanism (PepT1 uptake → NF-κB inhibition). But there is no randomised controlled trial in humans for IBD, skin, or any other indication, and no regulator has approved it. The honest summary: a mechanistically convincing animal-stage compound, not a proven human therapy.
evi.whatItShows: In mice and cells, KPV reduces intestinal inflammation — lower disease severity in chemically-induced colitis, less immune-cell infiltration, and suppressed pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6). What it has NOT shown is efficacy or safety in humans, because those studies simply haven't been done.
evi.effect: In the mouse studies the effect is clear and consistent — improved body-weight recovery, lower myeloperoxidase (a neutrophil marker), and reduced histological damage in DSS- and TNBS-induced colitis. But no human effect size exists to quote, so any percentage or dose response you see marketed is not backed by human trial data.
evi.whoBenefits: Studied only in animal models of gut inflammation. Use in people — for IBD, skin conditions, or general 'anti-inflammatory' purposes — is entirely unstudied and off any label, because there is no label.
evi.caveats: The whole evidence base is preclinical, so treat every claim as a hypothesis, not a result. Add poor tripeptide stability and bioavailability, no established human dose, no long-term safety data, and a gray-market supply chain where products are frequently mislabeled or contaminated. The gap between 'works in a colitis mouse' and 'helps a person' is where most candidate drugs die.

trials.finding: Nanomolar KPV (10 nM) is taken into intestinal epithelial and immune cells by the PepT1 (SLC15A1) transporter, where it inhibits NF-κB and MAP-kinase signalling; oral KPV reduced DSS- and TNBS-induced colitis in mice.
trials.ref: Dalmasso et al., 2008, Gastroenterology 134:166–178

trials.finding: The melanocortin-derived tripeptide KPV reduced inflammation across two murine colitis models — improving weight recovery and lowering myeloperoxidase and inflammatory infiltrate.
trials.ref: Kannengiesser et al., 2008, Inflammatory Bowel Diseases 14(3):324

trials.finding: KPV's anti-inflammatory effect is distinct from the core α-MSH peptides and appears melanocortin-receptor-independent (not blocked by an MC3/4-R antagonist in crystal-induced peritonitis).
trials.ref: Getting, Schiöth & Perretti, 2003, J Pharmacol Exp Ther 306:631–637

tech.chem: KPV is L-lysyl-L-prolyl-L-valine (Lys-Pro-Val), the C-terminal tripeptide (residues 11–13) of α-melanocyte-stimulating hormone, itself cleaved from pro-opiomelanocortin (POMC). It is a bare, unmodified tripeptide — no steroid or complex scaffold — which makes it small enough to be a PepT1 substrate but also proteolytically fragile. It retains the anti-inflammatory activity of the α-MSH C-terminus while lacking the core sequence responsible for melanocortin-receptor binding and pigmentation.

targets.action: proton-coupled peptide transporter — the uptake route, not an effector target
targets.affinity: a transporter substrate, not a receptor ligand — no Ki applies
targets.role: carries KPV into intestinal epithelial and immune cells; enriched in gut and upregulated in inflamed intestine, making the gut its primary site of action

targets.action: intracellular inhibition — blocks the IKK/IκB step, preventing NF-κB nuclear translocation
targets.affinity: acts intracellularly, not by receptor binding
targets.role: the master inflammatory transcription factor; suppressing it lowers TNF-α, IL-1β and IL-6 transcription

targets.action: parent molecule — KPV is its C-terminal fragment
targets.affinity: n/a
targets.role: source hormone; the melanocortin-receptor-binding core lies outside the KPV sequence, which is why KPV drops the pigment effect

tech.signaling: KPV's core action is intracellular and, for its anti-inflammatory effect, largely melanocortin-receptor-independent. After PepT1-mediated uptake, it interferes with the IKK complex so that IκBα is not degraded, keeping NF-κB sequestered in the cytoplasm and out of the nucleus — the genes it would otherwise switch on (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, adhesion molecules) stay quiet. It additionally dampens MAP-kinase (p38/ERK) inflammatory signalling. This contrasts with full-length α-MSH, whose classic anti-inflammatory route is MC1R/MC3R activation on immune cells raising cAMP.

adme.absorb: Taken into cells by the PepT1 (SLC15A1) di/tripeptide transporter rather than by passive diffusion; oral and systemic bioavailability of the unprotected tripeptide is limited by peptidase degradation, so local gut delivery is where it acts most reliably.
adme.distribute: Distributes to PepT1-expressing tissues, chiefly the intestinal epithelium and immune cells; systemic distribution of the intact peptide is poorly characterised.
adme.metabolise: As a natural tripeptide it is hydrolysed by peptidases into its constituent amino acids (lysine, proline, valine); no cytochrome-P450 pathway is involved.
adme.excrete: Cleared as amino acids and small fragments; no verified human elimination half-life exists — figures circulating on vendor sites are not from peer-reviewed pharmacokinetic studies.

tech.pgx: No validated pharmacogenetic marker. Mechanistically, PepT1 (SLC15A1) expression level sets how much KPV a tissue can take up, and PepT1 is upregulated in inflamed intestine — a plausible modifier of local response — but this has not been characterised as a personal-response predictor. Treat individual response as unknown.
tech.dose: No approved or trial-validated human dose exists. Preclinical work used defined concentrations (e.g. ~10 nM in cell studies; mg-scale oral dosing in mouse colitis models), which do not translate directly to a human regimen. Gray-market gut/skin protocols cite various injected or oral doses, none validated for safety or efficacy — this is a description, not a recommendation.
tech.tolerance: No receptor-downregulation tolerance is described, since the anti-inflammatory action is receptor-independent. There is no characterised tolerance, dependence, or withdrawal profile in humans — simply because the human studies to define one have not been done.

questions: It's a fragment of a thymus hormone — but your thymus shrivels after puberty, so what does injecting a piece of it actually do?
Why is it a prescription drug in 35+ countries yet the FDA has never approved it for anything?
How can the same peptide seem to help immune-exhausted sepsis patients and yet be linked to WORSE outcomes in severe COVID?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Thymosin alpha-1 isn't a hormone you're topping up — it's a 28-amino-acid signal that rings the immune system's innate alarm bells (TLR9/TLR2) on dendritic cells, which then re-school your T-cells. It retrains commanders that have gone quiet, it doesn't rebuild the organ. (2) Foreign approval rests mostly on modest hepatitis B/C trials; the FDA's higher bar was never met, so in the US it's an unapproved 'research' compound, not a slow-walked drug. (3) Because it tunes a DYSREGULATED immune system rather than blindly boosting — it helps where the immune response has collapsed (a marginal sepsis signal), but in severe COVID, where over-inflammation is already the problem, one large cohort tied its use to higher non-recovery. Same tuner, opposite starting states.

thymosin-alpha-1-zadaxin.bigIdea: Thymosin alpha-1 is not an immune 'booster' — it's an immune tuner: a natural peptide that re-activates the dendritic cells and T-cells that coordinate your response, which is why it helps a suppressed or exhausted immune system and has no clear job in a healthy, balanced one.
thymosin-alpha-1-zadaxin.analogy: Think of it as a coach for your immune system's officer corps — the dendritic cells and T-cells that give the orders. It doesn't hand you more soldiers or floor any accelerator. It walks into a barracks where the commanders have gone silent (immune exhaustion) and gets them issuing orders again. Bring that same coach into a unit that's already rioting from over-inflammation, and more shouting is the last thing you need — which is the whole story of why it can cut both ways.

mechSteps.t: It's a snipped-off piece of a thymus protein
mechSteps.predict: Your thymus makes a big precursor protein. Thymosin alpha-1 is just the front end of it — why would a 28-amino-acid fragment carry the signal?
mechSteps.d: Thymosin alpha-1 is the acetylated N-terminal 28 residues cleaved from prothymosin alpha, a 113-amino-acid nuclear protein. That small, natural peptide is the messenger; the acetyl cap on its front end is essential for its activity.

mechSteps.t: It rings the innate-immune alarm bells
mechSteps.predict: Instead of a hormone receptor, it trips the same sensors your body uses to detect bacterial and viral DNA. Which sensors — and on which cells?
mechSteps.d: It engages Toll-like receptors TLR9 and TLR2 on dendritic cells (the immune system's scouts). Through MyD88 signalling this makes the dendritic cells mature — as if they'd just spotted a real pathogen.

mechSteps.t: Matured dendritic cells re-school your T-cells
mechSteps.predict: Once the scouts are switched on, what do they do to the T-cell arm of the immune system?
mechSteps.d: The activated dendritic cells drive T-cell maturation and differentiation — pushing toward Th1 and cytotoxic (CD8) phenotypes — and lift interferon-gamma, IL-2 and NK-cell activity. It's the coordination layer of immunity being turned back up.

mechSteps.t: It restores balance, rather than blindly stimulating
mechSteps.predict: In a collapsed immune system, monocytes stop displaying their 'ready' flag (HLA-DR). What would re-tuning look like on the ground?
mechSteps.d: It helps restore the balance between regulatory and effector T-cells and — measurably in sepsis trials — raises monocyte HLA-DR expression, a marker of immune readiness. This tuning, not a one-way boost, is why context decides whether it helps or hurts.

framework.target: Toll-like receptors TLR9 and TLR2 on dendritic cells, plus direct promotion of T-cell (thymocyte) maturation. It activates these innate sensors — it doesn't block anything — kicking off dendritic-cell maturation and a downstream T-cell response.
framework.affinity: Here's the honest reframe: there is no clean nanomolar Ki to quote, because this isn't a classic lock-and-key drug. It's an endogenous immunomodulatory peptide, not a high-affinity single-receptor ligand — so don't expect a caffeine-style 'does the dose reach the target' number. What matters instead is the immune state it acts on: the same signal that helps a suppressed system does little in a balanced one.
framework.adme: It's a peptide, so a pill would just be digested — it's given by subcutaneous injection. Its plasma half-life is short, on the order of ~2 hours, and it's broken down by peptidases into amino acids. Crucially, the effect long outlasts the blood level, because what you're triggering is a multi-day immune cascade, not a drug sitting on a receptor.
framework.genetics: No validated pharmacogenetic switch exists for it. The real determinant of response is your baseline immunology — it has the clearest rationale in immune-suppressed or immune-exhausted states (chronic viral infection, sepsis-induced immunoparalysis, ageing), and little expected benefit in the immunocompetent.
framework.evidence: Moderate and uneven. Genuine (if modest) hepatitis B/C and vaccine-adjuvant trials underpin approval in ~35 countries; a large sepsis RCT (ETASS) showed only a marginal mortality signal; COVID data are mixed-to-negative; and the popular 'general immune boost for healthy people' use is essentially unstudied. No FDA approval for any indication.

journey.d: It's a peptide, so it can't survive your stomach — it's injected under the skin. (Note: outside its approved markets, most vials are unregulated gray-market product of unknown purity and sterility, going straight into your body.)
It circulates only briefly — a plasma half-life around 2 hours — before peptidases start chopping it up. The short blood life is why its effect can't be read like a stimulant's clearance curve.

It reaches dendritic cells and engages their TLR9/TLR2 sensors, driving them to mature as if they'd detected a pathogen. This is the true trigger point — the blood level is already fading while this cascade begins.

Matured dendritic cells drive T-cell differentiation toward Th1 and cytotoxic phenotypes and lift interferon-gamma, IL-2 and NK-cell activity. The coordination layer of immunity is turned back up over days.
Regulatory-versus-effector T-cell balance shifts and markers of immune readiness (like monocyte HLA-DR) rise. Whether this rebalancing helps depends entirely on where your immune system started.
The peptide itself is degraded to amino acids and cleared — it doesn't accumulate. What lingers is the immunological change it set in motion, not the molecule.

myths.myth: It's a general immune booster everyone should take for resilience.
myths.truth: It's a tuner, not a booster. Its rationale is correcting a suppressed or dysregulated immune system; in an already-balanced one there's no demonstrated benefit, and blindly pushing immune activity isn't automatically good — a large severe-COVID cohort even linked its use to worse outcomes.

myths.myth: Approved in 35+ countries means it's proven and the FDA is just behind.
myths.truth: The FDA has never approved it for any indication. Foreign approval rests largely on modest hepatitis trials and a lower regulatory bar — it isn't US-grade proof that was simply delayed.

myths.myth: It's a natural peptide, so it's basically a safe supplement.
myths.truth: Being natural doesn't make immune modulation harmless, and in the US it's an unapproved compound sold as a 'research chemical' — injected from unregulated vials of uncertain purity, not a vetted supplement.

myths.myth: It regrows or restores your shrunken thymus.
myths.truth: No. It's a signalling peptide that activates dendritic cells and T-cells; it doesn't rebuild the thymic organ or reverse its age-related involution.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the genuine, evidence-backed uses abroad are chronic hepatitis B/C and as a vaccine adjuvant; it's also used off-label as a cancer-therapy adjunct and, in the wellness world, for vague 'immune resilience' and longevity. The infection and adjuvant rationale is real; the healthy-person 'immune boost' pitch is not supported.
**The regulatory cost:** no FDA approval for anything. In the US it's sold as a research chemical, and self-injecting an unapproved immunomodulator sits well outside any monitored medical setting.

**The evidence honesty:** hepatitis and adjuvant data are modest; the pivotal severe-sepsis RCT (ETASS) showed only a marginal, borderline-significant mortality signal; COVID evidence is mixed and includes a large cohort associating it with worse outcomes in severe disease. Long-term safety in healthy people using it for physique or 'wellness' is essentially unstudied.
**The product-quality cost:** gray-market peptide vials are frequently of unknown purity, dose and sterility — and this one is injected, which raises the stakes of contamination.

**The mechanism caveat:** it re-tunes a dysregulated immune system, so there's no coherent reason to expect benefit in a healthy, balanced one — and modulating immunity carries a theoretical autoimmune/inflammatory risk. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a protocol.

start.q: In one line: is thymosin alpha-1 an immune booster?
start.a: No — it's an immune tuner. It re-activates dendritic cells and T-cells to correct a suppressed or dysregulated response, which is different from blindly turning immunity up.

how.q: What does it physically engage, and what happens next?
how.a: TLR9/TLR2 sensors on dendritic cells. That matures the dendritic cells, which then drive T-cell maturation and raise interferon-gamma, IL-2 and NK activity.

use.q: Why can't you read its ~2-hour half-life like a caffeine clearance curve?
use.a: Because the effect isn't the blood level — it's a multi-day immune cascade the peptide sets off. The molecule is long gone while the immunological change plays out.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, and where is it approved?
evidence.a: Moderate and uneven: modest hepatitis B/C and adjuvant trials underpin approval in ~35 countries, a big sepsis RCT showed only a marginal benefit, and COVID data are mixed-to-negative. The FDA has never approved it.

canExplain: Why a fragment of a thymus protein can act as an immune signal without being a hormone you're topping up
The exact sensors it triggers — TLR9/TLR2 on dendritic cells — and why it has no classical receptor Ki
Why it's a tuner, not a booster, and how the same drug can help sepsis yet be tied to worse severe-COVID outcomes
Why its short ~2-hour half-life tells you almost nothing about how the effect unfolds
Why it's a prescription drug in 35+ countries but an unapproved research compound in the US

selfTest.q: A friend wants to take it as a 'daily immune booster' while healthy. Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) It's a tuner, not a booster — its rationale is fixing a suppressed or dysregulated immune system, and there's no demonstrated benefit in a balanced one. (2) It's unapproved in the US, injected from unregulated vials, and blindly amplifying immunity isn't automatically safe — a large severe-COVID cohort tied its use to worse outcomes.

selfTest.q: Its plasma half-life is only ~2 hours, yet dosing is intermittent (e.g. twice weekly in hepatitis). How is that consistent?
selfTest.a: Because the effect isn't a blood-level phenomenon. The peptide briefly triggers dendritic-cell maturation and a downstream T-cell cascade that unfolds over days — so the immunological signal, not the circulating molecule, is what carries the effect.

selfTest.q: Why might it plausibly help in sepsis-induced immunoparalysis but backfire in hyperinflammatory severe COVID?
selfTest.a: In sepsis the immune system has collapsed (low monocyte HLA-DR), so re-activating dendritic cells and T-cells can restore a failed response. In severe COVID the problem is often excess inflammation, so pushing the same activation can worsen things — the ETASS sepsis signal versus the negative severe-COVID cohort is exactly this context dependence.

evi.howStrong: Uneven, and honest framing matters. Its strongest footing is chronic hepatitis B/C and use as a vaccine adjuvant — the basis for approval in ~35 countries — but those effects are modest and the trials are older and heterogeneous. In critical care, the standout is one sizeable randomised trial in severe sepsis (ETASS) whose mortality benefit was only borderline. COVID evidence is genuinely mixed, including a large cohort that found the opposite of benefit. And the popular 'immune resilience for healthy people' use has essentially no controlled evidence at all.
evi.whatItShows: Where it acts, it measurably shifts immune markers — most concretely, it raised monocyte HLA-DR (a readiness marker) in sepsis patients. What it has NOT robustly shown is a large, consistent survival or cure benefit that clears a US regulatory bar. It moves the immune dials; whether that translates into better hard outcomes depends heavily on the disease and the patient's immune state.
evi.effect: In the ETASS sepsis trial, 28-day mortality was 26.0% with thymosin alpha-1 versus 35.0% with control (relative risk ~0.74) — a meaningful-looking gap that was only borderline statistically significant (log-rank P=0.049; non-stratified 28-day P=0.062). That is the honest shape of the best acute-care evidence: suggestive, not definitive.
evi.whoBenefits: The coherent candidates are people with a suppressed or exhausted immune system — chronic viral infection, sepsis-induced immunoparalysis, and possibly the immune decline of ageing or cancer therapy. Healthy people seeking a general 'boost' are exactly the group with no supporting evidence and a plausible downside.
evi.caveats: Much of the trial base is from China and older hepatitis studies, with heterogeneous quality; the sepsis benefit is borderline and needs confirmation; and the severe-COVID cohort is a stark reminder that immune modulation can go the wrong way in the wrong context. None of this validates gray-market self-injection for physique or wellness.

trials.finding: ETASS RCT, 361 severe-sepsis patients: 28-day all-cause mortality 26.0% (Tα1) vs 35.0% (control), RR ~0.74 (95% CI 0.54–1.02); borderline significance (log-rank P=0.049; 28-day P=0.062), with greater improvement in monocyte HLA-DR at days 3 and 7.
trials.ref: Wu J et al., 2013, Critical Care 17(1):R8 (ETASS)

trials.finding: Multicenter cohort of 2,282 COVID-19 patients (306 treated): thymosin alpha-1 use was associated with a higher non-recovery rate (adjusted OR ~1.5 overall), especially in more severe disease (adjusted OR ~2.0 in patients with max SOFA ≥2).
trials.ref: Liu J et al., 2021, Frontiers in Immunology 12:673693

tech.chem: Thymosin alpha-1 (thymalfasin; brand Zadaxin) is a 28-amino-acid peptide (~3108 Da) with an acetylated N-terminal serine — the acetyl cap is required for activity. It is the N-terminal fragment of prothymosin alpha, a 113-residue nuclear polypeptide, released by cleavage (via the lysosomal asparaginyl endopeptidase legumain, which cuts the Asn28–Gly29 bond). Being a peptide, it is not orally bioavailable (it would be digested) and is administered subcutaneously.

targets.action: activator/agonist on dendritic cells → MyD88 signalling
targets.affinity: no classical small-molecule Ki; acts as an endogenous immunomodulatory peptide, not a high-affinity single-receptor ligand
targets.role: primary innate sensor engaged; drives dendritic-cell maturation

targets.action: co-engaged pattern-recognition receptor on myeloid dendritic cells
targets.affinity: not characterised as a defined-Ki drug target
targets.role: contributes to dendritic-cell activation and cytokine output

tech.signaling: Engagement of TLR9/TLR2 on plasmacytoid and myeloid dendritic cells signals through MyD88, with downstream IRF7/NF-κB and p38-MAPK activity, driving dendritic-cell maturation. Matured DCs then promote T-cell differentiation toward Th1/cytotoxic phenotypes, raise interferon-gamma and IL-2, activate NK cells, and engage IDO-related tolerogenic pathways — net effect a re-balancing of regulatory versus effector T-cells and restoration of markers such as monocyte HLA-DR in immunoparalysis. It is immunomodulation, not one-directional stimulation.

adme.absorb: Not orally bioavailable (peptide, degraded in the gut); given by subcutaneous injection.
adme.distribute: Distributes systemically to reach dendritic cells and lymphoid tissue; it is the immunological cascade, not tissue residence, that carries the effect.
adme.metabolise: Broken down by plasma and tissue peptidases into constituent amino acids; not dependent on hepatic CYP metabolism.
adme.excrete: Cleared as peptide fragments/amino acids; short plasma half-life on the order of ~2 hours, with no meaningful accumulation.

tech.pgx: No validated pharmacogenetic marker. Response is governed by baseline immune state rather than a single-gene switch — the clearest rationale is in immune-suppressed or immune-exhausted patients, with little expected effect in the immunocompetent.
tech.dose: Trial dosing has centred on the 1.6 mg subcutaneous unit: e.g. 1.6 mg twice daily (then once daily) in the severe-sepsis ETASS protocol, and roughly 1.6 mg twice weekly over months for chronic hepatitis B. These are the studied, monitored regimens under specific indications — no FDA-approved dose exists, and gray-market wellness use is off-label and unstandardised. This is description, not recommendation.
tech.tolerance: There is no receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense. As an intermittently dosed immunomodulator it doesn't produce classic tachyphylaxis; the relevant consideration is instead the direction of immune modulation in a given clinical context, which can be beneficial or harmful depending on the starting immune state.

questions: How can one small peptide punch holes in bacteria yet leave your own cells intact?
If it's your body's own natural antibiotic, why is it also blamed for psoriasis and rosacea?
Why can you inject vitamin D's worth of interest into it, yet swallowing LL-37 as a supplement does almost nothing?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) LL-37 is a positively charged (+6) peptide that is pulled onto the strongly negative surface of bacterial membranes and shreds them like a carpet; your own cell membranes are near-neutral and armoured with cholesterol, so the peptide barely sticks. (2) The very same DNA/RNA-binding stickiness that helps it clear infection lets it clump with your own released DNA in the skin and trip a false alarm (type I interferon), which is exactly the engine of psoriasis and rosacea flares. (3) It's a 37-amino-acid peptide with no oral survival and no receptor to sit on for hours — it's chewed up by proteases in minutes, which is why the only real human data comes from putting it directly on a wound, not from a capsule.

ll-37.bigIdea: LL-37 isn't a drug your body borrows — it's a peptide your body already makes, a single charged molecule that acts as both a physical antibiotic (tearing microbial membranes) and an immune megaphone (summoning and steering white blood cells) — and that same dual power is why it heals in one context and inflames in another.
ll-37.analogy: Think of LL-37 as a magnetic strip of tape that is sticky on one side and only clings to strongly negative surfaces. Bacterial membranes are coated in negative charge, so the tape slaps on and rips them open. Your own cells are nearly neutral and waxed with cholesterol, so the tape slides off. But that same stickiness has a dark side: drop the tape near your own spilled DNA and it clumps into a wad your immune system mistakes for an invader — the false alarm behind psoriasis.

mechSteps.t: Your body builds it from a stored pro-peptide
mechSteps.predict: LL-37 isn't floating around ready-made. Where does it come from, and what has to happen before it's active?
mechSteps.d: The CAMP gene makes an inactive pro-protein called hCAP18, stockpiled in neutrophils and epithelial cells. An enzyme (proteinase 3, or kallikrein-5 in skin) snips off the tail — a 37-residue peptide starting with two leucines, 'LL', hence LL-37.

mechSteps.t: Vitamin D turns up the supply
mechSteps.predict: One common nutrient is the master switch on the CAMP gene. Which — and why does deficiency blunt this defence?
mechSteps.d: Active vitamin D binds the vitamin D receptor, which sits on the CAMP gene's promoter and drives its transcription. This is why circulating LL-37 tracks vitamin D status, and why the peptide is a real mechanistic link between low vitamin D and weaker innate immunity.

mechSteps.t: It shreds microbial membranes by charge
mechSteps.predict: The killing isn't a lock-and-key. It's physics. What makes a bacterium a target and your own cell safe?
mechSteps.d: LL-37 carries a +6 charge and folds into an amphipathic helix. It's electrostatically pulled onto the anionic lipids that coat bacterial membranes, then inserts and disrupts them (a 'carpet'/toroidal-pore effect). Human membranes are near-neutral and cholesterol-rich, so it largely spares them — that charge difference IS the selectivity.

mechSteps.t: It doubles as an immune signal
mechSteps.predict: Beyond killing bugs directly, it talks to your white blood cells. Through what, and to what end?
mechSteps.d: LL-37 activates the receptor FPR2 (and engages P2X7, EGFR) to recruit and steer neutrophils, monocytes and T cells, promote wound closure and angiogenesis, and neutralise bacterial LPS before it can ignite a sepsis-level cytokine storm. This immunomodulation, not the raw killing, is much of its appeal for healing.

mechSteps.t: The same stickiness can misfire
mechSteps.predict: The property that makes it useful also makes it dangerous in the wrong tissue. What flips it from healer to instigator?
mechSteps.d: LL-37 binds nucleic acids. In inflamed skin it complexes with your own released DNA/RNA and shuttles it into immune sensors, triggering type I interferon and NLRP3 inflammasome activation. That is a validated driver of psoriasis and rosacea — the peptide becomes a self-antigen.

framework.target: Two targets, not one. (1) The microbial membrane itself — a physical, charge-driven target, not a protein. (2) Host immune receptors — chiefly FPR2 (a G-protein-coupled formyl-peptide receptor), plus P2X7 and EGFR — through which it steers inflammation, chemotaxis and wound repair.
framework.affinity: There is no single clean Ki to quote, and pretending otherwise would be false precision. LL-37's primary antimicrobial action is a bulk electrostatic membrane interaction governed by its +6 charge and lipid composition, not a defined binding pocket. Its receptor actions (FPR2 etc.) are real but reported across varied assays without one agreed affinity constant. Honest framing: this is a multi-target peptide, not a single-receptor ligand.
framework.adme: It's a 37-amino-acid peptide, so the pharmacokinetics that matter are a peptide's, not a small molecule's: no meaningful oral bioavailability (digested to fragments), and rapid proteolytic degradation in tissue and blood. This is precisely why every credible human study delivers it locally to a wound, not systemically or by mouth. No reliable human systemic elimination half-life is established.
framework.genetics: No validated pharmacogenetic switch. The dominant modifier of your own LL-37 is vitamin D status (VDR-driven CAMP transcription), plus the local proteases that activate hCAP18. Skin conditions like psoriasis and rosacea show pathologically elevated LL-37 — dysregulation, not a dosing target.
framework.evidence: Thin, and on the best test negative. A small first-in-man Phase I/II trial of topical LL-37 on hard-to-heal venous leg ulcers showed an encouraging healing signal, but the larger, better-powered Phase IIb randomised trial did not meet its primary efficacy endpoint (no significant benefit over placebo overall). No regulatory approval for any indication, and essentially no human data supporting LL-37 as a systemic supplement.

journey.d: It starts as code. The CAMP gene — dialled up by vitamin D — builds an inactive pro-protein, hCAP18, stored inside your neutrophils and skin cells until needed.
At a site of infection or injury, an enzyme (proteinase 3, or kallikrein-5 in skin) snips hCAP18. The freed 37-residue tail folds into a charged helix — now it's active LL-37.

Drawn by charge onto the negatively charged surface of a bacterium, it inserts and disrupts the membrane. It also grabs LPS, defusing the molecule that would otherwise trigger a runaway inflammatory response.
Via FPR2 and other receptors it recruits and directs white blood cells, nudges new blood vessels to grow, and pushes skin cells to migrate and close a wound. This is the healing/immunomodulatory job.
In chronically inflamed skin it binds your own spilled DNA/RNA into complexes that trip interferon and inflammasome alarms — turning a defender into a driver of psoriasis and rosacea.

As a peptide it's short-lived: proteases chop it into inactive fragments. There's no long circulating tail — which is exactly why swallowing it or expecting a systemic effect makes little pharmacological sense.

myths.myth: It's a natural antibiotic, so it must be safe to supplement.
myths.truth: 'Natural' and 'made by your body' don't mean safe as an add-on. LL-37 is a validated psoriasis autoantigen, drives rosacea-like inflammation via the NLRP3 inflammasome, and has documented pro-tumorigenic activity in some cancers. More LL-37 in the wrong tissue is not obviously better.

myths.myth: You can take LL-37 as an oral supplement to boost immunity.
myths.truth: It's a peptide with no meaningful oral bioavailability — your gut digests it into fragments. The only credible human evidence uses it applied directly to a wound. If you want to raise your own LL-37, the evidence-based lever is correcting vitamin D deficiency, not swallowing the peptide.

myths.myth: Bacteria can't develop resistance to a membrane-disrupting peptide.
myths.truth: Resistance is slower than to a single-target antibiotic, because remodelling a whole membrane is hard — but it's not impossible. Bacteria can reduce their surface negative charge, secrete proteases, or shed decoy molecules to blunt LL-37. 'Harder to resist' is the honest claim, not 'resistance-proof'.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a 'natural, body-made antibiotic that also speeds wound healing' without fostering classical antibiotic resistance. The membrane-killing and immunomodulatory biology is genuinely real and well-characterised — that's the legitimate draw.

**The evidence reality:** the best human data is topical, on chronic venous leg ulcers — a small Phase I/II trial showed an encouraging healing signal, but the larger, better-powered Phase IIb randomised trial failed its primary healing endpoint. Nothing is approved, and there is essentially no human evidence for LL-37 as a systemic or oral supplement.

**The auto-inflammation cost:** LL-37 is a validated psoriasis autoantigen and a driver of rosacea-like inflammation through inflammasome activation and self-nucleic-acid sensing. Adding it, especially in or on inflamed skin, can plausibly feed the very fires it's marketed to calm.

**The cancer signal:** in several cancer types (breast, ovarian, lung) LL-37 has shown pro-tumorigenic effects in laboratory and animal work — a real reason for caution that the supplement marketing omits.

**The product-quality cost:** peptides sold as 'research chemicals' are frequently mislabeled, underdosed, or contaminated, and there's no dosing regimen validated for safety in healthy people. You often don't know what's actually in the vial.
**The honest bottom line:** interesting antimicrobial and wound-healing biology, minimal human supplement data. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a protocol.

start.q: In one line: what IS LL-37, and where does it come from?
start.a: It's a 37-amino-acid antimicrobial peptide your own body makes — encoded by the CAMP gene as the pro-protein hCAP18, then cut into its active form by enzymes at sites of infection or injury.

how.q: How does LL-37 kill a bacterium without killing your own cells?
how.a: By charge. It's +6 and is electrostatically pulled onto the strongly anionic bacterial membrane, which it inserts into and disrupts. Your own membranes are near-neutral and cholesterol-rich, so it barely binds them — the charge difference is the selectivity.

use.q: Why is LL-37 studied as a topical wound treatment rather than a pill?
use.a: It's a peptide: no meaningful oral bioavailability (it's digested) and rapid proteolytic breakdown, so it can't act systemically from a capsule. Delivering it directly onto a wound is the only route with credible human data.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, honestly?
evidence.a: Thin. A small first-in-man Phase I/II trial of topical LL-37 on venous leg ulcers showed encouraging healing, but the larger Phase IIb randomised trial did NOT meet its primary endpoint. Nothing is approved, and there's essentially no human evidence for supplemental or systemic use.

canExplain: Why a positively charged peptide can rupture bacteria yet spare your own cells
How your body builds LL-37 from the CAMP gene — and why vitamin D turns the supply up
The difference between its two jobs: physically killing microbes vs. steering the immune system through FPR2
Why the same DNA-binding stickiness that helps it fight infection makes it a driver of psoriasis and rosacea
Why it's tested as a topical wound treatment and why an oral 'immune-boost' supplement makes little pharmacological sense

selfTest.q: A friend with psoriasis reads that LL-37 is a 'natural antibiotic' and wants to try a topical version to improve his skin. Give the two-part caution.
selfTest.a: (1) LL-37 is a validated psoriasis autoantigen — it complexes with self-DNA/RNA and triggers type I interferon and inflammasome activation, which is a core driver of psoriatic and rosacea inflammation, so adding it to inflamed skin could worsen exactly what he's treating. (2) There's no approved product or safety-validated regimen; the only credible human data is on chronic leg ulcers, not psoriatic skin — and even there the larger trial failed its primary endpoint.

selfTest.q: Why does raising your vitamin D do more for your own LL-37 than swallowing an LL-37 supplement would?
selfTest.a: Vitamin D, via the vitamin D receptor, drives transcription of the CAMP gene, so your cells actually make more endogenous LL-37 where it belongs. A swallowed peptide is digested into inactive fragments with no meaningful oral bioavailability, so it never arrives intact.

selfTest.q: Membrane-disrupting peptides are said to resist resistance. Why is that only partly true?
selfTest.a: Remodelling an entire membrane is harder for bacteria than mutating one drug target, so resistance evolves more slowly — but bacteria can still lower their surface negative charge, secrete proteases to degrade the peptide, or release decoys, so LL-37 is 'harder to resist,' not resistance-proof.

evi.howStrong: Weak for humans, and it's important to be blunt about this. LL-37's cellular and animal biology is extensively characterised — membrane disruption, FPR2 signalling, LPS neutralisation, roles in psoriasis and rosacea are all well-supported. But controlled human efficacy data is confined to topical wound-healing trials, and the pattern there is a small early positive followed by a larger negative: the confirmatory Phase IIb trial did not meet its primary endpoint. There is no approved indication and essentially no human trial of LL-37 as a systemic or oral supplement. Treat the mechanism as strong and the human clinical proof as early and unconfirmed.
evi.whatItShows: Where it's been tested properly — applied directly to chronic venous leg ulcers — a small first-in-man trial showed a signal toward faster healing versus placebo, but a larger, better-powered trial did not reproduce a significant benefit on its primary endpoint. What has NOT been demonstrated in humans is any benefit from taking it as a supplement, any systemic antimicrobial use, or long-term safety.
evi.effect: In the first-in-man Phase I/II trial (Grönberg 2014, 34 patients) the two lower concentrations produced healing-rate constants several-fold higher than placebo — roughly six-fold for 0.5 mg/mL (p=0.003) and three-fold for 1.6 mg/mL (p=0.088). But the larger Phase IIb trial (Mahlapuu 2021, 148 patients) failed its primary endpoint: no significant healing benefit over placebo overall, with only a secondary-endpoint numeric edge for the low dose (about 62% vs 33% of ulcers reaching ≥50% area reduction). The honest read is an early, unconfirmed local signal — not an established effect.
evi.whoBenefits: The studied population is people with hard-to-heal chronic wounds (venous leg ulcers), where boosting local immune and repair signalling is the goal. Its use by healthy people for general 'immunity' or physique is entirely off-label, unstudied, and unsupported by human data.
evi.caveats: The human evidence is small, topical-only, unapproved, and negative on its largest test. Against even a hoped-for benefit sit real hazards: LL-37 drives psoriasis and rosacea inflammation, shows pro-tumorigenic activity in some cancers, and gray-market peptide products are frequently mislabeled or contaminated. Cell/animal findings do not guarantee human benefit, and 'made by your body' does not mean safe to add.

trials.finding: First-in-man Phase I/II randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 34 patients with hard-to-heal venous leg ulcers (0.5, 1.6, 3.2 mg/mL vs placebo, twice weekly): topical LL-37 was safe, and the 0.5 and 1.6 mg/mL groups showed healing-rate constants roughly six- and three-fold higher than placebo (significant for 0.5 mg/mL, p=0.003; p=0.088 for 1.6 mg/mL).
trials.ref: Grönberg et al., 2014, Wound Repair and Regeneration

trials.finding: Phase IIb multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (148 patients) of topical LL-37 (0.5 and 1.6 mg/mL vs placebo) for hard-to-heal venous leg ulcers: did NOT meet its primary efficacy endpoint — no significant improvement in healing versus placebo overall. A secondary endpoint numerically favoured the 0.5 mg/mL dose (about 62% vs 33% of ulcers reaching ≥50% area reduction). No approval.
trials.ref: Mahlapuu et al., 2021, Wound Repair and Regeneration

tech.chem: LL-37 is the sole human cathelicidin: a 37-residue, C-terminal cationic host-defence peptide (net charge ≈ +6 at physiological pH) named for its two N-terminal leucines. It is generated by extracellular proteolytic cleavage of the pro-protein hCAP18 (the product of the CAMP gene) — chiefly by proteinase 3 in neutrophils and by kallikrein-5/-7 in skin. In membrane or anionic environments it adopts an amphipathic α-helix, segregating hydrophobic and cationic faces; this amphipathicity, not a defined binding pocket, underlies its membrane activity.

targets.action: source gene/pro-protein — cleaved to release active LL-37
targets.role: the origin of the peptide; vitamin-D/VDR-inducible, the physiological control point on LL-37 levels

targets.action: electrostatic adsorption then insertion → membrane permeabilisation (carpet/toroidal-pore models)
targets.role: the primary antimicrobial 'target' — a physical, charge-driven interaction, not a protein receptor; selectivity comes from bacterial anionic lipids vs cholesterol-rich zwitterionic host membranes

targets.action: agonist at a G-protein-coupled formyl-peptide receptor
targets.role: chemotaxis of neutrophils, monocytes and T cells; a central node of LL-37's immunomodulatory and wound-healing effects

targets.action: engagement modulating inflammasome and proliferation/migration signalling
targets.role: contribute to keratinocyte migration, wound closure and inflammasome regulation

tech.signaling: LL-37 is a multi-pathway peptide rather than a single-receptor ligand. Antimicrobially it acts by direct membrane disruption and by binding/neutralising LPS, blunting TLR4-driven cytokine release. Immunologically it signals through FPR2 (and P2X7, EGFR) to activate PI3K/Akt and MAPK/Erk cascades, driving chemotaxis, angiogenesis and re-epithelialisation. Its pathological face runs through nucleic-acid sensing: LL-37–self-DNA/RNA complexes activate endosomal TLR9/TLR7 in plasmacytoid dendritic cells to produce type I interferon, and it promotes NLRP3 inflammasome assembly — the mechanistic basis of its role as a psoriasis autoantigen and a rosacea inflammatory mediator.

adme.absorb: A 37-mer peptide with no meaningful oral bioavailability (digested to fragments); delivered topically/locally in human trials.
adme.distribute: Acts locally at sites of infection, injury and inflammation; binds avidly to anionic surfaces, LPS and nucleic acids, which shapes where it concentrates.
adme.metabolise: Degraded by host and microbial proteases; activity and half-life are strongly modulated by the local protease and lipid environment.
adme.excrete: Cleared as proteolytic peptide fragments; no established human systemic elimination half-life, consistent with its short-lived, locally-acting profile.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. Endogenous LL-37 is governed chiefly by vitamin-D status via VDR-driven CAMP transcription (circulating hCAP18/LL-37 correlates with 25-hydroxyvitamin D), and by the local proteases that activate hCAP18. Pathologically elevated LL-37 characterises psoriasis and rosacea lesions.
tech.dose: No approved therapeutic dose exists. Human trials used topical wound application at 0.5 and 1.6 mg/mL (with 3.2 mg/mL in the earlier study), applied locally to venous leg ulcers under monitoring — a description of trial protocols, not a recommendation. Gray-market injectable/oral 'peptide' use is unstudied for safety and has no validated regimen.
tech.tolerance: Not a receptor-tolerance (tachyphylaxis) story. The time-dependent concerns are different: potential microbial resistance via surface-charge remodelling and protease secretion, and the auto-inflammatory/pro-tumorigenic liabilities of sustained high LL-37 in the wrong tissue.

questions: Why does a drug invented to shut down the immune system of transplant patients get taken by healthy people trying to live longer?
Why do longevity doctors dose it once a week, when transplant patients take it every single day?
If it extends lifespan in every animal ever tested, why can't anyone yet say it works in humans?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) The same brake that slows immune cells also eases the master 'grow' switch (mTORC1) in every cell — dial it down periodically and cells flip from growth into clean-and-repair mode (autophagy), the state linked to longer life. (2) Rapamycin's target isn't mTOR alone but a complex; daily dosing eventually jams a second complex, mTORC2, which drives insulin resistance — weekly dosing inhibits mTORC1 while sparing mTORC2, keeping the benefit and dropping much of the metabolic cost. (3) It is the single most reproducible lifespan-extending drug in animals (the NIA ITP replicated it across three sites), but the human longevity data is still early — the PEARL trial showed a year of low-dose weekly rapamycin is safe, not that it extends human life.

rapamycin-sirolimus.bigIdea: Rapamycin doesn't repair you — it periodically flips the cell's master switch from 'grow' to 'maintain,' turning on the clean-up and stress-defence programs that growth normally keeps switched off. Hold onto that one sentence and everything else on this page follows from it.
rapamycin-sirolimus.stakes: It is a prescription medicine, not a supplement — approved to stop the body rejecting a transplanted kidney — yet it is also the single most reliable lifespan-extending drug science has ever found in animals, working in yeast, worms, flies, and mice alike. That combination is exactly why healthy people want it and exactly why it needs a doctor, not a self-experiment.
rapamycin-sirolimus.analogy: Think of mTOR as the accelerator pedal for cell growth — held down whenever fuel is around, it drives building and dividing but neglects maintenance. Rapamycin doesn't cut the fuel line; it briefly eases that pedal off, and in the lull the cell finally does its housekeeping: recycling worn-out parts (autophagy), shoring up stress defences, and dividing less recklessly. Take your foot off weekly, not constantly, and the engine gets serviced without stalling.

mechSteps.t: It hijacks a small adaptor protein first
mechSteps.predict: Rapamycin can't grab its real target directly — it needs a partner protein already sitting inside the cell. Why borrow a middleman instead of binding mTOR itself?
mechSteps.d: Rapamycin first binds a small intracellular protein called FKBP12. Alone it barely touches mTOR, but the rapamycin–FKBP12 pair becomes a new shape that docks into mTOR's FRB pocket roughly 2,000-fold more tightly than rapamycin could on its own — the reason it needs the adaptor.

mechSteps.t: The pair jams mTOR complex 1
mechSteps.predict: With FKBP12 in hand, rapamycin wedges into one of mTOR's two complexes. If mTORC1 is the cell's 'grow now' command centre, what happens to growth signalling when you jam it?
mechSteps.d: The rapamycin–FKBP12 complex slots into mTOR Complex 1 (mTORC1) and throttles it. mTORC1 is the hub that reads nutrients and growth factors and orders protein-building and cell division, so jamming it dials the whole 'grow' program down.

mechSteps.t: The cell flips to maintenance mode
mechSteps.predict: If the cell can no longer prioritise building and dividing, what does it do with the resources instead?
mechSteps.d: With growth throttled, the cell switches on the opposite program: autophagy (recycling damaged proteins and organelles), stress resistance, and reduced cellular senescence. This growth-to-maintenance flip is the state repeatedly tied to longer, healthier lifespan.

mechSteps.t: Weekly dosing spares the second complex
mechSteps.predict: There's a second complex, mTORC2, that rapamycin doesn't hit right away. What would happen to that clean 'benefit' if you dosed daily and eventually jammed it too?
mechSteps.d: Rapamycin doesn't bind mTOR Complex 2 (mTORC2) directly, but chronic daily dosing gradually blocks its assembly — and losing mTORC2 is what drives the insulin resistance seen in transplant patients. Intermittent weekly dosing inhibits mTORC1 while largely sparing mTORC2, which is the whole rationale for the longevity dosing schedule.

framework.target: mTOR Complex 1 (mTORC1) — but note the twist: rapamycin does not bind mTOR directly. It's an allosteric inhibitor that only works as a rapamycin–FKBP12 complex wedging into mTOR's FRB domain. Contrast the site's other molecules: caffeine blocks its receptor, semaglutide activates its receptor; rapamycin inhibits a kinase, but only with a borrowed adaptor protein.
framework.affinity: Genuinely high-affinity, and the numbers show why the adaptor matters. Rapamycin alone binds mTOR's FRB pocket only weakly (Kd ≈ 26 µM), but the rapamycin–FKBP12 complex binds it at Kd ≈ 12 nM — roughly 2,000-fold tighter (Choi/Banaszynski characterisation of the FKBP·rapamycin·FRB complex). It reaches its target at real clinical blood levels, unlike many 'targets' hit only at impossible doses.
framework.adme: Oral bioavailability is low (~14%) and erratic; heavily metabolised by liver CYP3A4 and pumped by intestinal P-glycoprotein (so grapefruit and CYP3A inhibitors sharply raise levels). It concentrates inside red blood cells, so it's monitored in whole blood, not plasma. Elimination half-life is long — roughly 60 hours — which is the pharmacological reason a once-weekly schedule is even possible.
framework.genetics: No single-gene on/off switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. Blood levels do vary with CYP3A4/CYP3A5 and ABCB1 (P-glycoprotein) genotype, which is why transplant medicine uses therapeutic drug monitoring rather than a fixed dose — but there is no validated pharmacogenetic test guiding longevity dosing.
framework.evidence: Split honestly by question. As a drug it has decades of human safety and efficacy data (transplant, LAM). As a longevity intervention, the animal evidence is the strongest of any compound — reproduced across independent labs by the NIA Interventions Testing Program — while the human longevity evidence is still early (PEARL showed one-year safety, not lifespan). 5/5 reflects the unmatched preclinical reproducibility plus a large human drug-safety record, not a proven human lifespan effect.

pk.onset: biological effects build over days to weeks, not minutes
pk.peak: blood level peaks ~1–3 h after an oral dose (tmax)
pk.halfLife: ~60 h (reported ~57–63 h; longer with liver impairment)
pk.duration: dosed weekly for longevity; daily for transplant
pk.note: The ~60-hour half-life is the key to the schedule. It's long enough that a single weekly dose transiently inhibits mTORC1 and then clears — hitting the 'grow' switch in pulses rather than pinning it down. That pulsing is precisely what's thought to spare mTORC2 and avoid the insulin resistance that constant daily exposure causes.

journey.d: Taken by mouth, but only about 14% actually gets into your blood — the gut wall's P-glycoprotein pumps much of it straight back out, and CYP3A enzymes start breaking it down before it's even absorbed.
What gets through largely hides inside red blood cells rather than floating in plasma — which is why doctors measure it in whole blood. It rides the circulation to every tissue.
Inside a cell it grabs the little adaptor protein FKBP12. Only as this pair can it wedge into mTOR Complex 1 and throttle the 'grow' command.
With mTORC1 quieted, the cell shifts resources from building to maintenance — autophagy ramps up, damaged parts get recycled, stress defences rise. This is the effect people are actually chasing.

CYP3A4 in the liver chops rapamycin into inactive metabolites. Anything that blocks CYP3A (grapefruit, certain antifungals and antibiotics) can send blood levels dangerously high — a real interaction, not a footnote.
Roughly 90% leaves in the stool as metabolites, with only a few percent in urine. Its long ~60-hour half-life means the tail lingers for days — the reason a weekly rhythm works.

myths.myth: It extends human lifespan — that's proven.
myths.truth: It's proven in animals, and reproducibly so (yeast, worms, flies, mice, across independent labs). In humans there is no lifespan trial and there cannot easily be one. The best human data so far — PEARL — shows a year of low-dose weekly use is reasonably safe, not that it makes people live longer.

myths.myth: It's an immunosuppressant, so it must weaken your immune system — the opposite of healthy ageing.
myths.truth: Dose and schedule change everything. High daily transplant doses do suppress immunity. But low intermittent mTOR inhibition did the reverse in older adults in trials of its cousin everolimus — improving flu-vaccine response and cutting infections. The immunosuppression is dose-dependent, not inevitable.

myths.myth: Daily dosing must work better than weekly.
myths.truth: For longevity it's likely worse. Daily exposure eventually blocks mTORC2 assembly, and that — not mTORC1 inhibition — is what drives rapamycin's insulin resistance. Intermittent weekly dosing keeps the mTORC1 benefit while largely sparing mTORC2 (shown directly in mouse work by Arriola Apelo and colleagues).

myths.myth: Rapamycin simply causes diabetes.
myths.truth: It can cause glucose intolerance at high, constant doses via mTORC2 loss — but that metabolic hit is at least partly uncoupled from the longevity benefit and is substantially reduced by intermittent dosing. It's a manageable, schedule-dependent effect, not an unavoidable one.

whenToUse.intro: How it's used clinically — this is prescription-only, and its longevity use is off-label and experimental, requiring a doctor to prescribe, dose, and monitor it. This is not a self-administration protocol:
items: **Preventing organ rejection (its core FDA approval):** given daily after kidney transplantation to suppress the immune attack on the new organ, with blood-level monitoring.
**Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM):** FDA-approved to slow this rare progressive lung disease driven by overactive mTOR signalling.
**Coronary stents and rare tumours:** sirolimus coats drug-eluting stents to prevent re-narrowing, and a nanoparticle form is approved for a rare mTOR-driven cancer (PEComa).

**Off-label longevity use:** taken as low-dose weekly rapamycin by some clinicians and biohackers to target healthspan — genuinely investigational, done under medical supervision, and not endorsed as a proven anti-ageing therapy.

**What a doctor screens and monitors:** infections and immunosuppression, blood lipids and glucose, mouth ulcers, wound healing, CYP3A drug/food interactions (e.g. grapefruit), and pregnancy — it is not for casual self-prescription.

start.q: In one line: does rapamycin repair cells, or change what the cell chooses to do?
start.a: It changes what the cell does. By throttling mTORC1 it flips the cell from 'grow' to 'maintain' — switching on autophagy and stress defences rather than repairing anything directly.

how.q: Rapamycin can't grab mTOR on its own — what does it do first, and which complex does it then inhibit?
how.a: It first binds the adaptor protein FKBP12; that pair then wedges into mTOR's FRB pocket and inhibits mTOR Complex 1 (mTORC1).

use.q: Why do longevity protocols use weekly dosing instead of daily?
use.a: Daily dosing eventually blocks mTORC2 assembly, which drives insulin resistance. Weekly (intermittent) dosing inhibits mTORC1 while largely sparing mTORC2 — keeping the benefit, dropping much of the metabolic cost. Its ~60-hour half-life makes the weekly rhythm feasible.

evidence.q: How strong is the longevity evidence — and where's the gap between animals and humans?
evidence.a: In animals it's the most reproducible lifespan drug known, replicated across independent labs (NIA ITP). In humans there's no lifespan trial; the best data (PEARL) shows one-year safety at low weekly doses, not extended life.

canExplain: Why rapamycin flips cells from growth to maintenance without directly repairing anything
Why it needs the adaptor protein FKBP12 before it can touch mTOR
The difference between mTORC1 and mTORC2 — and why that difference dictates weekly dosing
Why the animal lifespan evidence is overwhelming while the human evidence is still early
Who it's actually prescribed for clinically, and why longevity use is off-label and experimental

selfTest.q: Rapamycin barely binds mTOR by itself (Kd ≈ 26 µM) yet is a potent mTORC1 inhibitor at clinical doses. Resolve the paradox.
selfTest.a: It doesn't act alone. It first binds FKBP12, and the rapamycin–FKBP12 complex binds mTOR's FRB domain about 2,000-fold more tightly (Kd ≈ 12 nM). The adaptor turns a weak binder into a high-affinity inhibitor.

selfTest.q: A transplant patient on daily rapamycin develops high fasting glucose and insulin; a biohacker on weekly rapamycin doesn't. What mechanistic difference explains it?
selfTest.a: Chronic daily exposure blocks mTORC2 assembly, and mTORC2 loss is what drives rapamycin's insulin resistance. Weekly dosing inhibits mTORC1 while largely sparing mTORC2, so the metabolic disruption is avoided.

selfTest.q: Rapamycin is an immunosuppressant, yet a related mTOR inhibitor improved vaccine responses in older adults. How can both be true?
selfTest.a: The effect is dose- and schedule-dependent. High continuous doses suppress immunity; low intermittent mTOR inhibition instead appeared to rejuvenate aspects of immune function (Mannick's everolimus trials in the elderly). Same pathway, opposite outcome depending on how hard and how often you hit it.

evi.howStrong: Two very different evidence bases sit under this one drug. As a medicine, rapamycin has decades of large-scale human use with well-characterised safety in transplantation. As a longevity intervention, the animal evidence is the strongest of any compound on this site — lifespan extension in yeast, worms, flies and mice, and critically reproduced across independent laboratories by the NIA Interventions Testing Program (the gold standard for filtering out lab-specific flukes). The honest gap: there is no human lifespan trial, and healthspan trials in healthy people are only just beginning.
evi.whatItShows: In mammals, rapamycin reliably extends median and maximum lifespan and delays multiple age-related diseases — even when started in late life. In humans, the signal so far is about safety and immune function rather than longevity: low intermittent mTOR inhibition improved vaccination responses and reduced infections in older adults, and one year of low-dose weekly rapamycin was tolerated without serious harm in a healthy cohort.
evi.effect: In the landmark ITP mouse study, rapamycin begun at 20 months of age (roughly late-middle-age for a mouse) raised lifespan measured at 90% mortality by about 14% in females and 9% in males — the first drug ever shown to extend mammalian lifespan when started late in life. Human effect sizes on ageing endpoints are not yet established.
evi.whoBenefits: Clinically: transplant recipients and LAM patients, for whom it's approved. For longevity, it remains investigational — pursued by some healthy adults under medical supervision, with no established profile of who benefits and no proven human lifespan effect.
evi.caveats: The chasm between animal and human evidence is the headline caveat: spectacular mouse data does not guarantee a human longevity benefit. Real risks (dose-dependent immunosuppression, mouth ulcers, raised lipids and glucose, impaired wound healing, CYP3A interactions) mean it belongs under medical supervision. Longevity dosing schedules are extrapolated from animals, not validated by human outcome trials.

trials.finding: Rapamycin fed from 20 months of age extended lifespan in genetically heterogeneous mice at three independent sites — ~14% (female) and ~9% (male) increase in age at 90% mortality; the first drug shown to extend mammalian lifespan started late in life.
trials.ref: Harrison et al., 2009, Nature

trials.finding: Intermittent rapamycin dosing extended lifespan while reducing the glucose and immune side effects of daily dosing, by more selectively inhibiting mTORC1 and sparing mTORC2.
trials.ref: Arriola Apelo et al., 2016, Aging Cell

trials.finding: An mTOR inhibitor (the rapalog everolimus) improved influenza-vaccine responses in healthy elderly adults, indicating mTOR inhibition can enhance rather than only suppress aspects of immune function.
trials.ref: Mannick et al., 2014, Science Translational Medicine

trials.finding: PEARL trial: 48 weeks of low-dose weekly compounded rapamycin (5–10 mg) in healthy adults aged 50–85 was well tolerated with no serious drug-related adverse events; it established safety rather than a proven lifespan or definitive healthspan benefit.
trials.ref: PEARL trial, 2024/2025 (Aging)

tech.chem: A macrocyclic lactone (a triene macrolide) natural product produced by the soil bacterium Streptomyces hygroscopicus, discovered in a soil sample from Rapa Nui (Easter Island) — the origin of the name. Structurally it is not a steroid or a stimulant; it carries an FKBP-binding domain and, adjacent to it, the effector domain that contacts mTOR. Poorly water-soluble and a substrate for both CYP3A and P-glycoprotein, which explains its low, variable oral bioavailability.

targets.action: high-affinity binding partner — rapamycin binds FKBP12 first, forming the active complex
targets.affinity: low-nanomolar binding to FKBP12; this obligatory adaptor is what enables potent mTOR inhibition
targets.role: the required intracellular adaptor — without it rapamycin only weakly engages mTOR

targets.action: allosteric inhibitor via the FKBP12–rapamycin complex binding the FRB domain
targets.affinity: FKBP12–rapamycin binds FRB at Kd ≈ 12 nM vs Kd ≈ 26 µM for rapamycin alone (~2,000-fold enhancement)
targets.role: the therapeutic target — mTORC1 inhibition drives autophagy, reduced protein synthesis, and the growth-to-maintenance shift

targets.action: not directly bound; chronic exposure blocks mTORC2 assembly in an FKBP12-dependent manner
targets.affinity: no direct binding — an indirect, time-dependent effect of sustained dosing
targets.role: the off-target liability — mTORC2 inhibition underlies rapamycin-associated insulin resistance, and sparing it is the goal of intermittent dosing
