tech.signaling: mTORC1 integrates nutrient (amino-acid), growth-factor (PI3K–Akt), and energy (AMPK) inputs. Its two best-known substrates are S6K1 (ribosomal protein S6 kinase) and 4E-BP1, which together drive cap-dependent protein translation and ribosome biogenesis. mTORC1 also phosphorylates and inhibits ULK1 and TFEB, suppressing autophagy. Rapamycin–FKBP12 binding the FRB domain relieves this suppression: translation and growth fall, autophagy and lysosomal biogenesis rise. mTORC2, by contrast, phosphorylates Akt (Ser473) and regulates cytoskeleton and metabolism — its chronic inhibition impairs insulin signalling.

adme.absorb: Low oral bioavailability (~14%), limited by intestinal CYP3A4 metabolism and P-glycoprotein efflux; absorption is variable and reduced/altered by high-fat meals.
adme.distribute: Extensively partitions into red blood cells (monitored in whole blood, not plasma); high volume of distribution (~12 L/kg); substantially protein-bound in plasma, chiefly to albumin.
adme.metabolise: Hepatic and intestinal CYP3A4 (with P-glycoprotein) generate multiple largely inactive metabolites; strong CYP3A inhibitors (grapefruit, ketoconazole, some macrolides) markedly raise exposure while inducers (rifampin) lower it.
adme.excrete: Predominantly faecal (~91%) as metabolites, with only ~2% in urine. Elimination half-life is long (~60 h; reported ~57–63 h), extended in hepatic impairment.

tech.pgx: No caffeine-style single-gene switch governs response. Inter-individual exposure is shaped by CYP3A4/CYP3A5 metabolic activity and ABCB1 (P-glycoprotein) variation, which is why transplant dosing relies on therapeutic drug monitoring rather than a fixed dose. No validated pharmacogenetic test guides longevity dosing.
tech.dose: Prescription-only. Transplant immunosuppression uses daily dosing titrated to whole-blood trough levels. LAM uses daily dosing targeting defined trough concentrations. Off-label longevity protocols use low-dose intermittent (typically weekly) regimens — around 5–10 mg once weekly in the PEARL trial — chosen to inhibit mTORC1 while sparing mTORC2; these schedules are extrapolated from animal data and are not validated by human outcome trials.
tech.tolerance: Not classic receptor tolerance. The central schedule issue is mTORC2: sustained daily exposure progressively inhibits mTORC2 assembly, producing insulin resistance and glucose intolerance, whereas intermittent dosing largely avoids this. Dose-dependent effects include immunosuppression, stomatitis (mouth ulcers), hyperlipidaemia, and impaired wound healing.

contrasts.title: Rapamycin vs everolimus — same lever, faster clearance
contrasts.point: Everolimus is a 'rapalog' — rapamycin with a small chemical tweak (a hydroxyethyl group) that gives it better oral absorption and a much shorter half-life (~30 h vs ~60 h). Same mechanism (FKBP12 → mTORC1 inhibition), but the shorter half-life makes everolimus easier to titrate. It's the drug behind the elderly-vaccine and infection trials that showed mTOR inhibition can enhance, not just suppress, immune function.

contrasts.title: Rapamycin vs metformin — direct brake vs indirect nudge
contrasts.point: Both are studied as geroprotectors, but they hit the growth axis from opposite ends. Rapamycin directly inhibits mTORC1, the growth hub itself. Metformin works upstream and indirectly — dampening mitochondrial energy output and activating AMPK, which in turn eases off mTORC1 among other targets. Rapamycin's animal lifespan data is far stronger and more reproducible; metformin's is weaker and its healthy-human longevity trial (TAME) is still the open question.

questions: Why does a 65-year-old diabetes drug keep turning up in longevity conversations — and does it actually make healthy people live longer?
Why does the same drug that protects a sedentary, at-risk person sometimes blunt the gains of someone who trains hard?
Why is metformin famous for wrecking your stomach, and why does the extended-release version fix it?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Metformin flips the same low-fuel switch (AMPK) that exercise and fasting do, which is why it's a plausible longevity candidate — but the human lifespan evidence is strong in diabetics (UKPDS 34; Bannister 2014) and still unproven in healthy people, which is exactly why the TAME trial was designed. (2) Because it partly duplicates the exercise signal by inhibiting mitochondrial Complex I, it can dull the mitochondrial and muscle adaptations hard training is trying to build (Konopka 2019; Walton/MASTERS 2019). (3) The GI upset comes from high metformin concentrations sitting in the gut wall; the extended-release form smooths delivery, so it's far gentler on the stomach.

metformin.bigIdea: Metformin doesn't lower blood sugar by adding insulin — it gently starves the cell's power plant, tripping the same 'low fuel, burn don't hoard' switch (AMPK) that exercise and fasting flip. Hold onto that one sentence and everything else on this page follows from it.
metformin.analogy: Metformin is like quietly dimming the power supply to your liver's engine room. It doesn't attack sugar directly — it makes the cell sense that fuel is running low, so it stops making and hoarding glucose and starts conserving and burning it. It's a whisper of the same alarm that exercise shouts.

mechSteps.t: It's escorted into your liver cells
mechSteps.predict: Metformin carries a positive charge, so it can't just drift across a cell membrane. How does it get inside the liver cells where it works?
mechSteps.d: Metformin is a positively charged molecule that can't freely cross membranes, so it depends on a transporter — OCT1 — to carry it into hepatocytes, where it concentrates. This is why the liver, and the gut wall, are its main sites of action.

mechSteps.t: It gently jams the power plant's first station
mechSteps.predict: Inside the cell it slows the mitochondria — the energy factories. If you throttle ATP production, which molecule piles up as a signal that fuel is low?
mechSteps.d: Metformin mildly inhibits Complex I, the first station of the mitochondrial electron-transport chain. Less ATP gets made, so its spent form — AMP — accumulates and the AMP:ATP ratio climbs. The cell now reads 'energy is low'.

mechSteps.t: The rising AMP flips the master fuel switch
mechSteps.predict: A high AMP:ATP ratio is the exact trigger for one master enzyme — the same one exercise activates. What switches on, and what's its motto?
mechSteps.d: The climbing AMP:ATP ratio activates AMPK, the cell's master 'burn, don't hoard' switch. AMPK turns down energy-expensive building programmes and turns up glucose and fat burning.

mechSteps.t: Your liver stops over-producing sugar
mechSteps.predict: In type 2 diabetes the liver leaks too much glucose into the blood overnight. If you lower the liver's energy charge, what happens to that glucose output?
mechSteps.d: Making new glucose (gluconeogenesis) is energy-expensive, so a lower hepatic energy charge slows it down — partly through AMPK, partly through AMPK-independent effects on the liver's energy state. Insulin sensitivity improves and the gut microbiome shifts too.

mechSteps.t: The same switch is why athletes should pause
mechSteps.predict: Hard training works partly by stressing mitochondria into adapting. If metformin already nudges that same pathway, what might it do to your training gains?
mechSteps.d: Because metformin flips the very AMPK/Complex-I signal that exercise uses to force adaptation, it can blunt the mitochondrial and muscle gains from hard training — the mechanism it shares with exercise is also the reason it can get in exercise's way.

framework.target: Not a classic receptor. The direct hit is mitochondrial Complex I (with a secondary action on mitochondrial glycerophosphate dehydrogenase); the master effector downstream is AMPK (PRKAA1). Note the contrast with caffeine or semaglutide: metformin doesn't dock a surface receptor at all — it works from inside the mitochondria.
framework.affinity: There's no receptor binding constant to quote, because there's no receptor. Metformin is a relatively weak Complex I inhibitor — what makes it potent enough to matter is that its positive charge, drawn by the mitochondrial membrane's voltage, concentrates it hundreds-fold inside mitochondria. So it's not high-affinity binding, it's accumulation that gets it to an effective dose right where it acts.
framework.adme: Not metabolised at all — no liver CYP step. It's carried into cells by OCT transporters (OCT1 into liver) and excreted unchanged by the kidney via OCT2 and MATE transporters. Plasma half-life is ~5–6 h. Because clearance is entirely renal, kidney function is the master safety variable.
framework.genetics: No clean single-gene switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. Variants in the transporter genes — SLC22A1 (OCT1) and SLC47A1 (MATE1) — shift how much drug reaches the liver, its blood levels, and how well the gut tolerates it, but the effect of any one variant is modest and not used to guide prescribing.
framework.evidence: 5/5 for the diabetes and cardiovascular story — UKPDS 34 is a landmark hard-outcome trial. The pure longevity claim in non-diabetics is genuinely promising but not yet proven; the TAME trial was designed precisely to test it.

pk.onset: glucose-lowering builds over days; full effect over ~1–2 weeks
pk.peak: plasma level peaks ~2–3 h after a dose
pk.halfLife: ~5–6 h in plasma (longer in the red-cell compartment)
pk.duration: dosed 1–2× daily to hold a steady effect
pk.note: Metformin breaks the usual timing rules: the benefit isn't chased by a daily blood peak but by keeping steady levels in the liver, so it's a habit, not a stopwatch. Because it leaves only through the kidneys and isn't metabolised, impaired kidney function lets it accumulate — the reason dosing is capped or stopped as renal function falls.

journey.d: You swallow it (or the extended-release version). It's absorbed from the small intestine — but incompletely, only ~50–60% — and a lot of the action starts right here in the gut wall.
It rides the bloodstream unbound and un-metabolised. Being charged, it doesn't slip freely into cells — it waits for transporters to let it in.
OCT1 pumps it into liver cells, and the mitochondrial voltage concentrates it hundreds-fold inside the mitochondria, where it gently jams Complex I. This is the heart of the effect.
The dip in ATP raises AMP, flipping the AMPK 'low-fuel' switch. The liver stops over-producing glucose, fat burning rises, and insulin works better.
There's no liver breakdown step. The kidneys filter and actively pump it out unchanged (via OCT2 and MATE1) within about a day — which is why kidney function sets the safe dose.

myths.myth: Metformin is a proven longevity drug — it makes healthy people live longer.
myths.truth: That's the hypothesis, not the verdict. The lifespan data are strongest in people with diabetes (UKPDS 34; Bannister 2014), and even there a later reanalysis questioned the healthy-person comparison. In non-diabetics it's unproven — which is the entire reason the TAME trial was designed to test aging as an outcome. Treat 'anti-aging metformin' as a serious, testable idea, not an established fact.

myths.myth: Metformin often causes dangerous lactic acidosis.
myths.truth: It's very rare in people with healthy kidneys. The risk climbs mainly when it accumulates — in severe kidney impairment, acute illness, or around dehydration — which is exactly why doctors screen kidney function and pause it around surgery, contrast scans, and acute illness.

myths.myth: It's a weight-loss drug like Ozempic.
myths.truth: Different league. Metformin is roughly weight-neutral to modestly weight-reducing — a few kilos at most — not the ~15% loss seen with GLP-1 drugs. It's a glucose and healthspan drug, not an obesity drug.

myths.myth: If some AMPK activation is good, take it around your workouts for extra benefit.
myths.truth: Backwards for athletes. Because it flips the same Complex-I/AMPK signal your training uses to force adaptation, metformin can blunt mitochondrial and muscle gains from hard exercise — the overlap is a liability, not a stack.

whenToUse.intro: How it's used clinically — this is prescription-only, and needs a doctor to prescribe, dose, and monitor it. This is not a self-administration protocol:
items: **Type 2 diabetes (on-label, first-line):** the standard initial drug for lowering blood sugar, backed by hard-outcome data (UKPDS 34) showing fewer diabetes complications and deaths in overweight patients.
**Prediabetes and PCOS (common uses):** prescribed to improve insulin sensitivity — to delay progression to diabetes, and in PCOS to help metabolic and ovulatory function.

**Longevity/healthspan (off-label, experimental):** used by some clinicians on the AMPK rationale, but the benefit in non-diabetics is unproven — the TAME trial was designed to test exactly this, and results aren't in.

**Contraindications a doctor screens for:** significant kidney impairment (low eGFR), acute illness or dehydration, and situations raising lactic-acidosis risk; it's paused around surgery and iodinated-contrast scans.

**Monitoring while on it:** periodic kidney function and vitamin B12 (long-term use can deplete B12); start low and use extended-release to limit GI upset. Athletes should weigh the blunting of training adaptations.

start.q: In one line: does metformin lower blood sugar by adding insulin, or by something else?
start.a: Something else. It gently inhibits the mitochondria (Complex I), raising AMP and flipping the AMPK switch, so the liver makes less glucose and insulin works better — it adds no insulin itself.

how.q: What does metformin physically inhibit, and what master switch does that flip?
how.a: It inhibits mitochondrial Complex I, which lowers ATP and raises AMP, activating AMPK — the cell's 'low-fuel, burn-don't-hoard' switch.

use.q: Why is kidney function the key safety check, when it's the liver where the drug acts?
use.a: Because metformin isn't metabolised — the kidneys are its only exit. If they underperform, it accumulates, which is what raises the rare risk of lactic acidosis.

evidence.q: How strong is the evidence — and where is it strong versus still open?
evidence.a: Very strong for diabetes and its complications (UKPDS 34). The longevity claim in healthy people is promising but unproven — that's what TAME was built to test.

canExplain: Why metformin lowers blood sugar without adding any insulin
The exact thing it inhibits (Complex I) and the master switch it flips (AMPK) — and why it has no receptor
Why it shares a mechanism with exercise, and why that same overlap can blunt training gains
Why kidney function — not the liver — is the key safety variable
How strong the human evidence really is: settled for diabetes, still open for longevity

selfTest.q: Metformin has no receptor and is a fairly weak Complex I inhibitor. How does it still reach an effective concentration where it acts?
selfTest.a: It's positively charged, and the voltage across the mitochondrial membrane draws it in and concentrates it hundreds-fold inside the mitochondria — so accumulation, not binding affinity, delivers the effective dose to Complex I.

selfTest.q: A masters athlete on metformin complains their strength and endurance gains have stalled despite hard training. What's the likely mechanism?
selfTest.a: Metformin activates the same Complex-I/AMPK signal that exercise uses to drive mitochondrial and muscle adaptation, so it can blunt those gains — shown for aerobic adaptation (Konopka 2019) and resistance-training hypertrophy (Walton/MASTERS 2019) in older adults.

selfTest.q: Why is metformin generally safe in healthy people but dangerous in someone with acute kidney injury or severe dehydration?
selfTest.a: It's cleared unchanged only by the kidneys and isn't metabolised. If kidney clearance drops, the drug accumulates, driving up the (otherwise rare) risk of lactic acidosis — which is why it's held around acute illness, surgery, and contrast scans.

evi.howStrong: Metformin's evidence splits cleanly into two tiers. For type 2 diabetes it sits near the top of the pyramid: decades of use plus a landmark randomised hard-outcome trial (UKPDS 34) showing it cuts diabetes complications and deaths, not just blood-sugar numbers. For longevity in people without diabetes, the evidence is intriguing but not established — the strongest signals are observational (diabetics on metformin doing surprisingly well versus controls), and observational data can't prove the drug caused it. The TAME trial was designed to fill that gap by testing aging-related outcomes directly, but it's a proposal/landmark-in-design, not a reported result.
evi.whatItShows: In diabetes: better glucose control plus fewer real complications and deaths. In the longevity conversation: a plausible mechanism (AMPK activation, mirroring exercise and calorie restriction) and some suggestive human data — but the honest framing is 'unproven in healthy people'. And in athletes, the evidence points the other way: it can blunt the very adaptations training is meant to produce.
evi.effect: In plain numbers: in UKPDS 34, overweight patients on metformin had roughly a third fewer all-cause deaths and diabetes-related deaths versus conventional treatment. In Bannister's 2014 database study, diabetics on metformin monotherapy had about 15% longer median survival than matched non-diabetic controls — a striking but observational finding that a later reanalysis called into question. For exercise, controlled trials show measurable blunting of mitochondrial and muscle-growth adaptations in older adults.
evi.whoBenefits: Clearest benefit: people with type 2 diabetes or high metabolic risk — the sedentary, insulin-resistant, overweight. Least suited: lean, well-trained people chasing performance, where the exercise-blunting effect can cost gains. The longevity case for otherwise-healthy people remains an open question, not a recommendation.
evi.caveats: The longevity signal is largely observational and one prominent survival finding was later disputed, so don't overread it. GI upset is common early (extended-release helps), long-term use can deplete vitamin B12, and because clearance is entirely renal, kidney impairment is a genuine safety limit. And for athletes, the mechanism that makes it interesting is the same one that can dull training.

trials.finding: In overweight type 2 diabetics, metformin reduced any diabetes-related endpoint by ~32% and all-cause mortality by ~36% versus conventional treatment — a hard-outcome benefit beyond glucose lowering.
trials.ref: UK Prospective Diabetes Study (UKPDS) 34, 1998, Lancet

trials.finding: Diabetics initiated on metformin monotherapy had ~15% longer adjusted median survival than matched non-diabetic controls (observational; later reanalyses have challenged this).
trials.ref: Bannister et al., 2014, Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism

trials.finding: Metformin abolished the exercise-training-induced increase in skeletal-muscle mitochondrial respiration in older adults.
trials.ref: Konopka et al., 2019, Aging Cell

trials.finding: Metformin blunted muscle hypertrophy from progressive resistance training in older adults (MASTERS trial).
trials.ref: Walton et al., 2019, Aging Cell

tech.chem: A biguanide — two guanidine groups joined, a small, hydrophilic, positively charged molecule (derived historically from galegine in the herb Galega officinalis). Its permanent positive charge is central to everything: it can't cross membranes unaided (so it needs transporters), and it's electrostatically drawn into the negatively charged interior of mitochondria, where it accumulates hundreds-fold. It is not metabolised.

targets.action: mild, partially reversible inhibitor → lowers ATP, raises AMP:ATP
targets.affinity: weak inhibitor; effect depends on hundred-fold mitochondrial accumulation driven by membrane potential, not on high-affinity binding
targets.role: the primary upstream event that raises AMP and drives AMPK activation

targets.action: activated indirectly (via rising AMP:ATP and LKB1), not bound directly by the drug
targets.affinity: downstream effector, not a drug-binding site
targets.role: the master 'burn, don't hoard' switch — suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis and lipogenesis, improves insulin sensitivity

targets.action: inhibitor
targets.affinity: secondary target
targets.role: an AMPK-independent route by which metformin curbs hepatic glucose production (altered redox/glycerophosphate shuttle)

targets.action: uptake transporter — carries metformin into hepatocytes
targets.affinity: transport substrate, not a receptor ligand
targets.role: the gateway that determines how much drug reaches the liver; loss-of-function variants alter response and GI tolerance

tech.signaling: There's no GPCR cascade. The chain is bioenergetic: Complex I inhibition → falling ATP / rising AMP → AMP binds the AMPK γ-subunit and, with LKB1 phosphorylating Thr172, activates AMPK. Active AMPK phosphorylates downstream targets (e.g. ACC, and it suppresses gluconeogenic gene programmes), shifting the liver from making and storing fuel toward conserving and burning it. A parallel, AMPK-independent arm lowers hepatic glucose output simply by dropping the cell's energy charge and altering redox via mGPD.

adme.absorb: Incomplete oral absorption (~50–60% bioavailability), from the small intestine; the gut wall itself is a major site of action. Extended-release formulations slow delivery to reduce GI upset.
adme.distribute: Hydrophilic and charged; distributes into gut and liver via OCT transporters, with notable accumulation in erythrocytes; minimal plasma-protein binding.
adme.metabolise: None — metformin is not metabolised by CYP enzymes or any other pathway; it circulates and is excreted as the unchanged parent drug.
adme.excrete: Eliminated unchanged by the kidneys, via glomerular filtration plus active tubular secretion through OCT2 and MATE1/MATE2-K. Plasma half-life ~5–6 h; clearance falls with declining renal function, so the drug accumulates in kidney impairment.

tech.pgx: No caffeine-style single-gene switch. Reduced-function variants in SLC22A1 (OCT1) and SLC47A1 (MATE1) shift metformin's tissue exposure, glycemic response, and GI tolerability, but each has only a modest effect and none is used to guide prescribing. The dominant clinical determinant of exposure is renal function, not genotype.
tech.dose: Prescription-only. Typically started low (e.g. 500 mg once or twice daily with food) and titrated up toward ~1500–2000 mg/day, immediate- or extended-release, to minimise GI upset. Dose is capped or the drug stopped as eGFR falls; it is held around surgery, acute illness, and iodinated-contrast imaging. Not to be self-dosed outside clinical care.
tech.tolerance: No receptor down-regulation that erodes efficacy. GI side effects usually settle over the first weeks (and are lower with extended-release). The main long-term issue isn't tolerance but vitamin B12 depletion with chronic use, warranting periodic monitoring.

questions: Why does a decades-old diabetes pill keep showing up on longevity lists — and why does it work noticeably better in male mice?
Why does acarbose only work if you swallow it with the very first bite of a meal?
Why does it give you gas — and is that a side effect, or a clue to how it actually works?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Acarbose slows carbohydrate digestion, flattening the post-meal glucose surge in a way that partly mimics eating fewer carbs — and in the NIH's Interventions Testing Program it reliably extended lifespan, most strongly in males (the sex difference is real but not fully explained). (2) It works only inside the gut, jamming the enzymes that digest starch, so it has to be physically present alongside the food — no carbs in the gut, nothing for it to slow. (3) The gas IS the mechanism showing up: the starch it stops you digesting travels to your colon, where bacteria ferment it into short-chain fatty acids (and gas). That fermentation eases as your microbiome adapts.

acarbose.bigIdea: Acarbose doesn't push sugar out of your blood — it slows how fast carbohydrates are digested, so glucose trickles in instead of spiking, giving you a partial low-carb effect from a pill. Hold onto that one sentence and everything else on this page follows from it.
acarbose.stakes: It's a cheap, off-patent prescription diabetes drug that turned out to be one of the most reproducible lifespan-extenders in the NIH's rigorous mouse-testing program — which is why longevity researchers keep coming back to it, even though no human lifespan trial exists.
acarbose.analogy: Acarbose is a decoy carbohydrate wedged into the molecular scissors that cut starch into sugar. The enzyme clamps onto it even more eagerly than onto real starch — but it can't cut the decoy. So the scissors stay jammed, digestion slows, and glucose seeps into your blood as a slow trickle instead of a flood.

mechSteps.t: Starch must be chopped to single sugars before you can absorb it
mechSteps.predict: You can't absorb a starch molecule whole — it has to be cut down first. Which enzymes at the gut wall do the final cutting?
mechSteps.d: Dietary starch is a long chain of glucose. Before any of it enters your blood, enzymes at the small-intestine brush border — the α-glucosidases like maltase-glucoamylase — must snip the terminal sugars off, one at a time.

mechSteps.t: Acarbose is a fake carbohydrate that jams the scissors
mechSteps.predict: Acarbose looks enough like a carbohydrate to fit the enzyme's active site, but it can't be cut. What happens to the enzyme's normal job?
mechSteps.d: Acarbose is a pseudo-carbohydrate — a transition-state mimic that the enzyme grabs far more tightly than real starch but cannot cleave. It sits in the active site as a competitive inhibitor, stalling the digestion of the sugars around it (it also blunts pancreatic α-amylase upstream).

mechSteps.t: Glucose enters slowly — the post-meal spike is blunted
mechSteps.predict: If starch is digested more slowly, how does the shape of your blood-glucose curve after a meal change — and what does your pancreas do less of?
mechSteps.d: With digestion slowed, glucose is released gradually across a longer stretch of gut. The sharp post-meal glucose peak flattens, and your pancreas releases less of an insulin surge to match it.

mechSteps.t: Spared carbs travel to the colon and get fermented
mechSteps.predict: The starch you didn't digest doesn't vanish — it keeps moving. Where does it end up, and what does that produce?
mechSteps.d: Undigested carbohydrate passes into the colon, where your gut bacteria ferment it into short-chain fatty acids like butyrate (and the gas behind the classic side effect). Those SCFAs feed gut-hormone signals such as GLP-1 — part of why acarbose's effect is more than just slowed absorption.

mechSteps.t: The net effect resembles mild caloric restriction
mechSteps.predict: Flattening the glucose-and-insulin surge, meal after meal — what long-studied longevity intervention does that partly imitate?
mechSteps.d: By steadily lowering the glycemic and insulin load of every meal, acarbose partially mimics eating fewer carbs, or eating less overall — a caloric-restriction-like signal. In genetically heterogeneous mice, that translated into extended median lifespan, preferentially in males.

framework.target: Intestinal α-glucosidases at the brush border — maltase-glucoamylase (MGAM) and sucrase-isomaltase (SI) — plus pancreatic α-amylase. Note the contrast with most compounds on this site: acarbose blocks digestive enzymes in the gut lumen, not a receptor and not any systemic target.
framework.affinity: There's no receptor binding constant to quote, because there's no receptor. Acarbose is a transition-state analog: a pseudo-carbohydrate the enzyme binds far more tightly than its real substrate but cannot cut, so it occupies the active site and stalls digestion. What matters isn't a blood level — it's the drug being physically present in your gut at the same time as the meal.
framework.adme: The unusual part: less than ~2% of an acarbose dose is absorbed intact into the bloodstream. It does its entire job in the gut lumen and is largely eliminated in the feces (bacteria and gut enzymes break down part of it). So there's no meaningful circulating drug level, and its action is tied to meals, not to a systemic half-life.
framework.genetics: No caffeine-style single-gene switch. Response depends on how much digestible starch you eat and on your gut microbiome, which ferments the spared carbohydrate. In the mouse lifespan studies the effect was diet-dependent — and, importantly, larger in males for reasons still not fully understood.
framework.evidence: 3/5 for humans. Reproducible mouse lifespan data (ITP); solid human evidence for post-meal glucose control and prevention of progression from prediabetes to diabetes; but NO human lifespan trial, and the cardiovascular benefit is genuinely contested (positive in STOP-NIDDM, null in the larger ACE trial).

journey.d: You swallow it with the first bite of a carbohydrate-containing meal. It travels down into your small intestine mixed in with the starch it's meant to slow.
At the intestinal wall it clamps onto the α-glucosidase enzymes, blocking them from cutting starch into absorbable glucose. This is the whole event — and it happens in the gut, not the blood.
Glucose is released gradually instead of all at once, so the post-meal spike flattens and your insulin surge shrinks. Meanwhile under ~2% of the drug itself crosses into your bloodstream.

The starch you didn't digest keeps moving into the colon, where gut bacteria ferment it into short-chain fatty acids like butyrate — producing gas along the way, and nudging gut hormones such as GLP-1.
The drug itself, largely unabsorbed, leaves in the feces; the small fraction that did get absorbed is cleared by the kidneys. Its work was done entirely inside your gut.

myths.myth: Acarbose is a fat blocker that makes you lose weight by blocking calories.
myths.truth: It blocks carbohydrate digestion, not fat — and it doesn't erase those calories so much as reroute them. Much of the spared starch is fermented by colon bacteria into absorbable short-chain fatty acids. Human weight effects are modest; it's a glucose-shaping drug, not a fat blocker.

myths.myth: The mouse lifespan data means acarbose extends human lifespan.
myths.truth: It's one of the most reproducible lifespan-extenders in mice — but that's mice. No human lifespan trial exists. Human evidence covers post-meal glucose and diabetes prevention only, so the mouse-to-human leap is exactly that: a leap.

myths.myth: The gas means it isn't working, or that it's harming you.
myths.truth: The flatulence is the mechanism made visible — undigested carbohydrate fermenting in your colon. It's expected, usually eases as your microbiome adapts, and is why doctors start at a low dose and titrate up.

myths.myth: You can take it whenever, like a daily vitamin.
myths.truth: It only works if it's in your gut alongside the carbs. Taken with the first bite of a starchy meal it does its job; swallowed on an empty stomach or with a carb-free meal, there's nothing for it to slow.

whenToUse.intro: How it's used clinically — acarbose is a prescription drug (FDA-approved for type 2 diabetes), and its longevity use is off-label and unproven in humans. This is not a self-administration protocol; it needs a doctor to prescribe, titrate and monitor.
items: **Type 2 diabetes / post-meal glucose control:** its main approved use — taken with the first bite of each main meal to blunt the post-meal glucose rise.
**Prediabetes (impaired glucose tolerance):** used to reduce progression to type 2 diabetes, the most reliable human finding (STOP-NIDDM, ACE).
**Longevity interest:** off-label, driven by the reproducible mouse ITP data — but there is no human lifespan evidence, so this is a conversation to have with a doctor, not a self-experiment.
**Contraindications a doctor screens for:** inflammatory bowel disease, colonic ulceration or partial bowel obstruction, cirrhosis, diabetic ketoacidosis, and significant renal impairment.
**Monitoring:** the dose is started low and titrated up with meals to limit gas and bloating; rare liver-enzyme elevations at higher doses mean periodic checks.

start.q: In one line: does acarbose lower blood sugar by pushing glucose into your cells?
start.a: No — it slows how fast carbohydrates are digested, so glucose enters the blood gradually. It never touches insulin's push-glucose-into-cells step; it just flattens the incoming spike.

how.q: What does acarbose physically block, and where?
how.a: The α-glucosidase enzymes (MGAM/SI) at the small-intestine brush border, plus pancreatic α-amylase — so starch is broken down into absorbable glucose more slowly.

use.q: Why must it be taken with the first bite of a meal?
use.a: It works only inside the gut lumen and has to be present at the same time as the carbohydrate it slows. No carbs in the gut means nothing for it to act on.

evidence.q: How strong is the longevity case, really?
evidence.a: Reproducible in mice (one of the ITP's most consistent hits), strong in humans for glucose control and diabetes prevention — but there's no human lifespan trial, and its cardiovascular benefit is contested.

canExplain: Why acarbose blunts a post-meal glucose spike without ever touching insulin's action
The exact enzymes it blocks — and why it's an inhibitor with no receptor
Why under 2% of it is absorbed yet it still works: it acts in the gut lumen, not the blood
Why it causes gas, and why that's the mechanism showing up rather than a malfunction
How strong the longevity evidence really is — reproducible in mice, still unproven in humans

selfTest.q: Acarbose barely enters the bloodstream (under ~2%), yet it clearly works. How is that possible?
selfTest.a: Its target is in the gut lumen, not the body. It inhibits the brush-border enzymes that digest starch right where the food is, so it never needs to be absorbed to act — and it leaves largely in the feces.

selfTest.q: Acarbose is called a 'caloric restriction mimetic.' What's the honest limit of that label?
selfTest.a: By slowing carb digestion it flattens the glucose-and-insulin surge, resembling eating fewer carbs. But the calories aren't fully lost — much of the spared starch is fermented by colon bacteria into short-chain fatty acids you absorb — and no human data confirm the CR-like lifespan effect seen in mice.

selfTest.q: STOP-NIDDM suggested acarbose cut cardiovascular events; the larger ACE trial didn't. What should you take from that?
selfTest.a: That single trials can disagree, and the bigger, later ACE trial found reduced diabetes but no cardiovascular benefit. So the CV claim is unproven — diabetes prevention is the more reliable human finding.

evi.howStrong: Split the claim in two, because the evidence is very different for each half. For blood-sugar control and diabetes prevention, human evidence is solid — multiple large randomised controlled trials. For longevity, the strong evidence is in mice: acarbose is one of the most reproducible lifespan-extenders in the NIH's Interventions Testing Program, replicated across cohorts and effective even when started in mid-life. But there is no human lifespan trial — so the jump from mouse longevity to human longevity is exactly that, a jump.
evi.whatItShows: In mice: consistent median-lifespan extension, notably larger in males. In humans: reliably blunted post-meal glucose and a lower rate of progression from prediabetes to type 2 diabetes. The cardiovascular story in humans is mixed rather than settled.
evi.effect: In ITP mice acarbose raised male median lifespan by roughly 20% and female lifespan by a few percent. In humans, STOP-NIDDM (in impaired glucose tolerance) reported less progression to diabetes and fewer cardiovascular events; the larger ACE trial confirmed the diabetes-prevention effect (~18% relative reduction) but found no reduction in cardiovascular events.
evi.whoBenefits: People with high post-meal glucose spikes and carbohydrate-heavy diets get the clearest metabolic benefit. The mouse longevity signal was strongest in males and depended on diet. If you eat very few digestible carbs, there's little for it to slow — and little to gain.
evi.caveats: The longevity evidence is animal-only; the human trials measured glucose and diabetes, not lifespan. Gas and bloating limit tolerability for many. The cardiovascular benefit is contested (positive in STOP-NIDDM, null in ACE). And the male-skewed mouse effect may not translate to humans at all.

trials.finding: Acarbose extended median lifespan in genetically heterogeneous (HET3) mice, preferentially in males (male median lifespan up ~20%), in the NIA Interventions Testing Program.
trials.ref: Harrison et al., 2014, Aging Cell

trials.finding: Acarbose improved lifespan and healthspan in aging HET3 mice even when started at 16 months of age (mid-life), confirming the earlier ITP result.
trials.ref: Harrison et al., 2019, Aging Cell

trials.finding: STOP-NIDDM: in people with impaired glucose tolerance, acarbose reduced progression to type 2 diabetes and was reported to reduce cardiovascular events and hypertension.
trials.ref: Chiasson et al., STOP-NIDDM, 2002 Lancet & 2003 JAMA

trials.finding: ACE trial: in 6,522 Chinese patients with coronary heart disease and impaired glucose tolerance, acarbose reduced incident diabetes (rate ratio 0.82) but did NOT reduce cardiovascular events.
trials.ref: Holman et al., ACE, 2017, Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology

tech.chem: A pseudotetrasaccharide of microbial origin (produced by Actinoplanes species). Its core acarviose unit — an unsaturated cyclitol (valienamine) joined through a nitrogen linkage to a deoxyaminohexose — is glycosidically attached to a maltose. That nitrogen-containing bond is the trick: it resembles the transition state of carbohydrate hydrolysis and resists enzymatic cleavage, so the enzyme binds acarbose tightly but cannot cut it, stalling in the active site. It is not a steroid, stimulant, or hormone, and it acts almost entirely in the gut lumen.

targets.action: competitive, reversible inhibitor (transition-state analog) at the brush-border active site
targets.affinity: an enzyme active-site inhibitor, not a receptor ligand — binds far more tightly than the natural substrate but is not cleaved
targets.role: the brush-border α-glucosidase that releases terminal glucose from starch/maltose; the primary target

targets.action: competitive inhibitor at the intestinal brush border
targets.affinity: active-site inhibition; no receptor binding constant applies
targets.role: second major brush-border α-glucosidase; its inhibition further slows breakdown of dietary di-/oligosaccharides

targets.action: competitive inhibition of luminal starch breakdown upstream of the brush border
targets.affinity: active-site inhibitor of the digestive enzyme
targets.role: slows the first step of starch digestion in the gut lumen, complementing brush-border inhibition

tech.signaling: There is no receptor cascade — acarbose works by enzyme inhibition in the gut lumen, not by intracellular signalling. The downstream consequences: a flattened postprandial glucose curve and reduced insulin secretion; and, because more carbohydrate reaches the colon, increased microbial fermentation to short-chain fatty acids (notably butyrate and propionate). Those SCFAs engage FFAR2/FFAR3 (GPR43/GPR41) on enteroendocrine L-cells, promoting GLP-1 and PYY release, and shift gut microbiome composition. The net metabolic signal — lower glycemic and insulin load per meal — is what is proposed to resemble mild caloric restriction.

adme.absorb: Very low systemic absorption of the intact drug (<~2%); it acts locally at the small-intestinal brush border and does not need to be absorbed to work.
adme.distribute: Minimal systemic distribution — its site of action is the gut lumen, not a body compartment.
adme.metabolise: Broken down within the gastrointestinal tract by intestinal bacteria and digestive enzymes into fragments, a portion of which (as metabolites) is absorbed.
adme.excrete: The unabsorbed drug is eliminated in the feces; the small absorbed/metabolite fraction is cleared renally.

tech.pgx: No single-gene pharmacogenetic switch like caffeine's CYP1A2 — clearance doesn't depend on a hepatic enzyme genotype because the drug largely isn't systemically metabolised. Response is governed instead by dietary carbohydrate load and by gut microbiome composition, which determines how the spared starch is fermented. In the ITP mouse studies the lifespan effect was diet-dependent and sex-biased (larger in males), a difference not yet mechanistically resolved.
tech.dose: Prescription-only, titrated under medical supervision: typically started at 25 mg three times daily and increased toward 50–100 mg three times daily, each dose taken with the first bite of a main meal. Pivotal trials (STOP-NIDDM, ACE) used 100 mg three times daily. Low starting doses and gradual titration limit the gas/bloating. Off-label longevity dosing in humans is not established.
tech.tolerance: No receptor down-regulation and no loss of glucose-lowering efficacy over time. The prominent early gastrointestinal effects (flatulence, bloating, loose stools) typically diminish over weeks as the colonic microbiome adapts to the increased fermentable carbohydrate load — which is why starting low and going slow matters.

questions: Why does NAD⁺ — the molecule these capsules are meant to refill — quietly fall by roughly half as you age, and why should you care?
NMN and NR cost different amounts and have different legal status — are you actually buying two different things, or the same molecule one step apart?
Why is the animal data on NAD⁺ boosters jaw-dropping while the human results are real but distinctly modest?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) NAD⁺ is spent every second by sirtuins, PARP repair crews and the age-rising enzyme CD38 that chews it up — supply can't keep pace, so the reserve drains and the enzymes that need it slow down. (2) They're the same pathway one phosphate apart: NR is the smaller, earlier precursor; NMN is NR plus a phosphate, one step closer to NAD⁺ — but likely has to shed that phosphate back to NR to even enter a cell. Neither is proven more potent; the real differences are legal status and depth of human data. (3) Mice get huge doses per kilo and dramatic endpoints; humans break much of the dose down to nicotinamide first, dose far lower, and measure subtler outcomes — so NAD⁺ rises reliably, but the downstream clinical payoff is small and matters most after 40.

nmn-nr-nad-precursors.bigIdea: NMN and NR do nothing on their own — they're raw material your cells forge into NAD⁺, the coenzyme that runs your energy metabolism, your sirtuins and your DNA-repair crews. Refill the raw material and you refill NAD⁺. Everything else on this page follows from that.
nmn-nr-nad-precursors.analogy: NAD⁺ is a coin your cells spend billions of times a day and recycle almost as fast — but the reserve leaks with age. NMN and NR are the blank metal you feed the mint (the NAD⁺ salvage pathway) so it can keep stamping fresh coins. You're not handing the cell energy; you're restocking the metal so it never runs short.

mechSteps.t: NAD⁺ is the coenzyme everything runs on
mechSteps.predict: One molecule is the fuel your sirtuins and DNA-repair enzymes literally cannot run without — and it falls with age. Name it.
mechSteps.d: NAD⁺ is a coenzyme sitting at the centre of energy metabolism. Your sirtuins (SIRT1) and your PARP DNA-repair enzymes consume it to do their jobs, so when NAD⁺ drops with age, that machinery quietly slows.

mechSteps.t: NAD⁺ is spent and recycled non-stop
mechSteps.predict: If NAD⁺ is consumed constantly, something must rebuild it just as fast. Which enzyme is the rate-limiter of that recycling line?
mechSteps.d: Every time an enzyme uses NAD⁺ it splits off nicotinamide, which NAMPT recycles back toward NAD⁺ — the salvage pathway that supplies most of your NAD⁺. Meanwhile the enzyme CD38 rises with age and burns NAD⁺ faster, tipping the balance toward decline.

mechSteps.t: NR and NMN feed that line one step apart
mechSteps.predict: If you pour more raw material into the salvage line, what happens to the amount of NAD⁺ it can press out?
mechSteps.d: NR is phosphorylated by a kinase (NRK) into NMN; NMN is then built into NAD⁺ by NMNAT. Both precursors funnel straight into the salvage pathway — NMN just enters one step later. (Intriguingly, NMN is thought to be dephosphorylated back to NR before crossing into the cell — a point still debated.)

mechSteps.t: Refilled NAD⁺ powers the machinery
mechSteps.d: With the NAD⁺ pool topped up, sirtuins, PARP repair and energy metabolism have more of the cofactor they need. In humans this shows up reliably as higher blood NAD⁺ — the felt benefits are real but modest, and larger when you started depleted.

framework.target: There's no receptor here — a crucial point. NMN and NR are substrates (precursors), not ligands: they feed the NAD⁺ salvage pathway (NRK → NMNAT, or NAMPT → NMNAT). The real 'target' is the NAD⁺ pool itself and the enzymes that depend on it — SIRT1, the PARPs, and CD38. Think creatine, not caffeine: raw material, not a switch.
framework.affinity: There's no receptor binding constant to quote, because nothing is bound and switched on or off. What matters instead is whether an oral dose measurably raises NAD⁺ — and it does: whole-blood NAD⁺ climbs dose-dependently (~22–142% across 100–1000 mg NR in an 8-week trial). It's a refill you can verify on a blood test, not a receptor hit you tune with a Ki.
framework.adme: NR and NMN are absorbed orally but much of the dose is cleaved to nicotinamide (and NR) in the gut and liver — relatively little intact NMN reaches distant tissue. Tissues largely rebuild NAD⁺ locally from precursors delivered as nicotinamide/NR. Whole-blood NAD⁺ rises over ~2 weeks and plateaus.
framework.genetics: No single-gene switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. Response tracks your baseline NAD⁺ status — set by age, metabolic health and how fast CD38 (which climbs with inflammation and age) is consuming NAD⁺. The more depleted you start, the more headroom to refill.
framework.evidence: Split in two: that oral dosing raises NAD⁺ in humans is solid and reproducible; that this yields large clinical benefits is not — effects are real but modest and inconsistent. A fair 3/5.

pk.onset: days to weeks (not minutes)
pk.peak: whole-blood NAD⁺ plateaus around 2–4 weeks of daily dosing
pk.halfLife: the parent NR/NMN clears from plasma within hours — but that number is beside the point; NAD⁺-pool turnover over days is what drives the effect
pk.duration: NAD⁺ drifts back toward baseline over days to weeks after you stop
pk.note: Like creatine, NMN/NR break the stopwatch rules. There's no acute blood-peak-to-effect curve to time — it's a daily habit that keeps the NAD⁺ pool topped up. Morning dosing is recommended because NAD⁺ tracks your body clock and peaks in the active phase.

journey.d: You take 250–500 mg of NR or NMN, ideally in the morning. It dissolves and is absorbed across your gut.

Much of it is broken down to nicotinamide (and NR) right here — the intact NMN molecule mostly doesn't survive the trip to distant tissue. What travels on is largely raw material for the salvage pathway.
Inside cells, kinases and NMNAT press the precursor into fresh NAD⁺ (NR → NMN → NAD⁺). This is the same recycling line your body runs all day; you're just feeding it more stock.
The replenished NAD⁺ fuels sirtuins, PARP DNA repair and energy metabolism. Measured in blood, NAD⁺ climbs over about two weeks and holds.
Surplus nicotinamide is disposed of by methylation — which spends methyl groups. That's the reasoning behind stacking TMG (a methyl donor) to replace them.
Methylated and oxidised metabolites (like N-methylnicotinamide) leave in your urine.

myths.myth: NMN is clearly better than NR because it's 'closer' to NAD⁺.
myths.truth: No human head-to-head shows either one beats the other, and both raise NAD⁺. On top of that, NMN likely has to be dephosphorylated back to NR to enter cells anyway — so 'one step closer' on paper doesn't translate into proven potency. NR simply has more human data and a cleaner legal status; NMN is newer and the most hyped.

myths.myth: Boosting NAD⁺ reverses ageing in people the way it does in mice.
myths.truth: The animal data is genuinely spectacular; the human data is real but modest. NAD⁺ rises reliably, but the downstream clinical outcomes are small, mixed, and matter most after 40. Don't read rodent headlines as human promises.

myths.myth: Swallowing NMN/NR floods your tissues with the intact molecule.
myths.truth: Much of the dose is cleaved to nicotinamide in the gut and liver first. Your body largely rebuilds NAD⁺ via the salvage pathway from that raw material — so 'more NAD⁺ in your blood' doesn't guarantee 'more NAD⁺ exactly where you wanted it.'

myths.myth: An NAD⁺ IV drip is the best way to do this.
myths.truth: It's the fastest and priciest, but the least evidence per dollar — and IVs from unlicensed providers carry real risk. It's a clinic procedure, not an obvious upgrade over a daily capsule.

whenToUse.intro: It's an OTC supplement — here's how to actually use it, honestly:
items: **Pick your form for the right reason:** NR for the cleanest legal status and the most human data; NMN if you specifically want the newer, most-hyped precursor and accept that it's a US legal grey area.
**250–500 mg in the morning.** NAD⁺ tracks your body clock and peaks in the active phase, so daytime dosing fits the rhythm.
**Consider stacking TMG** (trimethylglycine) to replace the methyl groups spent clearing excess nicotinamide; pterostilbene is a common companion too.
**Treat it as a daily habit, not a pre-workout hit.** The benefit builds over weeks as the NAD⁺ pool refills — expect quiet, modest effects, most noticeable after 40.
**Mind the real cautions:** NMN's US status is unsettled (an FDA drug-exclusion ruling) — NR is the clean supplement. No prescription drug is involved, but skip NAD⁺ IVs from unlicensed providers.

start.q: In one line: do NMN and NR give your cells energy?
start.a: No — they're raw material your cells forge into NAD⁺, the coenzyme that energy metabolism, sirtuins and DNA repair actually run on. The precursor itself does nothing.

how.q: What do NR and NMN actually raise, and how do the two differ?
how.a: Both feed the salvage pathway to raise NAD⁺. NR is one step earlier (NR → NMN → NAD⁺); NMN is one step closer but likely converts back to NR to get into the cell. Neither is proven more potent.

use.q: Why does day-to-day timing barely matter, and why take it in the morning?
use.a: The effect comes from keeping the NAD⁺ pool topped up over weeks, not from a blood peak — so it's a daily top-up. Morning aligns with NAD⁺'s circadian high.

evidence.q: What's solid about the human evidence, and what's shaky?
evidence.a: Solid: oral dosing reliably raises NAD⁺ in people. Shaky: whether that produces big clinical benefits — impressive in mice, real-but-modest and inconsistent in humans.

canExplain: Why NMN and NR act on no receptor — they're precursors feeding the NAD⁺ salvage pathway, like creatine feeds phosphocreatine
What NAD⁺ actually does, why it falls with age (partly thanks to CD38), and why that matters
How NMN and NR differ — one phosphate apart — and why neither is proven superior
Why the rodent data dwarfs the human results, and how strong the human evidence really is
Why NR is the clean legal choice while NMN sits in a US grey area, and why to avoid unlicensed NAD⁺ IVs

selfTest.q: A friend insists NMN must beat NR because it's 'closer to NAD⁺.' What's the catch?
selfTest.a: NMN likely has to be dephosphorylated back to NR to cross into cells, and no human head-to-head shows it beats NR — both raise NAD⁺ similarly. 'Closer' on the pathway diagram doesn't equal more potent in a person.

selfTest.q: Mouse studies show dramatic reversal of metabolic and vascular ageing, yet a healthy 30-year-old might feel nothing. Why the gap?
selfTest.a: Humans metabolise much of the dose to nicotinamide before it reaches tissue, dose far lower per kilo than the mice, and the interesting endpoints are slow and subtle. NAD⁺ rises reliably, but the downstream clinical effect is modest — and biggest in those who started depleted, typically after 40.

selfTest.q: Why do people stack TMG with NMN or NR?
selfTest.a: Disposing of surplus nicotinamide happens by methylation (to N-methylnicotinamide), which spends methyl groups. TMG (trimethylglycine) is a methyl donor that replaces them, addressing the theoretical methyl drain.

evi.howStrong: The human evidence splits cleanly in two. That oral NR — and NMN — raises NAD⁺ in people is well established: multiple randomized, placebo-controlled trials show a reproducible, dose-dependent rise in blood NAD⁺, and the compounds are well tolerated. Whether that translates into meaningful clinical benefit is where the evidence thins — trials are mostly small, short, and scattered across different outcomes, so the picture is 'promising and real, but modest and not yet settled.' That's why this earns 3/5: well ahead of a random supplement, well behind creatine or caffeine.
evi.whatItShows: Consistently, the biomarker moves — NAD⁺ goes up. Functionally, the single strongest human signal is an improvement in skeletal-muscle insulin sensitivity in prediabetic women (Yoshino 2021); elsewhere — blood pressure, physical performance, inflammation — results are mixed or null. The honest read: NAD⁺ elevation is proven, downstream benefit is compound- and context-dependent.
evi.effect: In numbers you can trust: whole-blood NAD⁺ rose roughly 22%, 51% and 142% at 100, 300 and 1000 mg of NR over 8 weeks (Conze 2019). Where clinical effects appear at all, the effect sizes are small — modest metabolic and functional gains, nothing like the dramatic reversals seen in rodents.
evi.whoBenefits: People starting with lower or more stressed NAD⁺ — older adults and the metabolically impaired — have the most headroom and the clearest signals; the benefits 'matter more after 40.' Young, healthy people can raise their NAD⁺ and still feel little.
evi.caveats: Trials are small, short, and heterogeneous, so it's easy to cherry-pick a win. Much of the excitement rests on animal work that doesn't scale linearly to humans. Long-term safety data remain limited, and because much of the precursor is broken down before reaching tissue, 'more NAD⁺ in blood' doesn't guarantee 'more NAD⁺ where you want it.'

trials.finding: Chronic nicotinamide riboside supplementation was well-tolerated and elevated blood NAD⁺ in healthy middle-aged and older adults (a small placebo-controlled RCT), with a suggestion of reduced blood pressure/arterial stiffness in those with elevated readings.
trials.ref: Martens et al., 2018, Nature Communications

trials.finding: NMN 250 mg/day for 10 weeks increased skeletal-muscle insulin sensitivity and insulin signalling in prediabetic postmenopausal women, without changing several other metabolic indices.
trials.ref: Yoshino et al., 2021, Science

trials.finding: NR dose-dependently raised whole-blood NAD⁺ (~22%, 51%, 142% at 100, 300, 1000 mg) and was well tolerated over 8 weeks in overweight adults.
trials.ref: Conze et al., 2019, Scientific Reports

tech.chem: NR (nicotinamide riboside) is nicotinamide joined to a ribose sugar — a vitamin-B3-family molecule. NMN (nicotinamide mononucleotide, PubChem CID 14180) is NR plus a phosphate group. Both are natural intermediates of the NAD⁺ salvage pathway; niacin (nicotinic acid) and nicotinamide are the classical B3 precursors that reach the same NAD⁺ endpoint by other routes. None is a hormone or a stimulant — they are metabolic building blocks.

targets.action: rate-limiting salvage enzyme; recycles nicotinamide → NMN
targets.affinity: an enzyme in the pathway, not a drug-binding receptor
targets.role: the bottleneck of NAD⁺ synthesis — notably, NR bypasses it by entering one step downstream via NRK

targets.action: adenylylates NMN → NAD⁺ using ATP
targets.affinity: an enzyme substrate step, not a receptor
targets.role: the final press that stamps out finished NAD⁺ from either precursor

targets.action: NAD⁺-consuming deacylase; a downstream beneficiary of a refilled pool
targets.affinity: its substrate/cofactor is NAD⁺, not NR or NMN directly
targets.role: a key reason NAD⁺ matters — links NAD⁺ level to metabolic and stress-response gene programs

targets.action: proposed direct NMN transporter (reported in mouse gut)
targets.affinity: disputed — independent groups have questioned whether it transports NMN at all
targets.role: contested; competing view is that NMN is dephosphorylated to NR before uptake

tech.signaling: There is no receptor cascade — these work through bioenergetics and enzyme supply, not signalling. Raising the NAD⁺ pool feeds three NAD⁺-dependent enzyme families: sirtuins (SIRT1–7, deacylation driving metabolic/stress-response programs), PARPs (DNA-damage repair), and CD38/CD157 (NAD⁺-consuming ectoenzymes whose activity rises with age and inflammation, driving the decline the precursors aim to offset). More NAD⁺ means more capacity for all three; which tissue feels it depends on where NAD⁺ was limiting.

adme.absorb: NR and NMN are absorbed orally but largely cleaved to nicotinamide (and NR) in the gut and liver; intact NMN reaching the systemic circulation is limited. NMN is proposed to enter cells via SLC12A8 (disputed) or after dephosphorylation to NR.
adme.distribute: NAD⁺ itself does not cross cell membranes; tissues rebuild NAD⁺ locally from precursors delivered largely as nicotinamide/NR via enterohepatic and salvage routes. Whole-blood NAD⁺ rises measurably within about two weeks and plateaus.
adme.metabolise: Precursors converge on the salvage pathway (NRK → NMNAT, or NAMPT → NMNAT) to form NAD⁺; NAD⁺ is then consumed by sirtuins, PARPs and CD38, regenerating nicotinamide to be recycled again.
adme.excrete: Surplus nicotinamide is methylated (via NNMT, spending SAM-derived methyl groups) to N-methylnicotinamide and oxidised metabolites, excreted in urine — the 'methyl drain' behind TMG co-supplementation.

tech.pgx: No single-gene pharmacogenetic switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. Response is governed by baseline NAD⁺ status — age, metabolic health, and CD38-driven NAD⁺ consumption (which rises with age and inflammation). NNMT activity shapes how quickly surplus nicotinamide (and methyl groups) is turned over.
tech.dose: NR or NMN 250–500 mg once daily, morning. Human NR trials have spanned roughly 100–2000 mg/day and been well tolerated to ≥1000 mg/day. Commonly stacked with TMG (methyl support) and pterostilbene. No established need to cycle.
tech.tolerance: No receptor down-regulation, so the effect doesn't 'wear off' in the tachyphylaxis sense. The ceiling is instead the saturable capacity of NAD⁺ synthesis — NAD⁺ elevation plateaus, so higher doses give diminishing returns. Stop, and NAD⁺ drifts back toward baseline over days to weeks.

nmn-nr-nad-precursors.stakes: Often called the most exciting longevity lever available over the counter right now — because NAD⁺ sits at the hub of energy metabolism, sirtuins and DNA repair, and it falls by roughly half across an adult lifespan. The open question is how much of the rodent magic survives the trip into humans.

contrasts.title: NMN vs NR — the same road, one step apart
contrasts.point: They're the same salvage pathway one phosphate apart. NR is the smaller, earlier precursor; a kinase (NRK) phosphorylates it into NMN inside the cell, then NMNAT builds NAD⁺. NMN is one step closer to NAD⁺ but larger — and likely has to shed its phosphate back to NR to cross into cells at all. Net effect: both raise NAD⁺, and the real differences are legal status and depth of human data, not a proven potency gap.

contrasts.title: NAD⁺ precursors vs niacin — same destination, older road
contrasts.point: Niacin (nicotinic acid) is the cheap classic B3 precursor that also raises NAD⁺ — but it triggers a prostaglandin-mediated skin flush by activating the HCAR2 (GPR109A) receptor. NR and NMN reach NAD⁺ without that flush. So niacin is the value option with a felt side effect; NR/NMN are the flush-free, pricier route to the same coenzyme.

questions: Resveratrol is famous as the "red wine molecule" — so how many glasses would you actually have to drink to hit the dose used in the studies?
If it flips on the same longevity genes that fasting does, why have the human results been so underwhelming?
Why does its near-twin pterostilbene — almost the same molecule — work so much better in the body?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Hundreds of glasses a day — the alcohol would harm you long before the resveratrol helped; the 'red wine' framing is marketing, not pharmacology. (2) Resveratrol barely reaches your blood — under ~1% survives as the free molecule, because your gut and liver bolt sugar and sulphate tags onto it within minutes; and the celebrated 'it directly switches on SIRT1' finding turned out to be largely a test-tube artifact. (3) Pterostilbene carries two methyl caps where resveratrol has bare hydroxyls — that small change makes it far more fat-loving, so it dodges the tagging enzymes, absorbs several-fold better, and lingers far longer.

resveratrol-pterostilbene.bigIdea: Both polyphenols are trying to press the same 'longevity switch' that fasting presses — the sirtuin/AMPK program — but the entire story of resveratrol is that it barely reaches your cells, and pterostilbene is the chemistry fix for that delivery problem, not a stronger or different drug.
resveratrol-pterostilbene.stakes: Resveratrol is arguably the most hyped molecule in the whole longevity market — a decade of 'red wine holds the secret to long life' headlines, built on yeast and mouse data that has stubbornly refused to reproduce in people. Learning to read it teaches the single most important lesson in supplements: a molecule that works in a dish is worthless if it never reaches your cells.
resveratrol-pterostilbene.analogy: Resveratrol is a letter with the right message but no envelope: the moment you post it (swallow it), the sorting office (your gut wall and liver) stamps it into oblivion before it can be delivered. Pterostilbene is the same letter sealed in a waterproof envelope — the two methyl caps let it slip past the stampers and actually arrive.

mechSteps.t: Fasting turns on a survival program
mechSteps.predict: When you go hungry, your cells switch on an ancient 'lean times' program. Name one of the master genes it runs through.
mechSteps.d: Caloric restriction activates a network built around the sirtuins (notably SIRT1) and the energy sensor AMPK, which together switch on PGC-1α and push cells toward mitochondrial repair and fat-burning. This is the program these polyphenols are trying to borrow.

mechSteps.t: The polyphenols nudge the same switch — probably indirectly
mechSteps.predict: The famous claim is that resveratrol 'directly binds and activates SIRT1'. What's the catch that later research uncovered?
mechSteps.d: The original 'direct SIRT1 activator' result leaned on an artificial peptide carrying a fluorescent tag, and the effect largely vanished with the natural substrate — an assay artifact. The more supported route is indirect: resveratrol inhibits cAMP-degrading enzymes (PDE4), which raises cAMP and activates AMPK, with SIRT1 engaged downstream.

mechSteps.t: The catch: resveratrol barely gets in
mechSteps.predict: You swallow a big dose of resveratrol. Roughly how much reaches your blood as the intact, active molecule?
mechSteps.d: Most of it is absorbed, but under ~1% circulates as free resveratrol — your gut wall and liver conjugate it with glucuronide and sulphate within minutes, and the parent molecule's plasma half-life is on the order of minutes. The dose on the label is not the dose your cells see.

mechSteps.t: Pterostilbene's two methyl caps dodge the tagging
mechSteps.predict: Pterostilbene differs from resveratrol by swapping two -OH groups for -OCH₃. Why would that hugely change how much reaches your blood?
mechSteps.d: Those two methoxy groups remove the exact handles the conjugating enzymes grab, and make the molecule ~4x more lipophilic. In animals oral bioavailability jumps to ~80% (vs ~20% for resveratrol) and the plasma half-life stretches to ~105 minutes (vs ~14 minutes) — far more drug, present far longer.

mechSteps.t: Downstream — but human proof stays thin
mechSteps.predict: Better delivery is real. Does that mean pterostilbene has stronger human longevity evidence?
mechSteps.d: Better absorption means more of the metabolic signalling (AMPK/PGC-1α, mitochondrial and anti-inflammatory effects) can plausibly happen — but pterostilbene has far fewer human trials than resveratrol, and the longevity claim itself remains unproven for either. Delivery solved does not equal benefit proven.

framework.target: The caloric-restriction signalling axis — SIRT1 (a sirtuin), the energy sensor AMPK, and their downstream hub PGC-1α. Note the honest wrinkle: whether resveratrol grabs SIRT1 directly is genuinely contested; the better-supported entry point is AMPK activation (via PDE4 inhibition), with sirtuins engaged downstream.
framework.affinity: There is no clean, trustworthy Ki to quote — and that is the whole point. The famous 'resveratrol activates SIRT1' potency came from an assay using a fluorophore-tagged peptide, and Pacholec et al. (2010) showed the compounds were binding the tag, not activating the natural enzyme. So instead of affinity, the number that actually governs the effect is bioavailability: how much intact molecule reaches your cells at all.
framework.adme: This is where the real action is. Resveratrol is well absorbed but almost entirely conjugated on first pass — under ~1% circulates free, parent half-life ~minutes. Pterostilbene's two methoxy groups block that conjugation and boost lipophilicity, lifting bioavailability to ~80% and half-life to ~105 min (rat data). Pterostilbene also crosses the blood–brain barrier far more readily.
framework.genetics: No single clean pharmacogenetic switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. What varies person to person is conjugation-enzyme (UGT/SULT) activity and gut-microbiome handling, which shift how much intact polyphenol you actually absorb — but this is far less characterised than the caffeine story, so treat individual response as uncertain rather than gene-predictable.
framework.evidence: Weak-to-moderate in humans (2/5). Resveratrol shows short-term metabolic signals in small trials (Timmers 2011) but no association with longevity in cohort data (Semba 2014). Pterostilbene has a couple of small human trials — blood pressure down, but LDL cholesterol up at higher doses (Riche 2014). No human trial demonstrates a lifespan or healthspan benefit for either.

pk.halfLife: resveratrol ~14 min; pterostilbene ~105 min (parent molecule, rat plasma)
pk.note: These half-lives are the crux of the whole comparison, not a footnote. Resveratrol's free form is gone in minutes and under ~1% ever circulates intact, which is the leading explanation for its flat human results. Pterostilbene's methyl caps stretch that window roughly seven-fold and lift absorption several-fold — the entire pharmacological case for choosing it. (Numbers are animal PK; human free-fraction data are sparse.)

journey.d: Resveratrol comes from grape skins, red wine and Japanese knotweed; pterostilbene from blueberries and grapevines. You take a capsule — the wine dose is trivially small by comparison.
Resveratrol crosses the gut wall — and is immediately ambushed. Conjugating enzymes tag it with glucuronide and sulphate within minutes, so almost none reaches your blood as the active free molecule.
The dimethylated cousin has no bare hydroxyls for those enzymes to grab and is far more fat-loving, so a large fraction survives intact, rides the blood longer, and even crosses into the brain.

Whatever reaches the cell nudges the caloric-restriction machinery — AMPK, then sirtuins and PGC-1α — toward mitochondrial repair, fat-burning and lower inflammation. How much of this happens at real human doses is the open question.
Both are cleared quickly as their conjugated metabolites in urine and bile. Resveratrol's rapid clearance is exactly why holding a useful blood level is so hard.

myths.myth: Drinking red wine gives you resveratrol's longevity benefits.
myths.truth: A glass of red wine holds roughly a milligram of resveratrol; the human studies used hundreds of milligrams. You'd need hundreds of glasses a day to approach the study dose — the alcohol would harm you long before the resveratrol could help. The 'French paradox' wine story is not a dosing plan.

myths.myth: Resveratrol directly switches on your longevity gene SIRT1.
myths.truth: The original evidence for direct activation used an artificial fluorophore-tagged substrate, and the effect largely disappeared with the natural substrate (Pacholec 2010). The real mechanism is indirect and still debated — most likely AMPK activation via PDE inhibition, with sirtuins engaged downstream.

myths.myth: Pterostilbene is just a strictly better resveratrol — all upside.
myths.truth: Its far superior absorption is real and is the honest reason to prefer it. But 'better delivered' isn't 'better proven': human trials are far fewer, and high-dose pterostilbene raised LDL cholesterol in a randomized trial. Better pharmacokinetics, unproven outcomes.

myths.myth: Weak human evidence means it definitely doesn't work.
myths.truth: Not quite — it means we don't yet know. The molecules do produce real metabolic signals in short trials; what's missing is proof that this translates into a lasting health or lifespan benefit at tolerable doses. 'Unproven' is honest; 'debunked' would overstate it.

whenToUse.intro: Put it together — here's how to approach it as a consumer, honestly:
items: **Prefer pterostilbene over plain resveratrol** if you're going to try either — the absorption difference is the one thing that's genuinely well established. A typical dose is 100–250 mg/day.
**Set expectations low.** No human trial shows a longevity or healthspan benefit for either. You're buying a plausible metabolic nudge, not a proven anti-ageing effect.
**Watch your lipids.** Higher-dose pterostilbene raised LDL cholesterol in a randomized trial. If you supplement it, it's reasonable to check a lipid panel rather than assume it's inert.
**It's often paired with NMN or NR** on the theory of feeding the same NAD⁺/sirtuin pathway — reasonable in principle, but the combination isn't proven in humans either.
**Don't rely on red wine as the delivery method.** The dose is negligible and the alcohol carries its own well-documented risks — that's a worse trade, not a bonus.

**Skip the fancy resveratrol forms** promising 'enhanced absorption' unless the actual data is shown; the fundamental conjugation problem is hard to beat, which is why the methylated molecule (pterostilbene) exists at all.

start.q: In one line: what switch are these two polyphenols trying to press, and what natural state normally presses it?
start.a: The sirtuin/AMPK 'caloric-restriction' program — the same survival program that fasting turns on.

how.q: Why does resveratrol disappoint in humans even though it looks powerful in a dish?
how.a: Under ~1% reaches your blood as the active molecule — the gut and liver conjugate it within minutes — so your cells never see the concentration that worked in vitro.

use.q: You're choosing between resveratrol and pterostilbene. Which and why?
use.a: Pterostilbene — its two methyl caps resist the conjugating enzymes, giving several-fold better absorption and a far longer half-life. It's the delivery problem solved.

evidence.q: How strong is the human longevity evidence, really?
evidence.a: Weak (2/5). Short-term metabolic signals exist, but cohort data found no link between resveratrol and mortality, and no trial shows a lifespan benefit for either compound.

canExplain: Why the 'red wine molecule' framing is marketing rather than a real dosing strategy
Why resveratrol looks strong in a test tube but disappoints in people — the sub-1% bioavailability problem
Why the celebrated 'direct SIRT1 activator' claim was largely an assay artifact, and what the likelier mechanism is
How two methyl groups turn resveratrol into the far better-absorbed pterostilbene
How weak the human longevity evidence actually is — and the LDL caution specific to pterostilbene

selfTest.q: A friend says he drinks red wine 'for the resveratrol and the longevity benefits.' Give him the two-part reality check.
selfTest.a: First, dose: a glass has ~1 mg while studies used hundreds — he'd need hundreds of glasses a day, and the alcohol would harm him first. Second, evidence: even at study doses, no human trial shows a longevity benefit, and cohort data found no link between resveratrol levels and mortality.

selfTest.q: Resveratrol and pterostilbene differ by just two methyl groups. Explain how that tiny change produces such a large difference in the body.
selfTest.a: The methyl groups replace the hydroxyls that conjugating enzymes (UGT/SULT) grab, so pterostilbene resists first-pass tagging, and they make it more lipophilic. Result: bioavailability ~80% vs ~20% and half-life ~105 min vs ~14 min — far more intact molecule, present far longer, including in the brain.

selfTest.q: Someone insists resveratrol 'directly activates the longevity enzyme SIRT1.' Why is that claim shaky, and what's the better-supported mechanism?
selfTest.a: The direct-activation data used a fluorophore-tagged artificial substrate; the compound bound the tag, and the effect largely vanished with the natural substrate (Pacholec 2010). The better-supported route is indirect — inhibiting PDE4 raises cAMP and activates AMPK, with sirtuins engaged downstream.

evi.howStrong: Weak-to-moderate and, crucially, inconsistent. Resveratrol has been studied in many small, short human trials — mostly weeks long, in specific groups like obese or type-2-diabetic adults — plus meta-analyses of metabolic markers. But the trials are small and heterogeneous, effects on hard outcomes are inconsistent, and a large observational cohort found no link to longevity. Pterostilbene has only a couple of small human trials. Nothing approaches the settled, hundreds-of-trials evidence base of caffeine or creatine.
evi.whatItShows: The most reproducible human signal is metabolic and short-term: modest shifts in blood pressure, glucose/insulin markers and inflammation in some trials, with the clearest single mechanistic study showing caloric-restriction-like changes in obese men. What's missing is any demonstration that these short-term signals translate into a lasting health or lifespan benefit.
evi.effect: Effects where seen are small and not consistent across studies — this is not a compound with a reliable, quotable effect size like caffeine's ~11% endurance boost. For pterostilbene specifically, the notable measured effects were a blood-pressure reduction and, as a downside, an LDL-cholesterol increase at higher doses.
evi.whoBenefits: If anyone, the metabolic signals appear in people with something to correct — elevated glucose, blood pressure or weight. Healthy young people have little documented to gain. No subgroup has demonstrated a longevity benefit.
evi.caveats: Beware the animal-to-human gap: the dramatic yeast/mouse lifespan data drove the hype but has not reproduced in people, largely because human bioavailability of resveratrol is so poor. Trials are small, short and industry-adjacent in places; publication bias likely inflates the positive metabolic signals. And pterostilbene's LDL increase is a real, measured caution, not a theoretical one.

trials.finding: 30 days of resveratrol 150 mg/day in obese men produced caloric-restriction-like effects: activated AMPK, raised SIRT1 and PGC-1α, improved muscle mitochondrial respiration, and lowered blood pressure, glucose and inflammatory markers.
trials.ref: Timmers et al., 2011, Cell Metabolism

trials.finding: In 783 community-dwelling adults over 65 (InCHIANTI), urinary resveratrol metabolite levels were not associated with inflammatory markers, cardiovascular disease, cancer, or all-cause mortality over ~9 years.
trials.ref: Semba et al., 2014, JAMA Internal Medicine

trials.finding: Randomized placebo-controlled trial in hypercholesterolemic adults: high-dose pterostilbene (125 mg twice daily) reduced systolic (−7.8 mmHg) and diastolic (−7.3 mmHg) blood pressure but raised LDL cholesterol (+17.1 mg/dL) as monotherapy.
trials.ref: Riche et al., 2014, Journal of Toxicology / Evidence-Based Complementary Med

trials.finding: Landmark in-vitro reappraisal: resveratrol and related 'sirtuin-activating compounds' did not activate SIRT1 against native substrates; the apparent activation was an artifact of the fluorophore-tagged peptide used in the original assay.
trials.ref: Pacholec et al., 2010, Journal of Biological Chemistry

contrasts.title: Resveratrol vs pterostilbene — two methyl caps change everything
contrasts.point: Look at the two structures: near-twins built on the same stilbene backbone. The only difference is that two of resveratrol's -OH groups become -OCH₃ (methyl ethers) in pterostilbene. Those two tiny caps remove the exact handles the conjugating enzymes grab and make the molecule far more fat-loving — turning a molecule that's ~20% absorbed with a ~14-min half-life into one that's ~80% absorbed with a ~105-min half-life. Same message, vastly better envelope.

contrasts.title: Resveratrol vs actual fasting — the mimic vs the real thing
contrasts.point: Resveratrol is sold as a 'caloric-restriction mimetic' — a pill that fakes the metabolic state of fasting. But fasting presses the sirtuin/AMPK program directly and reliably, with strong human data; resveratrol presses it faintly, indirectly, and only if the sub-1% that reaches your blood is enough. The mimic is a plausible echo of the real thing, not a substitute for it.

tech.chem: Both are stilbenoid polyphenols. Resveratrol is trans-3,5,4′-trihydroxystilbene (C₁₄H₁₂O₃, PubChem CID 445154); pterostilbene is its 3,5-dimethyl ether, trans-3,5-dimethoxy-4′-hydroxystilbene (C₁₆H₁₆O₃, CID 5281727). Replacing two hydroxyls with methoxy groups removes primary sites for phase-II conjugation and raises lipophilicity (~4x), which is the entire basis for pterostilbene's superior pharmacokinetics. Both occur naturally as plant phytoalexins (grape skin/knotweed for resveratrol; blueberries/grapevine for pterostilbene).

targets.action: claimed direct allosteric activator — but direct activation is contested; likely engaged downstream of AMPK
targets.affinity: no reliable Ki; the original potency was an artifact of a fluorophore-tagged substrate (Pacholec 2010)
targets.role: the marquee target of the caloric-restriction hypothesis, but probably not the primary point of contact

targets.action: activated (indirectly), raising the phosphocreatine-sensing energy-stress response and PGC-1α
targets.affinity: activated secondarily — via PDE4 inhibition → ↑cAMP, and at high concentrations via mild mitochondrial inhibition
targets.role: the better-supported proximal mechanism in cell and human muscle data
