targets.action: inhibited → raises cAMP → activates the CamKKβ/AMPK axis
targets.affinity: proposed primary molecular target for resveratrol's metabolic effects (Park et al., 2012)
targets.role: the leading explanation for how resveratrol reaches AMPK without directly touching SIRT1

tech.signaling: The intended cascade is the caloric-restriction axis: raise cellular AMP/cAMP stress signalling → activate AMPK → increase NAD⁺ and deacetylate/activate PGC-1α (with SIRT1 participating), driving mitochondrial biogenesis, fatty-acid oxidation and reduced NF-κB inflammatory signalling. The debated node is the entry point — direct SIRT1 binding (Sinclair camp) versus PDE4 inhibition upstream of AMPK (Chung/Park camp). Structural work (Hubbard/Sinclair, Genes Dev 2015) argues resveratrol can allosterically stimulate SIRT1 in a substrate-sequence-dependent way, so the truth is likely partial and context-dependent rather than all-or-nothing.

adme.absorb: Resveratrol: high uptake across the gut but extensive first-pass glucuronidation/sulphation, leaving <1% as free parent in plasma. Pterostilbene: ~80% oral bioavailability in animals owing to the methoxy groups resisting conjugation.
adme.distribute: Both lipophilic; pterostilbene's higher logP gives greater tissue and blood-brain-barrier penetration. Circulating resveratrol is overwhelmingly present as glucuronide/sulphate conjugates rather than free molecule.
adme.metabolise: Dominated by phase-II conjugation (UGT and SULT enzymes) in gut wall and liver, plus gut-microbial transformation. Pterostilbene's blocked hydroxyls slow this markedly.
adme.excrete: Rapid; mainly as conjugated metabolites in urine and bile. Resveratrol's free-form plasma half-life is on the order of minutes (~14 min); pterostilbene's parent half-life ~105 min (rat).

tech.pgx: No caffeine-style single-gene switch. Inter-individual variation is driven chiefly by UGT/SULT conjugation-enzyme activity and gut-microbiome composition (which govern how much intact polyphenol is absorbed), rather than a well-mapped functional polymorphism. Treat individual response as poorly predictable.
tech.dose: Resveratrol human trials commonly used 150 mg–1 g/day; pterostilbene trials used 50–250 mg/day (e.g., 125 mg twice daily). Given the bioavailability gap, a smaller pterostilbene dose can deliver more intact compound than a much larger resveratrol dose. High-dose pterostilbene (250 mg/day) was associated with raised LDL cholesterol.
tech.tolerance: No receptor down-regulation or classic tolerance is described. The practical ceiling is bioavailability (for resveratrol) and the LDL signal (for high-dose pterostilbene), not tachyphylaxis. Generally well tolerated short-term; long-term human safety data are limited.

questions: If fisetin is a plant pigment found in strawberries, why would you take 500 mg of it — hundreds of strawberries' worth — in a single day?
Why do you take it for just 2 days a month instead of every day like most supplements?
If the mouse studies were so striking, why does everyone still call the human evidence 'limited'?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Ordinary food doses clear damaged 'zombie' cells too weakly to matter; the senolytic effect only shows up at pharmacological doses far above what any diet delivers. (2) Killing a senescent cell is permanent — once it's dead it stays dead — so you only need a short, hard 'hit-and-run' pulse to clear the batch, then let them slowly re-accumulate before the next one. (3) The lifespan and healthspan data are almost entirely in mice; the human trials that would confirm it are running now but have not yet reported efficacy outcomes for ageing.

fisetin.bigIdea: Fisetin doesn't rejuvenate your cells — it kills the small population of damaged 'zombie' cells that refuse to die, by cutting the survival wires they depend on. Hold onto that one sentence and everything else on this page follows from it.
fisetin.analogy: A senescent cell is a smoke detector that has jammed in the 'on' position — it can't do its job anymore, but instead of switching off it sits there shrieking inflammation at everything nearby. Fisetin is the technician who walks the building and pulls the battery on the jammed units, while leaving every working detector untouched.

mechSteps.t: Some damaged cells refuse to die
mechSteps.predict: When a cell is too damaged to divide safely, the body usually retires it. What goes wrong when instead of dying quietly it just... stays?
mechSteps.d: As you age, some stressed cells enter senescence — they stop dividing but don't die. They linger and secrete a stew of inflammatory signals (the SASP) that damages the healthy tissue around them.

mechSteps.t: They survive by propping up anti-death shields
mechSteps.predict: A cell that should have self-destructed but hasn't must be actively blocking the self-destruct button. What kind of proteins would it lean on to do that?
mechSteps.d: Senescent cells stay alive by over-relying on 'survival' networks — the anti-apoptotic BCL-2/BCL-xL proteins and PI3K/AKT signalling. Mayo Clinic named these the Senescent Cell Anti-Apoptotic Pathways (SCAPs). They are the wires keeping the zombie plugged in.

mechSteps.t: Fisetin cuts the wires
mechSteps.predict: If a senescent cell is only alive because it's straining to hold the self-destruct button down, what happens when you jam that mechanism?
mechSteps.d: Fisetin interferes with several SCAPs at once. Tipped past their apoptotic threshold, the senescent cells finally self-destruct. Healthy cells, which aren't leaning on those shields, are largely spared — that selectivity is the whole point of a senolytic.

mechSteps.t: Hit-and-run, then re-accumulate
mechSteps.predict: Fisetin is gone from your blood within hours. So why can a 2-day dose keep working for weeks?
mechSteps.d: Death is permanent — a cleared cell doesn't come back. So the drug only needs to be present long enough to trigger the kill, not to stay around. This is the 'hit-and-run' logic behind a short monthly pulse rather than daily dosing.

framework.target: The Senescent Cell Anti-Apoptotic Pathways (SCAPs) — chiefly the BCL-2 / BCL-xL anti-apoptotic proteins and PI3K/AKT survival signalling that keep zombie cells alive. Note the key difference from a drug like caffeine: fisetin has no single receptor it switches on or off. It's a broad-acting flavonoid that nudges several survival pathways at once.
framework.affinity: There's no clean Ki to quote, and that's the honest truth about fisetin: it is a promiscuous flavonoid, not a potent, selective inhibitor of any one target. In cell studies it kills senescent cells at low-micromolar concentrations (roughly 0.5–20 µM), and it was the most potent senolytic of 10 flavonoids screened — but 'most potent flavonoid' is a much weaker statement than the nanomolar precision of a designed BCL-xL drug like navitoclax.
framework.adme: This is fisetin's Achilles' heel. It's lipophilic and poorly water-soluble, so it's badly absorbed, and what does get in is hit by rapid phase-II conjugation (glucuronidation and sulfation) in the gut wall and liver — often leaving <10% of the dose as intact fisetin. Plasma half-life is short (~1–2 h). Taking it with fat helps absorption; this is also why 'enhanced-bioavailability' formulations exist.
framework.genetics: There's no single pharmacogenetic switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. What most shifts the response is your senescent-cell burden — which rises with age, obesity, and prior tissue damage — and how much intact fisetin your gut actually absorbs.
framework.evidence: Strong and striking in mice (reduced senescent markers, extended median and maximum lifespan when given late in life). In humans the picture is still open: trials are running but have not yet reported efficacy outcomes for ageing. Treat this as the most credible dietary senolytic, not a proven one.

journey.d: You swallow 500–1,000 mg — orders of magnitude more than any strawberry — ideally with a fatty meal, because fisetin dissolves in fat, not water.
Absorption is poor and immediate: enzymes in the gut lining (UGT and SULT) tag much of it with glucuronide and sulfate groups before it even reaches your bloodstream, blunting most of the dose.
A modest fraction of intact fisetin slips through and circulates. Plasma levels peak within about an hour and fade fast — it's barely detectable after ~2 hours.
Where it reaches senescent cells, it disrupts their SCAP survival network and pushes them into apoptosis — a selective cull of the zombies, sparing healthy neighbours.

The liver conjugates the rest and dumps it largely into bile; the flavonoid and its metabolites leave the body within hours. The drug is gone — but the cleared cells don't return, which is the whole idea.

myths.myth: Eating strawberries or a daily fisetin capsule gives you the senolytic benefit.
myths.truth: The zombie-cell clearing seen in studies happens at high, pulsed pharmacological doses (hundreds of mg). Ordinary dietary amounts — even a strawberry-heavy diet — are far too low, and fisetin's poor absorption makes the gap even wider.

myths.myth: If some is good, take it every day.
myths.truth: Senolytics are designed to be intermittent. The point is to kill a batch of senescent cells and stop — 'hit-and-run.' Daily dosing isn't how the mouse benefit was produced, and continuous senescent-cell killing isn't obviously safe or useful once the batch is cleared.

myths.myth: The mouse lifespan results mean it's proven in people.
myths.truth: They don't. The headline lifespan and healthspan data are in mice. Human trials (including senolytic trials run around the COVID era) are underway, but published efficacy outcomes for human ageing aren't in yet. 'Most credible dietary senolytic' is an honest ceiling — not 'proven.'

whenToUse.intro: A quick, honest guide — this is an experimental longevity supplement, not a proven therapy, so use it with clear eyes:
items: **The studied protocol:** 500–1,000 mg/day for 2 consecutive days, once a month — the intermittent 'hit-and-run' pulse, not daily use.
**Always take it with fat.** Fisetin is fat-soluble and poorly absorbed; a fatty meal meaningfully improves how much actually gets in.
**Don't expect to feel anything.** There is no acute effect to notice — this targets slow biological damage, not how you feel today. Judging it by 'how I feel' will mislead you.
**Know the honest evidence tier:** compelling in mice, human efficacy still unproven. You are an early adopter, not a patient on an established treatment.

**Check interactions if you take medication.** As a broad flavonoid it can affect drug-metabolising enzymes; if you're on prescriptions (especially blood thinners) or have a health condition, clear it with a doctor first.

start.q: In one line: does fisetin repair your cells or remove them?
start.a: It removes them — it selectively kills damaged 'senescent' zombie cells. It doesn't rejuvenate or repair anything directly.

how.q: What does fisetin actually interfere with to make a zombie cell die?
how.a: The senescent cell's survival network (SCAPs) — the BCL-2/BCL-xL and PI3K/AKT anti-apoptotic pathways it leans on to avoid self-destruction.

use.q: Why is it dosed as a 2-day monthly pulse instead of daily?
use.a: Because cell death is permanent — a 'hit-and-run' pulse clears the batch, and the cleared cells don't come back, so continuous dosing isn't needed.

evidence.q: How solid is the human evidence really?
evidence.a: The strong lifespan/healthspan data are in mice. Human trials are ongoing but haven't reported ageing-efficacy results yet — so it's the most credible dietary senolytic, not a proven one.

canExplain: Why fisetin clears damaged cells rather than repairing or energising them
What a 'senescent' zombie cell is and why the SASP it leaks is harmful
The SCAP survival pathways (BCL-2/BCL-xL, PI3K/AKT) and why cutting them kills zombies selectively
Why it's pulsed 2 days a month — the 'hit-and-run' logic — instead of taken daily
Why the mouse data are strong but the human evidence is honestly still limited

selfTest.q: A friend says 'I eat strawberries every day, so I'm already getting fisetin's anti-ageing effect.' Where does that reasoning break down?
selfTest.a: On dose. The senolytic effect appears only at high pulsed pharmacological doses (hundreds of mg), and fisetin absorbs poorly, so dietary amounts fall far short of clearing senescent cells.

selfTest.q: Fisetin's plasma half-life is only about 1–2 hours. How can a 2-day dose still matter weeks later?
selfTest.a: Because the effect isn't the drug lingering — it's the cells the drug killed. Apoptosis is permanent, so a brief 'hit-and-run' exposure clears the batch, and the benefit persists long after fisetin itself is gone.

selfTest.q: Why is fisetin called a 'senolytic' and not a receptor drug, and what does that cost it in precision?
selfTest.a: It doesn't bind and toggle one receptor — it's a broad flavonoid that nudges several senescent-cell survival pathways at once. That breadth is why it lacks a clean nanomolar affinity and is far less precise than a designed BCL-xL inhibitor.

evi.howStrong: Be honest about the tier here: the compelling data are preclinical. In cells and mice, fisetin is a genuine, repeatable senolytic — the most potent of a panel of 10 flavonoids screened, and one of the few dietary compounds shown to extend lifespan in aged mice. But the human clinical picture is early. Senolytic trials in older adults have been launched (including around the COVID-19 era), yet published, peer-reviewed efficacy outcomes for human ageing are not yet in. The curated 3/5 human-evidence rating reflects the strong mechanism and safety signal, not proven anti-ageing benefit in people.
evi.whatItShows: The mouse work shows two things clearly: fisetin lowers markers of senescence across multiple tissues, and — given late in life — it improves tissue health and extends both median and maximum lifespan. The 'hit-and-run' pattern (acute or intermittent dosing still reducing senescence) is what makes the monthly-pulse protocol biologically reasonable.
evi.effect: In humans, there is no verified effect-size number to give yet — which is itself the honest headline. The mouse lifespan extension is real but is a mouse result and does not translate to a human percentage. Anyone quoting a specific human 'years added' or biomarker figure is going beyond the published evidence.
evi.whoBenefits: Mechanistically, the people with the most senescent cells to clear — older adults, and those with high senescent burden from obesity or prior tissue damage — are the plausible responders, which is exactly why trials target older and post-illness populations. This is a reasoned expectation from the biology, not a proven human finding.
evi.caveats: The core caveat is the species gap: striking in mice, unproven in humans. Add poor and variable oral bioavailability (so the real dose delivered is uncertain), the absence of long-term human safety data at these doses, and fisetin's broad enzyme effects (possible drug interactions). Treat it as a promising, low-risk experiment — not an established therapy.

trials.finding: Fisetin was the most potent senolytic of 10 flavonoids screened; given to aged wild-type mice late in life it reduced senescence markers across tissues and extended median and maximum lifespan ('hit-and-run' senolytic).
trials.ref: Yousefzadeh et al., 2018, EBioMedicine

trials.finding: Identified fisetin (alongside BCL-XL inhibitors) as an agent that selectively clears senescent cells in vitro and in mice, mapping the senolytic action to anti-apoptotic survival pathways.
trials.ref: Zhu et al., 2017, Aging (Albany NY)

trials.finding: Design and rationale for an NIH-funded, placebo-controlled trial of pulsed oral fisetin (20 mg/kg) in older skilled-nursing residents with SARS-CoV-2 — a launched human senolytic trial, reporting rationale rather than efficacy results.
trials.ref: Verdoorn et al., 2021, J Am Geriatr Soc

tech.chem: 3,3',4',7-tetrahydroxyflavone — a naturally occurring flavonol (a flavonoid subclass) with a catechol B-ring, found in strawberries, apples, persimmons and onions (PubChem CID 5281614). Lipophilic and poorly water-soluble; the catechol and multiple hydroxyls make it a substrate for rapid phase-II conjugation, which dominates its disposition.

targets.action: interferes with anti-apoptotic BCL-2/BCL-xL survival signalling in senescent cells, lowering the apoptotic threshold
targets.affinity: not a high-affinity, selective inhibitor — senolytic effect seen at low-micromolar concentrations; far less potent/precise than designed BCL-xL drugs (e.g. navitoclax)
targets.role: a core node of the Senescent Cell Anti-Apoptotic Pathways (SCAPs); disrupting it is central to fisetin's selective clearing of zombie cells

targets.action: modulates PI3K–AKT anti-apoptotic signalling (also implicated in autophagy effects)
targets.affinity: broad, non-selective flavonoid modulation rather than defined-Ki binding
targets.role: a second SCAP that fisetin nudges; hitting multiple SCAPs at once is thought to underlie senolytic selectivity

tech.signaling: Fisetin is not a receptor ligand but a multi-pathway senolytic. By simultaneously interfering with several SCAPs — the BCL-2/BCL-xL family that blocks mitochondrial apoptosis and PI3K/AKT survival signalling — it tips senescent cells past their apoptotic threshold (caspase-3/7 activation) while largely sparing healthy dividing cells that don't depend on those shields. Clearing the senescent cells in turn removes their inflammatory SASP output.

adme.absorb: Poor and variable oral bioavailability; lipophilic and poorly water-soluble. Much of an oral dose is conjugated in the gut wall before reaching the portal blood — often leaving <10% as intact fisetin. Fat improves absorption; specialised formulations aim to raise Cmax/AUC.
adme.distribute: Lipophilic, distributes into tissues; intact plasma fisetin is low and transient (measurable only up to ~2 h in humans).
adme.metabolise: Extensive phase-II conjugation — glucuronidation (UGT) and sulfation (SULT) — beginning in the gut wall and continuing in the liver; conjugates are the dominant circulating species and are essentially inactive as senolytics.
adme.excrete: Largely biliary as sulfate and glucuronide conjugates; short plasma half-life (~1–2 h).

tech.pgx: No single dominant pharmacogenetic switch (unlike caffeine's CYP1A2). Inter-individual response is driven more by senescent-cell burden (age, obesity, tissue damage) and by variable absorption/first-pass conjugation than by one gene. As a broad flavonoid it can modulate drug-metabolising enzymes and transporters, so interaction potential — not a genotype test — is the practical concern.
tech.dose: Studied human protocol: 500–1,000 mg/day for 2 consecutive days, monthly (intermittent 'hit-and-run' pulse), taken with fat. Mouse senolytic dosing was ~100 mg/kg. Continuous daily dosing is not the regimen the benefit was demonstrated with.
tech.tolerance: No receptor down-regulation or classic tolerance — the effect is elimination of a cell population, not a signalling adaptation. The intermittent schedule reflects biology (clear the batch, let senescent cells slowly re-accumulate before the next pulse), not the need to avoid tachyphylaxis. Long-term human safety data at these doses are limited.

pk.peak: ~1 h (plasma), measurable only up to ~2 h
pk.note: Here's the twist that makes fisetin different from a stimulant: the blood curve does NOT track the effect. Fisetin is gone within hours, yet the cells it kills stay dead — so the benefit outlives the drug by weeks. That short half-life plus heavy first-pass metabolism is exactly why intact fisetin barely reaches your blood, and why it's dosed as a high, brief pulse rather than a steady daily level.

contrasts.title: Fisetin vs dasatinib + quercetin — the other senolytic
contrasts.point: Same goal, different toolkit. Fisetin is a single dietary flavonoid taken as a monthly pulse. 'D+Q' pairs a prescription cancer drug (dasatinib) with the flavonoid quercetin, and is the other leading senolytic in human trials. Both work by knocking out senescent cells' SCAP survival pathways — but D+Q uses a potent prescription agent, while fisetin is over-the-counter, gentler, and (in the head-to-head flavonoid screen) the more potent flavonoid. Different potency, different risk profile, same 'hit-and-run' clearing logic.

contrasts.title: Fisetin vs a receptor drug — culling vs signalling
contrasts.point: Most compounds on this site bind a receptor and turn a signal up or down while the drug is present. Fisetin does something categorically different: it removes cells. That's why its 1–2 h half-life is almost irrelevant to its effect, why it's pulsed rather than dosed to a steady level, and why you feel nothing acutely — the payoff is a slow reduction in biological damage, not a signal you can sense.

questions: How can a nine-day course of pills keep working for weeks or months after you stop taking them?
If dasatinib is a serious leukemia chemo drug, why is it being paired with a supplement you can buy at any health store?
Why does this combo need two different drugs instead of one 'zombie-cell killer'?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) It's a 'hit-and-run' drug: senescent cells survive by keeping their self-destruct button jammed down, and D+Q only has to knock that button loose once — the cell then kills itself and is gone for good, so the benefit outlasts the drug's ~3-5 h half-life by weeks. (2) Because zombie cells aren't identical: dasatinib (the chemo drug, hitting Src-family kinases and ephrin signalling) clears senescent fat-cell progenitors, while quercetin (the flavonoid, hitting PI3K/AKT and BCL-2) clears senescent endothelial cells — two different survival networks, two different drugs, complementary coverage. (3) The human evidence is genuinely early — a handful of tiny open-label pilots (14 lung-fibrosis patients, 9 kidney-disease patients) plus strong mouse data, not the large controlled trials that would prove it slows human ageing.

quercetin-dasatinib-d-q.bigIdea: D+Q doesn't repair old cells or continuously suppress anything — it briefly disables the survival wiring that lets 'zombie' senescent cells refuse to die, so they self-destruct and are cleared, and the benefit comes from their absence, not from the drug staying in your blood.
quercetin-dasatinib-d-q.analogy: Senescent cells are like faulty machines that should have shut themselves down but instead jammed their own kill-switch in the 'off' position and keep leaking smoke (inflammatory signals) over everything nearby. D+Q are two specialised tools that reach in and un-jam that kill-switch — one tool fits the fat-cell machines, the other fits the blood-vessel machines. Once the switch is freed, each machine finishes the shutdown it was always supposed to do. You don't have to keep holding the tool in place; you just have to trip the switch once.

mechSteps.t: Zombie cells cheat death by jamming their own kill-switch
mechSteps.predict: A senescent cell is damaged enough that it should self-destruct — yet it lingers for months. What must it be doing to its own apoptosis machinery to survive?
mechSteps.d: Senescent cells stop dividing but refuse to die. To pull that off, they crank up a set of pro-survival networks — collectively 'SCAPs' (senescent-cell anti-apoptotic pathways) — that hold their own self-destruct program (apoptosis) permanently switched off. That dependence is their weakness.

mechSteps.t: Dasatinib un-jams the switch in senescent fat-cell progenitors
mechSteps.predict: Dasatinib is a real tyrosine-kinase inhibitor. If it blocks the survival kinases one type of zombie cell leans on, what happens to that cell?
mechSteps.d: Dasatinib inhibits Src-family kinases and ephrin-dependent (EPHB) survival signalling that senescent fat-cell progenitors rely on. Take that prop away and those cells tip into the apoptosis they'd been suppressing.

mechSteps.t: Quercetin un-jams the switch in senescent endothelial cells
mechSteps.predict: Quercetin covers a different cell type. Which survival levers would it have to pull to kill senescent blood-vessel cells?
mechSteps.d: Quercetin inhibits the PI3K/AKT survival pathway and BCL-2-family anti-apoptotic proteins (and PAI-1/SERPINE1). Those are the props senescent endothelial cells depend on — so quercetin clears the cells dasatinib misses. Together they cover more cell types than either alone.

mechSteps.t: The cells die, and their toxic smoke clears with them
mechSteps.predict: Senescent cells constantly secrete inflammatory signals (the SASP). What happens to that inflammation once the cells are gone?
mechSteps.d: Senescent cells pump out the 'senescence-associated secretory phenotype' (SASP) — a cloud of inflammatory cytokines that damages neighbouring tissue. Remove the cells and the SASP source is gone, lowering local and systemic inflammation.

mechSteps.t: 'Hit and run' — why a short course lasts
mechSteps.predict: Dasatinib's half-life is only a few hours. So why can a nine-day course have effects weeks later?
mechSteps.d: Because clearance is a one-way door: a killed cell doesn't come back when the drug wears off. You only need brief, intermittent exposure to trip the switch, so the drug's short half-life is irrelevant to how long the benefit lasts. This is the opposite of a daily maintenance drug.

framework.target: Not one target but two survival networks in two cell types. Dasatinib = a broad tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (Src family, ABL1, ephrin/EPHB pathways) that removes the survival prop of senescent fat-cell progenitors. Quercetin = a flavonoid that inhibits PI3K/AKT and BCL-2/BCL-xL (and PAI-1) to remove the prop of senescent endothelial cells. Both are senolytics: they don't switch a receptor on or off for a felt effect — they push a specific defective cell into suicide.
framework.affinity: Two different stories. Dasatinib is a genuinely high-affinity, low-nanomolar kinase inhibitor — the same potency that makes it an approved leukemia drug is what makes it hit its senolytic targets at real doses. Quercetin is the opposite: a promiscuous, relatively low-potency polyphenol that touches many enzymes weakly and is famously poorly absorbed — which is one honest reason the flavonoid half of the combo is the less certain half.
framework.adme: Dasatinib: orally absorbed, cleared by liver CYP3A4, eliminated mainly in feces, elimination half-life ≈ 3-5 h. Quercetin: poor oral bioavailability, extensively conjugated in gut and liver, so blood levels are low and variable. Crucially, for a hit-and-run senolytic the felt effect is decoupled from these blood curves — clearance of the cells, not drug level, is what persists.
framework.genetics: No validated senolytic-specific pharmacogenetic switch. Dasatinib exposure is affected by CYP3A4 activity and strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers and acid-reducing drugs (a real interaction concern), but this governs dasatinib's oncology safety, not a proven senolytic-response gene. Treat individual response as uncharacterised.
framework.evidence: Foundational but early. Strong, landmark mouse data (clears senescent cells, improves physical function, extends remaining lifespan in aged mice) and a compelling mechanism — but human evidence is a few very small open-label pilot trials showing biomarker and short-term function changes, not large controlled trials proving it slows human ageing or disease.

journey.d: You take dasatinib (a prescription chemo tablet) and quercetin (a flavonoid supplement) together, typically as a short intermittent course rather than daily. Both are absorbed across the gut — dasatinib well, quercetin poorly and variably.

Both circulate and seek out cells. Dasatinib distributes and reaches its kinase targets at meaningful levels; quercetin is rapidly conjugated so free levels stay low. Their job is done in tissue, not in the bloodstream.

Dasatinib disarms the survival kinases of senescent fat-cell progenitors; quercetin disarms the PI3K/BCL-2 survival network of senescent endothelial cells. Each un-jams a stuck kill-switch, and the cell finishes the apoptosis it had been blocking.

The dying senescent cells are cleared, and with them goes their SASP — the inflammatory smoke they were leaking. Senescent-cell markers and circulating SASP factors drop, which is exactly what the human kidney-disease pilot measured.

Dasatinib is broken down by CYP3A4 and leaves mainly in feces within hours; quercetin's conjugates are excreted too. The drugs are long gone — but because the cells they killed don't return, the effect outlives the dose by weeks.

myths.myth: It's basically a supplement stack you can safely DIY.
myths.truth: Half of it is dasatinib — an FDA-approved leukemia chemotherapy with serious documented risks (fluid retention, pleural effusion, bleeding, cytopenias, QT effects, drug interactions). The senolytic use is off-label and investigational. This belongs under a clinician, not in a self-mixed longevity stack.

myths.myth: The human trials prove it reverses ageing.
myths.truth: They don't. The strongest human data so far are tiny open-label pilots — 14 IPF patients and 9 diabetic-kidney-disease patients — showing feasibility, reduced senescent-cell markers, and short-term physical-function signals. That's promising proof-of-concept, not proof it extends healthspan or lifespan in people. The lifespan extension is a mouse result.

myths.myth: Quercetin alone does the same thing.
myths.truth: On its own quercetin is a weak senolytic with poor bioavailability. In the original work the combination was synergistic and covered cell types quercetin couldn't clear by itself. 'Quercetin as a standalone senolytic' overstates what the data support.

myths.myth: You need to take it continuously for it to work.
myths.truth: It's the reverse. Senolytics are 'hit-and-run' — brief, intermittent dosing is enough to trip the kill-switch, and continuous exposure would only add toxicity without adding benefit, since dead cells don't come back.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** it's the original, most-cited senolytic combination, with a genuinely elegant mechanism and landmark mouse data showing cleared senescent cells, better physical function, and longer remaining lifespan. The appeal is real; the human proof is not yet there.

**The evidence cost:** human data is limited to small open-label pilots (n=14 IPF, n=9 diabetic kidney disease) measuring biomarkers and short-term function — no large randomized controlled trial has shown it slows ageing or disease in people. Treat mouse lifespan results as hypothesis, not promise.

**The dasatinib cost:** this is real chemotherapy. Documented risks include fluid retention and pleural/pericardial effusions, bleeding, low blood counts, and cardiac (QT) effects, plus significant drug and food interactions via CYP3A4. These are not supplement-grade risks.
**The quercetin caveat:** poorly absorbed and promiscuous; it also inhibits drug-metabolising enzymes, which can raise levels of other medications. It is not the harmless half it looks like.

**The supervision point:** every published human protocol was physician-run with monitoring and intermittent 'hit-and-run' dosing. The curated bottom line is blunt for a reason — dasatinib's toxicity makes this clinician-only, not a DIY stack.

**The honest picture:** foundational senolytic research worth understanding deeply — but this page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to hand you a protocol. If ageing-related disease is the concern, this is a conversation for a doctor tracking the actual trials.

start.q: In one line: what does D+Q actually do to old cells?
start.a: It doesn't repair them — it pushes damaged 'zombie' (senescent) cells that have been refusing to die into finally self-destructing, then the body clears them.

how.q: Why does the combo use two drugs instead of one?
how.a: Different senescent cell types lean on different survival networks. Dasatinib clears senescent fat-cell progenitors (Src/ephrin kinases); quercetin clears senescent endothelial cells (PI3K/AKT, BCL-2). Together they cover more than either alone.

use.q: Why can a short, intermittent course keep working for weeks?
use.a: Because it's 'hit-and-run': a killed cell doesn't come back when the drug wears off. You only need to trip the kill-switch once, so dasatinib's ~3-5 h half-life doesn't limit how long the benefit lasts.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, honestly?
evidence.a: Early. Landmark mouse data (cleared senescent cells, better function, ~36% longer post-treatment survival) plus two tiny open-label human pilots showing feasibility and reduced senescent-cell markers — but no large controlled human trial proving it slows ageing or disease.

canExplain: Why senescent 'zombie' cells survive when they should die — and how a senolytic exploits that
Why the cocktail needs two drugs: dasatinib for fat-cell progenitors, quercetin for endothelial cells
What 'hit-and-run' dosing means, and why the drug's short half-life doesn't limit the benefit
Why clearing senescent cells lowers inflammation (removing the SASP source)
Why the human evidence is still early despite strong mouse data — and why dasatinib's toxicity makes it clinician-only

selfTest.q: A friend says 'senolytics must be taken daily like a statin to keep senescent cells suppressed.' Correct him.
selfTest.a: Senolytics aren't suppressors — they kill the cells outright, and dead cells don't return. So brief, intermittent 'hit-and-run' dosing is enough; continuous daily exposure would only add dasatinib's toxicity without adding benefit.

selfTest.q: Why is dasatinib's ~3-5 hour half-life almost irrelevant to how long D+Q 'works'?
selfTest.a: Because the effect isn't a blood-level phenomenon. The benefit comes from the absence of the senescent cells the drug eliminated, not from ongoing drug presence — so the effect persists for weeks after the drug has cleared.

selfTest.q: The mouse studies showed longer lifespan, but the human trials didn't measure that. What did the human pilots actually show, and why is that gap important?
selfTest.a: The human pilots (IPF, n=14; diabetic kidney disease, n=9) showed feasibility, reduced senescent-cell markers and circulating SASP factors, and short-term physical-function signals — not lifespan or hard disease outcomes. The gap matters because mouse lifespan extension is proof-of-concept, not proof the same holds in humans; only larger controlled trials could show that.

evi.howStrong: Split evidence. The mechanistic and mouse foundation is strong and landmark: the drug class was identified from senescent-cell survival biology (Zhu 2015), and intermittent D+Q cleared senescent cells, improved physical function, and extended remaining lifespan in aged mice (Xu 2018, Nature Medicine). But the human evidence is genuinely early — a few very small open-label pilot trials, not large randomized controlled trials. No regulator has approved D+Q as a senolytic for any indication; the combination is investigational.
evi.whatItShows: In mice: senescent cells causally drive physical dysfunction, and removing them with D+Q reverses some of it and extends survival. In humans so far: it's feasible and tolerable in small groups, it measurably reduces senescent-cell abundance and circulating SASP inflammatory factors in tissue, and early physical-function signals appeared in the IPF pilot. What it has NOT shown in humans is a proven effect on lifespan, or on hard clinical disease endpoints in adequately powered trials.
evi.effect: In aged mice, intermittent D+Q increased post-treatment survival by ~36% and reduced the mortality hazard, alongside improved physical function. In the human diabetic-kidney-disease pilot, a single short course reduced senescent-cell burden in fat and skin and lowered several circulating SASP factors within days. Human functional and disease effects remain unproven at scale — the honest headline is 'target engaged in humans,' not 'benefit established.'
evi.whoBenefits: Studied populations are people with senescence-associated disease — idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and diabetic kidney disease — under physician supervision. There is no evidence base for healthy people taking it to slow normal ageing, and that use is entirely off-label and unstudied for safety.
evi.caveats: The human trials are tiny, mostly open-label (no or minimal placebo control), short, and biomarker-focused — treat them as early signals, not outcomes. Add dasatinib's real chemotherapy toxicity (effusions, bleeding, cytopenias, cardiac and interaction risks) and quercetin's poor bioavailability and enzyme-inhibition interactions. Mouse lifespan results famously do not always translate to humans. This is foundational, promising science that is not ready to be a personal protocol.

trials.finding: Bioinformatic identification of senescent-cell anti-apoptotic pathways (SCAPs) established the senolytic concept; dasatinib and quercetin were the first agents shown to selectively kill senescent cells (D more effective on fat-cell progenitors, Q on endothelial cells).
trials.ref: Zhu et al., 2015, Aging Cell

trials.finding: In naturally-aged mice, intermittent oral D+Q alleviated physical dysfunction and increased post-treatment survival by ~36% while reducing mortality hazard; transplanted senescent cells causally induced dysfunction even in young mice.
trials.ref: Xu et al., 2018, Nature Medicine

trials.finding: First-in-human open-label pilot in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (n=14; D 100 mg + Q 1250 mg, 3 days/week for 3 weeks): the regimen was feasible and tolerable, with improvements in some physical-function measures (e.g. 6-minute walk, gait speed).
trials.ref: Justice et al., 2019, EBioMedicine

trials.finding: Open-label pilot in diabetic kidney disease (n=9): a short 3-day course of D+Q reduced senescent-cell abundance in adipose tissue and skin and lowered several circulating SASP factors — first evidence senolytics reduce senescent-cell burden in humans.
trials.ref: Hickson et al., 2019, EBioMedicine

tech.chem: A two-drug combination, not a single molecule. Dasatinib (Sprycel; CAS 302962-49-8) is a synthetic small-molecule, ATP-competitive multi-tyrosine-kinase inhibitor, FDA-approved for chronic myeloid leukemia and Ph+ acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Quercetin (CID 5280343) is a naturally occurring plant flavonol (a polyphenol) found in onions, apples and capers, sold as an OTC supplement. They are paired because they are senolytic against complementary senescent cell types, not because they are chemically related.

targets.action: ATP-competitive tyrosine-kinase inhibitor
targets.affinity: low-nanomolar, high-potency (the same class-defining potency that makes it an approved leukemia drug)
targets.role: inhibiting Src-family and ephrin-dependent (EPHB) survival signalling removes the anti-apoptotic prop of senescent fat-cell progenitors, tipping them into apoptosis

targets.action: kinase inhibition of a SCAP node
targets.affinity: part of dasatinib's broad kinase-inhibition profile
targets.role: ephrin-dependence is one of the senescent-cell anti-apoptotic pathways identified in the original screen

targets.action: inhibition of PI3K/AKT survival signalling
targets.affinity: low-potency, promiscuous polyphenol activity
targets.role: removes a survival prop of senescent endothelial cells

targets.action: partial inhibition of anti-apoptotic BCL-2-family proteins (also PAI-1/SERPINE1)
targets.affinity: weak/partial
targets.role: de-represses the intrinsic apoptosis pathway in senescent cells quercetin covers

tech.signaling: The unifying logic is 'senescent-cell anti-apoptotic pathways' (SCAPs). Senescent cells are primed for apoptosis but survive by upregulating overlapping pro-survival networks — Src/ephrin kinase signalling, PI3K/AKT, BCL-2/BCL-xL/BCL-W, p21, and PAI-1. Different senescent cell types depend on different nodes, so no single agent clears them all. Dasatinib disables the kinase-dependent nodes (favouring fat-cell progenitors); quercetin disables PI3K/BCL-2 nodes (favouring endothelial cells). Transiently disabling these nodes lets the cell's own pre-primed intrinsic apoptosis program execute — a 'hit-and-run' kill that does not require sustained drug levels.

adme.absorb: Dasatinib: orally absorbed, absorption pH-dependent (reduced by acid-suppressing drugs). Quercetin: poor and variable oral bioavailability, extensively conjugated presystemically.
adme.distribute: Dasatinib distributes widely to reach kinase targets in tissue; quercetin circulates largely as low-level glucuronide/sulfate conjugates.
adme.metabolise: Dasatinib: hepatic CYP3A4 to active and inactive metabolites (hence CYP3A4-inhibitor/inducer interactions). Quercetin: gut and hepatic conjugation, and it inhibits several CYP/UGT enzymes, creating its own interaction potential.
adme.excrete: Dasatinib eliminated predominantly in feces; elimination half-life ≈ 3-5 h. Quercetin conjugates excreted in urine and bile. Note: for a senolytic, these clearance kinetics do not set the duration of effect — cleared cells do.

tech.pgx: No validated senolytic-response pharmacogenetic marker. Clinically relevant modifiers are pharmacokinetic: dasatinib exposure rises with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors and falls with inducers, and its absorption drops with proton-pump inhibitors/H2 blockers — all governing dasatinib's oncology safety rather than a proven senolytic-efficacy gene. Individual response should be treated as uncharacterised.
tech.dose: No approved senolytic dose exists. The published human pilots used intermittent 'hit-and-run' regimens under physician supervision — e.g. dasatinib 100 mg/day plus quercetin 1250 mg/day for 3 consecutive days per week (IPF pilot), or a short 3-day course (diabetic kidney disease pilot). These are descriptions of investigational protocols, not recommendations; dasatinib is chemotherapy and this is off-label.
tech.tolerance: No receptor-downregulation tolerance — the mechanism is cell killing, not receptor signalling, so there is nothing to desensitise. The intermittent schedule is driven by the hit-and-run pharmacology (brief exposure suffices) and by dasatinib's toxicity ceiling, not by tolerance. The relevant time-dependent concern is cumulative dasatinib adverse effects with repeated courses.

contrasts.title: D+Q vs fisetin — chemo-grade combo vs single OTC flavonoid
contrasts.point: Same senolytic goal, very different risk profile. D+Q pairs a potent prescription chemotherapy (dasatinib) with a flavonoid to cover two senescent cell types — powerful mechanism, real chemotherapy toxicity, clinician-only. Fisetin is a single plant flavonoid (structurally a quercetin cousin) also being studied as a senolytic, with a gentler safety profile and OTC availability. The teaching point: 'senolytic' describes a strategy, not a safety class — the flavonoid-only route trades some of D+Q's mechanistic reach for a far lower toxicity ceiling, and both still rest on early human evidence.

questions: How can a molecule in wheat germ and aged cheese possibly do the same thing to your cells that fasting does?
If a big 20-year study links eating more of it to living longer, why did a careful year-long memory trial come up empty?
You barely absorb it into your blood — so how could a few milligrams a day matter at all?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Spermidine takes the brakes off autophagy — your cells' recycling crew — by blocking the same enzyme (EP300) that a fed state uses to keep that crew idle; fasting reaches the same 'clean house' signal by a different door. (2) The Bruneck cohort (Kiechl 2018) shows a strong association between higher dietary spermidine and lower mortality, but association isn't proof — and the SmartAge RCT (Schwarz 2022) found no memory benefit over 12 months, which is exactly why the evidence sits at a middling 3/5. (3) Blood levels barely move because your body tightly regulates polyamines; the interesting action is inside cells and over years, not a blood spike you'd feel today.

spermidine.bigIdea: Spermidine doesn't 'boost' anything — it releases a brake, switching on the cell's self-cleaning program (autophagy) so your cells clear out their own junk the way fasting makes them do. Hold that one sentence and the rest of this page follows.
spermidine.analogy: Autophagy is your cell's overnight cleaning crew, and when food is plentiful an enzyme called EP300 tells the crew to stand down. Spermidine is like slipping your cells a 'we're fasting now' note — it removes that stand-down order, so the cleanup runs even while you're well fed.

mechSteps.t: Your cells have a recycling crew that fades with age
mechSteps.predict: Cells constantly break down and recycle their own damaged parts. What is this self-eating housekeeping process called — and which way does it drift as you get older?
mechSteps.d: Autophagy is how a cell digests and reuses its own worn-out proteins and organelles. It runs hardest when nutrients are scarce (like fasting) and slows down with age — and that decline is one of the recognised hallmarks of ageing.

mechSteps.t: An enzyme holds the crew back when you're well fed
mechSteps.predict: When nutrients are plentiful, something actively keeps autophagy switched off so the cell builds rather than recycles. What kind of chemical 'off switch' does it use on the autophagy machinery?
mechSteps.d: EP300 is an acetyltransferase — it hangs acetyl tags onto autophagy proteins and transcription factors, and those tags keep the recycling crew idle. In a fed, growth-favouring state, EP300 is the brake pressed down.

mechSteps.t: Spermidine releases that brake
mechSteps.predict: If spermidine sits on EP300 and blocks its tagging activity, what happens to the autophagy crew that EP300 was holding back?
mechSteps.d: Spermidine inhibits EP300's acetyltransferase activity (shown directly on the recombinant enzyme by Pietrocola 2015). With fewer inhibitory acetyl tags, autophagy genes switch on and autophagic flux climbs — the same clean-out signal fasting produces, reached by a different route.

mechSteps.t: Several signals converge on the same clean-out
mechSteps.predict: A single clean molecular switch is rarely the whole story. Name one other cellular 'low-fuel' sensor that would also push toward recycling.
mechSteps.d: Alongside EP300 inhibition, spermidine nudges AMPK (the low-energy sensor) and dampens mTORC1 (the growth sensor) — all pointing the cell toward recycling over building. Spermidine is also the sole raw material for 'hypusination' of the protein eIF5A, a separate, well-established job that keeps translation of certain proteins running.

framework.target: EP300, a histone/protein acetyltransferase — spermidine inhibits it, taking the brake off autophagy. Note this is not a classic receptor: EP300 is an enzyme, and spermidine also acts as a substrate and a general polyamine touching several nodes (AMPK, mTORC1, and eIF5A hypusination).
framework.affinity: There's no clean receptor Ki to quote, and that's the honest answer, not a gap. Spermidine is a small, positively charged polyamine your own cells make and tightly regulate — it works at millimolar-ish intracellular concentrations across multiple targets, not as a high-affinity drug locking onto one receptor. The number that actually matters is not a binding constant but whether cellular levels shift enough to change autophagy.
framework.adme: Absorbed from the gut, but here's the twist: your body holds circulating polyamine levels on a tight leash, so oral doses barely move blood spermidine — an exploratory RCT giving 40 mg/day to older men found minimal change in circulating polyamines. Gut bacteria also produce and consume it. This is a homeostatically buffered nutrient, not a dose-in, level-up drug.
framework.genetics: No single-gene on/off switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. Response is shaped by baseline diet (how much you already eat), age (autophagy and polyamine levels fall with age, so older cells may have more headroom), and your gut microbiome, which makes and metabolises polyamines.
framework.evidence: Middling and mixed (3/5). Strong, coherent mechanism plus a striking 20-year epidemiological association — but the flagship randomised memory trial was negative, and most hard longevity data is still animal. Credible and food-derived, not proven to extend human lifespan.

spermidine.stakes: Decline of autophagy is one of the recognised hallmarks of ageing — so a food-derived molecule that switches it back on is exactly the kind of lever longevity science is hunting for, which is why spermidine draws so much attention despite still-modest human proof.

journey.d: You eat it — richest in raw wheat germ (roughly 20–30 mg per 100 g), plus aged cheese, natto, mushrooms and legumes. Your gut also gets a steady supply made by your own bacteria.
It's absorbed across the gut wall, but your microbiome is a major player here — bacteria both produce and consume polyamines, so what reaches you isn't just what you ate.
It enters the bloodstream, yet circulating levels stay on a tight leash — the body buffers polyamines hard, which is why even large oral doses barely move the blood number.
Inside cells it inhibits EP300, lifting the brake on autophagy, while also feeding eIF5A hypusination and nudging AMPK and mTORC1 toward recycling.
Autophagy ramps up: damaged proteins and organelles are digested and their parts reused — the slow, quiet housekeeping that keeps cells functional as they age.
Excess polyamines are acetylated and cleared, largely in urine, keeping the whole system within its tightly regulated range.

myths.myth: The 20-year study proves spermidine makes you live longer.
myths.truth: It shows a strong association, not causation. In the Bruneck cohort, people eating more spermidine died less — but high-spermidine diets (vegetables, legumes, whole grains, fermented foods) travel with many other healthy habits. It's genuinely promising; it is not proof a supplement extends your life.

myths.myth: If it fasting-mimics, more must be better.
myths.truth: Because your body tightly buffers polyamine levels, piling on dose doesn't linearly pile on effect — even 40 mg/day barely shifted circulating levels in one trial. This is a saturation-and-homeostasis molecule, not a turn-the-dial-up drug.

myths.myth: Autophagy is always good, so spermidine is risk-free for everyone.
myths.truth: Polyamines also fuel cell proliferation, and many tumours run high polyamine levels — the cancer story is genuinely two-sided (autophagy can protect, but proliferating cells crave polyamines). Anyone with an active cancer, or who is pregnant, should treat the safety data as incomplete and check with a doctor rather than assume 'natural = safe'.

whenToUse.intro: Put it together — here's how to actually use it, honestly:
items: **Food first is the strongest bet.** The best human signal comes from *diets* rich in spermidine — wheat germ, aged cheese, natto, mushrooms, legumes, whole grains. That's the pattern the mortality data actually tracked.

**If supplementing, 1–5 mg/day (or a wheat-germ extract) is the studied range.** Trials used low single-digit milligrams; there's no proven advantage to megadosing, and blood levels barely move regardless.
**Treat it as a slow, daily habit, not a pre-workout hit.** The mechanism is cumulative housekeeping over months and years — there's no acute effect to feel or time.
**Set honest expectations.** The strong mechanism and epidemiology are real; the one big randomised memory trial was negative. This is a credible longevity bet, not a proven intervention.

**Check with a doctor first if you have active cancer or are pregnant.** Polyamines feed proliferating cells and the safety data in these groups is incomplete — this is the one real caution to respect.

start.q: In one line: does spermidine add anything to the cell, or release a brake?
start.a: It releases a brake. By inhibiting EP300 it lets autophagy — the cell's recycling crew — switch on, the same clean-out that fasting triggers.

how.q: What enzyme does spermidine inhibit, and what does that inhibition switch on?
how.a: EP300, an acetyltransferase that keeps autophagy idle when you're well fed. Blocking it lifts the brake, so autophagy genes turn on and autophagic flux rises.

use.q: Why is spermidine a daily-habit supplement rather than a timed dose you feel?
use.a: Its effect is slow, cumulative cellular housekeeping, and the body tightly buffers polyamine levels — so timing barely matters and there's no acute hit to feel.

evidence.q: Why does the evidence sit at a middling 3/5 despite a striking mortality study?
evidence.a: The mechanism and the 20-year association are strong, but association isn't causation and the flagship year-long randomised memory trial (SmartAge) was negative — so human proof is real but incomplete.

canExplain: Why spermidine is called a 'fasting mimetic' — and how EP300 links it to autophagy
Why there's no clean receptor Ki to quote, because it's a polyamine acting at several nodes, not a high-affinity single-target drug
Why oral doses barely move blood levels, and what that means for how you use it
The gap between the 20-year mortality association and the negative year-long memory RCT — and why both can be true
Why the polyamine–cancer link makes 'autophagy is always good, so it's risk-free' too simple

selfTest.q: Your friend says 'spermidine boosts my cells' energy like a vitamin.' What's the more accurate one-sentence correction?
selfTest.a: It doesn't add energy or act like a vitamin — it inhibits EP300 to release the brake on autophagy, the cell's own recycling and clean-out program.

selfTest.q: A 20-year study links higher spermidine intake to lower mortality, yet a 12-month randomised trial found no memory benefit. How can both be right?
selfTest.a: The cohort shows association, not causation — high-spermidine diets ride alongside many other healthy habits — while the RCT isolated the supplement on one outcome (memory) over a short window and found no effect. Correlation over decades and a null causal test on a single endpoint aren't contradictory.

selfTest.q: Someone doubles their spermidine dose expecting double the effect. Why is that reasoning flawed here specifically?
selfTest.a: Polyamines are homeostatically buffered — the body actively holds circulating levels within a tight range, so even large oral doses barely shift blood spermidine. It's a saturation system, not a linear dose-response like a stimulant.

evi.howStrong: The evidence is a genuine split-decision, which is why it lands at 3/5. On the strong side: a coherent, biochemically demonstrated mechanism (spermidine inhibits recombinant EP300 to induce autophagy), robust animal data including mouse lifespan extension and cardioprotection, and a striking 20-year human cohort where higher dietary intake tracked lower mortality. On the weak side: the human data is mostly observational, and the single largest randomised controlled trial for cognition was negative. There is no completed human RCT showing spermidine extends lifespan or prevents disease.
evi.whatItShows: Consistently across models, spermidine switches on autophagy and, in animals, blunts age-related decline (heart, cognition, lifespan). In humans, the clearest finding is an epidemiological one — more dietary spermidine, lower all-cause mortality — plus a small positive memory pilot that did not replicate in a larger, longer trial.
evi.effect: In the Bruneck cohort the mortality gap between the top and bottom third of intake was on the order of a 5.7-years-younger age — but that is an observational association across whole diets, not a measured effect of a pill. Randomised human cognitive results range from a small pilot benefit to no effect in the definitive trial.
evi.whoBenefits: Mechanistically, older adults (whose autophagy and polyamine levels have declined) have the most theoretical headroom, and population data centre on older cohorts. In practice, no group has proven clinical benefit from supplementation yet — the honest status is 'promising, unproven'.
evi.caveats: Association is not causation: high-spermidine diets are healthy diets overall, a hard confounder to remove. Oral dosing barely shifts blood levels. And the polyamine–cancer relationship is two-sided, so 'more autophagy = safe for everyone' overreaches. Treat the numbers as directional, not a personal guarantee.

trials.finding: Spermidine directly inhibited the acetyltransferase activity of recombinant EP300 and induced autophagy in cultured human cells — the mechanistic basis for the whole page.
trials.ref: Pietrocola et al., 2015, Cell Death & Differentiation

trials.finding: Prospective 20-year cohort (Bruneck, ~829 adults): higher dietary spermidine intake was associated with lower all-cause mortality; top-vs-bottom-third gap comparable to being ~5.7 years younger.
trials.ref: Kiechl et al., 2018, Am J Clin Nutr

trials.finding: Small 3-month pilot RCT (n≈30, subjective cognitive decline): spermidine-rich extract associated with improved memory performance.
trials.ref: Wirth et al., 2018, Cortex

trials.finding: 12-month randomised, placebo-controlled trial (SmartAge, subjective cognitive decline): spermidine did NOT improve memory or biomarkers versus placebo — the key negative human result.
trials.ref: Schwarz et al., 2022, JAMA Network Open

trials.finding: Animal: dietary spermidine extended lifespan in mice and improved cardiac function (diastolic relaxation), via induction of cardiomyocyte autophagy.
trials.ref: Eisenberg et al., 2016, Nature Medicine (animal)

tech.chem: A naturally occurring aliphatic polyamine (N-[3-aminopropyl]butane-1,4-diamine; PubChem CID 1102), positively charged at physiological pH. Your cells synthesise it from putrescine (via ornithine, using spermidine synthase), and it's a precursor to spermine; it's ubiquitous in food, highest in wheat germ, aged cheese and fermented soy. Being a small, charged, endogenous metabolite, it behaves nothing like a lipophilic single-target drug — it's tightly homeostatically regulated and acts at multiple cellular nodes.

targets.action: inhibits acetyltransferase activity → de-represses autophagy
targets.affinity: no clean drug-binding Ki; inhibition demonstrated on recombinant EP300 in vitro (Pietrocola 2015)
targets.role: the curated primary mechanism — releasing EP300's brake on autophagy genes and factors

targets.action: sole substrate for hypusination of eIF5A — a required post-translational modification
targets.affinity: substrate, not a receptor ligand
targets.role: a distinct, well-established function: hypusinated eIF5A supports translation of specific (e.g. polyproline) proteins, implicated in mitochondrial and immune effects

targets.action: AMPK activation and mTORC1 inhibition (net pro-autophagy)
targets.affinity: indirect, downstream nodes
targets.role: converging low-fuel/growth signals that reinforce the autophagy switch

tech.signaling: The organising theme is autophagy de-repression. EP300 normally acetylates autophagy machinery (ATG proteins) and factors such as TFEB to keep recycling idle in nutrient-rich conditions; spermidine's inhibition of EP300 lowers those inhibitory acetyl marks, mTORC1 tone falls and AMPK tone rises, and autophagic flux increases (more LC3 puncta, p62/SQSTM1 depletion). In parallel, spermidine-dependent hypusination of eIF5A tunes translation — a genuinely separate axis with its own downstream effects on mitochondria and immune cells.

adme.absorb: Absorbed from the gut, but circulating polyamine levels are homeostatically buffered — an exploratory RCT of 40 mg/day in older men reported minimal change in circulating polyamines. The gut microbiome is a major independent source and sink.
adme.distribute: Distributes into tissues where it's incorporated into the tightly regulated cellular polyamine pool (interconverting with putrescine and spermine); does not behave as a free-equilibrating lipophilic drug.
adme.metabolise: Interconverted within the polyamine pathway; excess is acetylated by spermidine/spermine N1-acetyltransferase (SAT1), the rate-limiting step of polyamine catabolism.
adme.excrete: Acetylated polyamines and catabolites are cleared largely via urine, keeping the pool within its regulated range.

tech.pgx: No caffeine-style single-gene switch. Determinants of response are baseline dietary intake, age (autophagy and polyamine levels decline with age), and the gut microbiome's polyamine production/consumption. Enzymes of the pathway (ODC1, SRM, SMS, SAT1) set tissue polyamine tone but aren't a consumer pharmacogenetic test.
tech.dose: Studied human supplemental range is roughly 1–5 mg/day, typically as wheat-germ extract; cognitive trials used low single-digit milligrams. Higher exploratory doses (e.g. 40 mg/day) still barely shifted circulating levels, consistent with homeostatic buffering. Generally well tolerated at food-level intakes.
tech.tolerance: No receptor down-regulation or classic tolerance, and nothing to 'cycle'. The practical ceiling is homeostatic: the body actively holds polyamine levels within range, so beyond a point extra intake is buffered and cleared rather than accumulating.

questions: Why can two people drink the same pomegranate juice and only one of them ends up with urolithin A in their blood?
How can a molecule make an aging muscle more durable without adding a single new mitochondrion?
If the human trials are real, why are the effects still described as 'modest' — and who actually benefits?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Urolithin A isn't in the fruit — your gut bacteria build it from pomegranate ellagitannins, and only ~40% of people carry the microbial relay (led by Gordonibacter) to finish the job; the rest make little or none. (2) It doesn't add mitochondria — it triggers mitophagy, the recycling of your damaged ones, so the pool that remains is cleaner and runs better. (3) The trials (Andreux 2019, Liu 2022, Singh 2022) are genuine RCTs but show small effects over months, clearest in middle-aged and older or less-active adults — not a jolt you feel today.

urolithin-a.bigIdea: Urolithin A doesn't build new mitochondria or hand your cells more fuel — it tells them to recycle their broken, damaged mitochondria (mitophagy), so the power plants that remain are cleaner and more efficient. Hold onto that, and the whole page follows.
urolithin-a.analogy: Think of your muscle cell as a power station full of aging generators. With age, broken ones pile up and drag down the whole plant, but the cleanup crew that hauls them away slows down too. Urolithin A is the call that puts that cleanup crew back to work — it doesn't install new generators, it clears out the junk so the good ones can run.

mechSteps.t: Damaged mitochondria accumulate with age
mechSteps.predict: A power plant whose broken generators are never removed — what happens to its overall output?
mechSteps.d: Mitochondria wear out and turn dysfunctional over time. Normally a cell clears the bad ones, but that housekeeping declines with age, so damaged units pile up and muscle energy quality drops.

mechSteps.t: Your gut has to make the molecule first
mechSteps.predict: Urolithin A isn't actually in pomegranate. So what has to happen between eating the fruit and having it in your blood?
mechSteps.d: You eat ellagitannins (pomegranate, walnuts, some berries); gut bacteria led by Gordonibacter convert them, through several steps, into urolithin A. Only about 40% of people carry the full microbial relay — the rest produce little or none, which is the whole reason for a standardised supplement.

mechSteps.t: It switches the recycling crew back on
mechSteps.predict: If the cell's cleanup machinery is the PINK1/Parkin system, what would 'activating mitophagy' physically do to a damaged mitochondrion?
mechSteps.d: Urolithin A induces mitophagy — the selective tagging and disposal of damaged mitochondria. In human muscle it raised phospho-Parkin (Ser65), the active mark that PINK1 stamps on Parkin to flag a broken mitochondrion for recycling.

mechSteps.t: A cleaner mitochondrial pool performs better
mechSteps.predict: Clear out the broken generators and leave only the working ones — what does that buy the muscle in a long, fatiguing effort?
mechSteps.d: With the damaged units cleared and biogenesis nudged, the remaining mitochondria run more efficiently. Trials measured this as more muscle contractions before fatigue and modest strength gains — endurance, not explosive power.

framework.target: The mitophagy quality-control pathway (PINK1/Parkin). Note the key difference from a drug like caffeine: urolithin A has no receptor it switches on or blocks as its therapeutic action. It's an inducer of a cellular clean-up process — it nudges the cell to recycle damaged mitochondria rather than binding a switch.
framework.affinity: There's no clean receptor binding constant to quote for the muscle effect, because the mechanism isn't a single-receptor lock-and-key. (In the lab it acts as an antagonist of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor, AhR, but that isn't the mitophagy story.) What matters instead is your metabotype and the standardised dose — whether your gut makes any at all, and whether you're getting a level shown to move the biomarkers.
framework.adme: Absorbed orally, then heavily processed by the liver into glucuronide and sulfate conjugates — so the conjugated forms dominate your bloodstream and free urolithin A is a trace. Plasma peaks around 6 h; the parent's half-life is ~17–22 h and the conjugates linger 25–58 h.
framework.genetics: Response is gated by your gut microbiome, not a single human gene. Roughly 40% of people (metabotype 'A', high in Gordonibacter) make urolithin A from food; some make none at all (metabotype 0). That variability is exactly why direct supplementation standardises a dose most people can't reliably produce themselves.
framework.evidence: Moderate (3/5). Several placebo-controlled RCTs show improved muscle endurance and mitochondrial biomarkers — but effects are small, trials run over months, and much of the work is industry-funded, with one trial missing its primary endpoint.

pk.onset: weeks (biomarker and functional changes build over a 2–4 month course)
pk.peak: plasma peaks ~6 h post-dose, but the felt/measured benefit is not tied to that peak
pk.duration: a daily habit — the effect comes from sustained mitophagy signalling, not a single blood peak
pk.note: Urolithin A breaks the usual timing rules, like creatine. The blood half-life exists but isn't what drives the benefit — chronic, repeated dosing over months is. So it's a daily supplement, not a pre-workout stopwatch game, and exactly when you take it barely matters.

journey.d: You eat ellagitannins — pomegranate, walnuts, some berries. Note: this is not urolithin A yet, just the raw precursor.

Gut bacteria led by Gordonibacter convert the precursor, step by step, into urolithin A. Only ~40% of people carry the full microbial relay — the rest make little or none, which is why food alone is unreliable.
Absorbed and immediately worked over by the liver into glucuronide and sulfate conjugates, so the conjugated forms are what actually circulate. Plasma peaks around 6 hours.
It reaches skeletal muscle and switches the PINK1/Parkin recycling crew back on — tagging damaged mitochondria (phospho-Parkin Ser65) for disposal.
Over weeks, the cleared, cleaner mitochondrial pool runs more efficiently — measured as more contractions before fatigue and modest strength gains.
The conjugated metabolites are cleared and leave the body. Because they linger longest (25–58 h), a steady daily dose keeps the signal topped up.

myths.myth: Just drink pomegranate juice and you get the benefit.
myths.truth: Pomegranate contains the precursor, not urolithin A itself. Only about 40% of people have the gut bacteria to finish the conversion — the rest produce little or none no matter how much they drink. That single fact is the entire case for a standardised supplement.

myths.myth: It builds new mitochondria and gives you more energy.
myths.truth: Its core action is the opposite direction: recycling damaged mitochondria (mitophagy), improving the quality of the pool rather than simply adding units or fuel. Some biogenesis may follow downstream, but the mechanism that's actually demonstrated is clean-up, not a fuel boost.

myths.myth: The muscle effects are dramatic.
myths.truth: They're modest. Endurance and strength gains in the trials are real but small, unfold over months, and show up most in middle-aged or older, less-active adults — not a pre-workout jolt and not a substitute for training.

whenToUse.intro: Put it together — here's how to actually use it:
items: **Daily, 500–1,000 mg (Mitopure), taken consistently.** This is a slow adaptation over months, not an acute dose — treat it like a habit, not a pre-workout.
**Timing barely matters; take it with a meal.** The benefit tracks sustained dosing, not a blood peak, and some fat may aid absorption.
**Best rationale if you're middle-aged or older, or less active** — that's where the trials show the clearest signal, and where declining mitophagy gives it the most to work on.

**Buy the standardised form.** Food conversion is unreliable (only ~40% of people make it), and Mitopure is the form used in the human trials — most other 'pomegranate extracts' are not the same thing.
**Set expectations honestly.** The gains are quiet and cumulative — modest endurance and mitochondrial-biomarker improvements over a 2–4 month course, not something you'll feel in a session.

start.q: In one line: does urolithin A add mitochondria, or clean up the ones you have?
start.a: It cleans up. Its core action is mitophagy — recycling damaged mitochondria — so the remaining pool runs better, rather than simply adding units or fuel.

how.q: Why can't most people just get this from pomegranate?
how.a: Pomegranate only supplies the precursor. Roughly 40% of people carry the gut bacteria (led by Gordonibacter) to convert it into urolithin A; the rest make little or none — so a supplement standardises the dose.

use.q: Why does the time of day you take it barely matter?
use.a: The effect comes from sustained mitophagy signalling over weeks, not from a blood peak. It's a daily top-up, like creatine — keep it consistent and timing is a non-issue.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, and what's the catch?
evidence.a: Moderate — several placebo-controlled RCTs show improved muscle endurance and mitochondrial biomarkers. The catch: effects are small, trials are months long and largely industry-funded, and one missed its primary endpoint.

canExplain: Why urolithin A improves mitochondrial quality without adding new mitochondria or fuel
Why only about 40% of people make it from food, and what a supplement standardises
The exact pathway it activates — PINK1/Parkin mitophagy — and why it has no therapeutic receptor
Why timing doesn't matter and it's a daily, months-long habit rather than a pre-workout dose
How strong the human evidence really is, and who benefits most

selfTest.q: Your friend takes urolithin A and 'feels nothing' after a week. Give two honest reasons that's expected.
selfTest.a: First, the effect builds over months, not days — a week is far too short. Second, the demonstrated benefits (endurance, mitochondrial biomarkers) are modest and mostly measured, not felt. It's a slow adaptation, not a stimulant.

selfTest.q: Two people eat identical amounts of walnuts and pomegranate, but only one shows urolithin A in a blood test. What explains it?
selfTest.a: Metabotype. Producing urolithin A requires a specific gut-bacterial relay (led by Gordonibacter); only ~40% of people carry it. The non-producer (metabotype 0) simply can't convert the precursor — the reason a standardised supplement exists.

selfTest.q: Someone says 'it boosts energy by making more mitochondria.' What's the more accurate one-sentence mechanism?
selfTest.a: It induces mitophagy — flagging damaged mitochondria (via PINK1/Parkin) for recycling — so the mechanism is quality-control clean-up of the existing pool, not simply making more or adding fuel.

evi.howStrong: Urolithin A sits in a genuinely interesting middle of the evidence pyramid: it has cleared the bar most longevity supplements never reach — real, randomised, placebo-controlled human trials with muscle and mitochondrial endpoints, not just mouse data. That's why it's one of the better-evidenced mitochondrial-longevity compounds. But keep the catch firmly in view: the trials are relatively small (tens of participants), run over a few months, are largely funded by the manufacturer (Amazentis/Timeline), and one of the flagship trials missed its primary endpoint. Moderate, not settled.
evi.whatItShows: Across the trials the consistent signal is on mitochondrial biomarkers and muscle endurance — more contractions before fatigue, improved markers of mitochondrial gene expression and plasma acylcarnitines — with smaller, less consistent effects on raw strength and power. It improves the quality and stamina of aging muscle at the margin; it is not a mass-builder and not a stimulant.
evi.effect: In plain numbers: in older adults, 1,000 mg/day improved muscle endurance (contractions to fatigue) versus placebo; in middle-aged adults, ~12% strength improvement was reported, though that same trial did not hit its primary power-output endpoint. Effects are modest and cumulative over a 2–4 month course.
evi.whoBenefits: The clearest signal is in middle-aged and older, sedentary or less-active adults — people whose natural mitophagy has declined and who often can't make urolithin A from food. Younger, well-trained, high-producing individuals have far less evidence behind them and likely less to gain.
evi.caveats: Treat the numbers as promising rather than proven: small samples, short durations, heavy industry funding, and a missed primary endpoint all argue for caution and assume some publication/funding bias. There is no long-term (multi-year) or hard-outcome data, and the mechanism-to-benefit chain, while plausible, is still being nailed down.

trials.finding: First-in-human RCT: 250–1,000 mg/day for 4 weeks was safe in sedentary elderly adults and modulated plasma acylcarnitines and skeletal-muscle mitochondrial gene expression — a molecular signature of improved mitochondrial health.
trials.ref: Andreux et al., 2019, Nature Metabolism

trials.finding: RCT in 66 older adults: 1,000 mg/day for 4 months significantly improved muscle endurance (contractions to fatigue) in both hand and leg muscles versus placebo.
trials.ref: Liu et al., 2022, JAMA Network Open

trials.finding: RCT in middle-aged adults: ~12% improvement in muscle strength and improved mitochondrial biomarkers, though the primary peak-power endpoint was not met.
trials.ref: Singh et al., 2022, Cell Reports Medicine

tech.chem: Urolithin A is 3,8-dihydroxy-6H-dibenzo[b,d]pyran-6-one (a dibenzo-α-pyranone), a gut-microbial metabolite — not a vitamin, hormone, or stimulant. It is not present in food itself: gut bacteria produce it from ellagitannins and ellagic acid (pomegranate, walnuts, some berries) through sequential lactone-ring and dehydroxylation steps. PubChem CID 5488186.

targets.action: pathway activated (not a direct ligand target) — urolithin A induces PINK1/Parkin-mediated mitophagy; human muscle shows increased phospho-Parkin (Ser65)
targets.affinity: not a receptor-binding interaction; urolithin A is an upstream inducer, not a direct binder of PINK1 or Parkin
targets.role: the mechanism that matters in vivo — selective recycling of damaged mitochondria

targets.action: antagonist (demonstrated in vitro)
targets.affinity: a real molecular interaction, but not the muscle/mitophagy mechanism and not the therapeutic target
targets.role: illustrates that urolithin A has receptor-level activity — just not the one driving its healthspan claims

tech.signaling: The load-bearing node is the PINK1/Parkin mitophagy cascade: on a damaged mitochondrion, PINK1 accumulates on the outer membrane and phosphorylates Parkin at Ser65, activating it to ubiquitinate outer-membrane proteins; the ubiquitin tags recruit autophagy receptors that engulf the organelle for lysosomal degradation. Urolithin A raises phospho-Parkin (Ser65) and downstream ubiquitin-conjugating enzymes (e.g. UBE2N, UBE2R2) in human skeletal muscle, consistent with switching this recycling pathway on. It is an inducer of the process rather than a receptor agonist.

adme.absorb: Absorbed orally; standardised supplemental urolithin A (Mitopure) is bioavailable across 250–1,000 mg doses, with dose-dependent plasma exposure.
adme.distribute: Rapidly subject to first-pass phase II conjugation, so systemic circulation is dominated by urolithin A glucuronide and sulfate; free (unconjugated) urolithin A is present only at trace levels. Skeletal muscle is a key site of the measured biological effect.
adme.metabolise: Extensive hepatic phase II metabolism (glucuronidation and sulfation) rather than a single-CYP pathway; conjugates are the major circulating species.
adme.excrete: Metabolites are cleared via urine and bile; plasma half-life ~17–22 h for the parent and 25–58 h for the conjugates.

tech.pgx: No single human pharmacogenetic switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. The dominant determinant of endogenous exposure is the gut microbiome: urolithin metabotype A (produces urolithin A, ~40%, high Gordonibacter), metabotype B (also isourolithin A / urolithin B), and metabotype 0 (produces none). Direct supplementation bypasses this variability entirely — the pharmacological rationale for a standardised product.
tech.dose: 500–1,000 mg/day of standardised urolithin A (Mitopure), taken daily and continuously; the effect is chronic-adaptation driven, so timing is not critical and taking it with food/fat may aid absorption. Doses to 1,000 mg/day for 4 months have been used safely in trials.
tech.tolerance: No receptor-desensitisation tolerance — there is no receptor to down-regulate, so the mechanism isn't expected to fade with continued use and there's nothing to cycle off. The benefit is cumulative over months rather than an acute effect that wanes within a day.

questions: Why would a plain fuel molecule from the Krebs cycle have anything to do with how old your DNA 'reads'?
Why did the same supplement add healthy years to mice but only get tested in about 40 humans, none of them against a placebo?
If your body already makes alpha-ketoglutarate, why would swallowing more of it do anything?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) AKG isn't just fuel, it's the co-substrate that powers a family of 'eraser' enzymes (TET, JmjC, the prolyl hydroxylases) that rewrite the chemical tags on your DNA and proteins, and those tags are exactly what the 'epigenetic aging clock' measures. (2) In mice CaAKG extended lifespan and compressed morbidity (Asadi Shahmirzadi 2020), but in humans the flagship study (Demidenko 2021) was a small, retrospective, uncontrolled look at 42 people taking a vitamin-containing formulation, promising but nowhere near proof. (3) Your own AKG levels fall as you age, so the idea is topping up a supply that's running low, not adding something foreign.
