contrasts.title: Tyrosine vs caffeine — refill vs unmask
contrasts.point: Both help a tired brain, by opposite logic. Caffeine unmasks energy by blocking the adenosine 'slow down' brake — it hides the fatigue. Tyrosine restocks raw material your stressed brain is burning through — it refills the tank. One conceals a deficit; the other prevents one. Which is why caffeine works on anyone and tyrosine only works when a stressor has created the shortfall.

questions: If it's a stimulant, why doesn't it leave you jittery or hand you a crash the way caffeine or amphetamine do?
How can a single course still be doing something weeks after you've stopped taking it?
Why did Russian athletes get banned for a drug that barely shows up in Western research?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) It doesn't force dopamine out or block its reuptake — it turns up the genes that BUILD dopamine, so you make more rather than dumping your reserves, and there's no emptied warehouse to crash from. (2) The effect is slow gene transcription plus a long ~11 h half-life and storage in brain and fat — a large (but open-label) Russian asthenia trial reported benefit still present about a month after stopping. (3) It went onto the WADA banned list in 1997 (both a stimulant and a masking agent) after 1996 Olympic positives, yet almost all human data is Russian-language and methodologically thin — so the Western evidence base is close to empty.

bromantane.bigIdea: Bromantane doesn't release dopamine or block its reuptake — it turns up the genes that build dopamine, so your brain makes more of it. That one idea explains the calm-not-jittery feel, the slow build, and the surprisingly thin evidence.
bromantane.analogy: Most stimulants raid the dopamine warehouse — amphetamine dumps the shelves onto the floor, cocaine jams the doors open. Bromantane instead sends a memo to the factory upstairs: make more product. You get a steadier supply with no ransacked warehouse to crash from — but retooling a factory takes hours to days, not seconds.

mechSteps.t: It works inside the nucleus, not on a surface receptor
mechSteps.predict: Almost every drug on this site grabs a receptor on the cell's outside. Bromantane barely binds any of them. So where does it act?
mechSteps.d: Bromantane has no meaningful high-affinity receptor. Instead it penetrates into the neuron and acts on gene expression — an 'indirect genomic' mechanism. This is exactly why you can't quote it a receptor Ki the way you can for caffeine or ostarine.

mechSteps.t: It turns up the dopamine-building genes
mechSteps.predict: Dopamine is made by an assembly line of enzymes. If you wanted more dopamine without touching release, which step would you speed up?
mechSteps.d: Bromantane upregulates expression of tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) — the rate-limiting enzyme of dopamine synthesis — and of aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (AADC), the next step. In rats a single dose raises TH expression roughly 2 to 2.5-fold within about 1.5 to 2 hours. More enzyme means more capacity to build dopamine.

mechSteps.t: More enzyme, more dopamine you make
mechSteps.predict: This is transcription, not a fast on/off switch. So does the effect land in seconds like a snorted stimulant, or build in over hours?
mechSteps.d: Because you're making new enzyme protein, the extra dopamine synthesis builds over hours and outlasts the dose — not the instant spike-and-fade of a releaser. That is the 'actoprotector' idea: anti-fatigue via more production, not forced release.

mechSteps.t: Stimulation without the crash — plus mild anxiolysis
mechSteps.predict: A drug that dumps dopamine borrows it from tomorrow. What's different about a drug that makes more?
mechSteps.d: Because nothing is being emptied out or reuptake-blocked, there's no depletion to rebound from — the 'stimulation without the crash' framing. Bromantane is also described as mildly anxiolytic (calming), so the felt profile is calm alertness rather than wired edginess. Note the anxiolytic mechanism itself is poorly characterised.

framework.target: Tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) and AADC — the dopamine-synthesis enzymes — at the level of gene expression. The key difference from most compounds here: bromantane is not a receptor drug. It doesn't agonise or antagonise a receptor; it's an indirect genomic 'actoprotector' that upregulates transcription of the rate-limiting enzyme.
framework.affinity: There is no meaningful receptor binding constant to quote — and that's the honest answer, not a gap. In vitro bromantane can inhibit dopamine and serotonin reuptake, but only at 50 to 500 µM, far above anything a real dose reaches in the body, so that is almost certainly not the mechanism. Contrast this with ostarine, where a clean low-nanomolar Ki tells you the target is engaged: here there simply is no validated high-affinity target.
framework.adme: Oral bioavailability ~42%; highly lipophilic, so it stores in brain lipids and adipose tissue. Metabolised in the liver by hydroxylation (chiefly to 6β-hydroxybromantane), with metabolites detectable in urine for up to ~2 weeks. Elimination half-life ~11 hours.
framework.genetics: No validated pharmacogenetic switch exists for bromantane — nothing like caffeine's CYP1A2. A sex difference in absorption is reported (faster time-to-peak in women than men), but individual response should be treated as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Weak-to-moderate and almost entirely Russian. Real trials exist — it's a prescription drug in Russia for asthenia — but the largest is open-label and non-comparative, there's essentially no independent Western replication, and no long-term safety data in healthy people using it as a nootropic. Two of five stars is generous framing, not an understatement.

pk.onset: 1.5–2 h (enzyme upregulation begins)
pk.peak: ~2.75 h (women) to ~4 h (men) plasma Tmax
pk.duration: ~8–12 h per dose
pk.note: Read this half-life differently from a stimulant's stopwatch. The felt effect isn't set by the blood curve — it's set by newly made enzyme and slow release from fat and brain stores, so benefit can build over days and, per the open-label Russian data, linger weeks after the last dose. The ~11 h half-life mainly explains why once-daily dosing keeps levels steady, and why urine stays positive for weeks.

journey.d: You swallow it. Real caution: bromantane is sold as a 'research chemical' on the gray market, where products are frequently mislabeled, underdosed, or contaminated — you often don't truly know what's in the bottle. About 42% of the dose gets absorbed.
It circulates — and being very fat-loving, it doesn't just pass through. It deposits into brain lipids and adipose tissue, forming a slow-release reservoir that drags out its action.
Inside dopamine neurons it acts on gene expression, upregulating the TH and AADC enzymes. Over the next hours the cell's capacity to synthesise dopamine climbs — the effect people are chasing.
With more dopamine being made (and a mild calming component layered on top), you get steady alertness and reduced fatigue without the wired edge or the rebound of a releaser.
The liver hydroxylates it, mainly to 6β-hydroxybromantane. There is little human safety data on long-term exposure, so the long tail of this drug is genuinely under-studied.
Metabolites leave in urine — and stay detectable for up to two weeks. That long trail is exactly why athletes get caught: bromantane has been WADA-banned since 1997 as a stimulant and masking agent.

myths.myth: It's a clean stimulant with no real downsides.
myths.truth: The 'no crash' part has a plausible mechanism, but 'no downsides' is not something the evidence can support. The human data is thin, largely Russian-language, and rarely replicated independently; there is no long-term safety data in healthy people using it as a nootropic; and it's WADA-banned. 'Milder felt profile' is not the same as 'proven safe.'

myths.myth: It permanently raises your dopamine / rewires your brain.
myths.truth: The gene upregulation is real but transient — enzyme levels rise and then return toward baseline. The persistence seen in one open-label trial is measured in weeks, not permanence. Claims of a permanent dopamine reset are overstated.

myths.myth: It's an approved, well-tested nootropic.
myths.truth: It's approved only in Russia (as Ladasten), and only for asthenia/neurasthenia — not as a cognitive enhancer for healthy people. It's unapproved everywhere else and sold as a research chemical. Approval in one country for one indication is not a Western nootropic green light.

myths.myth: You feel it kick in fast like caffeine.
myths.truth: The core mechanism is gene transcription — making new enzyme protein — which builds over hours and days, not seconds. Anything you feel in the first hour is not the main event.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is 'calm, no-crash stimulation and anti-fatigue — an actoprotector that helps you make dopamine instead of dumping it.' The dopamine-synthesis mechanism is genuinely interesting and animal data supports it; the human benefit claims are far softer than the marketing.

**The evidence cost:** almost all human data is Russian-language. The largest study (728 asthenia patients) was open-label with no placebo control, so expectation effects can't be separated out. There is essentially no independent Western replication.
**The safety-data gap:** there is no long-term safety data in healthy people using bromantane as a nootropic. 'No crash' is a mechanism, not a safety record.

**The legal and testing cost:** WADA-banned since 1997 as a stimulant and masking agent, with metabolites detectable in urine for ~2 weeks. For any tested athlete this is a doping positive waiting to happen.

**The product-quality cost:** sold as a gray-market research chemical, where independent testing repeatedly finds mislabeled, under- or over-dosed, or contaminated product — so you often don't know the dose or even the compound.
**The honest bottom line:** this page exists to explain the pharmacology, which is legitimately novel — not to help you add it to a stack. The mechanism is the value here; the human evidence is not.

start.q: In one line: how does bromantane raise dopamine differently from a normal stimulant?
start.a: It doesn't release dopamine or block reuptake — it upregulates the genes (TH, AADC) that build dopamine, so you make more rather than dumping your stores.

how.q: Bromantane has almost no receptor affinity — so where and how does it actually act?
how.a: Inside the neuron, on gene expression: it upregulates transcription of tyrosine hydroxylase (the rate-limiting dopamine-synthesis enzyme) and AADC, increasing the cell's capacity to make dopamine over hours.

use.q: Why does the effect build slowly and linger, instead of spiking and crashing?
use.a: Because the mechanism is making new enzyme protein (transcription) plus slow release from fat and brain stores — not a fast blood-level hit. Nothing is emptied out, so there's no depletion crash.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really?
evidence.a: Weak-to-moderate and almost entirely Russian. It's approved in Russia for asthenia, but the biggest trial was open-label with no placebo, there's no Western replication, and no long-term safety data in healthy users.

canExplain: Why bromantane can raise dopamine without releasing it or blocking reuptake
Why it has no receptor Ki to quote — it acts on gene expression, not a receptor
Why the effect builds over hours and lingers rather than spiking and crashing
Why 'no crash' is a mechanism, not a proof of safety — and how thin the human data really is
Why athletes test positive for it, and why it's banned despite sparse Western research

selfTest.q: A friend says bromantane 'boosts dopamine just like Adderall but with no crash.' Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) The route is opposite: amphetamine forces dopamine release and blocks reuptake (borrowing from your stores), while bromantane upregulates the TH/AADC genes so you synthesise more — which is genuinely why there's less crash. (2) 'No crash' isn't 'well-proven or safe': the human evidence is thin and largely open-label Russian data, with no long-term safety record in healthy users.

selfTest.q: Why does bromantane's ~11 h half-life tell you very little about when you'll feel it work?
selfTest.a: Because the effect isn't a blood-level phenomenon — it's driven by newly transcribed enzyme and slow release from fat/brain stores, which build over hours to days. The half-life mostly sets dosing frequency and explains the multi-week urine detection window, not the onset of benefit.

selfTest.q: The largest human trial reported ~90% improvement in asthenia. Why should you not take that number at face value?
selfTest.a: It was open-label and non-comparative — no placebo group, and both patients and doctors knew they were getting the active drug. Expectation and natural recovery can't be separated from the drug effect, so the headline number almost certainly overstates the true benefit.

evi.howStrong: Honestly weak-to-moderate, and lopsided. The mechanistic and animal pharmacology is reasonably developed — the TH/AADC upregulation is a real, characterised finding in rodents. The human clinical evidence is much thinner: it clears the bar to be a prescription drug in Russia (for asthenia/neurasthenia), but the largest trial is open-label and non-comparative, the controlled trials are small and Russian-language, and there is essentially no independent Western replication and no long-term safety data in healthy people using it as a nootropic. This is squarely a 'promising mechanism, sparse human proof' compound.
evi.whatItShows: In the Russian clinical literature, bromantane is reported to reduce symptoms of asthenia (chronic fatigue-type states) and neurasthenia and to normalise the sleep–wake cycle, with generally good short-term tolerability. What it does NOT have is robust, blinded, placebo-controlled, independently replicated evidence for cognitive enhancement in healthy people.
evi.effect: Effect sizes are hard to state honestly because the strongest-sounding numbers come from the weakest design. The open-label asthenia trial reported very high responder rates, but with no placebo arm those figures can't be trusted as the true drug effect. Treat any specific percentage as an upper bound inflated by expectation, not a clean estimate.
evi.whoBenefits: The studied populations were clinical — people with asthenia and neurasthenia, plus historical use in Soviet/Russian military and sports settings for fatigue and recovery. Use by healthy people for cognitive enhancement is off-label, gray-market, and essentially unstudied for both efficacy and safety.
evi.caveats: The core caveats: the flagship trial lacks a placebo control; the controlled trials are small and not independently replicated outside Russia; there is no long-term safety data in healthy users; product sold as a research chemical is frequently mislabeled or contaminated; and it is WADA-banned. Treat the trial claims as the best case under clinical, monitored conditions — not what a gray-market capsule delivers.

trials.finding: Large open-label, non-comparative post-marketing study of ~728 patients with asthenia given 50–100 mg/day for 28 days reported high clinician-rated improvement and normalisation of the sleep–wake cycle, with benefit reportedly persisting after discontinuation. Note: no placebo control — expectation effects are uncontrolled.
trials.ref: Voznesenskaia, Fokina & Iakhno, 2010 (Russian asthenia study)

trials.finding: Placebo-controlled clinical study of ladasten (bromantane) in patients with neurasthenia reported symptom improvement and good tolerability — one of the few controlled human datasets, but small and Russian-language.
trials.ref: Neznamov et al., 2009, Zhurnal Nevrologii i Psikhiatrii imeni S.S. Korsakova

tech.chem: Bromantane (ladasten, N-(4-bromophenyl)adamantan-2-amine; CAS 87913-26-6) is an adamantane derivative — an adamantane cage linked to a bromoaniline. It's a structural cousin of amantadine and memantine, but its pharmacology is distinct: rather than an NMDA/dopaminergic surface action, it works as an indirect genomic 'actoprotector.' High lipophilicity drives its brain and adipose distribution and long tissue residence.

targets.action: indirect transcriptional upregulation (increased gene/enzyme expression)
targets.affinity: not a ligand-binding target — no receptor Ki; bromantane increases TH mRNA/protein rather than binding the enzyme
targets.role: rate-limiting enzyme of catecholamine synthesis; the central node of bromantane's dopamine-synthesis-enhancing effect

targets.action: indirect transcriptional upregulation
targets.affinity: not a receptor target; no Ki
targets.role: converts L-DOPA to dopamine; its co-upregulation with TH supports increased net dopamine synthesis

targets.action: weak in-vitro reuptake inhibition
targets.affinity: only at ~50–500 µM — far above concentrations reached at real doses, so almost certainly not the operative mechanism
targets.role: a textbook 'it also blocks reuptake' claim that does not apply at physiological exposures

tech.signaling: The mechanism is indirect and genomic rather than a receptor cascade: bromantane is thought to enter dopaminergic neurons and act via intracellular signalling — activation of cAMP-, Ca2+- and phospholipid-dependent kinases (notably protein kinase A and especially protein kinase C) has been associated with its effects — leading to upregulated transcription of the TH and AADC genes and thus increased dopamine biosynthetic capacity. The precise upstream trigger remains unknown. A separate mild anxiolytic component is described clinically, but its molecular basis is not well characterised.

adme.absorb: ~42% oral bioavailability; rapidly absorbed, with a reported faster absorption/time-to-peak in women (Tmax ~2.75 h) than men (~4 h).
adme.distribute: Highly lipophilic; distributes widely and deposits in brain lipids and adipose tissue, creating a slow-release reservoir that prolongs action.
adme.metabolise: Hepatic hydroxylation, chiefly at the 6-position of the adamantane ring, yielding metabolites such as 6β-hydroxybromantane.
adme.excrete: Excreted mainly as metabolites in urine, detectable for up to ~2 weeks; elimination half-life ~11 h.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. A sex difference in absorption rate is reported, but there is no established gene (nothing analogous to caffeine's CYP1A2) that predicts individual response, which should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: Clinical dosing in Russia is 50–100 mg/day for asthenia (typically a ~28-day course). No approved therapeutic dose exists outside Russia, and no dose is validated for cognitive enhancement in healthy people. This is a description of the clinical literature, not a recommendation.
tech.tolerance: There is no receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense, since it doesn't act through a receptor. The gene-expression effect is transient (enzyme levels rise then normalise), and clinical reports describe sustained benefit within a course rather than tachyphylaxis — but long-term and repeated-course data in healthy users is essentially absent, so tolerance and dependence risk are genuinely uncharacterised.

questions: Why do you have to swallow phenibut by the gram, when a real anti-anxiety drug works at a few milligrams?
Why does it feel like almost nothing for the first few hours — right up until someone takes a second dose and gets floored?
Why can a few weeks of daily use turn simply stopping into a medical emergency, with seizures and hallucinations?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Phenibut grips its targets only weakly (micromolar affinity), so it takes gram doses to occupy enough receptors — and a brain drug you measure in grams is a red flag, not a feature. (2) Onset is slow, roughly 2–4 h, peaking around 4–6 h; that delay tempts people to redose, so both doses peak together and combine. (3) Daily use makes your GABA system adapt; stop abruptly and rebound excitation breaks through as a benzodiazepine-like withdrawal — anxiety, insomnia, hallucinations, even seizures — documented in case reports and often needing hospital care and a baclofen taper.

phenibut.bigIdea: Phenibut is your brain's own calming molecule, GABA, wearing a greasy phenyl-ring disguise so it can sneak across the blood–brain barrier — which is exactly why it soothes anxiety, and why leaning on that borrowed brake breeds tolerance and a brutal withdrawal fast.
phenibut.analogy: GABA is your brain's brake fluid, but swallowed as a pill it can't get in — it's too water-loving to cross the oily blood–brain barrier. Phenibut is GABA with a greasy phenyl ring bolted on that smuggles it across. Once inside it presses the very same brake — but it's a brake your brain quickly reinforces against, so you need more and more, and letting go snaps back hard.

mechSteps.t: GABA can't get in — so they redesigned it
mechSteps.predict: Your brain already makes a 'calm down' molecule, but you can't just swallow it and feel calmer. Why not — and what single change fixes that?
mechSteps.d: GABA is too polar to cross the oily blood–brain barrier, so oral GABA barely reaches the brain. Bolt a phenyl ring onto it and the molecule turns lipophilic enough to slip across — that molecule is phenibut.

mechSteps.t: Inside, it presses the GABA-B brake
mechSteps.predict: Once across, it docks the same receptor as your brain's own GABA. What does switching that receptor ON do to a neuron?
mechSteps.d: Phenibut is a GABA-B receptor (GABBR1) agonist: activating it opens potassium channels and hyperpolarises neurons, quieting their firing — felt as reduced anxiety, sociability and, higher up, sedation.

mechSteps.t: It also works like gabapentin
mechSteps.predict: Modern binding studies found a second target it hits even harder than GABA-B. What kind of drug does that make it?
mechSteps.d: Phenibut also binds the α2δ subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels — the same site as gabapentin and pregabalin — and in fact grips it about 4-fold more tightly than it grips GABA-B. That dampens the release of excitatory neurotransmitters.

mechSteps.t: Weak grip forces gram doses
mechSteps.predict: Its affinity at both targets sits in the micromolar range. What does that weak grip force you to do with the dose — and why is that a warning sign?
mechSteps.d: Because it engages both targets only weakly, it takes doses measured in grams — orders of magnitude more than a genuine anxiolytic — to occupy enough receptors. A psychoactive you have to dose by the gram is a red flag in itself.

mechSteps.t: Adapt fast → dependence
mechSteps.predict: Press the same brake every day and the brain compensates. What happens the day you stop?
mechSteps.d: With daily use the GABA system adapts, so the effect fades (tolerance) and you escalate. Stop abruptly and rebound excitation floods through — a benzodiazepine/baclofen-like withdrawal with anxiety, insomnia, hallucinations and even seizures.

framework.target: GABA-B receptor (GABBR1) — a G-protein-coupled receptor — where phenibut is an agonist (it switches the receptor ON, unlike caffeine which blocks its target). Modern binding data add a second target: the α2δ subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels, the same site gabapentin and pregabalin hit.
framework.affinity: Weak on both counts. R-phenibut (the active enantiomer) binds the α2δ subunit with a Ki around 23 µM and the GABA-B receptor roughly 4-fold weaker still — all micromolar. With caffeine you ask whether a real dose even reaches the target; here the grip is so loose that it takes gram doses to engage enough receptors. Dosing a brain drug by the gram is itself the warning.
framework.adme: Orally active; the phenyl ring is precisely what lets it cross the blood–brain barrier (the whole point of its design). Onset is notably slow — roughly 2–4 h — peaking around 4–6 h, with an elimination half-life near 5.3 h (from limited human data). Largely renally excreted.
framework.genetics: No validated pharmacogenetic switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. The one molecular subtlety is chirality: phenibut is sold as a racemate, but the R-enantiomer carries essentially all the GABA-B activity while the S-enantiomer barely binds. Individual response is otherwise unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Weak and old. The efficacy literature is largely Soviet-era, open-label, Russian-language work with no randomised controlled trials and no rigorous statistics; a 2020 systematic review could not extract usable efficacy data. The strongest modern, English-language literature on phenibut is case reports — of dependence, withdrawal and poison-center exposures. 2/5.

pk.onset: 2–4 h (notably slow)
pk.duration: hours
pk.note: The slow onset is a trap: because little is felt in the first few hours, people assume it isn't working and redose — then both doses peak together and floor them, especially alongside alcohol or benzodiazepines. Treat the ~5.3 h half-life as a rough figure from limited human data, not a well-characterised value.

journey.d: You swallow a dose — measured anywhere from hundreds of milligrams into grams. Sold gray-market as loose powder or capsules, with no guarantee of the dose or purity on the label. It's absorbed across the gut.
It circulates, but builds slowly — for the first 2–4 h you may feel very little, which is exactly what tempts people into a second dose.
Here the phenyl ring earns its keep: it makes the molecule lipophilic enough to cross the blood–brain barrier that blocks plain GABA. Without that ring, none of this happens.
It binds GABA-B receptors and the α2δ calcium-channel subunit, quieting neural firing — the calm, sociable, sedated feeling, peaking around 4–6 h in.
With repeated daily use the GABA system compensates; the effect fades (tolerance), and the brain increasingly needs the drug just to feel level.
It's cleared largely by the kidneys (half-life ~5.3 h). Stop abruptly after regular use and rebound excitation breaks through — the documented, sometimes life-threatening withdrawal.

myths.myth: It's just GABA, a natural calming molecule, so it's gentle and safe.
myths.truth: It's a synthetic GABA analog engineered to cross into the brain — something real GABA can't do. That brain access, plus fast tolerance, is exactly what makes it habit-forming and its withdrawal dangerous. It's the opposite of gentle.

myths.myth: It's legal, so it's basically a supplement.
myths.truth: The FDA ruled phenibut is not a lawful dietary ingredient and it cannot be sold as a supplement in the US. It's a controlled substance in Australia and illegal in Germany, among others. It's an approved medicine only in a handful of post-Soviet countries.

myths.myth: Tolerance builds slowly, the way it does with caffeine.
myths.truth: Tolerance and physical dependence can develop within days to a few weeks of regular use. Daily users escalate quickly, and abrupt cessation produces a benzodiazepine-like withdrawal — anxiety, insomnia, hallucinations, even seizures — that frequently requires hospitalisation.

myths.myth: If it goes wrong, you can just stop.
myths.truth: Cold-turkey withdrawal can be genuinely dangerous. Clinicians often manage it with a baclofen taper — baclofen being phenibut's close chemical cousin. This is not a substance you quit casually.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a calming, anti-anxiety, pro-social 'smart drug' — relaxed and sociable without alcohol. The acute anxiolytic feeling is real; almost everything else about the pitch is not.
**The dependence cost:** it hooks fast. Tolerance and physical dependence can set in within days to weeks of regular use, and because its grip on the receptor is weak, users escalate into gram doses.

**The withdrawal cost:** stopping after regular use produces a benzodiazepine-like withdrawal — severe anxiety, insomnia, tremor, hallucinations, psychosis and seizures — documented in case reports and often requiring hospitalisation and a baclofen taper.

**The overdose and interaction cost:** the slow onset invites redosing, and combined with alcohol or benzodiazepines it can cause heavy sedation, unresponsiveness and respiratory depression. US poison-center exposures rose sharply through the 2010s.

**The legal and quality cost:** not approved as a medicine or supplement in the US, controlled in Australia, illegal in Germany and elsewhere. Gray-market powder has no dosing standard and no purity guarantee.

**The honest evidence picture:** there are no randomised controlled trials. The efficacy claims rest on old, non-English, open-label reports, while the robust modern literature is about its harms. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you use it.

start.q: In one line, why is phenibut essentially 'GABA that can reach the brain'?
start.a: GABA is your brain's calming molecule but can't cross the blood–brain barrier from a pill; phenibut is GABA with a phenyl ring added that lets it slip across and press the same brake.

how.q: Phenibut has two molecular targets — name them, and say which it grips more tightly.
how.a: The GABA-B receptor (GABBR1) and the α2δ subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels (gabapentin's target). It binds α2δ about 4x more tightly than GABA-B.

use.q: Why is a psychoactive that you dose by the gram a warning sign?
use.a: Because its affinity is weak (micromolar), it takes gram quantities to occupy enough receptors — far more than a genuine anxiolytic — and those large doses are exactly what drive dependence, oversedation and overdose.

evidence.q: How good is the human evidence that phenibut actually works?
evidence.a: Poor. No randomised controlled trials exist; efficacy claims come from old, open-label, mostly Russian-language studies, while the strong modern literature documents dependence, withdrawal and overdose.

canExplain: Why phenibut is essentially GABA engineered to cross the blood–brain barrier
The two targets it hits — GABA-B and the α2δ calcium-channel subunit — and which one it grips harder
Why its weak affinity forces gram-sized doses, and why that's a red flag
Why tolerance and a dangerous, benzodiazepine-like withdrawal develop so fast
How thin the efficacy evidence really is, and what the real modern literature is actually about

selfTest.q: A friend says phenibut is safe because 'it's just natural GABA.' Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) It isn't natural GABA — it's a synthetic analog with a phenyl ring added precisely so it can cross into the brain, which real GABA can't. (2) That brain access plus fast tolerance is what makes it dependence-forming, with a withdrawal (seizures, hallucinations) that plain GABA could never cause.

selfTest.q: Why does phenibut's slow onset make overdose more likely?
selfTest.a: Because little is felt for the first 2–4 hours, people assume it isn't working and take a second dose; then both doses peak together (around 4–6 h) and combine — especially dangerous alongside alcohol or benzodiazepines.

selfTest.q: Doctors treat phenibut withdrawal with baclofen. What does that tell you about the two molecules?
selfTest.a: They're close chemical cousins — baclofen is phenibut with a chlorine added to the ring — and both are GABA-B agonists, so baclofen can substitute at the same receptor and then be tapered down in a controlled, medical way.

evi.howStrong: Weak — and it's worth being blunt about it. There are no randomised controlled trials of phenibut for anxiety or anything else. The efficacy claims trace back to Soviet-era, open-label, Russian-language reports that a 2020 systematic review found impossible to analyse (no English text, no statistics). The strongest, most rigorous modern literature on phenibut is not about whether it works — it's case reports and poison-center data about dependence, withdrawal and overdose. Curated as 2/5, and even that reflects an acute effect people clearly feel rather than any proven clinical benefit.
evi.whatItShows: That phenibut acutely calms and sedates is not really in doubt — it's a GABA-B agonist and gabapentinoid, and users reliably feel anxiolysis and sociability. What is entirely unshown by modern controlled evidence is that it safely or durably treats any condition. The well-documented outcomes are the harms.
evi.effect: No reliable effect size exists, because no controlled trial ever measured one. What the literature does quantify is harm: a sharp rise in US poison-center exposures across the 2010s, and case series in which the large majority of phenibut-withdrawal presentations required hospitalisation or clinical care.
evi.whoBenefits: There is no evidence-based 'who benefits' — no population has been shown in controlled trials to gain a durable benefit. The clearest identifiable group is the one that gets harmed: regular, escalating users who develop dependence and then face a dangerous withdrawal.
evi.caveats: Treat the 'anxiolytic' label as a description of an acute feeling, not a proven therapy. The absence of RCTs, the reliance on inaccessible old data, the fast tolerance and the severe withdrawal together mean the risk-to-evidence ratio is poor. Gray-market product quality adds a further unknown.

trials.finding: Systematic review of phenibut clinical trials and case reports: found 11 clinical trials (583 patients), but none available in English and none containing statistical analysis, precluding any efficacy conclusion; catalogued dependence and withdrawal case reports.
trials.ref: Kupats et al., 2020 (systematic review)

trials.finding: Pharmacology review characterising phenibut (β-phenyl-GABA) as a GABA-B-acting tranquilizer and nootropic, based largely on older, non-controlled Russian data.
trials.ref: Lapin, 2001, CNS Drug Reviews

trials.finding: R-phenibut binds the α2-δ subunit of voltage-dependent calcium channels (Ki ≈ 23 µM) about 4x more tightly than the GABA-B receptor, with gabapentin-like anti-nociceptive effects in animal models.
trials.ref: Zvejniece et al., 2015, Pharmacol Biochem Behav

trials.finding: Notes from the Field: US poison-center phenibut exposures rose markedly (2009–2019), with reports of dependence, withdrawal and severe toxicity.

tech.chem: Phenibut is β-phenyl-γ-aminobutyric acid (β-phenyl-GABA; PubChem CID 14113) — the neurotransmitter GABA with a phenyl ring added at the β (3) carbon. That aromatic ring is the whole design: it makes the otherwise polar GABA lipophilic enough to cross the blood–brain barrier. Its close relative baclofen is the same molecule with a chlorine on that ring. Phenibut is chiral and sold as a racemate; the R-enantiomer carries the GABA-B activity, the S-enantiomer barely binds. It was synthesised in the 1960s USSR (Perekalin and colleagues, Herzen Institute, Leningrad) and was once carried in cosmonaut medical kits.

targets.action: agonist
targets.affinity: micromolar; R-phenibut binds ~4x more weakly here than at α2δ, and the S-enantiomer is essentially inactive
targets.role: the classic curated mechanism — a Gi/o-coupled GPCR that opens K+ channels and inhibits Ca²⁺ channels, hyperpolarising neurons → anxiolysis and sedation

targets.sym: CACNA2D1 (α2δ-1 subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels)
targets.action: ligand at the gabapentinoid site → reduces excitatory neurotransmitter release
targets.affinity: R-phenibut Ki ≈ 23 µM (vs baclofen ≈ 156 µM and gabapentin ≈ 0.05 µM at the same site)
targets.role: the target phenibut actually grips most tightly — the gabapentin/pregabalin mechanism that helps explain both its calming effect and its gabapentinoid-like withdrawal

targets.action: mild stimulatory activity reported
targets.affinity: not well quantified
targets.role: may add the pro-social, mildly stimulating tint at higher doses; weakly characterised and secondary to the GABA-B/α2δ actions

tech.signaling: GABA-B is a Gi/o-coupled GPCR that works as an obligate heterodimer (GABBR1 + GABBR2). Agonism inhibits adenylyl cyclase, opens GIRK potassium channels and inhibits presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels — the net effect is neuronal hyperpolarisation and reduced neurotransmitter release, i.e. inhibition and sedation. Independently, binding the α2δ auxiliary subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels (the gabapentinoid mechanism) further curbs excitatory release. Chronic activation drives compensatory adaptation of this inhibitory tone, so abrupt withdrawal unmasks rebound excitation — the neurobiology behind the benzodiazepine/baclofen-like withdrawal syndrome. A mild dopaminergic component is reported but poorly characterised.

adme.absorb: Orally active; the phenyl ring confers the lipophilicity needed to cross the gut and, crucially, the blood–brain barrier. Onset is slow — roughly 2–4 h.
adme.distribute: Distributes into brain, kidney and liver and crosses the BBB (its design purpose). Peak effects land around 4–6 h.
adme.metabolise: Human metabolism is not well characterised; phenibut appears to be largely eliminated unchanged rather than extensively metabolised.
adme.excrete: Excreted largely by the kidneys; elimination half-life reported near 5.3 h, though based on limited human data.

tech.pgx: No validated pharmacogenetic marker. The main molecular nuance is stereochemistry: the racemate's GABA-B activity resides almost entirely in the R-enantiomer, while both enantiomers bind α2δ similarly. Individual response and dependence risk are otherwise unpredictable.
tech.dose: There is no approved or validated dose for nootropic/anxiolytic self-use. Because affinity is low, self-reported use runs from hundreds of milligrams into multiple grams — orders of magnitude above a true anxiolytic. This is a description of gray-market practice, not a recommendation, and gram-range dosing is precisely what drives dependence and overdose. In the few countries where it's a licensed medicine, it's prescribed and monitored.
tech.tolerance: Rapid. Unlike creatine (no tolerance) and more aggressively than caffeine, regular use produces tolerance within days to weeks, driving dose escalation. Abrupt cessation after sustained use unmasks rebound excitation — a benzodiazepine/baclofen-like withdrawal (anxiety, insomnia, tremor, hallucinations, psychosis, seizures) that frequently requires hospitalisation and is often managed with a baclofen taper.

contrasts.title: Phenibut vs baclofen — one chlorine apart
contrasts.point: They're near-twins. Baclofen is phenibut with a single chlorine added to the phenyl ring, and both are GABA-B agonists built by bolting an aromatic ring onto GABA so it can cross into the brain. The difference is regulatory, not chemical: baclofen is an approved, prescribed, monitored medicine (a muscle relaxant), while phenibut is the unapproved gray-market version. Tellingly, doctors reach for baclofen to treat phenibut withdrawal — the same brake, in a medical setting.

contrasts.title: Phenibut vs gabapentin — the shared calcium-channel target
contrasts.point: Phenibut isn't only a GABA-B drug. It binds the α2δ subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels — gabapentin's and pregabalin's target — and in binding studies grips it more tightly than it grips GABA-B. That gabapentinoid action helps explain both its calming effect and why its withdrawal syndrome looks so much like a gabapentinoid/benzodiazepine one.

questions: How can a lab-made peptide be 'ten million times more potent than BDNF' — and does a number that extreme actually hold up?
If it builds new brain connections by revving a growth-factor pathway, why does that same pathway make cancer doctors nervous?
It's marketed as one of the most powerful nootropics ever made — so why has no human ever been dosed with it in a trial, and why did its flagship mechanism paper get retracted?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) The '10-million-fold vs BDNF' figure comes from the developing group's own spinogenesis assays, and the keystone paper that pinned down the HGF-binding mechanism was retracted in 2025 for fabricated figures — so treat the headline number as a bold claim, not a settled fact. (2) HGF/c-Met is one of the best-characterised oncogenic pathways in medicine: the FDA approves c-Met *inhibitors* (capmatinib, tepotinib) to fight cancer, and dihexa pushes that same pedal the opposite way, with zero carcinogenicity studies ever done. (3) No human has been dosed with dihexa in a published trial; the closest clinical relative, Athira's fosgonimeton, did reach large Alzheimer's trials — and failed them in 2023.

dihexa.bigIdea: Dihexa doesn't act on your neurons directly — it's proposed to turbo-charge a growth-factor pathway (HGF/c-Met) that sprouts new synapses, which is why it's astonishingly potent in rodents and, for the exact same reason, carries a real tumour question.
dihexa.analogy: Think of HGF as a 'grow' signal your brain uses sparingly, and c-Met as the receiver that reads it. Dihexa is reportedly a volume knob that makes a faint HGF whisper land like a shout — so a tiny amount of the growth signal is suddenly enough to trigger a burst of new synaptic wiring. But a volume knob on a 'grow' signal doesn't know the difference between wiring a memory and feeding a tumour.

mechSteps.t: It's a rebuilt fragment of a blood-pressure hormone
mechSteps.predict: Angiotensin IV is a tiny hormone better known for blood pressure — how do you turn it into a brain-growth molecule?
mechSteps.d: Dihexa is a chemically armoured piece of angiotensin IV — its Tyr-Ile core capped with a hexanoic acid at one end and an aminohexanoic amide at the other. Those caps aren't decoration: they let it resist the enzymes that shred ordinary peptides, dissolve into fat, and cross the blood-brain barrier where the raw hormone can't.

mechSteps.t: It amplifies a growth-factor whisper
mechSteps.predict: On its own it does little — it needs a partner already present in the brain. Which one, and what does dihexa do to it?
mechSteps.d: The proposed mechanism is that dihexa potentiates hepatocyte growth factor (HGF), lowering the threshold at which HGF fires — so a below-threshold amount of the growth signal suddenly becomes enough to act.

mechSteps.t: The c-Met receiver switches on
mechSteps.predict: HGF has one receptor — a receptor tyrosine kinase. What kind of switch is it, and how did researchers show dihexa needs it?
mechSteps.d: Boosted HGF activates c-Met (the MET receptor tyrosine kinase), kicking off a growth cascade inside the neuron. In the original rodent work, blocking c-Met — with an antagonist or by knocking it down — abolished the effect, the evidence that this is the route. (Note: the paper reporting that step was later retracted for fabricated data.)

mechSteps.t: New synapses physically sprout
mechSteps.predict: The end result isn't a fast 'feeling' like a stimulant — it's a structural change that takes time. What actually grows?
mechSteps.d: The cascade drives dendritic spines and new synapses to form in the hippocampus — physical new wiring, built over days, not a same-hour buzz. That structural growth is why the animal memory effects are striking, and why the timescale is slow.

framework.target: The HGF/c-Met system — hepatocyte growth factor and its receptor tyrosine kinase c-Met (MET). Dihexa isn't a classic receptor agonist you simply flip on; it's proposed to act as a positive modulator/potentiator of HGF, so the growth signal it amplifies is one your body already makes.
framework.affinity: Here's the honest catch. The headline figure — a binding affinity of roughly 65 pM to HGF — comes from Benoist et al., 2014, a paper retracted in 2025 for fabricated images. So the single most-quoted affinity number for dihexa should be treated as unverified, not gospel. What isn't in dispute is that it's active at very low doses in animals (orally effective at ~1.25–2 mg/kg in rodents).
framework.adme: Genuinely well-engineered PK for a peptide — that was the whole point of the redesign. The hexanoic/aminohexanoic caps make it metabolically stable (serum stability on the order of hours in vitro), lipophilic, orally active, and brain-penetrant in rodents. No verified human PK exists.
framework.genetics: No characterised pharmacogenetics. c-Met signalling and MET amplification matter enormously in cancer biology, but no validated marker predicts an individual's response — or risk — to dihexa. Treat both as unknown.
framework.evidence: The weakest lens, and the one that matters most. The striking rodent memory data come substantially from one research group; the keystone mechanistic paper was retracted for data fabrication; there are zero human trials of dihexa; and the closest clinical relative (fosgonimeton) failed its pivotal Alzheimer's trial. A 1/5-star rating is generous.

journey.d: You take it — a gray-market 'research chemical,' unapproved anywhere, with no guarantee the vial contains what the label claims. It's absorbed across the gut unusually well for a peptide, thanks to its engineered fat-loving caps.
It circulates, resisting the enzymes that normally shred peptides within minutes. Small and lipophilic, it's built to reach the brain rather than be filtered out.
It crosses the blood-brain barrier and, per the proposed mechanism, potentiates HGF at c-Met on neurons — sparking a growth cascade concentrated in the hippocampus.
Over days, new dendritic spines and synapses form. This structural rewiring — not a fast chemical buzz — is the effect being chased, and it's why the timescale is slow.

c-Met sits on far more than neurons, and it's a master oncogenic switch. The same amplification that may wire a synapse could, in principle, feed a dormant tumour — and no carcinogenicity study has ever checked.

myths.myth: Dihexa is proven to be 10 million times stronger than BDNF.
myths.truth: That figure comes from the developing lab's own assays, and the paper that nailed down the underlying HGF mechanism was retracted in 2025 for fabricated data. The potency may well be large, but 'proven' is the wrong word — treat it as a striking claim awaiting independent replication.

myths.myth: It grows new brain cells (neurons).
myths.truth: The described effect is synaptogenesis — new connections between existing neurons — not new neurons. Different process, and the distinction matters when you read the claims.

myths.myth: No reported side effects means it's safe.
myths.truth: 'No reported side effects' mostly means almost no one has been studied. There are no human trials and no carcinogenicity studies, while the mechanism itself amplifies a well-known cancer pathway. Silence is absence of data, not evidence of safety.

myths.myth: It's basically an approved Alzheimer's drug in disguise.
myths.truth: No. Dihexa is approved nowhere. Its clinical cousin fosgonimeton did reach large Alzheimer's trials — and failed to beat placebo in 2023.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is 'the most potent nootropic ever made' — a peptide that grows new brain connections and sharpens memory. The rodent data behind that pitch are genuinely striking; the human data behind it are nonexistent.

**The evidence cost:** there is not a single published human trial of dihexa. The animal work leans heavily on one research group, and its keystone mechanistic paper was retracted in 2025 for fabricated figures — so even the mechanism is on shakier ground than the marketing implies.

**The cancer cost (the big one):** dihexa amplifies HGF/c-Met, one of the most firmly established oncogenic pathways in medicine — the same pathway cancer drugs (capmatinib, tepotinib) are approved to *block*. No carcinogenicity study has ever been done. This is the risk that should stop you.
**The clinical reality check:** the closest, far-better-developed relative — Athira's fosgonimeton — failed its Phase 2/3 Alzheimer's trial in 2023, a sobering signal for the whole approach.
**The product-quality cost:** sold only as a gray-market 'research chemical,' with no oversight of purity, dose, or identity — you often don't know what's actually in the vial.

**The honest bottom line:** this page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you dose it. Striking animal potency, zero human evidence, and a real theoretical tumour risk is not a stack you add to — it's a molecule to understand.

start.q: In one line: what does dihexa actually do — does it act on neurons directly?
start.a: No — it's proposed to potentiate a growth-factor pathway (HGF/c-Met) that then sprouts new synapses. It works one step upstream, on a growth signal, not directly on the neuron's firing.

how.q: What is the proposed chain from dihexa to a new synapse?
how.a: Dihexa potentiates HGF → HGF activates its receptor c-Met (MET) → a growth cascade drives new dendritic spines and synapses in the hippocampus over days.

use.q: Why is the HGF/c-Met mechanism also the reason to be cautious?
use.a: c-Met is a canonical oncogenic driver — cancer drugs are approved to block it. Dihexa pushes it the other way, and no carcinogenicity study has ever been done.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really?
evidence.a: There is none — zero human trials of dihexa. The animal data are striking but concentrated in one group, the keystone mechanism paper was retracted, and the closest clinical relative failed its Alzheimer's trial.

canExplain: Why dihexa is called a 'synaptogenic' compound — and how that differs from making new neurons
The proposed HGF/c-Met mechanism, acting one step upstream of the neuron itself
Why the same growth pathway that may build synapses raises a serious cancer question
Why '10 million times more potent than BDNF' is a claim to hold at arm's length, not a proven fact
Why zero human trials, a retracted keystone paper, and a failed clinical cousin add up to a 1-star evidence rating

selfTest.q: A friend says: 'c-Met inhibitors like capmatinib are cancer drugs, so dihexa — which activates the same pathway — must be an anti-cancer nootropic.' Where's the error?
selfTest.a: It's backwards. Capmatinib treats cancer by *blocking* c-Met, because c-Met signalling drives tumour growth. Dihexa *amplifies* that same pathway — pushing the pedal cancer drugs are designed to release — which is precisely why it carries a theoretical tumour risk, not a protective one.

selfTest.q: Dihexa's most-cited affinity figure is a ~65 pM binding to HGF. Why should you not repeat that number confidently?
selfTest.a: Because it comes from Benoist et al., 2014, which was retracted in 2025 for fabricated figures. The rule is to omit or flag numbers you can't verify — and a retracted source is exactly that.

selfTest.q: Fosgonimeton reached large human Alzheimer's trials but dihexa never has. What does fosgonimeton's 2023 result tell you about the approach?
selfTest.a: It's a caution, not a validation. Fosgonimeton — a better-developed molecule in the same angiotensin-IV / HGF–c-Met lineage — failed to beat placebo in its Phase 2/3 LIFT-AD trial, so even the clinically-tested version of this idea has not shown a reliable human benefit.

evi.howStrong: Weak — about as weak as human evidence gets. There is not one published human clinical trial of dihexa itself. Its reputation rests on rodent memory studies (Morris water maze, scopolamine-reversal) that are genuinely striking but come substantially from a single Washington State University group. Critically, the keystone paper mapping the HGF/c-Met mechanism — Benoist et al., 2014 — was retracted in 2025 after a university investigation found fabricated and falsified figures (the first author, who later became CEO of Athira Pharma, had her PhD revoked). So both the human evidence and part of the mechanistic evidence are compromised.
evi.whatItShows: In rodents, dihexa reverses chemically- and lesion-induced memory deficits and drives hippocampal spinogenesis. What it has NOT shown is any effect in humans, or long-term safety in any species relevant to chronic human use.
evi.effect: The famous figure — reportedly ~10 million-fold (seven orders of magnitude) more potent than BDNF at promoting synapse formation — is a claim from the developing group's own assays, not an independently replicated benchmark. In rodents it was orally active at roughly 1.25–2 mg/kg. Translate none of this to a human dose or effect; that leap has never been made.
evi.whoBenefits: Only laboratory rodents have 'benefited' in controlled studies. No human population has been shown to gain anything, and the closest clinical relative, fosgonimeton, did not beat placebo when tested in Alzheimer's patients.
evi.caveats: Retracted keystone mechanism paper; single-group animal data; zero human trials; no carcinogenicity studies on a compound that amplifies a canonical oncogenic pathway; and gray-market products of unknown purity, dose and identity. Every one of these points the same way: this is an early research compound, not a validated nootropic.

trials.finding: Metabolically stabilised angiotensin-IV analogs (including dihexa) reversed scopolamine-induced memory deficits in rats, were orally active and brain-penetrant, and showed serum stability on the order of hours — the pharmacokinetic case for dihexa as a drug candidate. (Non-retracted.)
trials.ref: McCoy et al., 2013, J Pharmacol Exp Ther 344:141–154

trials.finding: Reported that dihexa binds HGF and that its synaptogenic effect is HGF/c-Met-dependent — this was the keystone mechanistic paper, and it was RETRACTED in 2025 after a Washington State University investigation found fabricated/falsified figures. Cited here only to flag that the mechanism's primary evidence is compromised.
trials.ref: Benoist et al., 2014, J Pharmacol Exp Ther (RETRACTED 2025)

trials.finding: Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017), a related HGF/c-Met-pathway molecule and the closest clinical relative, failed to meet its primary or key secondary endpoints in the Phase 2/3 LIFT-AD trial of mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease (safety was acceptable).
trials.ref: Athira Pharma LIFT-AD Phase 2/3 topline results, 2023

tech.chem: N-hexanoic-Tyr-Ile-(6)aminohexanoic amide (also designated PNB-0408); molecular formula C₂₇H₄₄N₄O₅, MW ≈ 504.7. A small, metabolically stabilised analog of angiotensin IV: the Tyr-Ile pharmacophore is N-capped with a hexanoic acid and C-extended with a 6-aminohexanoic amide — modifications that block peptidase degradation, raise lipophilicity, and confer blood-brain-barrier penetration. Not a steroid and not a stimulant; an engineered peptide.

targets.action: proposed positive modulation via potentiation of its ligand HGF (not direct receptor agonism)
targets.affinity: no verified affinity constant — the widely-cited ~65 pM HGF-binding figure is from a retracted paper (Benoist et al., 2014) and should not be relied on
targets.role: receptor tyrosine kinase; the growth-cascade switch whose activation was required for the synaptogenic effect in rodent studies — and, separately, a canonical oncogenic driver

targets.action: proposed binding/potentiation — dihexa is reported to lower the threshold at which HGF activates c-Met
targets.affinity: unverified (see MET note; the primary source was retracted)
targets.role: the endogenous growth factor whose signal dihexa is thought to amplify

targets.action: the original proposed target of angiotensin-IV analogs; dihexa's procognitive effect was later re-attributed to HGF/c-Met rather than IRAP inhibition
targets.role: historical/uncertain — included for accuracy, as the AT4-receptor story predates the HGF hypothesis

tech.signaling: c-Met is a receptor tyrosine kinase. HGF binding triggers receptor dimerisation, autophosphorylation, and downstream PI3K/AKT and RAS/MAPK cascades that, in neurons, promote dendritic-spine formation and synaptogenesis. Dihexa is proposed to act upstream, at the HGF ligand, to potentiate this signal. The very same c-Met cascade, when dysregulated, drives tumour proliferation, invasion and metastasis — which is why the procognitive and oncogenic mechanisms are two faces of one pathway.

adme.absorb: engineered for oral activity; the lipophilic caps give unusually good absorption for a peptide — orally effective in rodents at ~1.25–2 mg/kg
adme.distribute: lipophilic and brain-penetrant in rodents (crossing the BBB was the design goal); metabolically stabilised against serum peptidases, with in-vitro serum stability on the order of hours (McCoy et al., 2013)
adme.metabolise: no verified human metabolism data
adme.excrete: no verified human data — human ADME is essentially uncharacterised

tech.pgx: No validated pharmacogenetics. MET amplification and mutation are central to several cancers, but no marker predicts an individual's response or risk to dihexa. Unknown.
tech.dose: No approved or validated human dose exists anywhere. Rodent studies used roughly 1.25–2 mg/kg orally (and 0.25–0.5 mg/kg intraperitoneal; 0.1–1 nmol intracerebroventricular). Gray-market anecdotal human use is unstudied and unvalidated for safety — described here, not recommended.
tech.tolerance: No characterised tolerance data. Because the effect is structural (synaptogenesis) rather than receptor-occupancy signalling, classic receptor-desensitisation tolerance may not apply — but this is unstudied, and the dominant unknown is cumulative safety, not tachyphylaxis.

contrasts.title: Dihexa vs BDNF — the endogenous grow-signal vs a synthetic amplifier
contrasts.point: BDNF is your brain's own neurotrophin — the growth factor it uses to strengthen and build synapses. Dihexa reportedly reaches the same destination (new synapses) but through a different door — the HGF/c-Met pathway, not the TrkB pathway BDNF uses — and is claimed to do so with vastly greater potency. The lesson: 'more potent than BDNF' doesn't mean 'does what BDNF does.' It's a different pathway to a similar structural end, and the potency figure itself traces back to compromised data.

contrasts.title: Dihexa vs capmatinib — same pathway, opposite direction
contrasts.point: Capmatinib and tepotinib are FDA-approved cancer drugs whose entire job is to block c-Met, because runaway c-Met signalling drives tumours. Dihexa does the reverse — it amplifies the HGF/c-Met signal. That single directional flip is the whole safety story: the exact pathway medicine spends billions learning to shut off in cancer is the one this nootropic turns up — with no carcinogenicity data to reassure you.

questions: Why does 0.5 mg of melatonin work just as well as 10 mg — and sometimes better?
Why does it fix jet lag but barely dent ordinary insomnia — shaving only a few minutes off how long you take to fall asleep?
Why does taking it in the evening pull your body clock earlier, while the same pill in the morning pushes it later?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Melatonin's receptors are saturated at picomolar-to-low-nanomolar blood levels, so a tiny 0.3-0.5 mg dose already tops out the signal — bigger doses just spill over, keep levels high into the morning, and leave you groggy (Zhdanova et al., 2001). (2) It's a clock-shifter, not a sedative: it re-times when you get sleepy rather than knocking you out, so it shines for jet lag and delayed sleep phase but only trims sleep-onset latency by roughly 7 minutes on average in primary insomnia (Ferracioli-Oda et al., 2013). (3) Its effect follows a phase-response curve — evening doses land on the 'advance' side of your clock and pull it earlier, morning doses land on the 'delay' side and push it later.

melatonin.bigIdea: Melatonin isn't a sleeping pill — it's your body's 'it's night now' signal, a hormone that re-times your internal clock rather than sedating you. Hold onto that one sentence and everything else on this page follows from it.
melatonin.analogy: Melatonin is the hands of a clock, not the brakes on a car. It doesn't force you to stop and sleep — it tells your brain what time your body thinks it is. Take it in the evening and you nudge the clock's hands earlier; take it at the wrong time and you nudge them the wrong way.

mechSteps.t: Your pineal gland makes it when the lights go out
mechSteps.predict: One gland releases melatonin only in darkness, and bright light shuts it off within minutes. Which gland — and why would a 'darkness hormone' be useful?
mechSteps.d: As night falls, your pineal gland converts serotonin into melatonin and pours it into the blood; light hitting your eyes slams this off almost instantly. Rising melatonin is your body's internal announcement that night has begun.

mechSteps.t: It docks onto clock receptors in the master timekeeper
mechSteps.predict: Melatonin's target sits in a tiny brain region that runs your whole 24-hour rhythm. What does binding there do — set the time, or force sleep?
mechSteps.d: Melatonin binds MT1 and MT2 receptors (MTNR1A/MTNR1B) concentrated in the suprachiasmatic nucleus, your brain's master clock. It doesn't sedate the clock — it adjusts what time the clock reads.

mechSteps.t: The timing of the dose sets the direction of the shift
mechSteps.predict: The same dose can move your clock earlier OR later depending on when you take it. What determines which way?
mechSteps.d: Melatonin obeys a phase-response curve: a dose in the biological evening advances your clock (makes you sleepy earlier), while a dose in the morning delays it. It's the timing, not the size, of the dose that steers the direction.

mechSteps.t: The receptors saturate at a whisper of a dose
mechSteps.predict: Melatonin binds its receptors at picomolar concentrations. If 0.3 mg already floods them, what does swallowing 10 mg actually buy you?
mechSteps.d: MT1/MT2 are exquisitely high-affinity — a physiological 0.3-0.5 mg dose already restores normal night-time blood levels and fully engages them. A 10-20x dose adds no extra clock signal; it just keeps melatonin elevated into daylight, causing morning grogginess.

framework.target: MT1 and MT2 receptors (MTNR1A/MTNR1B) — Gi-coupled GPCRs in the suprachiasmatic nucleus master clock. Note the contrast with a sedative: melatonin activates a clock receptor (an agonist), it doesn't blanket the brain with inhibition the way a benzodiazepine does.
framework.affinity: Genuinely high-affinity — picomolar to low-nanomolar (Kd roughly 20-160 pM at the cloned human receptors), with MT1 slightly tighter than MT2. This is the crux of the dosing story: because the target saturates at such tiny concentrations, 0.3-0.5 mg already maxes out the signal and everything above that is wasted (and lingers).
framework.adme: Well absorbed orally but heavily first-pass metabolised, so bioavailability is low and variable (~3-15%). Immediate-release melatonin peaks ~45-60 min after a dose and has a very short half-life (~40-50 min); a prolonged-release prescription form stretches this to ~3.5-4 h to better mimic a natural night-time curve.
framework.genetics: Cleared chiefly by liver CYP1A2 to 6-hydroxymelatonin — the same enzyme that clears caffeine — so CYP1A2 inhibitors (notably the antidepressant fluvoxamine) can raise melatonin levels many-fold. MTNR1B variants are a well-replicated signal in type-2-diabetes and fasting-glucose genetics, a reminder these receptors do more than time sleep.
framework.evidence: Strong and consistent for the circadian uses (jet lag, delayed sleep-wake phase) and for modestly speeding sleep onset; the honest catch is that the sleep-latency effect size is small (~7 minutes).

pk.onset: ~30 min (immediate-release)
pk.peak: ~45-60 min (tmax)
pk.duration: a few hours for the acute soporific effect; the clock-shifting effect outlasts the blood level
pk.note: The short half-life is a feature, not a bug: a physiological dose mimics a brief natural melatonin pulse and clears by morning. High doses defeat this — they keep melatonin elevated into daylight, which is the main reason people wake groggy. The clock-shift the dose triggers persists long after the molecule itself is gone.

journey.d: As darkness falls (or you swallow a tablet), melatonin enters the blood — your body's 'night has begun' announcement.
It circulates freely and, being small and lipophilic, crosses easily into the brain. Immediate-release levels peak within about an hour.
It reaches the suprachiasmatic nucleus and activates MT1/MT2 receptors there — reading out the time and, depending on when you took it, nudging the clock earlier or later.
CYP1A2 (the caffeine enzyme) quickly converts most of it to 6-hydroxymelatonin. This is why a physiological dose clears within a few hours.
The metabolite is conjugated and leaves in the urine as 6-sulfatoxymelatonin — the marker researchers measure to track your natural melatonin rhythm.

myths.myth: More melatonin means deeper sleep.
myths.truth: The receptors saturate at a tiny dose. Above roughly 0.5-1 mg you get no extra clock signal — just melatonin lingering into the morning, which causes grogginess and can even blunt the effect. Zhdanova's data found 0.3 mg optimal; 3 mg caused hypothermia and daytime carry-over.

myths.myth: Melatonin is a sedative that knocks you out.
myths.truth: It re-times when you feel sleepy rather than forcing sleep. That's why it's powerful for jet lag and delayed sleep phase but only shaves about 7 minutes off sleep onset in ordinary insomnia — a real but modest effect, nothing like a sleeping pill.

myths.myth: It's harmless because it's 'natural', so take it whenever.
myths.truth: Timing is the active ingredient. Taken at the wrong point on your phase-response curve it shifts your clock the wrong way and can worsen the problem. And 'natural' doesn't mean well-regulated — US supplement melatonin content has been measured well off its label.

myths.myth: Melatonin fixes insomnia caused by stress or poor sleep habits.
myths.truth: It targets clock timing, not the racing-mind or conditioned-arousal drivers behind most chronic insomnia. For those, cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) is the first-line, higher-yield tool.

whenToUse.intro: How it's used — melatonin is sold OTC in the US but is a prescription medicine (e.g. prolonged-release Circadin) in the UK/EU, and chronic sleep problems warrant a doctor rather than escalating doses. This is a timing tool, not a sledgehammer:
items: **Jet lag (its best-proven use):** 0.5-3 mg taken close to target bedtime at the destination, most useful crossing 5+ time zones and especially flying eastward (Cochrane review).
**Delayed sleep-wake phase (night owls):** a small dose a few hours before the desired — not current — bedtime, to advance the clock. Timing matters more than amount here.
**Start low:** 0.3-1 mg is the physiological range that already saturates the receptors. The common 5-10 mg gummies are 10-20x more than needed and mainly add morning fog.
**Prescription prolonged-release** exists for insomnia in adults over 55, designed to mimic a natural overnight curve rather than a short IR spike — a doctor's call.
**When it's the wrong tool:** for stress-driven or long-standing insomnia, CBT-I outperforms it; melatonin fixes clock timing, not the causes of most chronic insomnia.

start.q: In one line: is melatonin a sedative, or a clock-shifter?
start.a: A clock-shifter. It signals 'it's night' and re-times when you get sleepy, rather than sedating you into sleep.

how.q: What receptors does melatonin activate, and where, to move your body clock?
how.a: MT1 and MT2 (MTNR1A/MTNR1B) in the suprachiasmatic nucleus — the brain's master clock — adjusting what time it reads.

use.q: Why does 0.5 mg work as well as 10 mg?
use.a: The receptors are picomolar-affinity and saturate at a tiny dose, so 0.3-0.5 mg already maxes the signal. Extra just lingers into the morning and causes grogginess.

evidence.q: What is melatonin strongly proven for, and what is its effect on ordinary insomnia?
evidence.a: Strong for jet lag and delayed sleep phase; for primary insomnia it only modestly speeds sleep onset (~7 minutes on average).

canExplain: Why melatonin re-times your body clock instead of sedating you
The exact receptors it hits (MT1/MT2 in the SCN) and why their picomolar affinity makes a tiny dose enough
Why 0.5 mg works as well as 10 mg — and why the big dose leaves you groggy
Why the same pill can shift your clock earlier or later depending on when you take it
How strong the evidence really is — solid for jet lag, modest for everyday insomnia

selfTest.q: Two friends take melatonin for insomnia; one swears by 10 mg, another uses 0.3 mg. Why might the smaller dose actually be the smarter choice?
selfTest.a: MT1/MT2 saturate at picomolar levels, so 0.3 mg already fully engages them and restores a normal night-time curve. The 10 mg dose adds no extra clock signal but keeps melatonin elevated into the morning, causing grogginess — and Zhdanova's data even found the physiological dose worked best.

selfTest.q: A traveller flying from New York to Tokyo takes melatonin at what feels like a sensible bedtime back home. Why might this backfire?
selfTest.a: Melatonin follows a phase-response curve — its effect depends on where the dose lands on your clock. Dosed at the wrong biological time it can shift the clock in the wrong direction. For eastward travel you want it timed to the destination's bedtime to advance your clock, not your origin's.

selfTest.q: Melatonin is cleared by CYP1A2. What does that predict about combining it with the antidepressant fluvoxamine?
selfTest.a: Fluvoxamine is a potent CYP1A2 inhibitor, so it can raise melatonin blood levels many-fold and prolong its action — a genuine interaction to be aware of, and a reason chronic use deserves medical oversight.

evi.howStrong: The evidence splits cleanly by use. For the circadian applications — jet lag and delayed sleep-wake phase disorder — it's strong and consistent, anchored by a Cochrane systematic review. For primary insomnia the evidence is also large (multiple RCTs pooled into meta-analysis) but shows only a modest effect. The honest limits: melatonin supplements are poorly regulated (actual content often strays from the label), and most trials are short, so very-long-term safety data are thin.
evi.whatItShows: It reliably reduces jet lag when crossing several time zones, and reliably speeds sleep onset in primary sleep disorders — but the sleep-onset effect is small in absolute terms. It's best understood as re-timing sleep, not deepening or lengthening it dramatically.
evi.effect: In plain numbers: a 2013 meta-analysis of 19 trials (1,683 people) found melatonin cut time-to-fall-asleep by about 7 minutes and modestly increased total sleep time and sleep quality. For jet lag the Cochrane review judged it 'remarkably effective' for 5+ time-zone crossings. Dose-finding work (Zhdanova, 2001) pinpointed ~0.3 mg as optimal for age-related insomnia, with 3 mg causing hypothermia and daytime carry-over.
evi.whoBenefits: Biggest wins: jet-lagged travellers (especially eastward, 5+ zones), night owls with delayed sleep phase, and older adults whose own melatonin production has declined. It helps least for stress-driven or conditioned insomnia, where CBT-I is the stronger tool.
evi.caveats: The sleep-latency effect is small and easy to oversell. Supplement quality is inconsistent (content has been measured well off-label), long-term data are limited, and CYP1A2 interactions (e.g. fluvoxamine) can amplify it. Timing is genuinely part of the dose — wrong timing can shift the clock the wrong way.

trials.finding: Meta-analysis of 19 RCTs (1,683 subjects): melatonin reduced sleep-onset latency by ~7 min and improved total sleep time and sleep quality; effects were modest and did not dissipate with continued use.
trials.ref: Ferracioli-Oda et al., 2013, PLoS One

trials.finding: Cochrane systematic review of 10 RCTs: melatonin near destination bedtime was effective at preventing/reducing jet lag when crossing 5+ time zones, particularly eastward.
trials.ref: Herxheimer & Petrie, 2002, Cochrane Database Syst Rev

trials.finding: Dose comparison in age-related insomnia: a physiological 0.3 mg dose restored sleep efficiency and normalised night-time plasma melatonin, whereas 3 mg induced hypothermia and left melatonin elevated into daytime.
trials.ref: Zhdanova et al., 2001, J Clin Endocrinol Metab

tech.chem: N-acetyl-5-methoxytryptamine — an indoleamine synthesised from the amino acid tryptophan via serotonin. The pineal enzymes AANAT (the rate-limiting, light-suppressed step) and ASMT (HIOMT) convert serotonin to melatonin at night. It's small and lipophilic, crossing the blood-brain barrier and placenta freely.

targets.action: agonist — activates a Gi/o-coupled GPCR (lowers cAMP)
targets.affinity: picomolar to low-nanomolar (Kd ~20-160 pM class); MT1 slightly higher-affinity than MT2
targets.role: principal SCN clock receptor; MT1 activation is linked to the acute sleep-promoting/soporific effect

targets.action: agonist — Gi/o-coupled GPCR
targets.affinity: picomolar to low-nanomolar; modestly lower affinity than MT1
targets.role: mediates much of the phase-shifting (clock-resetting) effect; coding/regulatory variants are a replicated type-2-diabetes and fasting-glucose signal

tech.signaling: Both receptors are Gi/o-coupled, so activation lowers cAMP and modulates ion channels in SCN neurons. Acting on the master clock, melatonin feeds back onto the core transcriptional-translational loop (BMAL1/CLOCK driving PER/CRY) to reset its phase — the molecular basis of the phase-response curve. This is signalling to a clock, not global CNS inhibition, which is why it re-times sleep rather than sedating like a GABAergic hypnotic.

adme.absorb: Rapidly absorbed orally but subject to extensive hepatic first-pass, so oral bioavailability is low and highly variable (~3-15%); IR tmax ~45-60 min.
adme.distribute: Small, lipophilic; crosses the blood-brain barrier and placenta and enters breast milk. Partially bound to albumin.
adme.metabolise: Predominantly hepatic CYP1A2 (with minor CYP2C19) → 6-hydroxymelatonin, then sulfate/glucuronide conjugation. Shared CYP1A2 dependence underlies interactions with fluvoxamine (inhibitor, raises levels) and smoking/cruciferous induction (lowers them).
adme.excrete: Excreted in urine chiefly as 6-sulfatoxymelatonin (aMT6s) — the standard biomarker of endogenous melatonin rhythm. IR half-life ~40-50 min.

tech.pgx: No single-gene switch governs the sleep response, but MTNR1B variants (e.g. rs10830963) are robustly associated with impaired insulin secretion and higher type-2-diabetes risk — evidence these receptors have metabolic roles beyond timing sleep. CYP1A2 activity (inducible by smoking, inhibited by fluvoxamine and oestrogens) shifts melatonin clearance, the main clinically relevant pharmacogenetic/interaction lever.
tech.dose: Physiological range 0.3-1 mg; ~0.3 mg was optimal in dose-finding for age-related insomnia. Jet lag: 0.5-3 mg near destination bedtime. Prolonged-release 2 mg is a prescription insomnia formulation for adults over 55. Common OTC 5-10 mg doses exceed the receptor-saturating need by 10-20x and mainly add morning grogginess.
tech.tolerance: No classic receptor-desensitisation tolerance or dependence syndrome at physiological doses, and meta-analytic effects did not wane with continued use. There is no withdrawal syndrome; the main pitfall is chronic overdosing and mistimed dosing rather than escalating tolerance.

questions: Chamomile tea has been the go-to "sleepy" drink for centuries — but what molecule in it actually does anything, and how?
Apigenin binds the exact same spot on your brain as Valium — so why is one a grocery-store tea and the other a controlled drug?
If the lab data looks so clean, why does apigenin only earn 2 out of 5 stars for human evidence?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) The active molecule is apigenin, a flavone that slips into the benzodiazepine site on your GABA-A receptor and gently amplifies the brain's main "slow down" signal. (2) It binds the very same site as Valium — but so weakly (Ki ~4 µM, versus low-nanomolar for real benzodiazepines) that it barely turns the dial; that mildness is exactly why it's safe enough to sell as tea. (3) Almost all of that clean mechanism comes from test tubes and mice — the human sleep evidence is thin, mostly tests whole chamomile (not pure apigenin), and is genuinely mixed, which is why it honestly rates 2/5.

apigenin.bigIdea: "Apigenin sits in the same lock on your brain as Valium — but turns it so gently that you get a faint calming nudge instead of a drug. Hold onto that: everything about why it's mild, safe, and only lightly proven follows from 'same site, tiny effect.'"
apigenin.analogy: "Your GABA-A receptor is the brain's brake pedal, and the benzodiazepine site is a sensitivity dial bolted onto it. A real benzodiazepine cranks that dial hard. Apigenin's hand rests on the very same dial — but barely turns it. The brake still works a little better; you just get a whisper of calm, not a shove into sleep."

mechSteps.t: GABA is your brain's master brake
mechSteps.predict: Before any tea molecule shows up, one neurotransmitter is already doing most of the brain's "quiet down" work. Which one — and what does it open to do it?
mechSteps.d: GABA is the brain's main inhibitory signal. When it docks on a GABA-A receptor, it opens a chloride channel that hushes the neuron — the physical basis of feeling calm and drowsy.

mechSteps.t: Apigenin slots into the benzodiazepine site
mechSteps.predict: Apigenin doesn't compete with GABA itself. It grabs a separate "tuning" pocket on the same receptor — the same one a class of famous sleep drugs uses. What's that pocket called?
mechSteps.d: Apigenin binds the benzodiazepine allosteric site on the GABA-A receptor — the same seat as Valium — with a mild affinity (Ki ≈ 4 µM in rat cortex, shown by displacing flunitrazepam). It doesn't open the channel itself; it sits alongside and tunes it.

mechSteps.t: It gently amplifies GABA's braking
mechSteps.predict: With apigenin resting in the tuning pocket, what happens to the effect of the GABA that's already there — and what would you feel?
mechSteps.d: By occupying that site, apigenin nudges the receptor so each hit of GABA produces a slightly bigger inhibitory response — a mild positive tilt on the brake. The felt result is a faint easing toward sleep onset, not sedation.

mechSteps.t: Side channels: adenosine and estrogen machinery
mechSteps.predict: Apigenin isn't a one-target molecule. Name one system unrelated to GABA that a flavone like this also touches.
mechSteps.d: Apigenin also modulates adenosine signalling (the other big sleep-pressure system) and, in the test tube, inhibits aromatase (CYP19A1) — the enzyme that makes estrogen from testosterone — at low-micromolar concentrations. These are real biochemistry, but their relevance at tea-level doses in a human body is unproven.

framework.target: The benzodiazepine allosteric site on the GABA-A receptor (GABRA1-containing subtypes) — apigenin is a mild positive modulator there, the same seat benzodiazepines use. Secondary, less-characterised targets: adenosine receptor modulation and inhibition of the aromatase enzyme (CYP19A1).
framework.affinity: Ki ≈ 4 µM at the benzodiazepine site (measured by displacing [³H]flunitrazepam in rat brain membranes). Put that next to a real benzodiazepine, which binds in the low-nanomolar range — roughly a thousand-fold tighter.
