start.q: In one line: does valerian sedate you directly, or work through something your brain already makes?
start.a: Through your own GABA. Valerenic acid makes the GABA-A receptor more responsive to the GABA you're already releasing — it doesn't open the channel or sedate you by itself.

how.q: Valerian and Valium both act on GABA-A. What's the key difference, and how do we know?
how.a: They bind different seats. Valerenic acid uses the loreclezole pocket on β2/β3 subunits, not the benzodiazepine site — and flumazenil, which reverses benzos, doesn't reverse valerenic acid. That's why it's weaker and non-habit-forming.

use.q: Why is buying a standardised extract and giving it two weeks the smart way to try valerian?
use.a: Because product potency varies enormously (a big reason trials conflict), and some benefit seems to build over repeated nightly use rather than the first dose.

evidence.q: How good is the evidence — and where does it fall apart?
evidence.a: Weak and mixed. Subjective sleep scores improve in several meta-analyses, but objective measures usually don't, bias is high across studies, and a 2024 umbrella review found no clear evidence of efficacy. Safety, though, is consistently good.

canExplain: Why valerian nudges your own GABA rather than sedating you directly
How it hits the same receptor as benzodiazepines yet is far weaker and non-addictive — and the flumazenil test that proves the difference
Why a normal dose may barely reach the concentration its target needs, and what that predicts
Why 'improves sleep' and 'does nothing' can both be true in the literature at once
How strong the evidence actually is, and why product quality matters more than your genes

selfTest.q: A friend says valerian is 'just herbal Valium.' Give the two-part mechanistic reason that's wrong.
selfTest.a: First, valerenic acid binds a different site (the loreclezole pocket on β2/β3 subunits), not the benzodiazepine site — flumazenil doesn't reverse it. Second, it only potentiates GABA rather than strongly driving the channel, so it's far weaker and, unlike benzodiazepines, non-habit-forming with no established withdrawal.

selfTest.q: Valerenic acid needs ~30 µM to work in a dish, but a 600 mg dose peaks near 10 nM in blood. Why does that gap matter for what you should expect?
selfTest.a: It's roughly a thousandfold shortfall, so how much valerenic acid ever engages the receptor in a living person is uncertain. That mismatch is a core mechanistic reason to expect only a mild, inconsistent effect — not a reliable sleep aid.

selfTest.q: Two well-run reviews seem to disagree on whether valerian works. Name two honest reasons that isn't a contradiction.
selfTest.a: (1) Subjective outcomes (how people rate their sleep) improve while objective ones (actigraphy, total sleep time) often don't — so 'works' depends on what you measure. (2) Extracts vary hugely in potency, so different trials effectively tested different doses of active compound.

evi.howStrong: The evidence is weak and genuinely mixed — a step or two up from anecdote, but nowhere near the settled ground of caffeine or creatine. Dozens of trials exist and several have been pooled into meta-analyses, but they are plagued by high risk of bias, small samples, short durations, and — most damagingly — wildly variable extracts, so different studies effectively tested different doses of the active compound. A 2024 umbrella review (a review of reviews) is the honest bottom line: after weighing eight systematic reviews, it found no clear evidence of efficacy for insomnia.
evi.whatItShows: A recurring split: people tend to rate their sleep as better (subjective sleep quality and sleep latency improve in several meta-analyses), while objective measures — actigraphy, total sleep time — usually don't move. That pattern is consistent with a real but small effect, a placebo contribution, or both. It is not consistent with a strong hypnotic.
evi.effect: Hard to pin to a clean number because outcomes and extracts differ so much. One earlier meta-analysis reported that valerian users were more likely to report improved sleep (relative risk ~1.8), but the same analysis flagged publication bias, and many individual trials were simply null. Treat any single effect size as soft.
evi.whoBenefits: If anyone, likely people with mild, self-reported sleep difficulty who use a decent standardised extract nightly for a couple of weeks. It is not a treatment for clinical insomnia, and it shouldn't displace evidence-based options like CBT-I or good sleep hygiene.
evi.caveats: The literature is biased toward positive subjective outcomes, extract quality is rarely controlled, and objective benefit is thin. The one thing that is well established is safety: no severe adverse events across trials spanning ages 7–80, with only mild, transient side effects.

trials.finding: Umbrella review of 8 systematic reviews: subjective sleep-quality and sleep-latency improvements in some meta-analyses, but objective outcomes generally unchanged and high risk of bias throughout — concluding no clear evidence of efficacy, alongside a good safety profile.
trials.ref: Valente et al., 2024, European Neuropsychopharmacology

trials.finding: Systematic review & meta-analysis: valerian users were more likely to report improved sleep quality (RR ≈ 1.8, 95% CI 1.2–2.9), but with evidence of publication bias and many null trials.
trials.ref: Bent et al., 2006, American Journal of Medicine

trials.finding: Systematic review & meta-analysis (60 studies, n≈6,894): valerian improved subjective sleep quality and reduced anxiety, with inconsistency attributed to variable herbal-extract quality; no severe adverse events (ages 7–80).
trials.ref: Shinjyo et al., 2020, Journal of Evidence-Based Integrative Medicine

tech.chem: Valerenic acid is a sesquiterpenoid carboxylic acid largely specific to Valeriana officinalis, and the usual marker used to standardise extracts. But valerian root is a whole-plant preparation: it also contains acetoxyvalerenic acid, the labile valepotriates, lignans, and small amounts of GABA itself. Attributing the whole effect to valerenic acid is a useful simplification, not a complete account.

targets.action: positive allosteric modulator — potentiates GABA-gated Cl⁻ currents; β2/β3-selective and inactive on β1-containing channels
targets.affinity: no clean Ki; potentiation seen in vitro at ~≥30 µM (and paradoxical inhibition/open-channel block at ≥100 µM) — far above the ~10 nM plasma peak reached at ordinary doses
targets.role: the curated, trusted mechanism, though whether therapeutic levels are reached in vivo is uncertain

tech.signaling: Valerenic acid binds the loreclezole pocket on β2/β3 subunits and shifts the GABA concentration–response curve leftward, enhancing chloride influx and neuronal hyperpolarisation. Two distinguishing facts: the effect does not require the γ subunit, and it is not reversed by flumazenil — confirming it acts away from the benzodiazepine site. The β2N265S point mutation (which also abolishes loreclezole's action) sharply reduces valerenic-acid sensitivity, pinpointing the site. Other valerian constituents have been proposed to touch additional targets, but GABA-A β2/β3 modulation is the best-characterised route.

adme.absorb: Valerenic acid is orally absorbed; after 600 mg valerian, peak serum concentration of ~0.9–2.3 ng/mL occurs around 1–2 h.
adme.distribute: Lipophilic enough to cross into the brain; plasma levels are low (nanomolar range), well below in-vitro active concentrations.
adme.metabolise: Cleared relatively rapidly — elimination half-life of the valerenic-acid marker is ~1.1 h in healthy adults.
adme.excrete: Valerenic acid remains measurable in serum for ~5 h after a dose; no meaningful accumulation was seen between single and multiple dosing.

tech.pgx: No established single-gene pharmacogenetic determinant analogous to caffeine's CYP1A2. The dominant source of variable response is pharmaceutical, not genetic: extracts differ markedly in valerenic-acid content and in whole-root versus concentrated-extract composition.
tech.dose: Commonly 300–600 mg of root extract 30–60 min before bed; some regimens use nightly dosing for 2–4 weeks, consistent with reports that benefit may build with repeated use. Standardisation to valerenic-acid content is the key quality variable.
tech.tolerance: Non-habit-forming. Unlike benzodiazepines, valerian has no established tolerance, dependence, or withdrawal syndrome, and its short half-life means it does not accumulate — so there is nothing to cycle off. Safety across trials is good, with only mild, transient adverse effects reported.

questions: Why does a molecule your body already makes in every single cell need to be swallowed as a pill at all — and why enteric-coated, on an empty stomach?
Why does the same supplement help two things as unrelated as depression and an aching, stiff knee?
Why must you never just add it to your antidepressant on your own, when it sits on the shelf next to the vitamins?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Your cells make plenty, but the molecule is fragile and destroyed in the stomach — so oral SAM-e is only ~2-3% absorbed, which is exactly why it's enteric-coated (to survive stomach acid) and taken on an empty stomach. (2) It isn't a drug for depression or for arthritis — it's the universal methyl donor, the raw currency dozens of unrelated enzymes need, so topping it up nudges both mood-chemical synthesis AND cartilage-building at once. (3) Because SAM-e feeds serotonin production and your SSRI blocks serotonin's removal, stacking them can push serotonin dangerously high (serotonin syndrome) — and in undiagnosed bipolar it can tip a person into mania. Over-the-counter does not mean consequence-free.

sam-e-s-adenosylmethionine.bigIdea: SAM-e doesn't act on a receptor — it's your body's universal methyl-group delivery currency, the single chemical tag that dozens of unrelated enzymes must borrow to build mood chemicals, DNA marks and cartilage, which is why one molecule reaches things as different as depression and arthritis.
sam-e-s-adenosylmethionine.analogy: SAM-e is a book of postage stamps for your cells. Each methyl group is one stamp. Enzymes can't 'send' their reactions — finish a neurotransmitter, mark a gene, build a membrane, patch cartilage — until SAM-e licks a stamp onto them. Run low on stamps and a whole pile of unrelated deliveries stalls at once; that shared currency is why a single molecule touches mood and joints together.

mechSteps.t: Your body forges it from an amino acid + ATP
mechSteps.predict: SAM-e isn't eaten in food in any real amount — your cells build it fresh. From which amino acid, and what high-energy molecule pays for the build?
mechSteps.d: An enzyme called methionine adenotransferase (MAT) fuses the amino acid methionine with ATP to make SAM-e. That fusion loads a methyl group onto a sulfur atom in a strained, 'primed' state — a chemical spring ready to fire.

mechSteps.t: It hands its methyl group to methyltransferase enzymes
mechSteps.predict: SAM-e carries one transferable methyl (-CH3) tag. It doesn't switch a receptor on or off — so what does it actually DO with that tag?
mechSteps.d: SAM-e is a substrate, not a signal. Dozens of methyltransferase enzymes grab it and transfer its single methyl group onto their targets — this is 'transmethylation,' and SAM-e is the donor for essentially all of it.

mechSteps.t: Those transfers build mood chemicals, DNA marks and membranes
mechSteps.predict: Name one place a stuck-on methyl group changes how your brain works.
mechSteps.d: Methyl transfers finish and regulate neurotransmitters (supporting dopamine and serotonin pathways), stamp methyl marks onto DNA that switch genes on and off, and build phospholipids for cell membranes. In joints the same currency feeds cartilage proteoglycan synthesis.

mechSteps.t: Spent, it becomes SAH — and must be recycled
mechSteps.predict: Once SAM-e gives away its methyl, what's left over, and why can't your body just throw it out?
mechSteps.d: Stripped of its methyl, SAM-e becomes SAH (S-adenosylhomocysteine), which is then broken to homocysteine. Homocysteine is remethylated back to methionine — a step that needs folate and vitamin B12 — closing the methylation cycle so the whole loop can run again.

mechSteps.t: The effect builds over weeks, not minutes
mechSteps.predict: SAM-e's blood half-life is only about 1-2 hours. So why does its mood benefit take weeks to appear?
mechSteps.d: Like creatine, the felt effect isn't chased by a daily blood peak — it comes from steadily shifting the cell's methylation capacity and downstream synthesis. That's a slow adaptation, which is why depression trials run for weeks and joint relief lags behind a painkiller's.

framework.target: There is no receptor. This is the key thing to grasp: SAM-e is a substrate — the methyl-donating cosubstrate for methyltransferase enzymes (COMT, DNA methyltransferases, PEMT and dozens more). Unlike caffeine (which blocks a receptor) or semaglutide (which activates one), SAM-e has nothing to switch on or off.
framework.affinity: There's no receptor binding constant to quote, because there's no receptor. What matters instead is the methylation potential — the SAM-e-to-SAH ratio inside the cell. Raise SAM-e and more enzymes can complete their transfers; but the loop is capped by how fast folate and B12 recycle the leftover homocysteine, so 'more' has a ceiling.
framework.adme: Poorly absorbed orally (only ~2-3% bioavailability), which is why it's enteric-coated to survive stomach acid; plasma peaks ~3-5 h after an enteric tablet. It crosses into the brain and CSF. It isn't cleared by a liver CYP enzyme — it's consumed inside the methylation cycle, becoming SAH then homocysteine. Plasma half-life is short (~1.5-2 h).
framework.genetics: No single clean switch like caffeine's CYP1A2. Response is shaped by the methylation cycle's cofactors: folate and B12 status, and MTHFR variants (which slow homocysteine recycling) all move the needle. COMT genotype (Val158Met) sets how fast SAM-e-driven methylation clears dopamine.
framework.evidence: Genuinely good for a supplement (curated 4/5): multiple RCTs and meta-analyses plus a Cochrane review for depression (mainly as an adjunct), and head-to-head RCTs versus an NSAID for osteoarthritis. Stronger than almost any other mood supplement, though many trials are older, small, or heterogeneous.

journey.d: You swallow an enteric-coated dose on an empty stomach. The coating exists for a reason: uncoated SAM-e is shredded by stomach acid, so only about 2-3% of an oral dose ever makes it into you.

What survives dissolves into the bloodstream, peaking around 3-5 hours later. It's a fragile passenger — blood levels rise and fall within hours, but that flicker isn't what does the therapeutic work.
It crosses into the brain and CSF and reaches joints. There it becomes the methyl currency: feeding neurotransmitter and DNA methylation in the brain, and proteoglycan synthesis in cartilage.
A methyltransferase enzyme grabs SAM-e and transfers its methyl group onto a target. Job done, SAM-e is now SAH — a spent stamp that must be cleared before it jams the enzymes.
SAH is broken to homocysteine, then remethylated back to methionine using folate and B12 — closing the loop so a fresh SAM-e can be forged. Without enough folate/B12, homocysteine backs up.

myths.myth: SAM-e is basically just an expensive B-vitamin.
myths.truth: Folate and B12 are helpers that recycle the methylation cycle upstream; SAM-e is the actual methyl currency being spent downstream. They're linked but not interchangeable — you can be replete in B-vitamins and still be limited by SAM-e supply, and SAM-e's leftover homocysteine still needs those B-vitamins to clear.

myths.myth: It works fast, like a mood boost you feel the same day.
myths.truth: Its blood half-life is only ~1.5-2 hours, but the benefit builds over weeks as methylation capacity and downstream synthesis shift. Depression trials run for weeks precisely because there's no same-day hit to chase.

myths.myth: It's over the counter, so it's safe to add to my antidepressant.
myths.truth: This is the dangerous one. SAM-e supports serotonin synthesis while SSRIs/SNRIs block serotonin's removal — together they can push serotonin toward serotonin syndrome. And in undiagnosed bipolar disorder it can trigger mania or hypomania. OTC availability is not medical clearance.

whenToUse.intro: How it's actually used — a note on status first: SAM-e is sold OTC in the US but is a prescription medicine (ademetionine) in parts of Europe. Because of real interaction and mania risks, treat the studied doses below as clinical information, not a green light to self-experiment — especially if you take any antidepressant or have any mood-disorder history. Talk to a clinician.
items: **Depression, as an add-on:** studied at 800-1600 mg/day (e.g. 800 mg twice daily) added to an antidepressant in people who didn't respond to the antidepressant alone — done under supervision because of serotonin-syndrome risk. The evidence is weaker for SAM-e used entirely on its own.
**Osteoarthritis:** ~1200 mg/day was compared head-to-head with the NSAID celecoxib for knee OA; SAM-e was slower to start working but reached comparable symptom relief, with a gentler GI profile.
**Formulation matters:** enteric-coated, on an empty stomach, and start low then titrate up — this is how the trials dosed it and how you limit early nausea and insomnia.

**Who must be careful:** anyone with bipolar disorder (mania/hypomania risk), anyone on an SSRI/SNRI or other serotonergic drug (serotonin-syndrome risk), and around a planned pregnancy — discuss with a clinician first.
**Support the cycle:** because SAM-e generates homocysteine as it's spent, adequate folate and B12 status matters to keep the methylation loop clearing properly.

start.q: In one line: does SAM-e work by switching a receptor on or off?
start.a: Neither — it has no receptor. It's a substrate, the methyl-group currency that methyltransferase enzymes borrow to do their jobs.

how.q: What does SAM-e physically hand over, and name two very different things that transfer builds.
how.a: A single methyl group (-CH3). It's used to build/regulate mood chemicals like dopamine and serotonin, to mark DNA, to build cell membranes, and to make cartilage proteoglycans — which is why one molecule reaches both brain and joints.

use.q: Why does its short ~1.5-2 hour blood half-life not mean you feel it fast?
use.a: Because the benefit comes from steadily raising methylation capacity and downstream synthesis over weeks, not from a daily blood peak. It's a slow adaptation, more like creatine than caffeine.

evidence.q: What's it best evidenced for, and what's the single biggest safety catch?
evidence.a: Osteoarthritis symptom relief and depression (best as an add-on to an antidepressant). The biggest catch: combined with SSRIs/SNRIs it risks serotonin syndrome, and it can trigger mania in bipolar disorder.

canExplain: Why SAM-e, unlike almost every other compound on this site, has no receptor — it's a methyl-donating substrate
How one molecule can plausibly help both depression and osteoarthritis through the same shared methyl currency
Why oral SAM-e is enteric-coated and only ~2-3% absorbed, and why timing on an empty stomach matters
Why the mood effect builds over weeks despite a short blood half-life
Why it's genuinely risky to combine with an SSRI, and why bipolar disorder is a hard stop

selfTest.q: SAM-e touches no receptor, yet it plausibly helps two unrelated problems — low mood and stiff knees. What single fact explains how one molecule can do both?
selfTest.a: It's the universal methyl donor. Dozens of unrelated enzymes borrow its methyl group — some to build mood chemicals and DNA marks in the brain, others to build cartilage proteoglycans in joints — so raising the shared currency nudges both at once.

selfTest.q: A friend says SAM-e 'did nothing' after taking it with orange juice and breakfast for three days. Give two reasons the trial was doomed.
selfTest.a: First, food and a non-empty stomach cut absorption of an already poorly-absorbed molecule (only ~2-3% oral bioavailability), and it should be enteric-coated on an empty stomach. Second, the mood benefit builds over weeks — three days is far too short to judge it.

selfTest.q: Why can adding SAM-e to an SSRI be dangerous, in mechanistic terms?
selfTest.a: SAM-e feeds serotonin synthesis while the SSRI blocks serotonin's reuptake/removal — two pushes in the same direction can drive serotonin too high, risking serotonin syndrome. That's why the combination needs a clinician, not self-experimentation.

evi.howStrong: For a supplement, SAM-e's evidence is unusually respectable: multiple randomised controlled trials pooled into several meta-analyses and a Cochrane review for depression, plus head-to-head RCTs against an NSAID for osteoarthritis. That's why it's one of the very few supplements said to 'compete with drugs.' The honest limits: many trials are older, relatively small, and clinically heterogeneous; the case is strongest for SAM-e as an add-on to an antidepressant and weaker for it used entirely on its own, where recent guidance is cautious.
evi.whatItShows: Two fairly consistent signals. In depression, SAM-e reduces depressive symptoms versus placebo and appears roughly comparable to older antidepressants, with the clearest role as an adjunct in people who didn't respond to an antidepressant alone. In osteoarthritis, it relieves pain and improves function about as well as an NSAID, but takes longer to get going.
evi.effect: In plain terms: a meaningful reduction in depression-rating-scale scores over several weeks, and osteoarthritis symptom relief that starts slower than an NSAID but catches up by the second month or so. It amplifies or substitutes for existing treatment rather than being a dramatic standalone cure.
evi.whoBenefits: For mood: adults with mild-to-moderate depression, especially as an add-on when an antidepressant hasn't fully worked. For joints: people with knee osteoarthritis who want NSAID-level relief with a gentler gastrointestinal profile and can accept a slower onset. It is not appropriate for people with bipolar disorder, and needs clinician oversight for anyone on serotonergic drugs.
evi.caveats: Trials skew older and smaller, formulations and doses vary, and monotherapy evidence is shakier than adjunct evidence — so treat effect sizes as encouraging rather than guaranteed. The real-world catch is safety, not efficacy: serotonin-syndrome risk with SSRIs/SNRIs and mania risk in bipolar disorder are the reasons 'OTC' should not be read as 'harmless.'

trials.finding: Adjunctive SAM-e 800 mg twice daily added to a serotonin reuptake inhibitor in antidepressant non-responders (n=73) produced significantly higher response and remission rates than added placebo over 6 weeks.
trials.ref: Papakostas et al., 2010, Am J Psychiatry

trials.finding: Cochrane review of SAM-e for depression in adults: some evidence of benefit versus placebo and comparability to older antidepressants, but limited by few high-quality trials.
trials.ref: Galizia et al., 2016, Cochrane Database Syst Rev

trials.finding: Randomised double-blind crossover trial of SAM-e 1200 mg/day versus celecoxib 200 mg/day for knee osteoarthritis (n=61): SAM-e was slower in onset but reached comparable symptom control by the second month.
trials.ref: Najm et al., 2004, BMC Musculoskeletal Disorders

tech.chem: S-adenosyl-L-methionine: a sulfonium compound formed by fusing the amino acid L-methionine with ATP. The methyl group sits on a positively charged sulfonium sulfur, which makes it a strained, high-energy leaving group — chemically 'primed' for transfer, which is the whole point of the molecule. It is synthesised endogenously in essentially every cell and is not obtained in meaningful amounts from food; supplements use stable salts (tosylate disulfate, butanedisulfonate) to improve shelf-stability, enteric-coated because the free molecule is acid-labile.

targets.action: SAM-e is the methyl-donating cosubstrate — it is consumed, not a receptor ligand
targets.affinity: no receptor binding constant applies; what governs the effect is the intracellular SAM-e:SAH ratio (methylation potential)
targets.role: the entire mechanism: SAM-e supplies the methyl group for DNA/histone methylation, neurotransmitter and phospholipid methylation, and cartilage synthesis

targets.action: a representative SAM-e-dependent methyltransferase; uses SAM-e to methylate (and thereby inactivate) catecholamines like dopamine
targets.affinity: SAM-e is its methyl-donor substrate, not an inhibitor or agonist
targets.role: illustrates the neurotransmitter link — SAM-e supply and COMT genotype together shape catecholamine handling

targets.action: methionine adenosyltransferase — the enzyme that synthesises SAM-e from methionine + ATP
targets.affinity: the biosynthetic source of endogenous SAM-e
targets.role: sets how much SAM-e the body makes on its own; liver-specific MAT1A dominates hepatic methylation capacity

tech.signaling: There is no receptor cascade — SAM-e works through biochemistry, not signalling. The governing variable is the SAM-e:SAH ratio ('methylation potential'): high ratio permits methyltransferases to run, and accumulating SAH inhibits them. Beyond transmethylation, SAM-e feeds two other branches: transsulfuration (its downstream homocysteine can be routed to cysteine and glutathione, an antioxidant link) and aminopropylation (SAM-e is decarboxylated to donate aminopropyl groups for polyamine synthesis).

adme.absorb: Low oral bioavailability (~2-3%); acid-labile, so tablets are enteric-coated and taken on an empty stomach. Plasma peaks ~3-5 h after an enteric dose. Intramuscular/IV routes (used clinically in some countries) achieve far higher exposure.
adme.distribute: Crosses the blood-brain barrier into the CNS/CSF, which underlies the central effects; distributes into liver and joint tissue.
adme.metabolise: Not a CYP substrate. It is consumed within the methylation cycle: after donating its methyl group it becomes S-adenosylhomocysteine (SAH), which is hydrolysed to homocysteine plus adenosine. Homocysteine is then remethylated to methionine (folate/B12/betaine-dependent) or diverted into transsulfuration.
adme.excrete: Metabolic fragments and downstream products are cleared renally; the intact molecule has a short plasma half-life (~1.5-2 h).

tech.pgx: No single actionable pharmacogenetic switch analogous to caffeine's CYP1A2. Response is modulated by the methylation cycle's cofactor status — folate and vitamin B12 — and by MTHFR variants (e.g., C677T) that slow homocysteine remethylation and can raise homocysteine. COMT Val158Met alters the rate of SAM-e-dependent dopamine methylation. None of these are used to guide dosing in practice.
tech.dose: Depression: studied at 400-1600 mg/day (commonly 800 mg twice daily as an adjunct); osteoarthritis: ~1200 mg/day. Enteric-coated, empty stomach, titrated up from a low start to limit nausea, GI upset and insomnia. Prescription-strength (ademetionine) is also given parenterally in some countries for cholestasis.
tech.tolerance: No receptor down-regulation, so there is no tolerance in the classic pharmacological sense and nothing to 'cycle.' The practical ceilings are the recycling capacity of the folate/B12-dependent cycle and side effects at higher doses (GI upset, anxiety/insomnia, and — critically — precipitation of mania in bipolar disorder and additive serotonergic risk with SSRIs/SNRIs).
